summary: Long bones consist of epiphyses (growth centers), diaphyses (support), and epiphyseal plates (growth in length).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0119
content: The periosteum forms a tough fibrous membrane that coats the bone. The periosteum contains nerves, blood vessels, and bone-producing cells. The inner surface of the periosteum provides the materials for nutrition repair and facilitates growth in the diameter of the bone. The periosteum also plays a fundamental role in movement by providing the point of attachment for tendons.
summary: The periosteum is a tough membrane covering bones, providing nutrients, facilitating growth, and serving as a tendon attachment point.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0120
content: The medullary cavity is a space that runs down through the center of the diaphysis and contains fatty yellow marrow. The medullary cavity is predominantly composed of adipose tissue and serves as a useful energy reserve.
summary: The medullary cavity in the diaphysis stores yellow marrow, serving as an energy reserve.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0121
content: Articular (hyaline) cartilage covers the ends of articulating bones. Articular (hyaline) cartilage is a hard, white, shiny tissue that, along with synovial fluid, helps reduce friction in freely movable (synovial joints). Articular (hyaline) cartilage is fundamental for smooth joint action.
summary: Articular cartilage covers joint ends, reducing friction and enabling smooth movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0122
content: Short bones are similar in length and width and appear somewhat cubical in shape. Short bones consist predominantly of spongy bone tissue to maximize shock absorption. The carpals of the hands and tarsals of the feet fit this category.
summary: Short bones, like carpals and tarsals, are cube-shaped and primarily composed of spongy bone for shock absorption.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0123
content: Flat bones are thin bones comprising two layers of compact bone tissue surrounding a layer of spongy bone tissue. These bones are involved in protection of internal structures and also provide broad attachment sites for muscles. The flat bones include the sternum, scapulae, ribs, ilium, and cranial bones.
summary: Flat bones, like the sternum and ribs, are thin and protect internal structures while providing muscle attachment sites.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0124
content: Irregular bones are bones of unique shape and function that do not fit the characteristics of the other categories. These include the vertebrae, pelvic bones, and certain facial bones.
summary: Irregular bones, like vertebrae and facial bones, have unique shapes and functions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0125
content: Sesamoid bones are small bones embedded in a joint capsule or found in locations where a tendon passes over a joint. Sesamoid bones develop within particular tendons at a site of considerable friction or tension. They serve to improve leverage and protect the joint from damage.
summary: Sesamoid bones, like those in tendons, improve leverage and protect joints from damage.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0126
content: The majority of all bones have specific distinguishing structures known as surface markings. These structures are necessary for increasing the stability in joints as well as providing attachment sites for muscles. Surface markings can be divided into two simple categories: depressions and processes. Depressions are simply flattened or indented portions of the bone. A common depression is called a fossa. An example is the supraspinous or infraspinatus fossa located on the scapulae (shoulder blades). These are attachment sites for the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles, respectively.
summary: Bones have surface markings, like depressions and processes, that enhance joint stability and provide muscle attachment points.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0127
content: Surface markings can be divided into two simple categories: depressions and processes. Depressions are simply flattened or indented portions of the bone. A common depression is called a fossa. An example is the supraspinous or infraspinous fossa located on the scapulae (shoulder blades). These are attachment sites for the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles, respectively. Another form of a depression is known as a sulcus. This is simply a groove in a bone that allows soft tissue (i.e., tendons) to pass through. An example of this is the intertubercular sulcus located between the greater and lesser tubercles of the humerus (upper arm bone). This is commonly known as the groove for the biceps tendon.
summary: Bone surface markings include depressions like fossae and sulci, which serve as attachment points for muscles and tendons.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0128
content: Processes are projections protruding from the bone to which muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach. Some of the more common processes are called process, condyle, epicondyle, tubercle, and trochanter. Examples of processes include the spinous processes found on the vertebrae and the acromion and coracoid processes found on the scapulae. Condyles are located on the inner and outer portions at the bottom of the femur (thigh bone) and top of the tibia (shin bone) to form the knee joint. Epicondyles are located on the inner and outer portions of the humerus to help form the elbow joint. The tubercles are located at the top of the humerus at the glenohumeral (shoulder) joint. There are the greater and lesser tubercles, which are attachment sites for shoulder musculature. Finally, the trochanters are located at the top of the femur and are attachment sites for the hip musculature. The greater trochanter is commonly called the hipbone.
summary: Bone processes, such as tubercles, trochanters, and condyles, provide attachment points for muscles and ligaments.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0129
content: The vertebral column (also called the backbone, or spinal column) consists of a series of irregularly shaped bones, called vertebrae. These bones are divided into five different categories depending on where they are located in the backbone. The first seven vertebrae starting at the top of the spinal column are called the cervical vertebrae (cervical spine, C1–C7). These bones form a flexible framework and provide support and motion for the head. The next 12 vertebrae located in the upper and middle back are called the thoracic vertebrae (thoracic spine, T1–T12). These bones move with the ribs to form the rear anchor of the rib cage. Thoracic vertebrae are larger than cervical vertebrae and increase in size from top to bottom. Below the thoracic spine are the five vertebrae comprising the lumbar vertebrae (lumbar spine, L1–L5). These bones are the largest in the spinal column. These vertebrae support most of the body's weight and are attached to many of the back muscles. The lumbar spine is often a location of pain for individuals because these vertebrae carry the most amount of body weight and are subject to the largest forces and stresses along the spine. The sacrum is a triangular bone located just below the lumbar vertebrae. It consists of four or five sacral vertebrae in a child, which become fused into a single bone during adulthood. The bottom of the spinal column is called the coccyx or tailbone. It consists of three to five bones that are fused together in an adult. Many muscles connect to the coccyx.
summary: The vertebral column is composed of cervical, thoracic, lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal vertebrae, providing support, flexibility, and attachment points for muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0130
content: In between the vertebrae are intervertebral discs made of fibrous cartilage that act as shock absorbers and allow the back to move. In addition to allowing humans to stand upright and maintain their balance, the vertebral column serves several other important functions. It helps to support the head and arms, while permitting freedom.
summary: Intervertebral discs cushion the vertebrae and facilitate movement, while the vertebral column supports the head, arms, and maintains balance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0131
content: The adult human spine has three major curvatures: a posterior cervical curvature, an anterior thoracic curvature, and a posterior lumbar curvature. The optimal arrangement of curves in the spine is referred to as a neutral spine.
summary: The adult spine exhibits three curvatures, and maintaining a neutral spine alignment is crucial.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0132
content: In addition to allowing humans to stand upright and maintain their balance, the vertebral column serves several other important functions. It helps to support the head and arms, while permitting freedom. The vertebral column consists of cervical vertebrae (C1–C7), thoracic vertebrae (T1–T12), lumbar vertebrae (L1–L5), sacrum (S1–S5 fused), and coccyx. The optimal arrangement of curves in the spine is referred to as a neutral spine. The adult human spine has three major curvatures: a posterior cervical curvature, an anterior thoracic curvature, and a posterior lumbar curvature.
summary: The vertebral column's functions include balance, support, and freedom of movement. It's composed of distinct vertebrae and characterized by specific curvatures.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0133
content: Joints are formed by one bone articulating with another bone. Joints can be categorized by their structure and function. Joint motion is referred to as arthrokinematics, with the three major motion types being roll, slide, and spin. In a rolling movement, one joint rolls across the surface of another. An example of roll in the body is the femoral condyles moving over the tibial condyles during a squat. In a sliding movement, one joint’s surface slides across another. An example of slide in the human body is the tibial condyles moving across the femoral condyles during a knee extension. In a spinning movement, one joint surface rotates on another. An example of a spin movement in the human body is the head of the radius rotating on the end of the humerus during pronation and supination of the forearm.
summary: Joints are categorized by structure and function, with motion types including roll, slide, and spin, each illustrated by specific examples.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0134
content: Synovial joints comprise approximately 80% of all the joints in the body and have the greatest capacity for motion. Synovial joints are characterized by a synovial membrane (the inner layer of the capsule) and hyaline cartilage which pads the ends of the articulating bones. Synovial joints produce synovial fluid which resembles egg whites and works like engine oil. Synovial fluid is secreted within the joint capsule from the synovial membrane and is essential for lubricating the joint surfaces to reduce excessive wear and to nourish the cartilage cells that line the joint.
summary: Synovial joints, the most mobile type, are characterized by a synovial membrane, hyaline cartilage, and synovial fluid, which lubricates and nourishes the joint.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0135
content: There are several types of synovial joints in the body including gliding (plane), condyloid (condylar or ellipsoidal), hinge, saddle, pivot, and ball-and-socket joints. A gliding (plane) joint is a nonaxial joint that has the simplest movement of all joints and moves either back and forth or side to side. Examples of gliding joints include the joint between the navicular bone and the second and third cuneiform bones in the foot or the carpals of the hand and in the facet (spine) joints. Condyloid (condylar or ellipsoidal) joints are termed so because the condyle of one bone fits into the elliptical cavity of another bone to form the joint. Movement in condyloid joints predominantly occurs in one plane (flexion and extension in the sagittal plane) with minimal movement in the others (rotation in the transverse plane; adduction and abduction in the frontal plane). Examples of condyloid joints are seen in the wrist between the radius and carpals and in the joints of the fingers (metacarpophalangeal).
summary: Synovial joints encompass various types, including gliding, condyloid, hinge, saddle, pivot, and ball-and-socket, each with unique characteristics and movements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0136
content: The hinge joint is a uniaxial joint allowing movement predominantly in only one plane of motion, the sagittal plane. Joints such as the elbow, interphalangeal (toe), and ankle are considered hinge joints. The saddle joint is named after its appearance. One bone looks like a saddle with the articulating bone straddling it like a rider. The saddle joint is only found in the carpometacarpal joint in the thumb and allows movement predominantly in two planes of motion (flexion and extension in the sagittal plane; adduction and abduction in the frontal plane) with some rotation to produce circumduction (circular motion). Pivot joints allow movement in predominantly one plane of motion (rotation, pronation, and supination in the transverse plane). These joints are found in the atlantoaxial joint at the base of the skull (top of spine) and the proximal radioulnar joint at the elbow.
summary: Hinge joints allow movement in one plane, while saddle joints permit movement in two planes and pivot joints allow rotation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0137
content: Ball-and-socket joints are the most mobile of the joints and allow movement in all three planes. Examples of ball-and-socket joints are the shoulder and hip. Nonsynovial joints are named as such because they have no joint cavity, fibrous connective tissue, or cartilage in the uniting structure. These joints exhibit little to no movement. Examples of nonsynovial joints are seen in the sutures of the skull, the distal joint of the tibia and fibula, and the symphysis pubis (pubic bones).
summary: Ball-and-socket joints offer the greatest mobility, while nonsynovial joints are immobile due to the lack of a joint cavity and cartilage.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0138
content: Joints serve numerous functional requirements of the musculoskeletal system; most importantly, joints allow for motion and thus movement. Joints also provide stability, allowing for movement to take place without unwanted movement. All joints in the human body are linked together, which implies that movement of one joint directly affects the motion of others. This is an essential concept for personal trainers to understand because it creates an awareness of how the body functionally operates and is the premise behind kinetic chain movement. The concept of kinetic chain movement is easy to demonstrate by standing with both feet firmly on the ground and rolling the feet inward and outward, noticing the movement of the knees and hips.
summary: Joints enable movement, provide stability, and are interconnected, influencing each other's motion. This interconnectedness forms the basis of kinetic chain movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0139
content: Joints serve numerous functional requirements of the musculoskeletal system; most importantly, joints allow for motion and thus movement. Joints also provide stability, allowing for movement to take place without unwanted movement. All joints in the human body are linked together, which implies that movement of one joint directly affects the motion of others. This is an essential concept for personal trainers to understand because it creates an awareness of how the body functionally operates and is the premise behind kinetic chain movement.
summary: Joints enable movement and stability, and their interconnectedness forms the basis of kinetic chain movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0140
content: The concept of kinetic chain movement is easy to demonstrate by standing with both feet firmly on the ground and rolling the feet inward and outward, noticing the movement of the knees and hips. Kinetic chain movement is demonstrated by keeping the feet stationary and rotating the hips, noticing the movement of the knees and feet. If one joint is not working properly, it will affect other joints.
summary: Kinetic chain movement demonstrates how interconnected joints influence each other.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0141
content: Nonsynovial joints have no joint cavity and fibrous connective tissue and allow little or no movement. Sutures of the skull are an example of nonsynovial joints. Synovial joints produce synovial fluid, have a joint cavity, and fibrous connective tissue. The knee is an example of a synovial joint.
summary: Nonsynovial and synovial joints differ in structure and range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0142
content: Gliding joints have no axis of rotation and move by sliding side-to-side or back and forth. The carpals of the hand are an example of gliding joints. Condyloid joints are formed by the fitting of condyles of one bone into elliptical cavities of another and move predominantly in one plane. The knee is an example of a condyloid joint. Hinge joints are uniaxial and move predominantly in one plane of motion (sagittal). The elbow is an example of a hinge joint.
summary: Different types of synovial joints allow for specific movements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0143
content: Saddle joints have one bone fitting like a saddle on another bone and move predominantly in two planes (sagittal, joint of thumb frontal). The only example of a saddle joint is the carpometacarpal joint. Pivot joints have only one axis and move predominantly in one plane of motion (transverse). The radioulnar joint is an example of a pivot joint. Ball-and-socket joints are the most mobile and move in all three planes of motion. The shoulder is an example of a ball-and-socket joint.
summary: Various joint types allow for diverse ranges of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0144
content: Ligaments are fibrous connective tissues that connect bone to bone and provide static and dynamic stability as well as input to the nervous system (proprioception). Ligaments are primarily made up of collagen with varying amounts of elastin. Collagen fibers are situated in a more parallel fashion to the forces placed on the ligament, providing tensile strength. The anterior cruciate ligament of the knee contains very little elastin and is predominantly composed of collagen, making it better suited for resisting strong forces and stabilizing the knee.
summary: Ligaments connect bones, provide stability, and contribute to proprioception.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0145
content: Ligaments have poor vascularity (blood supply), meaning they do not heal or repair well and may be slower to adapt to stresses placed on the body.
summary: Ligament healing is limited due to poor blood supply.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0146
content: Bone is living tissue that responds to exercise by becoming stronger. Individuals who exercise regularly generally achieve greater peak bone mass than those who do not. Exercising helps maintain muscle strength, coordination, and balance, which helps prevent falls and related fractures.
summary: Exercise strengthens bones and improves balance, reducing fall risk.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0147
content: Weight-bearing exercise is the best kind of exercise to help strengthen bones because it forces bones to work against gravity. Examples of weight-bearing exercises include resistance training, walking, body weight squats, push-ups, jogging, climbing stairs, and dancing.
summary: Weight-bearing exercises are crucial for bone health.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0148
content: Examples of non-weight-bearing exercises include swimming and bicycling.
summary: Non-weight-bearing exercises offer benefits but don't directly strengthen bones.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0149
content: The skeletal system is the body's framework and is made up of bones and joints in two divisions: axial and appendicular. There are many types of bones, all of which have markings of depressions or processes. Bones are connected (via ligaments) by either synovial or nonsynovial joints, which both provide movement as well as stability. Joints are interconnected, and movement of one will affect the others. Like muscle, bone is living tissue that responds to exercise by becoming stronger. Individuals who exercise regularly generally achieve greater peak bone mass (maximal bone density and strength) than those who do not.
summary: The skeletal system comprises bones, joints, and ligaments, all of which are influenced by exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0150
content: Bones are connected (via ligaments) by either synovial or nonsynovial joints, which both provide movement as well as stability. Joints are interconnected, and movement of one will affect the others. The nervous system is the control center for movement production, and the skeletal system provides the structural framework for our bodies. Muscles generate internal tension that, under the control of the nervous system, manipulates the bones of our body to produce movements. Muscles are the movers and stabilizers of our bodies.
summary: This chunk describes the interconnectedness of the skeletal and muscular systems, highlighting their roles in movement and stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0151
content: There are many types of bones, all of which have markings of depressions or processes. Like muscle, bone is living tissue that responds to exercise by becoming stronger. Individuals who exercise regularly generally achieve greater peak bone mass (maximal bone density and strength) than those who do not.
summary: This chunk focuses on the nature of bones, their responsiveness to exercise, and the benefits of regular physical activity for bone health.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0152
content: Skeletal muscle is one of three major muscle types in the body; the others are cardiac and smooth muscle. Skeletal muscle is made up of individual muscle fibers, and the term 'muscle' literally refers to multiple bundles of muscle fibers held together by connective tissue.
summary: This chunk defines skeletal muscle and its structure, differentiating it from other muscle types.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0153
content: Bundles of muscle fibers can be further broken down into layers from the outer surface to the innermost layer. The first bundle is the actual muscle itself wrapped by an outer layer of connective tissue called fascia and an inner layer immediately surrounding the muscle called the epimysium. The fascia and epimysium are also connected to bone and help to form the muscle's tendon.
summary: This chunk describes the hierarchical organization of skeletal muscle, starting from the outermost layer (fascia) and progressing to individual muscle fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0154
content: The next bundle of muscle fiber is called a fascicle. Each fascicle is wrapped by connective tissue called perimysium. Each fascicle is in turn made up of many individual muscle fibers that are wrapped by connective tissue called endomysium. Connective tissues within the muscle play a vital role in movement. They allow the forces generated by the muscle to be transmitted from the contractile components of the muscle to the bones, creating motion. Each layer of connective tissue extends the length of the muscle, helping to form the tendon.
summary: This chunk continues the description of muscle structure, focusing on fascicles, perimysium, endomysium, and their role in muscle function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0155
content: Tendons are the structures that attach muscles to bone and provide the anchor from which the muscle can exert force and control the bone and joint. Tendons are very similar to ligaments in that they have poor vascularity (blood supply), which leaves them susceptible to slower repair and adaptation.
summary: This chunk defines tendons, their function, and their similarities to ligaments.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0156
content: Muscle fibers are encased by a plasma membrane known as the sarcolemma and contain cell components such as cellular plasma called sarcoplasm (which contains glycogen, fats, minerals, and oxygen-binding myoglobin), nuclei, and mitochondria (which transform energy from food into energy for the cell). Unlike other cells, they also have structures called myofibrils.
summary: This chunk describes the internal structure of a muscle fiber, highlighting its unique features compared to other cell types.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0157
content: Myofibrils contain myofilaments that are the actual contractile components of muscle tissue. These myofilaments are known as actin (thin stringlike filaments) and myosin (thick filaments). The actin (thin) and myosin (thick) filaments form a number of repeating sections within a myofibril. Each one of these particular sections is known as a sarcomere. It lies in the space between two Z lines. Each Z line denotes another sarcomere along the myofibril.
summary: This chunk focuses on the contractile components of muscle tissue, explaining the structure of myofibrils, sarcomeres, and their arrangement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0158
content: A sarcomere is the functional unit of the muscle, much like the neuron is for the nervous system. Two protein structures that are also important to muscle contraction are tropomyosin and troponin. Tropomyosin is located on the actin filament and blocks myosin binding sites located on the actin filament, keeping myosin from attaching to actin when the muscle is in a relaxed state. Troponin, also located on the actin filament, plays a role in muscle contraction by providing binding sites for both calcium and tropomyosin when a muscle needs to contract.
summary: This chunk delves into the sarcomere's function as the basic unit of muscle contraction and explains the roles of tropomyosin and troponin in this process.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0159
content: Epimysium is deep fascia. Perimysium is a connective tissue surrounding muscle fascicles. Endomysium is connective tissue surrounding individual muscle fibers.
summary: This chunk defines the terms epimysium, perimysium, and endomysium, highlighting their roles in muscle structure.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0160
content: A capillary is a small blood vessel. Nuclei are the control centers of cells. The sarcolemma is the cell membrane of a muscle fiber. Sarcoplasm is the cytoplasm of a muscle fiber.
summary: This chunk defines key terms related to muscle cell structure and function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0161
content: A fasciculus is a bundle of muscle fibers.
summary: This chunk defines the term fasciculus.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0162
content: A single muscle fiber is a single cell. The sarcolemma is the cell membrane of a muscle fiber. Sarcoplasm is the cytoplasm of a muscle fiber. Endomysium is connective tissue surrounding individual muscle fibers. A fasciculus is a bundle of muscle fibers. Muscle belly refers to the main part of a muscle. Tendon connects muscle to bone.
summary: This chunk describes the basic anatomical structure of a muscle, from the individual fiber to the whole muscle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0163
content: Skeletal muscles require stimulation from motor neurons to contract. Neural activation is the communication link between the nervous system and the muscular system. A motor unit consists of a motor neuron and all the muscle fibers it innervates. The neuromuscular junction is the point where a motor neuron meets an individual muscle fiber. The neuromuscular junction is a synapse between a nerve and a muscle fiber.
summary: This chunk explains the role of the nervous system in muscle contraction, focusing on the motor unit and neuromuscular junction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0164
content: Electrical impulses called action potentials are transported from the central nervous system down the axon of a neuron. Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers that cross the neuromuscular junction. Acetylcholine (ACh) is the neurotransmitter used by the neuromuscular system. ACh stimulates muscle fibers to contract.
summary: This chunk details the process of neural transmission at the neuromuscular junction, highlighting the role of action potentials and acetylcholine.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0165
content: The sliding filament theory describes how thick and thin filaments within a sarcomere slide past one another. The sliding filament theory shortens the sarcomere and produces force. Excitation-contraction coupling is the process of neural stimulation creating a muscle contraction. Excitation-contraction coupling involves a series of steps starting with neural activation and ending with muscle contraction. A sarcomere shortens as a result of the Z lines moving closer together. Myosin heads attach to the actin filament and pull the actin filament across the myosin, resulting in shortening of the muscle fiber.
summary: This chunk explains the mechanism of muscle contraction, from the sliding filament theory to the role of sarcomeres and excitation-contraction coupling.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0166
content: Motor units cannot vary the amount of force they generate; they either contract maximally or not at all. The overall strength of a skeletal muscle contraction depends on the size of the motor unit recruited and the number of motor units activated. Muscles that control precise movements have many small motor units. Large muscles and muscle groups have many motor units with a larger number of muscle fibers.
summary: This chunk discusses the recruitment of motor units and how it influences muscle force production.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0167
content: Muscle fiber types vary in their chemical and mechanical properties. There are two main categories of muscle fibers, type I and type II fibers. Type I (slow-twitch) muscle fibers contain a large number of capillaries, mitochondria, and myoglobin. Myoglobin is similar to hemoglobin, the red pigment found in red blood cells, and therefore type I muscle fibers are often referred to as red fibers. Type II (fast-twitch) muscle fibers are subdivided into type IIa and type IIx based again on their chemical and mechanical properties. Type II muscle fibers are often referred to as white fibers.
summary: This chunk describes the two main types of muscle fibers, type I and type II, and their distinguishing characteristics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0168
content: Type IIx muscle fibers have a low oxidative capacity (ability to use oxygen) and fatigue quickly. Type IIa muscle fibers have a higher oxidative capacity and fatigue more slowly than type IIx. Type IIa muscle fibers are also known as intermediate fast-twitch fibers. Type IIa muscle fibers can use both aerobic and anaerobic metabolism almost equally to create energy. Type I, or slow-twitch, muscle fibers, are smaller in size (diameter), slower to produce maximal tension, and more resistant to fatigue. Type I fibers are important for muscles that need to produce the long-term contractions necessary for stabilization and postural control. Fast-twitch or type II muscle fibers are larger in size, quick to produce maximal tension, and fatigue more quickly than type I fibers.
summary: This chunk provides further details about the properties and functions of type IIa and type IIx muscle fibers, as well as the general characteristics of type I fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0169
content: Type I, or slow-twitch, muscle fibers, are smaller in size (diameter), slower to produce maximal tension, and more resistant to fatigue. Type I fibers are important for muscles that need to produce the long-term contractions necessary for stabilization and postural control. All muscles have a combination of slow- and fast-twitch fibers that will vary depending on the function of the muscle. The human anterior tibialis muscle has approximately 73% slow-twitch type I muscle fibers, whereas the lateral head of the gastrocnemius has approximately 49% slow-twitch type I muscle fibers.
summary: Describes the characteristics and distribution of slow-twitch muscle fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0170
content: Fast-twitch or type II muscle fibers are larger in size, quick to produce maximal tension, and fatigue more quickly than type I fibers. These fibers are important for muscles producing movements requiring force and power such as performing a sprint.
summary: Describes the characteristics and function of fast-twitch muscle fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0171
content: Type IIa muscle fibers are also known as intermediate fast-twitch fibers. Type IIa muscle fibers can use both aerobic and anaerobic metabolism almost equally to create energy.
summary: Describes the characteristics and metabolic properties of type IIa muscle fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0172
content: The muscular system is made up of many individual fibers and attaches to bones by way of tendons. There are different muscle fiber types and arrangements of them that affect how they move. Muscles generate force through neural activation. The nervous system receives and delivers information throughout the body by way of neurons. The stimulation of the nervous system activates sarcomeres, which generates tension in the muscles. This tension is transferred through tendons to the bones, and this produces motion.
summary: Explains the basic structure and function of the muscular system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0173
content: The endocrine system is a system of glands that secrete hormones into the bloodstream to regulate a variety of bodily functions, including the control of mood, growth and development, tissue function, and metabolism. The endocrine system consists of host organs (known as glands), chemical messengers (hormones), and target (receptor) cells. Once a hormone is secreted from a gland, it travels through the bloodstream to target cells designed to receive its message. The target cells have hormone-specific receptors ensuring that each hormone will communicate only with specific target cells. Along the way, special proteins bind to some hormones, acting as carriers that control the amount of hormone that is available to interact with and affect the target cells. The endocrine system is responsible for regulating multiple bodily functions to stabilize the body’s internal environment much like a thermostat regulates the temperature in a room. The term endocrine literally means ‘hormone secreting’. Hormones produced by the endocrine system virtually affect all forms of human function including (but not limited to) triggering muscle contraction, stimulating protein and fat synthesis, activating enzyme systems, regulating growth and metabolism, and determining how the body will physically and emotionally respond to stress.
summary: Provides a comprehensive overview of the endocrine system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0174
content: The primary endocrine glands are the hypothalamus, pituitary, thyroid, and adrenal glands. The pituitary gland is often referred to as the ‘master’ gland of the endocrine system, because it controls the functions of the other endocrine glands. The pituitary has three different sections or lobes, the anterior, intermediate, and posterior lobes, and each lobe secretes specific types of hormones. The anterior lobe secretes growth hormone, prolactin (to stimulate milk production after giving birth), adrenocorticotropic hormone or ACTH (to stimulate the adrenal glands), thyroid-stimulating hormone or TSH (to stimulate the thyroid gland), follicle-stimulating hormone or FSH (to stimulate the ovaries and testes), and luteinizing hormone or LH (to stimulate the ovaries or testes). The intermediate lobe of the pituitary gland secretes melanocyte-stimulating hormone (to control skin pigmentation), and the posterior lobe secretes antidiuretic hormone. The pituitary gland is often referred to as the master gland. The hypothalamus and pituitary gland are located in the brain.
summary: Focuses on the pituitary gland and its role as the 'master gland' of the endocrine system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0175
content: The thyroid gland produces hormones that regulate the rate of metabolism and affect the growth and rate of function of many other systems in the body. Adrenal glands secrete hormones such as corticosteroids and catecholamines, including cortisol and adrenaline (epinephrine) in response to stress.
summary: Describes the functions of the thyroid and adrenal glands.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0176
content: Carbohydrate, specifically glucose, is the primary energy source during vigorous exercise. Carbohydrates are the human body’s key source of energy, and glucose is the principal fuel for the brain.
summary: Highlights the importance of carbohydrates as the primary energy source, especially during exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0177
content: The pancreas produces two specific hormones: insulin and glucagon. Insulin helps regulate energy and glucose metabolism in the body. After consuming a meal, glucose enters the blood at the small intestine, causing a rise in blood glucose levels. Elevated levels of glucose trigger the release of insulin from the pancreas. Insulin causes cells in the liver, muscle, and fat tissue to take up glucose from the blood, storing it as glycogen in the liver and muscle.
summary: Explains the roles of insulin and glucagon in regulating blood glucose levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0178
content: Glucagon is one of the two hormones secreted by the pancreas that regulate blood glucose levels. Glucagon functions to raise blood glucose levels by triggering the release of glycogen stores from the liver.
summary: Describes the function of glucagon in raising blood glucose levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0179
content: After consuming a meal, glucose enters the blood at the small intestine, causing a rise in blood glucose levels. Elevated levels of glucose trigger the release of insulin from the pancreas. Insulin causes cells in the liver, muscle, and fat tissue to take up glucose from the blood, storing it as glycogen in the liver and muscle.
summary: Glucose metabolism: After a meal, glucose enters the bloodstream, triggering insulin release, which promotes glucose uptake and storage as glycogen.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0180
content: Glucagon is one of the two hormones secreted by the pancreas that regulate blood glucose levels. Glucagon functions to raise blood glucose levels by triggering the release of glycogen stores from the liver.
summary: Glucagon's role: Glucagon counteracts low blood glucose by releasing stored glycogen from the liver.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0181
content: Hours after a meal, or as a result of a combination of normal metabolic processes and physical activity, the body will begin to exhibit lower blood glucose levels. As activity levels increase, glucose uptake by the body’s cells also increases. This is the result of an increased sensitivity of the cells to insulin; thus, insulin levels will drop during physical activity (52). At the same time glucagon secretion by the pancreas increases, thus helping maintain a steady supply of blood glucose.
summary: Blood glucose regulation during activity: Blood glucose levels decrease after meals, and physical activity increases glucose uptake, aided by glucagon.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0182
content: The two catecholamines—epinephrine (also known as adrenaline) and norepinephrine—are hormones produced by the adrenal glands, which are situated on top of each kidney. These hormones help prepare the body for activity; more specifically, they are part of the stress response known as the fight or flight response. In preparation for activity, the hypothalamus (part of the brain) triggers the adrenal glands to secrete more epinephrine.
summary: Catecholamines and stress response: Epinephrine and norepinephrine, produced by the adrenal glands, prepare the body for activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0183
content:
Epinephrine will have a number of specific physiological effects that will help sustain exercise activity (51, 52): N increases heart rate and stroke volume
N elevates blood glucose levels
N redistributes blood to working tissues
N opens up the airways
summary: Epinephrine's effects on exercise: Epinephrine increases heart rate, blood glucose, blood flow to working tissues, and airway openness during exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0184
content: Testosterone is produced in the testes of the male and in small amounts in the ovaries and adrenal glands of the female. Males produce up to 10 times more testosterone than females (53), and this is primarily responsible for the development of the male secondary sexual characteristics, such as facial and body hair and greater muscle mass.
summary: Testosterone: Primarily produced in males, testosterone is responsible for male secondary sexual characteristics and muscle mass.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0185
content: Estrogen is produced primarily in the ovaries in the female, with small amounts produced in the adrenals in males. Women of reproductive age have significantly higher levels of estrogen than males, which gives rise to female secondary sexual characteristics such as breast development and regulation of the menstrual cycle.
summary: Estrogen: Primarily produced in females, estrogen is responsible for female secondary sexual characteristics and menstrual cycle regulation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0186
content: For both males and females, however, testosterone plays a fundamental role in the growth and repair of tissue. Raised levels of testosterone are indicative of an anabolic (tissue-building) training status.
summary: Testosterone's role in tissue growth: Testosterone is essential for tissue growth and repair in both sexes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0187
content: Estrogen has many functions, but in particular has an influence on fat deposition around the hips, buttocks, and thighs.
summary: Estrogen's influence on fat deposition: Estrogen contributes to fat storage in specific areas of the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0188
content: In contrast to testosterone, cortisol is typically referred to as a catabolic hormone (associated with tissue breakdown). Under times of stress, such as exercise, cortisol is secreted by the adrenal glands and serves to maintain energy supply through the breakdown of carbohydrates, fats, and protein.
summary: Cortisol's role: Cortisol is a catabolic hormone that breaks down carbohydrates, fats, and protein to provide energy during stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0189
content: High levels of cortisol brought about through overtraining, excessive stress, poor sleep, and inadequate nutrition can lead to significant breakdown of muscle tissue, along with other potentially harmful side effects (53).
summary: Cortisol's negative effects: High cortisol levels can lead to muscle breakdown and other health issues.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0190
content: Growth hormone is released from the pituitary gland in the brain and is regulated by the nearby hypothalamus. Growth hormone is stimulated by estrogen, testosterone, deep sleep, and vigorous exercise.
summary: Growth hormone regulation: Growth hormone is released from the pituitary gland and stimulated by various factors, including sex hormones and exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0191
content: Growth hormone is primarily an anabolic hormone that is responsible for most of the growth and development during childhood up until puberty. Growth hormone increases the development of bone, muscle tissue, and protein synthesis.
summary: Growth hormone's role: Growth hormone is primarily responsible for growth and development during childhood and promotes bone, muscle, and protein synthesis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0192
content: Growth hormone increases fat burning and strengthens the immune system.
summary: Additional benefits of growth hormone: Growth hormone also increases fat burning and immune function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0193
content: The thyroid gland is located at the base of the neck just below the thyroid cartilage. The thyroid gland releases vital hormones that are primarily responsible for human metabolism.
summary: Thyroid gland function: The thyroid gland releases hormones crucial for human metabolism.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0194
content: The release of thyroid hormones is regulated by the pituitary gland. Thyroid hormones are responsible for carbohydrate, protein, and fat metabolism, basal metabolic rate, protein synthesis, sensitivity to epinephrine, heart rate, breathing rate, and body temperature.
summary: Thyroid hormone regulation and effects: Thyroid hormone release is controlled by the pituitary gland, and these hormones regulate various metabolic processes, including heart rate, breathing, and body temperature.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0195
content: Low thyroid function has become a well-recognized disorder leading to low metabolism, fatigue, depression, sensitivity to cold, and weight gain.
summary: Consequences of low thyroid function: Low thyroid function can lead to various symptoms, including fatigue, depression, and weight gain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0196
content: Research has indicated that testosterone and growth hormone levels increase after strength training and moderate to vigorous aerobic exercise. A similar pattern also seems to emerge for cortisol.
summary: Hormonal responses to exercise: Strength training and aerobic exercise increase testosterone, growth hormone, and cortisol levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0197
content: The presence of cortisol in the bloodstream is often taken to be indicative of overtraining.
summary: Cortisol as an indicator of overtraining: Elevated cortisol levels can suggest overtraining.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0198
content: Thyroid hormones are responsible for carbohydrate, protein, and fat metabolism, basal metabolic rate, protein synthesis, sensitivity to epinephrine, heart rate, breathing rate, and body temperature. Low thyroid function has become a well-recognized disorder leading to low metabolism, fatigue, depression, sensitivity to cold, and weight gain.
summary: Thyroid hormones regulate metabolism, protein synthesis, and various physiological functions. Dysfunction can lead to metabolic issues, fatigue, and weight gain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0199
content: Research has indicated that testosterone and growth hormone levels increase after strength training and moderate to vigorous aerobic exercise. A similar pattern also seems to emerge for cortisol.
summary: Strength training and aerobic exercise elevate testosterone, growth hormone, and cortisol levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0200
content: The presence of cortisol in the bloodstream is often taken to be indicative of overtraining. Cortisol is a necessary part of maintaining energy levels during normal exercise activity and may even facilitate recovery and repair during the postexercise period. Problems may arise as a result of extremely intense or prolonged bouts of endurance training, which have been found to lower testosterone levels while raising cortisol levels. Under these circumstances, catabolism (breakdown) is likely to outstrip anabolism (build up) and give rise to symptoms of overtraining.
summary: Cortisol is essential for exercise, but excessive levels from overtraining can lead to hormonal imbalances and catabolism.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0201
content: The endocrine system is responsible for regulating multiple bodily functions to stabilize the body’s internal environment. Hormones produced by the endocrine system affect virtually all forms of human function and determine how the body physically and emotionally responds to stress. The endocrine system consists of host organs (known as glands), chemical messengers (or hormones), and target (or receptor) cells. Some of the major endocrine organs include the pituitary, hypothalamus, thyroid, and adrenal glands. Several other organs contain discrete areas of endocrine tissue that also produce hormones, including the pancreas and reproductive organs.
summary: The endocrine system regulates bodily functions through hormones, influencing physical and emotional responses to stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0202
content: Exercise programming has a significant impact on hormone secretion.
summary: Exercise influences hormone production.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0204
content: The cardiorespiratory system is composed of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems. The cardiovascular system consists of the heart, blood vessels, and blood. The respiratory system includes the trachea, bronchi, alveoli, and the lungs. The cardiovascular and respiratory systems work together to provide the body with adequate oxygen and nutrients and to remove waste products such as CO2 from cells in the body.
summary: The cardiorespiratory system, comprising the cardiovascular and respiratory systems, delivers oxygen and nutrients while removing waste products from the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0205
content: The cardiovascular system is composed of the heart, blood, and blood vessels that transport the blood from the heart to the tissues of the body. The heart is a muscular pump that rhythmically contracts to push blood throughout the body. The heart is positioned obliquely in the center of the chest, lying anteriorly to the spine and posteriorly to the sternum. The left and right lungs lie on either side of the heart.
summary: The cardiovascular system, including the heart, blood, and blood vessels, circulates blood throughout the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0206
content: Edin B, and Abbs JH. wrote *Finger movement responses of cutaneous mechanoreceptors in the dorsal skin of the human hand* in 1991. Gandevia SC, McClosky DI, and Burke D. wrote *Kinesthetic signals and muscle contraction* in 1992. McClosky DJ. wrote *Kinesthetic sensibility* in 1978. Lephart SM, Rieman BL, and Fu FH. wrote *Introduction to the sensorimotor system* in 2000. Lephart SM, Pincivero D, and Giraldo J, Fu F. wrote *The role of proprioception in the management and rehabilitation of athletic injuries* in 1997. Proske U, Schaible HG, and Schmidt RF. wrote *Joint receptors and kinaesthesia* in 1988.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0207

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0208
content: Chaffi n DB, Andersson GJ, and Martin BJ. wrote *Occupational Biomechanics* in 1999. Whiting WC, and Zernicke RF. wrote *Biomechanics of Musculoskeletal Injury* in 1998. Bogduk N. wrote *Clinical Anatomy of the Lumbar Spine and Sacrum* (3rd ed.) in 1997.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0209

The National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke website address is http://www.ninds.nih.gov/disorders/backpain/detail_backpain.htm#102183102. Accessed May 5, 2010.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0210
content: Hertling D, and Kessler RM. wrote *Management of Common Musculoskeletal Disorders* in 1996. Alter MJ. wrote *Science of Flexibility* (2nd ed.) in 1996. Gross J, Fetto J, and Rosen E. wrote *Musculoskeletal Examination* in 1996.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0211
content: Nordin M, Lorenz T, and Campello M. wrote *Biomechanics of tendons and ligaments* in 2001. Solomonow M, Baratta R, Zhou BH, et al. wrote *The synergistic action of the anterior cruciate ligament and thigh muscles in maintaining joint stability* in 1987.
summary: These works explore the biomechanics of tendons and ligaments, and the role of the anterior cruciate ligament in joint stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0212
content: McComas AJ. wrote *Skeletal Muscle: Form and Function* in 1996. Kannus P. wrote *Structure of the tendon connective tissue* in 2000.
summary: These works focus on skeletal muscle structure and function, and tendon connective tissue.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0213
content: Al-Amood WS, Buller AJ, and Pope R. wrote *Long-term stimulation of cat fast twitch skeletal muscle* in 1973. Buller AJ, Eccles JC, and Eccles RM. wrote *Interaction between motorneurons and muscles in respect of the characteristic speeds of their responses* in 1960.
summary: These works investigate muscle physiology, specifically focusing on fast-twitch muscle stimulation and motor neuron-muscle interactions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0214
content: Dubowitz V. published a work titled 'Cross-innervated mammalian skeletal muscle: histochemical, physiological and biomechanical observations' in the Journal of Physiology in 1967. Hennig R and Lomo T published a work titled 'Eff ects of chronic stimulation on the size and speed of long-term denervated and innervated rat fast and slow skeletal muscles' in Acta Physiologica Scandinavica in 1987.
summary: These publications explore the characteristics and adaptations of mammalian skeletal muscle, including cross-innervation and the effects of chronic stimulation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0215
content: Johnson MA, Polgar J, Weightman D, and Appleton D published a work titled 'Data on the distribution of fi ber types in thirty-six human muscles' in the Journal of Neurological Sciences in 1973. Green HJ, Daub B, Houston ME, Th omson JA, Fraser I, and Ranney D published a work titled 'Human vastus lateralis and gastrocnemius muscles. A com-parative histochemical analysis' in the Journal of Neurological Sciences in 1981.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0216
content: McArdle W, Katch F, and Katch V published a work titled 'Exercise Physiology: Nutrition, Energy and Human Performance' in its 7th edition in 2010. Tortora GJ and Grabowski SR published a work titled 'Principles of Anatomy and Physiology' in its 8th edition in 1996. Wilmore JH and Costill DL published a work titled 'Physiology of Sport and Exercise' in 2004. McArdle W, Katch F, and Katch V published a work titled 'Exercise Physiology: Nutrition, Energy and Human Performance' in its 5th edition in 2001.
summary: These textbooks cover exercise physiology, anatomy, and physiology, providing comprehensive insights into human performance and the body's systems.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0217
content: The cardiovascular and respiratory systems work together to provide the body with adequate oxygen and nutrients and to remove waste products such as CO2 from cells in the body. The cardiovascular system is composed of the heart, blood, and blood vessels that transport the blood from the heart to the tissues of the body. The heart is a muscular pump that rhythmically contracts to push blood throughout the body. The heart is positioned obliquely in the center of the chest, lying anteriorly to the spine and posteriorly to the sternum. The left and right lungs lie on either side of the heart. The heart is contained in the area of the chest known as the mediastinum. The adult heart is approximately the size of a typical adult fist and weighs roughly 300 g (approximately 10 ounces).
summary: The cardiovascular system, including the heart, blood, and blood vessels, works with the respiratory system to deliver oxygen and nutrients while removing waste products.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0218
content: Cardiac muscle is one of three major types of muscle, the others being skeletal and smooth muscle. Cardiac muscle is similar to skeletal muscle in that cardiac muscle cells contain myofibrils and sarcomeres aligned side by side, which give them their striated appearance. Cardiac muscle is involuntary muscle, meaning that it cannot typically be consciously controlled. Cardiac muscle fibers are shorter and more tightly connected than skeletal muscle. Another unique feature of cardiac muscle is the presence of irregularly spaced dark bands between cardiac cells called intercalated discs. Intercalated discs help hold muscle cells together during contraction and create an electrical connection between the cells that allows the heart to contract as one functional unit. The heart has its own built-in conduction system, unlike skeletal muscle, that sends an electrical signal rapidly throughout all the cardiac cells.
summary: Cardiac muscle, responsible for heart contractions, shares similarities with skeletal muscle but possesses unique features like intercalated discs and an intrinsic conduction system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0219
content: The typical resting heart rate is between 70 and 80 beats per minute. The electrical conduction system of the heart consists of specialized cells that allow an electrical signal to be transmitted from the sinoatrial (SA) node through both atria and down into the ventricles. The electrical conduction system of the heart stimulates the mechanical myocardial cells to contract in a regular rhythmic pattern. The SA node, located in the right atrium, is referred to as the pacemaker for the heart because it initiates the electrical signal that causes the heart to beat. The internodal pathways transfer the impulse from the SA node to the atrioventricular (AV) node. The AV node delays the impulse before allowing it to move on to the ventricles. The AV bundle conducts the impulse to the ventricles for contraction via the left and right bundle branches of the Purkinje fibers.
summary: The heart's electrical conduction system, initiated by the SA node, regulates the rhythmic contractions of the heart.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0220
content: The heart is composed of four hollow chambers that are delineated into two interdependent (but separate) pumps on either side. These two pumps are separated by the interatrial septum (separates the atria) and interventricular septum (separates the ventricles). Each side of the heart has two chambers: an atrium and a ventricle. The right side of the heart is referred to as the pulmonic side because it receives blood from the body that is low in O2 and high in CO2 (deoxygenated) and pumps it to the lungs and then back to the left atrium. The left side of the heart is referred to as the systemic side because it pumps blood high in O2 and low in CO2 (oxygenated) to the rest of the body. The atria are smaller chambers, located superiorly (on top) on either side of the heart. The right atrium gathers deoxygenated blood returning to the heart from the entire body, whereas the left atrium gathers oxygenated blood coming to the heart from the lungs. The ventricles are larger chambers located inferiorly (on bottom) on either side of the heart. Unlike the right ventricle, which has thin walls and pumps under low pressure because it only has to pump blood a short distance (to the lungs), the left ventricle has thicker walls and pumps under high pressure because it pumps blood out to the rest of the body. The right ventricle receives the deoxygenated blood from the right atrium and then pumps it to the lungs to be saturated with incoming oxygen. The left ventricle receives the oxygenated blood from the left atrium and proceeds to pump it to the entire body.
summary: The heart's four chambers, divided into two pumps, circulate blood throughout the body, delivering oxygenated blood to the tissues and returning deoxygenated blood to the lungs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0221
content: Each chamber of the heart is separated from one another and major veins and arteries via valves to prevent a backflow or spillage of blood back into the chambers. These valves include the atrioventricular valves (tricuspid and mitral valves) and the semilunar valves (pulmonary and aortic valves). The amount of blood pumped out of the heart with each contraction is referred to as stroke volume (SV). The SV is the difference between the ventricular end-diastolic volume (EDV) and the end-systolic volume (ESV). The EDV is the filled volume of the ventricle before contraction, and the ESV is the residual volume of blood remaining in the ventricle after ejection. In a typical heart, the EDV is about 120 mL of blood and the ESV about 50 mL of blood. The difference in these two volumes, 70 mL, represents the SV. The rate at which the heart beats is referred to as heart rate (HR).
summary: Heart valves prevent backflow, while stroke volume (SV) and heart rate (HR) quantify blood flow.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0222
content: The SV is the difference between the ventricular end-diastolic volume (EDV) and the end-systolic volume (ESV). The EDV is the filled volume of the ventricle before contraction, and the ESV is the residual volume of blood remaining in the ventricle after ejection. In a typical heart, the EDV is about 120 mL of blood and the ESV about 50 mL of blood. The difference in these two volumes, 70 mL, represents the SV.
summary: This chunk defines stroke volume (SV) as the difference between EDV and ESV, explaining their roles in ventricular function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0223
content: Cardiac output (Q) is the volume of blood pumped by the heart per minute (mL blood/min). Cardiac output is a function of heart rate and stroke volume. If an average person has a resting heart rate of 70 bpm and a resting stroke volume of 70 mL/beat, cardiac output at rest would be: 4,900 mL/min or 4.9 L/min. Monitoring heart rate during exercise provides a good estimate of the amount of work the heart is doing at any given time.
summary: This chunk defines cardiac output (Q) and its relationship to heart rate and stroke volume, emphasizing its importance in exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0224
content: The rate at which the heart beats is referred to as heart rate (HR). An average resting heart rate for an untrained adult is approximately 70 to 80 beats per minute (bpm). Heart rate (HR) is the rate at which the heart pumps. Cardiac output (Q) is heart rate / stroke volume, the overall performance of the heart.
summary: This chunk defines heart rate (HR) and its relationship to cardiac output (Q), highlighting their interconnectedness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0225
content: Some people use the carotid artery in the neck to measure pulse rate, but NASM does not recommend this location. Pressure on the carotid artery reduces blood flow to the brain, which can cause dizziness or an inaccurate measurement. When measuring the pulse during rest, count the number of beats in 60 seconds. When measuring the pulse during exercise, count the number of beats in 6 seconds and add a zero to that number. One can also multiply the number by 10 to get the pulse rate in beats per minute.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on measuring heart rate, emphasizing safety and accuracy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0226
content: Another common procedure used to monitor heart rate is with a heart rate monitor, which is worn on the body and automatically derives the beats per minute.
summary: This chunk introduces heart rate monitors as a convenient method for tracking heart rate.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0227
content: Blood is a unique life-sustaining fluid that supplies the body’s organs and cells with oxygen and nutrients and helps regulate body temperature, fight infections, and remove waste products. Blood consists of cells suspended in a watery liquid called plasma that also contains nutrients such as glucose, hormones, and clotting agents. There are three kinds of cells in the blood: red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.
summary: This chunk provides a general overview of blood, its composition, and its vital functions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0228
content: Red blood cells carry oxygen from the lungs throughout the body. White blood cells help fight infection. Platelets help with clotting. Plasma makes up about 55% of the total volume of the blood, and the remaining 45% is made up of red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets. The average adult has between 4 to 6 L of blood in his or her body.
summary: This chunk elaborates on the specific roles of blood cells and plasma, providing quantitative information about blood volume.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0229
content: Blood transports life-sustaining oxygen to all bodily tissues and removes waste products. Blood also transports hormones that act as chemical messengers and nutrients from the gastrointestinal tract to various organs and tissues throughout the body and helps remove heat from internal to external regions of the body.
summary: This chunk highlights the diverse transport functions of blood, including oxygen, waste, hormones, and nutrients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0230
content: Blood helps regulate body temperature by transferring heat from the internal core out to the periphery of the body as blood circulates throughout the body. As blood travels close to the skin it gives off heat to the environment or can be cooled depending on the environment.
summary: This chunk focuses on blood's role in thermoregulation, explaining how it transfers heat.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0231
content: Blood is also essential in the regulation of the pH levels (acid balance) in the body as well as maintaining the water content of body cells.
summary: This chunk emphasizes blood's role in maintaining pH balance and cellular hydration.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0232
content: Blood provides protection from excessive blood loss through its clotting mechanism, which seals off damaged tissue until a scar forms. Blood provides specialized immune cells to fight against foreign toxins within the body, which helps to reduce the risk of disease and illness.
summary: This chunk highlights blood's protective functions, including clotting and immune defense.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0233
content: Blood vessels form a closed circuit of hollow tubes that allow blood to be transported to and from the heart. There are three major types of blood vessels: arteries, capillaries, and veins.
summary: This chunk introduces the circulatory system, outlining the three main types of blood vessels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0234
content: Arteries carry blood away from the heart. Capillaries are the site of exchange of chemicals and water between the blood and the tissues. Veins carry blood back to the heart.
summary: This chunk describes the directional flow and functional roles of arteries, capillaries, and veins.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0235
content: The largest artery in the body is the aorta, which carries blood away from the heart. The branches of the aorta include medium-sized arteries, including the carotid artery, the subclavian artery, the mesenteric arteries, the renal artery, and the iliac artery.
summary: This chunk focuses on the aorta and its major branches, illustrating the branching pattern of the arterial system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0236
content: Arteries carry blood away from the heart. The largest artery in the body is the aorta, which carries blood away from the heart. The branches of the aorta include medium-sized arteries, including the carotid artery, the subclavian artery, the mesenteric arteries, the renal artery, and the iliac artery. Medium-sized arteries further divide into smaller arteries called arterioles and eventually into microscopic vessels known as capillaries.
summary: This chunk describes the structure of arteries, starting from the aorta and branching down to capillaries.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0237
content: Capillaries are the site of exchange of chemicals and water between the blood and the tissues. Capillaries are where substances such as oxygen, nutrients, hormones, and waste products are exchanged between tissues. Vessels that collect blood from the capillaries are called venules.
summary: This chunk focuses on the function and structure of capillaries, emphasizing their role in exchange between blood and tissues.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0238
content: Venules progressively merge with other venules to form veins. Veins then transport all of the blood from the body back to the heart.
summary: This chunk describes the pathway of blood flow from capillaries to veins, ultimately returning to the heart.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0239
content: The cardiorespiratory system is composed of the cardiovascular system and the respiratory system. Together, they provide the body with oxygen, nutrients, protective agents, and a means to remove waste products. The cardiovascular system is composed of the heart, blood, and blood vessels.
summary: This chunk introduces the cardiorespiratory system, highlighting its components and overall function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0240
content: The heart is located in the mediastinum and is made up of involuntary cardiac muscle, which contracts according to a built-in rhythm to regularly pump blood throughout the body. The heart is divided into four chambers: two atria (which gather blood from the body) and two ventricles (which pump blood out to the body) on each side.
summary: This chunk describes the heart's location, structure, and function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0241
content: The heart rate and the stroke volume make up the overall performance of the heart. Cardiac output is the combination of how many times the heart beats per minute and how much blood is being pumped out with each beat. Heart rate can be monitored manually or through use of a heart rate monitor.
summary: This chunk explains key cardiac performance indicators: heart rate, stroke volume, and cardiac output.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0242
content: Blood acts as a medium to deliver and collect essential products to and from the tissues of the body, providing an internal transportation, regulation, and protection system.
summary: This chunk describes the multifaceted role of blood in the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0243
content: The vessels that transport blood away from the heart are called arteries (which have smaller components called arterioles). The vessels that bring blood back to the heart are called veins (which have smaller components called venules). Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels and connect venules with arterioles.
summary: This chunk clarifies the different types of blood vessels and their connections.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0244
content: The function of the respiratory system (also known as the pulmonary system) is to bring oxygen into the lungs and remove carbon dioxide from the lungs to the outside air. The respiratory system includes airways, lungs, and the respiratory muscles.
summary: This chunk defines the respiratory system and its primary function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0245
content: The primary role of the respiratory system is to ensure proper cellular function. The respiratory system works intimately with the cardiovascular system. The respiratory system's function is to transport oxygen from the environment to the bloodstream and carbon dioxide from the blood to the lungs.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the respiratory system's crucial role in cellular function and its close relationship with the cardiovascular system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0246
content: This process is accomplished by the integrated functioning of the respiratory pump to move air in and out of the body and the respiratory passageways to channel air. Breathing, or ventilation, is the process of moving air in and out of the body and requires optimal functioning of the respiratory pump and all its components.
summary: This chunk explains the mechanics of breathing and its dependence on the respiratory pump.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0247
content: Breathing is divided into two phases: inspiration (inhalation) and expiration (exhalation). Inspiratory ventilation is active, requiring contraction of inspiratory muscles to increase thoracic cavity volume, decreasing intrapulmonary pressure. When intrapulmonary pressure decreases below atmospheric pressure, air is drawn into the lungs.
summary: This chunk details the two phases of breathing: inspiration and expiration, focusing on the active process of inhalation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0248
content: Expiration is the process of actively or passively relaxing inspiratory muscles to move air out of the body. Inspiratory ventilation occurs in two forms: normal resting state (quiet) breathing and heavy (deep, forced) breathing. Normal breathing requires the use of the primary respiratory muscles (diaphragm, external intercostals), while heavy breathing requires the additional use of secondary respiratory muscles (scalenes, pectoralis minor).
summary: This chunk describes the process of expiration and the different types of breathing patterns.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0249
content: Expiratory ventilation can be both active and passive. During normal breathing, expiratory ventilation is passive, resulting from relaxation of contracting inspiratory muscles. During heavy or forced breathing, expiratory ventilation relies on the activity of expiratory muscles to compress the thoracic cavity and force air out.
summary: This chunk explains the active and passive aspects of expiratory ventilation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0250
content: Breathing helps regulate blood flow back to the heart. The respiratory pump acts as a mechanism that helps pump blood back to the heart during inspiration.
summary: This chunk highlights the connection between breathing and blood flow.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0251
content: Expiratory ventilation can be both active and passive. During normal breathing, expiratory ventilation is passive, resulting from relaxation of contracting inspiratory muscles. During heavy or forced breathing, expiratory ventilation relies on the activity of expiratory muscles to compress the thoracic cavity and force air out.
summary: Expiratory ventilation can be passive during normal breathing or active during heavy breathing, involving expiratory muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0252
content: Breathing helps regulate blood flow back to the heart. The respiratory pump acts as a mechanism that helps pump blood back to the heart during inspiration. During inspiration, intrathoracic pressure decreases, causing a drop in pressure in the right atrium of the heart, improving blood flow back to the heart. The respiratory pump is composed of skeletal structures (bones) and soft tissues (muscles) that work together to allow proper respiratory mechanics and help pump blood back to the heart during inspiration.
summary: Breathing assists blood flow back to the heart through the respiratory pump mechanism, which lowers pressure in the right atrium during inspiration.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0253
content: Inspiration is the process of actively contracting inspiratory muscles to move air into the body. Expiration is the process of actively or passively relaxing inspiratory muscles to move air out of the body. The purpose of ventilation is to move air in and out of the body.
summary: Ventilation involves active inspiration and passive or active expiration to move air in and out of the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0254
content: The respiratory passages are divided into two categories: conducting airways and respiratory airways. The conducting airways consist of structures that air travels through before entering the respiratory airways (nasal and oral cavities, mouth, pharynx, larynx, trachea, and bronchioles). The conducting airways provide a gathering station for air and oxygen to be directed into the body and allow incoming air to be purified, humidified, and warmed or cooled to match body temperature. The respiratory airways collect channeled air from the conducting airways and end in the alveoli, which are made up of clusters of alveolar sacs. It is in the alveolar sacs that gases such as oxygen (O2) and carbon dioxide (CO2) are transported in and out of the bloodstream through diffusion.
summary: The respiratory system comprises conducting airways that purify and condition air, and respiratory airways that facilitate gas exchange in the alveoli.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0255
content: Diffusion is the process of getting oxygen from the environment to the tissues of the body. The respiratory system collects oxygen from the environment and transports it to the bloodstream. Breathing is divided into the inspiratory phase (or inhalation) and expiratory phase (or exhalation). Inspiratory ventilation is active, whereas expiratory ventilation can be both active and passive. There are two groups of respiratory passages. The first group is the conducting airways, which consist of all the structures that air travels through before entering the respiratory airways. The conducting airways purify, humidify, and warm or cool air to match body temperature. The second group is the respiratory airways, which collect the channeled air coming from the conducting airways and allow gases such as oxygen and carbon dioxide to be transferred into and out of the bloodstream.
summary: Breathing involves active inhalation and passive or active exhalation, facilitating gas exchange through the conducting and respiratory airways.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0256
