Shoulder elevation during the pulling assessment is a potential compensation. Head migration forward during the pulling assessment is a potential compensation.
summary: The pulling assessment evaluates pulling movement efficiency and potential muscle imbalances.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0491
content: Low back arches during the pulling assessment may indicate overactive hip flexors and underactive erector spinae and intrinsic core stabilizers. Shoulder elevation during the pulling assessment may indicate overactive upper trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, and levator scapulae and underactive mid/lower trapezius. Head migration forward during the pulling assessment may indicate overactive upper trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, and levator scapulae and underactive deep cervical flexors.
summary: Potential muscle imbalances indicated by compensations during the pulling assessment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0492
content: Posture is the alignment and function of all parts of the kinetic chain. The main purpose of posture is to overcome constant forces placed on the body by maintaining structural efficiency. The kinetic chain requires constant postural equilibrium. Proper postural alignment puts the body in a state of optimal neuromuscular efficiency, allowing for proper joint mechanics and effective distribution of force throughout the kinetic chain. Proper posture allows the body to produce high levels of functional strength. Without proper posture, the body may degenerate or experience postural distortion patterns.
summary: Posture's importance for structural efficiency, neuromuscular function, and strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0493
content: A dynamic postural observation examines basic movements and provides crucial information about how muscles and joints interact. A dynamic postural observation searches for any imbalances in anatomy, physiology, or biomechanics.
summary: Dynamic postural observation assesses movement and identifies imbalances.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0494
content: Performance assessments can be used for clients looking to improve athletic performance. Performance assessments measure upper extremity stability and muscular endurance, lower extremity agility, and overall strength. Basic performance assessments include the push-up test, Davies test, shark skill test, bench press strength assessment, and squat strength assessment.
summary: Performance assessments evaluate athletic potential and include various tests.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0495
content: The push-up test measures muscular endurance of the upper body, primarily the pushing muscles. The push-up assessment involves touching the chest to a partner's fist or the floor for 60 seconds or until exhaustion without compensating (arches low back, extends cervical spine). A variation to the push-up assessment includes performing push-ups from a kneeling position.
summary: The push-up test assesses upper body muscular endurance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0496
content: Does the torso lean forward excessively? Do the arms fall forward?
summary: Questions to assess form during exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0497
content: Performance assessments measure upper extremity stability and muscular endurance, lower extremity agility, and overall strength. Basic performance assessments include the push-up test, Davies test, shark skill test, bench press strength assessment, and squat strength assessment.
summary: Performance assessments evaluate various fitness components, including upper extremity stability, muscular endurance, lower extremity agility, and overall strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0498
content: The push-up test measures muscular endurance of the upper body, primarily the pushing muscles. The push-up assessment involves touching the chest to a partner's fist or the floor for 60 seconds or until exhaustion without compensating (arches low back, extends cervical spine). A variation to the push-up assessment includes performing push-ups from a kneeling position. The client or athlete should be able to perform more push-ups when reassessed.
summary: The push-up test assesses upper body muscular endurance, with variations for different fitness levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0499
content: The Davies Test measures upper extremity agility and stabilization. The Davies Test procedure involves placing two pieces of tape on the floor, 36 inches apart. The client assumes a push-up position with one hand on each piece of tape. The client quickly moves their right hand to touch their left hand. The client performs alternating touching on each side for 15 seconds. The client repeats the Davies Test for three trials. The Davies Test may not be suitable for clients or athletes who lack shoulder stability.
summary: The Davies Test evaluates upper extremity agility and stability, but may not be appropriate for individuals with shoulder instability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0500
content: The Shark Skill Test assesses lower extremity agility and neuromuscular control. The Shark Skill Test is a progression from the single-leg squat and may not be suitable for all individuals. The client positions themselves in the center box of a grid, with hands on hips and standing on one leg. The client hops to each box in a designated pattern, always returning to the center box. The client performs one practice run through the boxes with each foot. The client performs the Shark Skill Test twice with each foot (four times total). The client's time is recorded. 0.10 seconds is added for each fault: non-hopping leg touches the ground, hands come off hips, foot goes into the wrong square, or foot does not return to the center square.
summary: The Shark Skill Test measures lower extremity agility and neuromuscular control, but may not be appropriate for all fitness levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0501
content: The Upper Extremity Strength Assessment: Bench Press is designed to estimate the one-rep maximum on overall upper body strength of the pressing musculature. The Bench Press test can also be used to determine training intensities of the bench press. The Bench Press test is considered an advanced assessment for strength-specific goals and may not be suitable for many clients. Personal trainers should not perform the Bench Press test for clients with general fitness or weight-loss goals. The client lies on a bench with feet pointed straight ahead and a neutral low back position.
summary: The Bench Press test estimates upper body strength and is suitable for advanced clients with strength-specific goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0502
content: The squat assessment is designed to estimate the one-repetition squat maximum and overall lower body strength. This test can also be used to determine training intensities for the squat exercise. This is considered an advanced assessment (for strength-specific goals) and may not be suitable for many clients. Generally, personal trainers should not perform this assessment for clients with general fitness or weight-loss goals. Feet should be shoulders-width apart, pointed straight ahead, and with knees in line with the toes. The low back should be in a neutral position. Instruct the client to warm up with a light resistance that can be easily performed for 8 to 10 repetitions. Take a 1-minute rest. Add 30 to 40 pounds (10–20% of initial load) and perform 3 to 5 repetitions. Take a 2-minute rest. Repeat steps 3 and 4 until the client achieves failure between 2 and 10 repetitions (3 to 5 repetitions for greater accuracy). Use the one-rep maximum estimation chart in the appendix to calculate one-repetition max.
summary: The squat assessment estimates lower body strength and is suitable for advanced clients with strength-specific goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0503
content: Brian is 24 years old. Brian is a semiprofessional soccer player. Brian travels often, competing in various soccer tournaments. Brian likes to work out with weights three to four times per week. Brian practices soccer five days per week. Brian plays in an organized soccer game at least two times per week.
summary: Brian's background: a 24-year-old semiprofessional soccer player who trains and competes frequently.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0504
content: Brian had surgery for a torn anterior cruciate ligament in his left knee three years ago. Brian has sprained his left ankle two times since his knee surgery. Brian went through physical therapy for his last ankle sprain six months ago. Brian was cleared to work out and play again six months ago.
summary: Brian's injury history: ACL surgery three years ago, two ankle sprains since, and recent clearance to return to activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0505
content: Brian's knee and ankle have not been giving him any trouble, other than some occasional soreness after games and practice. Brian recently went through a physical to begin playing again. Brian's physician gave him a clean bill of health.
summary: Brian's current health status: no major issues, cleared to play by his physician.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0506
content: Brian wants to increase his overall performance by enhancing his flexibility, speed, cardiorespiratory efficiency, and leg strength. Brian wants to decrease his risk of incurring other injuries. Brian increased his body fat percentage after being out of soccer because of his injury. Brian wants to lower his body fat percentage.
summary: Brian's fitness goals: improved performance, injury prevention, and body fat reduction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0507
content: Recommended objective assessments for Brian include body fat measurement, the 3-minute step test or Rockport Walk Test, the overhead squat assessment, the single-leg squat assessment, the Davies test, the Shark skill test, and the lower extremity strength assessment: squat.
summary: Suggested assessments for Brian: body composition, cardiovascular fitness, mobility, strength, and skill.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0508
content: Take a 2-minute rest. Repeat steps 3 and 4 until the client achieves failure between 2 and 10 repetitions (3 to 5 repetitions for greater accuracy). Use the one-rep maximum estimation chart in the appendix to calculate one-repetition max.
summary: Instructions for a training protocol: rest, repetitions to failure, and 1RM estimation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0509
content: DeCarlo M, Klootwyk T, and Shelbourne D. published a study on ACL surgery and accelerated rehabilitation in the Journal of Sport Rehabilitation in 1997.  Irrgang J and Harner C. published a study on recent advances in ACL rehabilitation: clinical factors in the Journal of Sport Rehabilitation in 1997.  Hewett TE, Lindenfeld TN, Riccobene JV, and Noyes FR. published a study on the effect of neuromuscular training on the incidence of knee injury in female athletes in the American Journal of Sports Medicine in 1999.
summary: Studies on ACL rehabilitation, including surgery, training, and injury prevention in female athletes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0510
content: Ekholm J, Eklund G, and Skoglund S. published a study on the reflex effects from the knee joint of the cat in Acta Physiologica Scandinavica in 1960. Johansson H. published a study on the role of knee ligaments in proprioception and regulation of muscle stiffness in the Journal of Electromyography and Kinesiology in 1991. Johansson H, Sjölander P, and Sojka P. published a study on a sensory role for the cruciate ligaments in Clinical Orthopaedics and Related Research in 1991. Johansson H, Sjölander P, and Sojka P. published a study on receptors in the knee joint ligaments and their role in the biomechanics of the joint in Critical Reviews in Biomedical Engineering in 1991. Nyland J, Smith S, Beickman K, Armsey T, and Caborn D. published a study on frontal plane knee angle affects dynamic postural control strategy during unilateral stance in the Medicine & Science in Sports & Exercise in 2002.
summary: Studies on knee joint reflexes, proprioception, ligament function, and postural control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0511
content: Feagin J. published a study on the syndrome of the torn ACL in Orthopedic Clinics of North America in 1979. Ireland ML, Willson JD, Ballantyne BT, and Davis IM. published a study on hip strength in females with and without patellofemoral pain in the Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy in 2003. Powers C. published a study on the influence of altered lower-extremity kinematics on patellofemoral joint dysfunction: a theoretical perspective in the Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy in 2003.
summary: Studies on ACL injuries, hip strength, and patellofemoral joint dysfunction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0512
content: Irrgang J, Whitney S, and Cox E. published a study on balance and proprioceptive training for rehabilitation of the lower extremity in the Journal of Sport Rehabilitation in 1994.
summary: Study on balance and proprioceptive training for lower extremity rehabilitation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0513
content: Bullock-Saxton JE, Janda V, and Bullock MI. published a study on reflex activation of gluteal muscles in walking in Spine in 1993. Hodges P, Richardson C, and Jull G. published a study on the evaluation of the relationship between laboratory and clinical tests of transversus abdominis function in Physiotherapy Research International in 1996. Hodges PW and Richardson CA. published a study on inefficient muscular stabilization of the lumbar spine associated with low back pain in Spine in 1996. Hodges PW and Richardson CA. published a study on contraction of the abdominal muscles associated with movement of the lower limb in Physical Therapy in 1997. Janda V. wrote about muscles and motor control in low back pain: assessment and management in Twomey L.'s Physical Therapy of the Low Back in 1987. O’Sullivan P, Twomey L, Allison G, Sinclair J, Miller K, and Knox J. published a study on altered patterns of abdominal muscle activation in patients with chronic low back pain in the Australian Journal of Physiotherapy in 1997. Richardson C, Jull G, Toppenberg R, and Comerford M. published a study on techniques for active lumbar stabilization for spinal protection in the Australian Journal of Physiotherapy in 1992.
summary: Studies on low back pain, abdominal muscle function, and lumbar stabilization techniques.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0514
content: Broström L-Å, Kronberg M, and Nemeth G. published a study on muscle activity during shoulder dislocation in Acta Orthopaedica Scandinavica in 1989.  Howell S and Kraft T. published a study on the role of the supraspinatus and infraspinatus muscles in glenohumeral kinematics of anterior shoulder instability in Clinical Orthopaedics and Related Research in 1991.  Kedgley A, Mackenzie G, Ferreira L, Johnson J, and Faber K. published a study on in vitro kinematics of the shoulder following rotator cuff injury in Clinical Biomechanics (Bristol, Avon) in 2007.  Kronberg M, Broström L-Å, and Nemeth G published a study in 1991 titled 'Diff erences in shoulder muscle activity between patients with generalized joint laxity and normal controls' in Clin Orthop Relat Res.  Y anagawa T, Goodwin C, Shelburne K, Giphart J, Torry M, and Pandy M published a study in 2008 titled 'Contributions of the individual muscles of the shoulder to glenohumeral joint stability during abduction' in J Biomech Eng.  Y asojima T, Kizuka T, Noguchi H, Shiraki H, Mukai N, and Miyanaga Y published a study in 2008 titled 'Diff erences in EMG activity in scapular plane abduction under variable arm positions and loading conditions' in Med Sci Sports Exerc.
summary: Studies on shoulder muscle activity, kinematics, and stability, including rotator cuff injuries.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0515
content: Glousman R. published a study on electromyographic analysis and its role in the athletic shoulder in Clinical Orthopaedics and Related Research in 1993. Glousman R, Jobe F, Tibone J, Moynes D, Antonelli D, and Perry J. published a study on dynamic electromyographic analysis of the throwing shoulder with glenohumeral instability in the Journal of Bone and Joint Surgery American Volume in 1988. Hanson ED, Leigh S, and Mynark RG. published a study on acute effects of heavy- and light-load squat exercise on the kinetic measures of vertical jumping in the Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research in 2007.
summary: Studies on electromyography, shoulder function, and squat exercise effects on jumping.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0516
content: Lewit K. wrote about muscular and articular factors in movement restriction in Manual Medicine in 1985.
summary: Study on muscular and articular factors contributing to movement restriction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0517
content: Graven-Nielsen T and Mense S published a study in 2001 titled 'Th e peripheral apparatus of muscle pain: evidence from animal and human studies' in Clin J Pain. Mense S and Simons D authored a book titled 'Muscle Pain. Understanding Its Nature, Diagnosis, and Treatment' published by Williams & Wilkins in 2001.
summary: Studies on the peripheral mechanisms of muscle pain and its diagnosis and treatment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0518
content: Lambert E, Bohlmann I, and Cowling K published a study in 2001 titled 'Physical activity for health: understanding the epidemiological evidence for risk benefi ts' in Int J Sports Med. Pate R, Pratt M, Blair S, et al. published a recommendation in 1995 titled 'Physical activity and public health: a recommendation from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and the American College of Sports Medicine' in JAMA. The American College of Sports Medicine published a position stand in 2001 titled 'Appropriate intervention strategies for weight loss and prevention for weight regain in adults' in Med Sci Sports Exerc. The American College of Sports Medicine published 'ACSM’s Resource Manual for Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription' (3rd ed.) in 1998.
summary: Studies and recommendations on physical activity, public health, and weight management.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0519
content: Going S and Davis R wrote about 'Body composition' in Roitman JL's (Ed.) ACSM’s Resource Manual for Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription (4th ed.) published by Lippincott Williams & Wilkins in 2001. Durnin JVGA and Womersley J published a study in 1974 titled 'Body fat assessed from total body density and its estimation from skinfold thickness measurements on 481 men and women aged 16-72 years' in Br J Nutr. Stevens J published a study in 1998 titled 'Th e eff ect of age on the association between body-mass index and mortality' in N Engl J Med. Sahrmann SA authored a book titled 'Diagnosis and Treatment of Movement Impairment Syndromes' published by Mosby in 2002.
summary: Studies and publications on body composition, its assessment, and movement impairment syndromes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0520
content: Durnin JVGA and Womersley J published a study in 1974 titled 'Body fat assessed from total body density and its estimation from skinfold thickness measurements on 481 men and women aged 16-72 years' in Br J Nutr. Stevens J published a study in 1998 titled 'Th e eff ect of age on the association between body-mass index and mortality' in N Engl J Med.
summary: Two studies examining body fat assessment and the relationship between age and body mass index.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0521
content: The American College of Sports Medicine published a position stand in 2001 titled 'Appropriate intervention strategies for weight loss and prevention for weight regain in adults' in Med Sci Sports Exerc. The American College of Sports Medicine published 'ACSM’s Resource Manual for Guidelines for Exercise Testing and Prescription' (3rd ed.) in 1998.
summary: Two publications from the American College of Sports Medicine addressing weight management and exercise prescription guidelines.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0522
content: Sahrmann SA authored a book titled 'Diagnosis and Treatment of Movement Impairment Syndromes' published by Mosby in 2002. Sahrmann SA published a study in 1992 titled 'Posture and muscle Imbalance. Faulty lumbo- pelvic alignment and associated musculoskeletal pain syndromes' in Orthop Div Rev Can Phys Th er.
summary: Two works by Sahrmann SA focusing on movement impairment syndromes and postural imbalances.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0523
content: Kendall FP, McCreary EK, and Provance PG authored a book titled 'Muscles: Testing and Function' (4th ed.) published by Williams & Wilkins in 1993. Norkin C and Levangie P authored a book titled 'Joint Structure and Function' (2nd ed.) published by FA Davis Company in 1992.
summary: Two books providing foundational knowledge on muscles and joint function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0524
content: Janda V published a chapter titled 'Muscle strength in relation to muscle length, pain and muscle imbalance' in Harms–Rindahl K's (ed.) 'Muscle Strength' published by Churchill–Livingston in 1993. Janda V published a chapter titled 'Muscles and motor control in cervicogenic disorders' in Grant R's (ed.) 'Physical Th erapy of the Cervical and Th oracic Spine' published by Churchill Livingstone in 2002.
summary: Two chapters by Janda V exploring muscle strength, imbalances, and their role in cervicogenic disorders.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0525
content: Powers CM, Ward SR, Fredericson M, Guillet M, and Shellock FG published a study in 2003 titled 'Patellofemoral kinematics during weight-bearing and non-weight-bearing knee extension in persons with lateral sub-luxation of the patella: a preliminary study' in J Orthop Sports Phys Th er. Newmann D authored a book titled 'Kinesiology of the Musculoskeletal System: Foundations for Physical Rehabilitation' published by Mosby in 2002.
summary: A study on patellofemoral kinematics and a book on musculoskeletal kinesiology.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0526
content: Zeller B, McCrory J, Kibler W, and Uhl T published a study in 2003 titled 'Diff erences in kinematics and electromyographic activity between men and women during the single-legged squat' in Am J Sports Med. Buckley BD, Th igpen CA, Joyce CJ, Bohres SM, and Padua DA published a study in 2007 titled 'Knee and hip kinematics during a double leg squat predict knee and hip kinematics at initial contact of a jump landing task' in J Athl Train.
summary: Two studies investigating kinematic differences between genders during squats and the relationship between squat kinematics and jump landing mechanics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0527
content: Ireland ML, Willson JD, Ballantyne BT, and Davis IM published a study in 2003 titled 'Hip strength in females with and without patellofemoral pain' in J Orthop Sports Phys Th er. Vesci BJ, Padua DA, Bell DR, Strickland LJ, Guskiewicz KM, and Hirth CJ published a study in 2007 titled 'Infl uence of hip muscle strength, fl exibility of hip and ankle musculature, and hip muscle activation on dynamic knee valgus motion during a double-legged squat' in J Athl Train. Bell DR and Padua DA published a study in 2007 titled 'Infl uence of ankle dorsifl exion range of motion and lower leg muscle activation on knee valgus during a double-legged squat' in J Athl Train.
summary: Three studies examining the relationship between hip strength, flexibility, and knee valgus during squats.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0528
content: Goldbeck T and Davies GJ published a study in 2000 titled 'Test-retest reliability of a closed kinetic chain upper extremity stability test: a clinical fi eld test' in J Sport Rehab.
summary: A study evaluating the reliability of a closed kinetic chain upper extremity stability test.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0529
content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. Research by Schoenfeld (2010) showed that deep squats performed below parallel produced 7% greater glute activation than parallel squats. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: Description of the squat exercise, its primary muscle targets, and a typical strength training prescription.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0530
content: Tissue will only achieve efficient extensibility if optimal control of movement is maintained throughout the entire range of motion. Optimal control of movement throughout a joint’s entire range of motion is referred to as dynamic range of motion. Dynamic ROM is the combination of flexibility and the nervous system’s ability to control this range of motion efficiently.
summary: Definition and explanation of dynamic range of motion and its importance for tissue extensibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0531
content: Factors that can influence flexibility include genetics, connective tissue elasticity, composition of tendons or skin surrounding the joint, joint structure, strength of opposing muscle groups, body composition, sex, age, activity level, previous injuries or existing medical issues, and repetitive movements (pattern overload).
summary: List of factors influencing flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0532
content: Neuromuscular efficiency is the ability of the nervous system to recruit the correct muscles (agonists, antagonists, synergists, and stabilizers) to produce force (concentrically), reduce force (eccentrically), and dynamically stabilize the body’s structure in all three planes of motion.
summary: Definition of neuromuscular efficiency and its role in movement control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0533
content: When performing a cable pulldown exercise, the latissimus dorsi (agonist) must be able to concentrically accelerate shoulder extension, adduction, and internal rotation while the middle and lower trapezius and rhomboids (synergists) perform downward rotation of the scapulae. At the same time, the rotator cuff musculature (stabilizers) must dynamically stabilize the glenohumeral (shoulder) joint throughout the motion. If these muscles (force-couples) do not work in tandem efficiently, compensations may ensue, leading to muscle imbalances, altered joint motion, and possible injury.
summary: Description of muscle activation patterns during a cable pulldown exercise and the importance of coordinated muscle function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0534
content: To allow for optimal neuromuscular efficiency, individuals must have proper flexibility in all three planes of motion. This allows for the freedom of movement needed to perform everyday activities effectively, such as bending over to tie shoes or reaching in the top cupboard for dishes. Flexibility requires extensibility, which requires dynamic range of motion, which requires neuromuscular efficiency.
summary: The importance of flexibility for neuromuscular efficiency and daily activities, highlighting the interconnectedness of flexibility, dynamic range of motion, and neuromuscular efficiency.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0535
content: Flexibility training must use a multifaceted approach, which integrates various flexibility techniques to achieve optimal soft tissue extensibility in all planes of motion.
summary: Recommendation for a comprehensive approach to flexibility training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0536
content: The human movement system (HMS), also known as the kinetic chain, comprises the muscular, skeletal, and nervous systems. Optimal alignment and function of each component of the HMS is the cornerstone of a sound training program. If one or more segments of the HMS are misaligned and not functioning properly, predictable patterns of dysfunction develop. These patterns of dysfunction are referred to as postural distortion patterns, which can lead to decreased neuromuscular efficiency and tissue overload.
summary: Description of the human movement system, the importance of proper alignment and function, and the concept of postural distortion patterns.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0537
content: Postural distortion patterns (poor static or dynamic posture) are represented by a lack of structural integrity, resulting from decreased functioning of one (or more) components of the HMS. A lack of structural integrity can result in altered length-tension relationships (altered muscle lengths), altered force-couple relationships (altered muscle activation), and altered arthrokinematics (altered joint motion).
summary: Explanation of postural distortion patterns, their impact on structural integrity, and the resulting consequences for muscle function and joint motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0538
content: If one or more segments of the HMS are misaligned and not functioning properly, predictable patterns of dysfunction develop. These patterns of dysfunction are referred to as postural distortion patterns, which can lead to decreased neuromuscular efficiency and tissue overload. Postural distortion patterns (poor static or dynamic posture) are represented by a lack of structural integrity, resulting from decreased functioning of one (or more) components of the HMS. A lack of structural integrity can result in altered length-tension relationships (altered muscle lengths), altered force-couple relationships (altered muscle activation), and altered arthrokinematics (altered joint motion).
summary: Postural distortion patterns arise from HMS misalignment, leading to decreased neuromuscular efficiency and altered joint mechanics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0539
content: There are several postural distortions that personal trainers must be aware of, all of which are reviewed in the Fitness Assessment chapter (chapter six). Maximal neuromuscular efficiency of the HMS can only exist if all components (muscular, skeletal, and neural) function optimally and interdependently. The ultimate goal of the HMS is to maintain homeostasis (or dynamic postural equilibrium).
summary: Personal trainers should be familiar with postural distortions, as optimal HMS function requires balanced muscular, skeletal, and neural components to maintain postural equilibrium.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0540
content: Poor flexibility can lead to the development of relative flexibility (or altered movement patterns), which is the process in which the HMS seeks the path of least resistance during functional movement patterns. A prime example of relative flexibility is seen in people who squat with their feet externally rotated. Because most people have tight calf muscles they lack the proper amount of dorsiflexion at the ankle to perform a squat with proper mechanics. By widening the stance and externally rotating the feet it is possible to decrease the amount of dorsiflexion required at the ankle to perform a squat using good technique.
summary: Relative flexibility, the body's tendency to find the path of least resistance during movement, can manifest as altered squat mechanics due to limited dorsiflexion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0541
content: A second example can be seen when people perform an overhead shoulder press with excessive lumbar extension (arched lower back). Individuals who possess a tight latissimus dorsi will have decreased sagittal-plane shoulder flexion (inability to lift arms directly overhead), and as a result, they compensate for this lack of range of motion at the shoulder in the lumbar spine to allow them to press the load completely above their head.
summary: Relative flexibility can also lead to compensatory movement patterns during overhead shoulder presses, such as excessive lumbar extension due to tight latissimus dorsi.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0542
content: Relative flexibility is the tendency of the body to seek the path of least resistance during functional movement patterns.
summary: Relative flexibility describes the body's tendency to adopt movement patterns that minimize effort, even if they are not optimal.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0543
content: Muscle imbalances are alterations in the lengths of muscles surrounding a given joint in which some are overactive (forcing compensation to occur) and others may be underactive (allowing for the compensation to occur). Muscle imbalance can be caused by a variety of mechanisms. Causes of muscle imbalance may include postural stress, emotional duress, repetitive movement, cumulative trauma, poor training technique, lack of core strength, and lack of neuromuscular efficiency.
summary: Muscle imbalances occur when some muscles are overactive and others are underactive, often due to factors like posture, stress, repetitive movements, or training technique.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0544
content: Muscle imbalances may be caused by or result in altered reciprocal inhibition, synergistic dominance, arthrokinetic dysfunction, and overall decreased neuromuscular control.
summary: Muscle imbalances can contribute to altered reciprocal inhibition, synergistic dominance, arthrokinetic dysfunction, and reduced neuromuscular control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0545
content: Reciprocal inhibition is a naturally occurring phenomenon that allows movement to take place. Reciprocal inhibition is defined as the simultaneous contraction of one muscle and the relaxation of its antagonist. Altered reciprocal inhibition is caused by a tight agonist muscle decreasing the neural drive to its functional antagonist.
summary: Reciprocal inhibition is the simultaneous contraction of a muscle and relaxation of its antagonist, essential for movement. Altered reciprocal inhibition occurs when a tight agonist muscle inhibits its antagonist.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0546
content: Altered reciprocal inhibition alters force-couple relationships, produces synergistic dominance, and leads to the development of faulty movement patterns, poor neuromuscular control, and arthrokinetic (joint) dysfunction.
summary: Altered reciprocal inhibition disrupts force-couple relationships, promotes synergistic dominance, and contributes to faulty movement patterns, poor neuromuscular control, and joint dysfunction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0547
content: Synergistic dominance is a neuromuscular phenomenon that occurs when synergists take over function for a weak or inhibited prime mover.
summary: Synergistic dominance happens when supporting muscles take over the role of a weak or inhibited primary mover.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0548
content: Arthrokinetic dysfunction is a biomechanical and neuromuscular dysfunction leading to altered joint motion. Altered joint motion can be caused by altered length-tension relationships and force-couple relationships, which affect the joint and cause poor movement efficiency. Arthrokinetic dysfunction is altered forces at the joint that result in abnormal muscular activity and impaired neuromuscular communication at the joint.
summary: Arthrokinetic dysfunction refers to altered joint motion caused by imbalances in muscle length, force-couple relationships, and neuromuscular communication.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0549
content: Performing a squat with excessively externally rotated feet forces the tibia and femur to also rotate externally. This posture alters the length-tension relationships of the muscles at the knees and hips. The gluteus maximus is put in a shortened position when the feet are excessively externally rotated during a squat. The gluteus maximus's ability to generate force is decreased when the feet are excessively externally rotated during a squat. The biceps femoris and piriformis become synergistically dominant when the feet are excessively externally rotated during a squat. The force-couple relationships and ideal joint motion are altered when the feet are excessively externally rotated during a squat. Stress on the knees and low back is increased when the feet are excessively externally rotated during a squat (17).
summary: Excessive external foot rotation during squats alters muscle length-tension relationships, weakens the gluteus maximus, and increases stress on the knees and lower back.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0550
content: Neuromuscular efficiency is the ability of the neuromuscular system to properly recruit muscles to produce force (concentrically), reduce force (eccentrically), and dynamically stabilize (isometrically) the entire kinetic chain in all three planes of motion.
summary: Neuromuscular efficiency refers to the ability of the neuromuscular system to effectively recruit and control muscles for force production, force reduction, and dynamic stabilization across all planes of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0551
content: Mechanoreceptors located in the muscles and tendons help to determine muscle balance or imbalance. Muscle spindles are the major sensory organ of the muscle and are composed of microscopic fibers that lie parallel to the muscle fiber. Muscle spindles are sensitive to change in muscle length and rate of length change (5, 18–25). The function of the muscle spindle is to help prevent muscles from stretching too far or too fast. When a muscle on one side of a joint is lengthened, the spindles of the lengthened muscle are stretched. The information from stretched muscle spindles is transmitted to the brain and spinal cord, exciting the muscle spindle and causing the muscle fibers of the lengthened muscle to contract.
summary: Muscle spindles, located in muscles and tendons, are sensitive to muscle length changes and help prevent excessive stretching. They transmit information to the brain and spinal cord, triggering muscle contraction when stretched.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0552
content: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body during training.
summary: Progressive overload involves systematically increasing training stress over time to stimulate adaptation and progress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0553
content: Muscle spindles are the major sensory organ of the muscle and are composed of microscopic fibers that lie parallel to the muscle fiber. Muscle spindles are sensitive to change in muscle length and rate of length change (5, 18–25). The function of the muscle spindle is to help prevent muscles from stretching too far or too fast. When a muscle on one side of a joint is lengthened, the spindles of the lengthened muscle are stretched. The information from stretched muscle spindles is transmitted to the brain and spinal cord, exciting the muscle spindle and causing the muscle fibers of the lengthened muscle to contract. Micro muscle spasms or a feeling of tightness often result from the muscle spindle contraction. The hamstring complex is a prime example of this response when the pelvis is rotated anteriorly. The anterior superior iliac spines move downward (inferiorly) and the ischium moves upward (superiorly) when the pelvis is rotated anteriorly. The complex does not need to be statically stretched because it is already in a stretched position. When a lengthened muscle is stretched, it increases the excitement of the muscle spindles and further creates a contraction (spasm) response.
summary: Muscle spindles are sensory organs that protect muscles from excessive stretching by contracting in response to lengthening.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0554
content: The shortened hip flexors are helping to create the anterior pelvic rotation that is causing the lengthening of the hamstring complex. The hip flexors need to be stretched.
summary: Shortened hip flexors contribute to anterior pelvic rotation, lengthening the hamstrings and requiring stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0555
content: An individual whose knees adduct and internally rotate (knock-knees) during a squat exercise has an underactive gluteus medius (hip abductor and external rotator). The overactive muscles in an individual whose knees adduct and internally rotate (knock-knees) during a squat exercise include the adductors (inner thighs) and tensor fascia latae (a hip flexor and hip internal rotator).
summary: Knock-knees during squats indicate an underactive gluteus medius and overactive adductors and tensor fascia latae.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0556
content: Golgi tendon organs are located within the musculotendinous junction (or the point where the muscle and the tendon meet). Golgi tendon organs are sensitive to changes in muscular tension and the rate of tension change. When excited, the Golgi tendon organ causes the muscle to relax, which prevents the muscle from being placed under excessive stress, which could result in injury. Prolonged Golgi tendon organ stimulation provides an inhibitory action to muscle spindles.
summary: Golgi tendon organs, located at the muscle-tendon junction, sense tension and promote muscle relaxation to prevent injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0557
content: Autogenic inhibition occurs when the neural impulses sensing tension are greater than the impulses causing muscle contraction. Autogenic inhibition is one of the main principles used in flexibility training, particularly with static stretching in which one holds a stretch for a prolonged period. Holding a stretch creates tension in the muscle. This tension stimulates the Golgi tendon organ, which overrides muscle spindle activity in the muscle being stretched, causing relaxation in the overactive muscle and allowing for optimal lengthening of the tissue. Stretches should be held long enough for the Golgi tendon organ to override the signal from the muscle spindle (approximately 30 seconds).
summary: Autogenic inhibition, triggered by prolonged static stretching, allows muscles to relax and lengthen.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0558
content: Limited flexibility decreases the potential of the HMS. Limited flexibility forces the body to move in an altered fashion, leading to relative flexibility (faulty movement patterns).
summary: Limited flexibility restricts movement potential and can lead to faulty movement patterns.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0559
content: Muscle imbalances result from altered length-tension relationships, force-couple relationships, and arthrokinematics. Muscle imbalances can be caused by poor posture, poor training technique, or previous injury. Muscle imbalances result in altered reciprocal inhibition, synergistic dominance, and arthrokinetic dysfunction. Altered reciprocal inhibition, synergistic dominance, and arthrokinetic dysfunction lead to decreased neuromuscular control and possibly injury.
summary: Muscle imbalances, stemming from various factors, disrupt movement mechanics, leading to neuromuscular dysfunction and potential injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0560
content: Flexibility training is a key component for all training programs. Flexibility training is used to correct muscle imbalances. Flexibility training increases joint range of motion. Flexibility training decreases the excessive tension of muscles. Flexibility training relieves joint stress. Flexibility training improves the extensibility of the musculotendinous junction. Flexibility training maintains the normal functional length of all muscles. Flexibility training improves neuromuscular efficiency. Flexibility training improves function.
summary: Flexibility training is essential for all training programs, addressing muscle imbalances, improving range of motion, and enhancing overall function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0561
content: Muscular imbalances are highly prevalent in today’s society and are oftentimes caused by pattern overload. Pattern overload is consistently repeating the same pattern of motion, such as baseball pitching, long-distance running, and cycling, which with time places abnormal stresses on the body.
summary: Pattern overload, from repetitive motions, is a common cause of muscular imbalances.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0562
content: Flexibility training maintains the normal functional length of all muscles. Flexibility training improves neuromuscular efficiency. Flexibility training improves function.
summary: Flexibility training benefits muscle length, neuromuscular efficiency, and overall function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0563
content: Muscular imbalances are highly prevalent in today’s society and are oftentimes caused by pattern overload. Pattern overload is consistently repeating the same pattern of motion, such as baseball pitching, long-distance running, and cycling, which with time places abnormal stresses on the body. Training with the same routine repetitively may lead to pattern overload and place abnormal stresses on the body.
summary: Muscular imbalances often stem from pattern overload, which occurs from repetitive movements placing stress on the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0564
content: Pattern overload may not necessarily be directly related to exercise. A loading-dock employee who has a particularly repetitive occupation lifting and loading packages all day is prone to pattern overload. Even sitting for long periods of time while working on a computer is a repetitive stress.
summary: Pattern overload can arise from various repetitive activities, not just exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0565
content: Poor posture and repetitive movements create dysfunction within the connective tissue of the body. This dysfunction is treated by the body as an injury, and as a result, the body will initiate a repair process termed the cumulative injury cycle.
summary: Repetitive movements and poor posture can lead to connective tissue dysfunction, triggering the cumulative injury cycle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0566
content: Any trauma to the tissue of the body creates inflammation. Inflammation activates the body’s pain receptors and initiates a protective mechanism, increasing muscle tension or causing muscle spasm. Heightened activity of muscle spindles in particular areas of the muscle create a microspasm, and as a result of the spasm, adhesions (or knots) begin to form in the soft tissue.
summary: Tissue trauma causes inflammation, leading to muscle spasms and the formation of adhesions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0567
content: Adhesions form a weak, inelastic matrix (inability to stretch) that decreases normal elasticity of the soft tissue, resulting in altered length-tension relationships (leading to altered reciprocal inhibition), altered force-couple relationships (leading to synergistic dominance), and arthrokinetic dysfunction (leading to altered joint motion).
summary: Adhesions impair soft tissue elasticity, affecting muscle function, force production, and joint movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0568
content: Left untreated, these adhesions can begin to form permanent structural changes in the soft tissue that is evident by Davis’s law. Davis’s law states that soft tissue models along the lines of stress. Soft tissue is remodeled (or rebuilt) with an inelastic collagen matrix that forms in a random
summary: Untreated adhesions can lead to permanent structural changes in soft tissue, as it adapts to the lines of stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0569
content: If muscle fibers are lengthened, inelastic connective tissue fibers act as roadblocks, preventing muscle fibers from moving properly. This creates alterations in normal tissue extensibility and causes relative flexibility. If a muscle is in a constant shortened state, it will demonstrate poor neuromuscular efficiency as a result of altered length-tension and force-couple relationships. This will affect joint motion (ankle, knee, hip, and lumbar spine) and alter movement patterns (leading to synergistic dominance).
summary: Inelastic connective tissue can restrict muscle movement, leading to altered flexibility and neuromuscular efficiency.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0570
content: An inelastic collagen matrix will form along the same lines of stress created by the altered muscle movements. Because the muscle is consistently short and moves in a pattern different from its intended function, the newly formed inelastic connective tissue forms along this altered pattern, reducing the ability of the muscle to extend and move in its proper manner.
summary: The body adapts to altered movement patterns by forming inelastic connective tissue, further restricting movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0571
content: It is imperative that an integrated flexibility training program be used to restore the normal extensibility of the entire soft tissue complex. It is essential for personal trainers to address their clients’ muscular imbalances through an integrated fitness assessment and flexibility training program. If personal trainers neglect these phases of programming and simply move their clients right into a resistance or cardiorespiratory training program, this will add additional stresses to joints and muscles because they have improper mechanics and faulty recruitment patterns.
summary: Integrated flexibility training is crucial for restoring normal tissue extensibility and addressing muscular imbalances before progressing to resistance or cardiorespiratory training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0572
content: The benefits of flexibility training include improving muscle imbalances, increasing joint range of motion and muscle extensibility, relieving excessive tension of muscles and joint stress, and improving neuromuscular efficiency and function.
summary: Flexibility training offers numerous benefits, including improved muscle balance, joint mobility, reduced muscle tension, and enhanced neuromuscular function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0573
content: People who train in a repetitive fashion (or have jobs that require moving their bodies in repetitive ways) are at risk for pattern overload, which places stress on the body and can result in injury. Poor posture and repetitive movements may create dysfunctions in connective tissue, initiating the cumulative injury cycle.
summary: Repetitive training or work can lead to pattern overload, increasing the risk of injury and initiating the cumulative injury cycle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0574
content: Tissue trauma creates inflammation, which leads to microspasms and decreases normal elasticity of the soft tissue.
summary: Tissue trauma triggers inflammation, leading to muscle spasms and reduced tissue elasticity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0575
content: Soft tissue rebuilds itself in a random fashion with an inelastic collagen matrix that usually does not run in the same direction as the muscle fibers.
summary: Damaged soft tissue repairs itself randomly, often forming inelastic collagen that doesn't align with muscle fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0576
content: Flexibility techniques should only be performed on tissues identified as overactive (tight) during the assessment process. Corrective flexibility is designed to increase joint range of motion, improve muscle imbalances, and correct altered joint motion.
summary: Flexibility techniques should target tight tissues, and corrective flexibility aims to improve joint mobility, muscle balance, and joint function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0577
content: Corrective flexibility includes self-myofascial release (foam roll) techniques and static stretching. Self-myofascial release uses the principle of autogenic inhibition to cause muscle relaxation, whereas static stretching can use either autogenic inhibition or reciprocal inhibition to increase muscle length depending on how the stretch is performed.
summary: Corrective flexibility utilizes self-myofascial release and static stretching, employing different mechanisms to increase muscle length.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0578
content: Corrective flexibility is appropriate at the stabilization level (phase 1) of the OPT model. Active flexibility uses self-myofascial release and active-isolated stretching techniques.
summary: Corrective flexibility is best implemented during the stabilization phase of training, while active flexibility incorporates self-myofascial release and active-isolated stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0579
content: Active-isolated stretching is designed to improve the extensibility of soft tissue and increase neuromuscular efficiency by using reciprocal inhibition. Active-isolated stretching allows for agonists and synergist muscles to move a limb through a full range of motion while the functional antagonists are being stretched.
summary: Active-isolated stretching enhances soft tissue extensibility and neuromuscular efficiency by utilizing reciprocal inhibition during movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0580
content: Self-myofascial release uses the principle of autogenic inhibition to cause muscle relaxation, whereas static stretching can use either autogenic inhibition or reciprocal inhibition to increase muscle length depending on how the stretch is performed.  Self-myofascial release is a stretching technique that focuses on the neural system and fascial system in the body. The fascial system is the fibrous tissue that surrounds and separates muscle tissue. By applying gentle force to an adhesion or 'knot', the elastic muscle fibers are altered from a bundled position into a straighter alignment with the direction of the muscle or fascia. Gentle pressure applied with implements such as a foam roll stimulates the Golgi tendon organ and creates autogenic inhibition. Autogenic inhibition decreases muscle spindle excitation and releases the hypertonicity (tension) of the underlying musculature. Gentle pressure breaks up knots within the muscle and helps to release unwanted muscular tension. When using self-myofascial release, a person must find a tender spot (which indicates the presence of muscle hypertonicity) and sustain pressure on that spot for a minimum of 30 seconds. Sustaining pressure for 30 seconds increases Golgi tendon organ activity and decreases muscle spindle activity, thus triggering the autogenic inhibition response. It may take longer than 30 seconds depending on the client's ability to consciously relax. Self-myofascial release helps restore the body back to its optimal level of function by resetting the proprioceptive mechanisms of the soft tissue. Self-myofascial release is suggested before stretching because breaking up fascial adhesions (knots) may potentially improve the tissue's ability to lengthen through stretching techniques. Self-myofascial release can also be used during the cool-down process.
summary: This chunk describes self-myofascial release, its mechanisms, application, and benefits for muscle relaxation and flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0581
content: Corrective flexibility is appropriate at the stabilization level (phase 1) of the OPT model. Active flexibility uses self-myofascial release and active-isolated stretching techniques. Active-isolated stretching is designed to improve the extensibility of soft tissue and increase neuromuscular efficiency by using reciprocal inhibition. Active-isolated stretching allows for agonists and synergist muscles to move a limb through a full range of motion while the functional antagonists are being stretched. For example, a supine straight-leg raise uses the hip flexors and quadriceps to raise the leg and hold it unsupported, while the antagonist hamstring complex is stretched. Active flexibility would be appropriate at the strength level (phases 2, 3, and 4) of the OPT model.
summary: This chunk outlines the different types of flexibility (corrective, active) and their appropriate application within the OPT model.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0582
content: Functional flexibility uses self-myofascial release techniques and dynamic stretching. Dynamic stretching requires integrated, multiplanar soft tissue extensibility, with optimal neuromuscular control, through the full range of motion, or essentially movement without compensations. Therefore, if clients are compensating when performing dynamic stretches during training, then they need to be regressed to active or corrective flexibility. This form of flexibility would be appropriate at the power level (phase 5) of the OPT model or before athletic competition. It is important to remember that all functional movements occur in all three planes of motion and that injuries most often occur in the transverse plane. If the appropriate soft tissue is not extensible through the full range of movement, the risk of injury dramatically increases. Exercises that increase multiplanar soft tissue extensibility and have high levels of neuromuscular demand are preferred.
summary: This chunk describes functional flexibility, its components, and its importance for injury prevention and athletic performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0583
content: There is moderate evidence that forms of active stretching can increase joint range of motion and muscle flexibility. Maddig and Harmer (7) in a randomized controlled trial with 30 recreational athletes found that active-isolated stretching is effective for increasing hamstring range of motion. Guroian et al. (8) in a randomized trial found that static stretching and active-isolated stretching were both effective for increased range of motion and flexibility in older adults.
summary: This chunk presents research supporting the effectiveness of active stretching for improving flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0584
content: Static stretching is the process of passively taking a muscle to the point of tension and holding the stretch for a minimum of 30 seconds. This is the traditional form of stretching that is most often seen in fitness today. By holding the muscle in a stretched position for a prolonged period, the Golgi tendon organ is stimulated and produces an inhibitory effect on the muscle spindle (autogenic inhibition). This allows the muscle to relax and provides for better elongation of the muscle. Contracting the antagonistic musculature while holding the stretch can reciprocally inhibit the muscle being stretched, allowing it to relax and enhancing the stretch. Static stretching should be used to decrease the muscle spindle activity of a tight muscle before and after activity.
summary: This chunk explains the mechanics and application of static stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0585
content: Porter et al. (6) in a randomized controlled trial with 94 subjects demonstrated that both sustained (3 minutes, 3 times daily) and intermittent stretching (5 sets of 20-second holds, 2 times daily) for the Achilles’ tendon increased flexibility and decreased pain.
summary: This chunk presents research findings on the effectiveness of different stretching protocols for Achilles tendon flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0586
content: Dynamic or functional stretching is used to increase flexibility with optimal neuromuscular control. This should be used once clients have demonstrated adequate control over motions to prevent injury. Sherry and Best (9) in a prospective randomized comparison study of two rehabilitation programs with 24 athletes demonstrated improved functional outcomes with patients using dynamic functional movements (progressive agility and trunk stabilization) that require stabilization, proprioception, and muscle lengthening to occur simultaneously.
summary: This chunk describes dynamic stretching, its purpose, and its benefits for functional movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0587
content: This is the traditional form of stretching that is most often seen in fitness today. By holding the muscle in a stretched position for a prolonged period, the Golgi tendon organ is stimulated and produces an inhibitory effect on the muscle spindle (autogenic inhibition). This allows the muscle to relax and provides for better elongation of the muscle. Contracting the antagonistic musculature while holding the stretch can reciprocally inhibit the muscle being stretched, allowing it to relax and enhancing the stretch.
summary: This chunk describes static stretching, its mechanism, and how it relaxes muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0588
content: Static stretching should be used to decrease the muscle spindle activity of a tight muscle before and after activity. Recently, researchers have suggested that static stretching, performed immediately before exercise or athletic events, may decrease muscular strength and impair performance. The research in this area is still developing, but there are several studies that have found that static stretching may decrease maximal strength and power for up to 10 minutes. There is mounting scientific evidence that static stretching may decrease strength and power. This appears to be limited to just static stretching, often performed in an acute fashion.
summary: This chunk discusses the potential downsides of static stretching before exercise, particularly its impact on strength and power.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0589
content: However, research has also demonstrated increases in vertical jump, muscular strength, power, and balance ability after a regular (chronic) stretching program. There is no conclusive evidence that patients and clients with known motion limitations and flexibility imbalances have the same reaction to static stretching. There is little evidence that stretching influences overall athletic performance.
summary: This chunk highlights the potential benefits of chronic static stretching and acknowledges the lack of conclusive evidence regarding its effects on all individuals and athletic performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0590
content: Acute static stretching, held for more than 30 seconds, may decrease strength and power. Athletes and others who will be engaging in maximal effort, explosive activities (such as high jump, sprinting, or power lifting) may not want to perform static stretching before the event unless muscle imbalances are present. If static stretching is used, care should be taken so that only the targeted (tight, overactive) muscles are stretched, and this should be followed by active-isolated or dynamic stretches to increase motorneuron excitability.
summary: This chunk provides specific recommendations regarding static stretching for athletes and individuals engaging in explosive activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0591
content: Static stretching may be used to correct muscle imbalances and increase joint range of motion before activity in most patients and clients, as part of a progressive, integrated program. Active and dynamic stretching may be used without risking a loss of strength or power. These two forms of stretching may be the most appropriate before physical activity in clients with no identified muscle imbalances.
summary: This chunk recommends static stretching for correcting muscle imbalances and suggests active and dynamic stretching for individuals without muscle imbalances.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0592
content: The Static Standing Adductor Stretch involves standing in a straddled stance with the feet beyond shoulders-width apart, extending one leg back until the toe of the back leg is in line with the heel of the other foot, both feet pointed straight ahead. To perform the Static Standing Adductor Stretch, draw the navel inward and posteriorly rotate the pelvis. Slowly move in a sideways motion (side lunge) until a stretch in the straight leg’s groin area is felt during the Static Standing Adductor Stretch. Hold the Static Standing Adductor Stretch for 30 seconds. Take a wider stance than shoulders-width apart to ensure optimal lengthening during the Static Standing Adductor Stretch. The Static Standing Adductor Stretch can also be performed from a kneeling position or seated on a stability ball to reduce demand caused by maintaining a static lunge position.
summary: This chunk describes the Static Standing Adductor Stretch, including its execution and variations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0593
content: The Static Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch involves kneeling in front of a stability ball. Place one arm on the ball with the thumb pointed straight up in the air during the Static Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Draw the navel upward during the Static Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Posteriorly rotate the pelvis during the Static Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Slowly reach the arm straight out by rolling the ball forward during the Static Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Hold the Static Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch for 30 seconds. If the Static Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch causes any pinching in the shoulder, perform the stretch with the palm down on the ball. To increase the stretch during the Static Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch, slightly adduct the outstretched arm across the body.
summary: This chunk describes the Static Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch, including its execution and modifications.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0594
content: The Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch begins with standing in optimal posture. Draw the navel inward during the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch. Retract and depress the scapula on the side being stretched during the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch. Tuck the chin and slowly laterally flex the head, pulling one ear toward the same side shoulder during the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch. Hold the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch for 30 seconds. Switch sides and repeat the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch.
summary: This chunk describes the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch, including its execution and repetition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0595
content: The Static Pectoral Stretch involves standing against an object and forming a 90-90-degree angle with your arm. Draw your navel inward during the Static Pectoral Stretch.
summary: This chunk describes the Static Pectoral Stretch, including its execution.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0596
content: Tuck the chin and slowly laterally flex the head, pulling one ear toward the same side shoulder during the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch. Hold the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch for 30 seconds. Switch sides and repeat the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch.
summary: Instructions for performing the Static Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0597
content: The Static Pectoral Stretch involves standing against an object and forming a 90-90-degree angle with your arm. Draw your navel inward during the Static Pectoral Stretch. Slowly lean forward until a slight stretch is felt in the anterior shoulder and chest region during the Static Pectoral Stretch. Make sure the shoulders do not elevate during the Static Pectoral Stretch. Keep the shoulder of the side being stretched down and retract it by depressing the scapula on the side being stretched during the Static Pectoral Stretch. One can place the arm on the side being stretched behind the body to help accomplish the retraction during the Static Pectoral Stretch. Hold the Static Pectoral Stretch for 30 seconds.
summary: Instructions for performing the Static Pectoral Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0598
content: Active-isolated stretching is the process of using agonists and synergists to dynamically move the joint into a range of motion. Active-isolated stretching increases motorneuron excitability, creating reciprocal inhibition of the muscle being stretched. The active supine biceps femoris stretch is a good example of active-isolated stretching. The quadriceps extends the knee during the active supine biceps femoris stretch, which enhances the stretch of the biceps femoris in two ways. The contraction of the quadriceps causes reciprocal inhibition (decreased neural drive and muscle spindle excitation) of the hamstring complex, which allows it to elongate during the active supine biceps femoris stretch.
summary: Description of active-isolated stretching and its mechanism.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0599
content: Active-isolated stretches are suggested for preactivity warm-up (such as before sports competition or high-intensity exercise) as long as no postural distortion patterns are present. If an individual possesses muscle imbalances, active-isolated stretching should be performed after self-myofascial release and static stretching for muscles determined as tight or overactive during the assessment process. Typically, 5 to 10 repetitions of each active-isolated stretch are performed and held for 1 to 2 seconds each.
summary: Guidelines for when to use active-isolated stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0600
content: To perform the Active Gastrocnemius Stretch with Pronation and Supination, stand near a wall or sturdy object and bring one leg forward for support, using the upper body to lean against the object. During the Active Gastrocnemius Stretch with Pronation and Supination, draw the navel inward, keep the rear foot on the ground with the opposite hip flexed, and slowly move through the hips, creating controlled supination and pronation through the lower extremity. Hold the Active Gastrocnemius Stretch with Pronation and Supination for 1–2 seconds and repeat for 5–10 repetitions. The majority of the motion during the Active Gastrocnemius Stretch with Pronation and Supination should come from internal and external rotation of the hip, which causes rotation at the knee and eversion and inversion on the foot and ankle.
summary: Instructions for performing the Active Gastrocnemius Stretch with Pronation and Supination.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0601
content: To perform the Active Supine Biceps Femoris Stretch, lie supine on the floor with legs flat. Flex, adduct, and slightly internally rotate the hip of the side being stretched while keeping the knee flexed during the Active Supine Biceps Femoris Stretch. Place the opposite hand behind the knee of the leg being stretched during the Active Supine Biceps Femoris Stretch. Draw the navel inward during the Active Supine Biceps Femoris Stretch. With the hand supporting the leg, extend the knee during the Active Supine Biceps Femoris Stretch. Hold the Active Supine Biceps Femoris Stretch for 1–2 seconds and repeat for 5–10 repetitions. Adducting and internally rotating the hip during the Active Supine Biceps Femoris Stretch places more emphasis on the short head of the biceps femoris.
summary: Instructions for performing the Active Supine Biceps Femoris Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0602
content: To perform the Active Standing TFL Stretch, stand in a staggered stance with the front leg slightly bent and the rear leg straight. Externally rotate the back leg during the Active Standing TFL Stretch. Draw the navel inward and raise the arm overhead during the Active Standing TFL Stretch. Squeeze the gluteal muscles while rotating the pelvis posteriorly during the Active Standing TFL Stretch. Stride forward in a controlled manner until a mild tension is achieved in the front of the hip being stretched during the Active Standing TFL Stretch. Side bend and rotate posteriorly during the Active Standing TFL Stretch. Hold the Active Standing TFL Stretch for 1–2 seconds and repeat for 5–10 repetitions.
summary: Instructions for performing the Active Standing TFL Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0603
content: To perform the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch, kneel with the front and back legs bent at a 90-degree angle. Internally rotate the back hip to target the psoas musculature or maintain a neutral position to target the rectus femoris during the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch. Draw the navel inward and raise the arm overhead during the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch. Squeeze the gluteal muscles of the side being stretched while rotating the pelvis posteriorly during the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch.
summary: Instructions for performing the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0604
content: To perform the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch, kneel with the front and back legs bent at a 90-degree angle. Internally rotate the back hip to target the psoas musculature or maintain a neutral position to target the rectus femoris during the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch. Draw the navel inward and raise the arm overhead during the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch. Squeeze the gluteal muscles of the side being stretched while rotating the pelvis posteriorly during the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch. Slowly move the body forward until a mild tension is achieved in the front of the hip being stretched during the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch. Side bend and rotate posteriorly during the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch. Hold the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch for 1–2 seconds and repeat for 5–10 repetitions. Internally rotating the back hip during the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch targets the psoas because this muscle concentrically performs hip flexion and external rotation.
summary: Instructions and targeting details for the Active Kneeling Hip Flexor Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0605
content: To perform the Active Standing Adductor Stretch, stand in a straddled stance with the feet more than shoulders-width apart. Extend one leg back until the toe of the back leg is in line with the heel of the other foot during the Active Standing Adductor Stretch. Both feet should be pointed straight ahead. Draw the navel inward and posteriorly rotate the pelvis during the Active Standing Adductor Stretch. Slowly move in a sideways motion (side lunge) until a stretch in the straight leg’s groin area is felt during the Active Standing Adductor Stretch. Hold the Active Standing Adductor Stretch for 1–2 seconds and repeat for 5–10 repetitions.
summary: Instructions for performing the Active Standing Adductor Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0606
content: To perform the Active Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch, kneel in front of a stability ball. Place one arm on the ball with the thumb straight up in the air during the Active Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Draw the navel upward during the Active Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Maintaining core control, roll the ball out until a comfortable stretch is felt during the Active Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Hold the stretch for 1–2 seconds and repeat for 5–10 repetitions during the Active Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Switch sides and repeat the Active Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Keep the hips level when going into the Active Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch. Initiate the Active Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch by going into a posterior pelvic tilt. This will take the origin and insertion of the latissimus dorsi further apart, enhancing the stretch.
summary: Instructions for performing the Active Latissimus Dorsi Ball Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0607
content: The Active Pectoral Stretch involves forming a 90-90-degree angle with the arm. During the Active Pectoral Stretch, the navel should be drawn inward. In the Active Pectoral Stretch, slowly lean forward until a slight stretch is felt in the anterior shoulder and chest region. Hold the Active Pectoral Stretch for 1–2 seconds and repeat for 5–10 repetitions.
summary: Instructions for performing the Active Pectoral Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0608
content: The Active Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch begins with standing in optimal posture. During the Active Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch, the navel should be drawn inward. In the Active Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch, tuck the chin and slowly laterally flex the head, pulling one ear toward the same-side shoulder while retracting and depressing the same-side shoulder complex. Hold the Active Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch for 1–2 seconds and repeat for 5–10 repetitions.
summary: Instructions for performing the Active Upper Trapezius/Scalene Stretch.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0609
content: When performing stretches, retract the scapulae to reciprocally inhibit the pectoralis major and minor, enhancing the stretch. If tingling is felt down the arm and into the finger during a stretch, decrease the range of motion to take stress off of the nerve. During a stretch, keep the head in a neutral position and do not allow it to jut forward.
summary: Tips for performing stretches safely and effectively.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0610
content: Dynamic stretching uses the force production of a muscle and the body’s momentum to take a joint through the full available range of motion. Dynamic stretching uses the concept of reciprocal inhibition to improve soft tissue extensibility. One can perform one set of 10 repetitions using 3 to 10 dynamic stretches. Hip swings, medicine ball rotations, and walking lunges are good examples of dynamic stretching. Dynamic stretching is suggested as a warm-up before athletic activity, as long as no postural distortion patterns are present.
summary: Description and benefits of dynamic stretching, including examples and recommendations for use.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0611
content: Dynamic stretching uses the force production of a muscle and the body’s momentum to take a joint through the full available range of motion. Dynamic stretching uses the concept of reciprocal inhibition to improve soft tissue extensibility. One can perform one set of 10 repetitions using 3 to 10 dynamic stretches. Hip swings, medicine ball rotations, and walking lunges are good examples of dynamic stretching.
