Dynamic stretching is suggested as a warm-up before athletic activity, as long as no postural distortion patterns are present. If an individual possesses muscle imbalances, self-myofascial release and static stretching should precede dynamic stretching for overactive or tight muscles identified during the assessment process. It is recommended that the client have good levels of tissue extensibility, core stability, and balance capabilities before undertaking an aggressive dynamic stretching program.
summary: Dynamic stretching involves active movements through a full range of motion, improving flexibility and preparing the body for activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0612
content: A dynamic stretch is the active extension of a muscle, using force production and momentum, to move the joint through the full available range of motion. Dynamic stretches include prisoner squats, multiplanar lunges, single-leg squat touchdowns, tube walking, and medicine ball lift and chop.
summary: Dynamic stretches utilize active movement and momentum to enhance flexibility and range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0613
content: The Prisoner Squat involves standing in proper alignment with the hands behind the head. During the Prisoner Squat, draw the navel inward and lower to a squat position under control and without compensation. Extend the hips, knees, and ankles and repeat the Prisoner Squat 10 times. As a progression in the Prisoner Squat, add a calf raise after extending the hips, knees, and ankles.
summary: The Prisoner Squat is a dynamic stretch targeting multiple muscle groups, progressing with a calf raise for increased challenge.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0614
content: The Multiplanar Lunge with Reach begins with standing in proper alignment with hands on hips and feet straight ahead. During the Multiplanar Lunge with Reach, draw the navel inward and while maintaining total body alignment, step forward (sagittal plane) and descend into a lunge position while reaching forward. Use hip and thigh muscles to push up and back to the start position in the Multiplanar Lunge with Reach and repeat 10 times on each leg. Progress the Multiplanar Lunge with Reach to side lunges with reach (frontal plane), followed by turning lunges with reach (transverse plane).
summary: The Multiplanar Lunge with Reach is a dynamic stretch that progresses through various planes of motion, enhancing mobility and stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0615
content: The Single-Leg Squat Touchdown starts with standing on one leg in optimal posture, keeping the raised leg parallel to the standing leg. During the Single-Leg Squat Touchdown, draw the navel inward and squat in a controlled manner, bending the ankle, knee, and hip while reaching the opposite hand near the standing leg toe. While maintaining the drawn-in maneuver and gluteal activity, return to the starting position in the Single-Leg Squat Touchdown and repeat 10 times on each side. Make sure the knee is tracking in line with the second and third toes during the Single-Leg Squat Touchdown. Do not allow the knee of the squatting leg to move inside the foot.
summary: The Single-Leg Squat Touchdown is a dynamic stretch that improves balance, coordination, and single-leg strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0616
content: Tube Walking: Side-to-Side is an exercise that is very effective for improving gluteus medius and core activation. To perform Tube Walking: Side-to-Side, stand with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and feet straight ahead. Place tubing around the lower leg for Tube Walking: Side-to-Side. To perform Tube Walking: Side-to-Side, draw the navel inward. To perform Tube Walking: Side-to-Side, keep feet straight ahead and take 10 small steps sideways without allowing knees to cave inward. Repeat Tube Walking: Side-to-Side in the opposite direction.
summary: Tube Walking: Side-to-Side is a dynamic exercise that strengthens the gluteus medius and core muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0617
content: The Medicine Ball Lift and Chop requires standing with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and feet straight ahead. To perform the Medicine Ball Lift and Chop, grasp a medicine ball with both hands and keep elbows fully extended. To perform the Medicine Ball Lift and Chop, draw the belly button inward. To perform the Medicine Ball Lift and Chop, initiate the rotational movement from the trunk outward, lifting the medicine ball from a low position to a high position. Allow the hips to pivot on the back foot as the motion nears end range during the Medicine Ball Lift and Chop. Perform 10 repetitions of the Medicine Ball Lift and Chop. Repeat the Medicine Ball Lift and Chop on the opposite side. Allow for the hips to rotate during both portions of the Medicine Ball Lift and Chop to improve arthrokinematics through the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex.
summary: The Medicine Ball Lift and Chop is a dynamic exercise that improves rotational power and core stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0618
content: Each type of flexibility training consists of specific stretching techniques. Corrective flexibility uses self-myofascial release and static stretching. Active flexibility uses self-myofascial release and active-isolated stretching. Functional flexibility uses self-myofascial release and dynamic stretching.
summary: Different types of flexibility training utilize various techniques, including self-myofascial release and dynamic or static stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0619
content: Self-myofascial release applies gentle pressure on muscle hypertonicity for a minimum of 30 seconds.
summary: Self-myofascial release involves applying pressure to tight muscles to improve flexibility and reduce tension.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0620
content: Each type of flexibility training consists of specific stretching techniques. Corrective flexibility uses self-myofascial release and static stretching. Active flexibility uses self-myofascial release and active-isolated stretching. Functional flexibility uses self-myofascial release and dynamic stretching. These techniques are suggested before stretching, as well as for cool-down.
summary: Different types of flexibility training utilize various techniques, including self-myofascial release and static, active-isolated, or dynamic stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0621
content: Self-myofascial release applies gentle pressure on muscle hypertonicity for a minimum of 30 seconds. The force applied during self-myofascial release creates autogenic inhibition, decreasing muscle spindle excitation and releasing muscle hypertonicity.
summary: Self-myofascial release involves applying pressure to tight muscles, reducing muscle spindle activity and releasing tension.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0622
content: Static stretching passively takes a muscle to the point of tension and holds it there for a minimum of 30 seconds. Static stretches can be used before activity to decrease overactivity of tight muscles and to increase range of motion. Care should be used when static stretching is used before certain activities that involve explosive movements or maximal strength. Static stretches should also be used after activity to reset muscles back to their optimal resting lengths.
summary: Static stretching involves holding a muscle at its point of tension for 30 seconds or more, used for pre- and post-activity flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0623
content: Active-isolated stretches use agonists and synergists to dynamically move joints into their ranges of motion. Active-isolated stretches are suggested for preactivity warm-up (5 to 10 repetitions, held for 1–2 seconds each).
summary: Active-isolated stretches use muscle contractions to dynamically move joints through their range of motion, often used for warm-ups.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0624
content: Dynamic stretches use force production and momentum to take a joint through the full available range of motion. Dynamic stretches are suggested for preactivity warm-up as well.
summary: Dynamic stretches involve controlled movements that take joints through their full range of motion, commonly used for warm-ups.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0625
content: The Inverted hurdler’s stretch is believed to place high stress on the inside of the knee (medial collateral ligament) and may cause pain and stress on the kneecap (patella). The Inverted hurdler’s stretch should not be performed by anyone with a history of knee or low-back pain.
summary: The Inverted hurdler’s stretch can be stressful on the knee and should be avoided by individuals with knee or back pain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0626
content: The Plow is a common posture from yoga. Because of the inverted nature of the Plow (head is lower than the hips), this stretch places high stress on the neck and spine. The Plow should not be performed by patients with a history of neck or back injury. The Plow should also be avoided by individuals with high blood pressure (hypertension). Many practitioners of yoga will discourage menstruating females from performing inverted stretches.
summary: The Plow yoga pose, while common, can be stressful on the neck and spine, and should be avoided by individuals with certain medical conditions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0627
content: The Shoulder stand is another common posture from yoga, and another inverted stretch. The Shoulder stand places high stress on the neck, shoulders, and spine. The Shoulder stand should be avoided in patients with hypertension or any history of neck or spine injury, or if exact technique is not used.
summary: The Shoulder stand yoga pose, being inverted, can strain the neck, shoulders, and spine, requiring caution for individuals with certain conditions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0628
content: The Straight-leg toe touch is one of the most common stretches for the hamstring complex. The Straight-leg toe touch may place the vertebrae and the cartilage discs in the low back under high stress. Clients or patients with a history of herniated discs or nerve pain that runs in the back of the leg should avoid this stretch. Clients with poor flexibility may attempt to hyperextend the knees during this stretch, which may place high stress on the ligaments of the knee.
summary: The Straight-leg toe touch, while common for hamstring stretching, can put stress on the lower back and knees, requiring caution for individuals with specific conditions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0629
content: The Arching quadriceps stretch is designed to stretch the quadriceps and hip flexors. This position places very high stress on the kneecap and the patellar tendon. Any client or patient with a history of knee injury should avoid the stretch described. The stretch described places high stress (compression) on the kneecap, which may cause cartilage damage. Most health-care professionals discourage the performance of the described stretch.
summary: The Arching quadriceps stretch, while targeting quadriceps and hip flexors, can be harmful to the knee, particularly for individuals with knee injuries.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0630
content: Some stretches are required in certain sports or activities, such as the inverted hurdler’s stretch which mimics the position of a hurdler going over a hurdle. Other stretches are traditional positions used in martial arts, gymnastics, or yoga. For most clients, there are safer positions that can be used to stretch the targeted muscles. All clients should be properly educated about stretching technique and posture. The safest exercises should be used to meet the goals of the exercise program. Proper flexibility is the first step to addressing movement compensations.
summary: Stretching techniques vary depending on the activity or discipline, but safety and proper technique are paramount. Flexibility is crucial for addressing movement issues.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0631
content: All clients should be properly educated about stretching technique and posture. For most clients, there are safer positions that can be used to stretch the targeted muscles. Proper flexibility is the first step to addressing movement compensations. Table 7.6 provides common movement compensations seen during the assessment process, associated overactive and underactive muscles, and corrective strategies for each (flexibility and strengthening exercises). Further chapters in the text will provide proper instruction for the sample strengthening exercises that can be implemented to help strengthen the underactive muscles in each of the compensations.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of proper stretching technique, client education, and addressing movement compensations through flexibility and strengthening exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0632
content: Other stretches are traditional positions used in martial arts, gymnastics, or yoga.
summary: This chunk describes alternative stretching methods commonly used in various disciplines.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0633
content: The safest exercises should be used to meet the goals of the exercise program.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of selecting safe exercises aligned with client goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0634
content:
A typical warm-up includes CORE / BALANCE / PLYOMETRIC
SPEED, AGILITY, QUICKNESS
summary: This chunk outlines a typical warm-up routine focusing on core, balance, plyometrics, speed, agility, and quickness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0635
content:
A typical cool-down includes SMR: Calves, IT Band, Adductors
Hold each tender area for 30 sec
Static Stretch: Calves, Hip Flexors, Adductors
Hold each stretch for 30 sec
summary: This chunk describes a typical cool-down routine involving self-myofascial release (SMR) and static stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0636
content: Integrated cardiorespiratory training is a way of planning training programs that systematically progress clients through various stages to achieve optimal levels of physiologic, physical, and performance adaptations by placing stress on the cardiorespiratory system. One of the most common errors made by personal trainers during the planning and implementation of cardiorespiratory exercise programs is the failure to consider rate of progression. Rate of progression is critical to helping clients achieve their personal health and fitness goals in the most efficient and effective use of time and energy. Failure to carefully consider and monitor rate of progression of each client on an individual basis can also result in injury if progression is too fast or in poor exercise adherence if the progression is too slow.
summary: This chunk explains integrated cardiorespiratory training, emphasizing the importance of rate of progression for client safety and adherence.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0637
content: The benefits of regular physical activity and exercise are numerous. Individuals can achieve numerous health-related benefits from modest amounts of moderate-intensity exercise, and even greater benefits from vigorous-intensity exercise, or a combination of both. Engaging in regular, sustained physical activity over the lifespan is one of the most reliable predictors of death and disability. Research has confirmed that an individual’s cardiorespiratory fitness level is one of the strongest predictors of morbidity and mortality (1–3). Poor cardiorespiratory fitness is related to a marked increase in risk of premature death from all causes, but particularly from cardiovascular disease. An improvement in cardiorespiratory fitness is related to a reduction in premature death from all causes (1–3).
summary: This chunk highlights the numerous health benefits of regular physical activity, emphasizing the strong link between cardiorespiratory fitness and mortality.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0638
content: Exercise training programs should be designed to meet the specific needs and goals of the individual client. The initial exercise prescription should reflect (a) the initial fitness level of the client, (b) fitness assessment results, and (c) whether the client has any significant risk factors or health limitations to exercise. Each exercise training session should also include the following phases: Warm-up phase, Conditioning phase, Cool-down phase.
summary: This chunk stresses the importance of individualized exercise program design, considering client fitness level, assessment results, and potential limitations. It also outlines the essential phases of an exercise training session.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0639
content: A warm-up is generally described as preparing the body for physical activity. A general warm-up consists of movements that do not necessarily have any movement specific to the actual activity to be performed. Examples of a general warm-up include warming up by walking on a treadmill or riding a stationary bicycle before weight training.
summary: This chunk defines a warm-up and provides examples of general warm-up activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0640
content: The initial exercise prescription should reflect (a) the initial fitness level of the client, (b) fitness assessment results, and (c) whether the client has any significant risk factors or health limitations to exercise.
summary: Exercise prescription should be tailored to the client's fitness level, assessment results, and any health limitations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0641
content: Each exercise training session should also include the following phases: Warm-up phase, Conditioning phase, Cool-down phase.
summary: A training session should consist of a warm-up, conditioning phase, and cool-down.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0642
content: A warm-up is generally described as preparing the body for physical activity. A general warm-up consists of movements that do not necessarily have any movement specific to the actual activity to be performed. Examples of a general warm-up include warming up by walking on a treadmill or riding a stationary bicycle before weight training.
summary: A warm-up prepares the body for activity, often starting with general movements like walking or cycling.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0643
content: A specific warm-up consists of movements that more closely mimic those of the actual activity, often referred to as dynamic stretches. Examples of a specific warm-up include performing body-weight squats and push-ups before weight training.
summary: A specific warm-up involves dynamic stretches that resemble the actual exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0644
content: The proposed benefits of a warm-up are outlined in Table 8.1 (4–7). The cardiorespiratory portion of a warm-up period typically lasts between 5 and 10 minutes. The cardiorespiratory portion of a warm-up should be performed at a low-to-moderate intensity level.
summary: Warm-ups offer various benefits and should last 5-10 minutes at a low-to-moderate intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0645
content: New clients who are sedentary or have medical or health limitations or those with limited previous exercise experience may require up to half or more of their dedicated workout time be directed to warm-up activities, at least initially. Personal trainers should begin each training session with new clients by explaining the benefits to be gained from each new exercise or activity introduced, followed by a demonstration of each new exercise, emphasizing safety and proper technique, and lastly by observing the client perform each new exercise prescribed to help ensure proper form and technique.
summary: New clients may need a longer warm-up, and trainers should explain exercises, demonstrate proper technique, and observe client form.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0646
content: It is vitally important that personal trainers have a comprehensive understanding of safe and effective dynamic and static warm-up activities. Once a client has demonstrated a complete understanding of the techniques necessary for self-myofascial release (foam rolling), static stretching, and operation of the cardiorespiratory equipment, they can begin performing the warm-up before the session with the fitness professional.
summary: Trainers need to be knowledgeable about warm-up techniques, and clients should demonstrate understanding before performing them independently.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0647
content: Strength level clients will use self-myofascial release and active-isolated stretching for muscles determined as tight or overactive during the assessment process before proceeding to the cardiorespiratory portion of the warm-up. Power level clients will use self-myofascial release and dynamic stretching exercises to complete their warm-up.
summary: Warm-up routines vary based on client level, with strength clients using self-myofascial release and active-isolated stretching, and power clients using dynamic stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0648
content: Dynamic stretches can be performed in a circuit format (performing one exercise after the other), thereby eliminating the need for additional cardiorespiratory warm-up activities. The warm-up period should prepare the body for activity; thus it is important to monitor the intensity at which clients are performing selected warm-up activities to ensure they do not unduly fatigue before the workout portion of their program actually begins. Keeping the activity to a moderate duration and intensity level will help ensure a proper warm-up.
summary: Dynamic stretches can be circuit-based, and warm-up intensity should be moderate to avoid fatigue before the workout.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0649
content: Individuals engage in cardiorespiratory exercise for various reasons, including burning calories to lose weight, stress reduction, improving health, and other reasons. Low-intensity cardiorespiratory exercise typically results in some improvements in health and well-being, but not necessarily significant improvements in fitness compared with higher training intensities.
summary: People exercise for various reasons, and while low-intensity cardio offers benefits, higher intensity often leads to greater fitness improvements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0650
content: Cardiorespiratory exercise examples include treadmill, stationary bicycle, StairClimber, rower, and elliptical trainer. Cardiorespiratory exercise should be performed for 5–10 minutes.
summary: Examples of cardio exercises include treadmills, stationary bikes, and ellipticals, performed for 5-10 minutes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0651
content: Self-myofascial release for the gastrocnemius/soleus, adductors, TFL and iliotibial band, and latissimus dorsi should be performed for 30 seconds each muscle. Active-isolated stretching for the gastrocnemius/soleus, adductors, tensor fascia latae, and latissimus dorsi should be performed for 1–2 seconds, 5–10 reps each muscle.
summary: Warm-up should include 30-second self-myofascial release and 1-2 second active-isolated stretching for specific muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0652
content: Dynamic stretching examples include hip swings (side to side), prisoner squats, lunge with rotation, lateral tube walking, medicine ball lift and chop, and single-leg squat touchdown. Dynamic stretching should be performed for 10 repetitions of each side.
summary: Dynamic stretching examples include hip swings, prisoner squats, and lunges, performed for 10 repetitions per side.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0653
content: Cardiorespiratory exercise has a profound effect on physical and mental health. Benefits of cardiorespiratory exercise include a stronger and more efficient heart, improved ability to pump blood, reduced risk of heart disease, lower resting heart rate, lower heart rate at any given level of work, improvement of lung ventilation, stronger respiratory muscles, thicker articular cartilage and bones with weight-bearing aerobic exercises, improved oxygen transport, reduced cholesterol levels, reduced arterial blood pressure, improved blood thinning and reduced risk of clot formation, improved fuel supply, improved ability of muscles to use oxygen, improvement in mental alertness, reduced tendency for depression and anxiety, improved ability to relax and sleep, improved tolerance to stress, increase in lean body mass, increase in metabolic rate, and reduced risk of obesity or diabetes mellitus.
summary: Cardio exercise offers numerous physical and mental health benefits, including cardiovascular improvements, reduced disease risk, enhanced lung function, and improved mood and sleep.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0654
content: Individuals vary in their response to aerobic training, but if performed properly with due regard for individual abilities, it has a positive effect on many components of health and fitness.
summary: While individual responses to cardio training vary, proper execution generally improves health and fitness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0655
content: A cool-down provides the body with a smooth transition from exercise back to a steady state of rest. A cool-down is the opposite of the warm-up. The goal of a cool-down is to reduce heart and breathing rates, gradually cool body temperature, return muscles to their optimal length-tension relationships, prevent venous pooling of blood in the lower extremities, and restore physiologic systems close to baseline.
summary: A cool-down helps the body transition back to rest by gradually lowering heart rate, body temperature, and blood pooling.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0656
content: Sufficient time for a cardiorespiratory cool-down period is approximately 5 to 10 minutes. Benefits of a cool-down include reducing heart and breathing rates, gradually cooling body temperature, returning muscles to their optimal length-tension relationships, preventing venous pooling of blood in the lower extremities, and restoring physiologic systems close to baseline.
summary: A 5-10 minute cool-down reduces heart rate, body temperature, and blood pooling, returning the body to a resting state.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0657
content: During the transition from rest to steady-state cardiorespiratory exercise, the body undergoes numerous and often dramatic physiologic changes, depending on the intensity and duration of the activity. Some cardiovascular responses to exercise include linear increases in heart rate and systolic blood pressure and an increase in stroke volume (up to 40–60% of maximum) after which it plateaus. Cardiac output increases from an average resting value of about 5 L/min to as high as 20 to 40 L/min during intense exercise. At rest, only about 15 to 20% of circulating blood reaches skeletal muscle, but during intense vigorous exercise it increases up to as much as 80 to 85% of cardiac output.
summary: Exercise causes physiological changes, including increased heart rate, blood pressure, stroke volume, and cardiac output, with a greater proportion of blood flowing to skeletal muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0658
content: Benefits of a cool-down include reducing heart and breathing rates, gradually cooling body temperature, returning muscles to their optimal length-tension relationships, preventing venous pooling of blood in the lower extremities, and restoring physiologic systems close to baseline. The cool-down period helps gradually restore physiologic responses to exercise close to baseline levels. A cool-down of 5 to 10 minutes provides the body with an essential transition from exercise back to a steady state of rest. Flexibility exercises are also important components of the warm-up and cool-down to reset muscles back to their optimal resting lengths.
summary: The cool-down period helps the body transition back to a resting state by reducing heart rate, body temperature, and blood pooling, while restoring muscle length and physiological systems.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0659
content: During the transition from rest to steady-state cardiorespiratory exercise, the body undergoes numerous and often dramatic physiologic changes, depending on the intensity and duration of the activity. Some cardiovascular responses to exercise include linear increases in heart rate and systolic blood pressure and an increase in stroke volume (up to 40–60% of maximum) after which it plateaus. Cardiac output increases from an average resting value of about 5 L/min to as high as 20 to 40 L/min during intense exercise. At rest, only about 15 to 20% of circulating blood reaches skeletal muscle, but during intense vigorous exercise it increases up to as much as 80 to 85% of cardiac output. During exercise, blood is shunted away from major organs such as the kidneys, liver, stomach, and intestines and is redirected to the skin to promote heat loss.
summary: The body undergoes significant physiological changes during exercise, including increases in heart rate, blood pressure, stroke volume, and blood flow to working muscles, while blood flow to other organs is reduced.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0660
content: Blood plasma volume also decreases with the onset of exercise, and as exercise continues, increased blood pressure forces water from the vascular compartment to the interstitial space. During prolonged exercise, plasma volume can decrease by as much as 10 to 20%.
summary: During exercise, blood plasma volume decreases due to fluid shifts from the vascular compartment to the interstitial space.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0661
content: Cardiorespiratory fitness is one of the most important components of health-related physical fitness. High levels of cardiorespiratory fitness are strongly linked to reduced risk of disease and improved mortality. Cardiorespiratory training should be preceded by a warm-up period and followed by a cool-down period. Typically, the cardiorespiratory portion of a warm-up should last 5 to 10 minutes at a low-to-moderate intensity.  A cool-down of 5 to 10 minutes provides the body with an essential transition from exercise back to a steady state of rest.
summary: Cardiorespiratory fitness is crucial for health, and training should include warm-up and cool-down periods to prepare the body for and recover from exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0662
content: Initially, personal trainers should closely monitor new clients during both the warm-up and the cool-down periods to make certain that the activities being performed are appropriate, safe, and effective. It is also important for the client to understand the importance of both the warm-up and the cool-down periods.
summary: Personal trainers should closely supervise clients during warm-up and cool-down to ensure safety and effectiveness, emphasizing the importance of these periods to clients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0663
content: At least 5 days per week is recommended for frequency. 40% to 110% of VO2R or 55% to 70% of HRmax is recommended for intensity. 150 minutes per week is recommended for time. Moderate-intensity aerobic activity, such as brisk walking, is recommended for type.
summary: General cardiorespiratory training recommendations include frequency of at least 5 days per week, intensity of 40-110% VO2R or 55-70% HRmax, duration of 150 minutes per week, and moderate-intensity activities like brisk walking.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0664
content: At least 3 days per week is recommended for frequency. 40% to 60% of VO2R or 50% to 70% of HRmax is recommended for intensity. 75 minutes per week is recommended for time. Vigorous-intensity aerobic activity, such as jogging or running, is recommended for type.
summary: For vigorous-intensity cardiorespiratory training, aim for at least 3 days per week, intensity of 40-60% VO2R or 50-70% HRmax, duration of 75 minutes per week, and activities like jogging or running.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0665
content: Any combination of moderate and vigorous intensity aerobic (cardiorespiratory fitness) activities is recommended for type. Initially choose endurance activities that require minimal skill or physical fitness to perform. Initially choose vigorous-intensity endurance activities that require minimal skill to perform.
summary: Combine moderate and vigorous-intensity activities for optimal cardiorespiratory fitness. Start with beginner-friendly endurance activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0666
content: Intensity refers to the level of demand that a given activity places on the body. Applied to cardiorespiratory exercise, intensity is established and monitored in numerous ways, including calculating heart rate, power output (watts), or by calculating a percentage of maximal oxygen consumption (VO2max) or oxygen uptake reserve (VO2R). For general health requirements, moderate intensity is preferred. Moderate exercise typically represents an intensity range of less than 60% VO2R. Moderate exercise is enough of a demand to increase heart and respiratory rate, but does not cause exhaustion or breathlessness for the average untrained apparently healthy adult. Higher intensities greater than 60% VO2R are generally required for improvements in overall fitness and conditioning.
summary: Exercise intensity is measured through various methods, such as heart rate or VO2max. Moderate intensity is recommended for general health, while higher intensities are needed for fitness improvements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0667
content: Peak VO2 is the traditional gold standard measurement for cardiorespiratory fitness. Peak VO2 is the maximal volume of oxygen per kilogram body weight per minute. Once VO2max is determined, a common method to establish exercise training intensity is to have clients exercise at a percentage of their VO2max. VO2R is another method that can be used to establish exercise training intensity.
summary: Peak VO2 is the gold standard for measuring cardiorespiratory fitness. Training intensity can be determined using VO2max or VO2R.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0668
content: Flexibility training, including corrective stretching (self-myofascial release and static stretching), has been shown to be effective at lengthening muscles back to their optimal length-tension relationships, promoting optimal joint range of motion.
summary: Flexibility training, such as corrective stretching, improves muscle length and joint range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0669
content: Higher intensities greater than 60% VO2R are generally required for improvements in overall fitness and conditioning. Peak VO2 is the traditional gold standard measurement for cardiorespiratory fitness. Peak VO2 is the maximal volume of oxygen per kilogram body weight per minute. Once VO2max is determined, a common method to establish exercise training intensity is to have clients exercise at a percentage of their VO2max. V O2R is another method that can be used to establish exercise training intensity. V O2R requires the calculation of VO2max and then a simple equation to calculate VO2R. The formula for VO2R is: Target VO2R = [(VO2max - VO2rest) / intensity desired] + VO2rest. VO2max can be estimated using a submaximal test or directly measured. VO2rest is usually always predicted (estimated at 1 MET or 3.5 mL O2 . kg . min).
summary: This chunk defines peak VO2 as the gold standard for cardiorespiratory fitness and outlines methods for establishing exercise intensity using VO2max and VO2R.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0670
content: NASM has reclassified the relative intensity of exercise in six incremental stages, from “very light” to “maximal effort,” with a corresponding oxygen uptake reserve (VO2R) or heart rate reserve (HRR) and rating of perceived exertion (RPE) at each progressive level. The ACSM recommends a relative training intensity of 40 or 50% to 85% of VO2R or HRR, where 50% VO2R or HRR was the threshold intensity for moderate-intensity exercise.
summary: This chunk describes exercise intensity classifications and recommended training percentages based on VO2R and HRR.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0671
content: One metabolic equivalent or MET is equal to 3.5 mL O2/kg/min or the equivalent of the average resting metabolic rate (RMR) for adults. METs are used to describe the energy cost of physical activity as multiples of resting metabolic rate. MET values are used to relate exercise intensity with energy expenditure. For example, a physical activity with a MET value of 4, such as jogging at a slow pace, would require 4 times the energy that that person consumes at rest (e.g., sitting quietly).
summary: This chunk defines METs, their relationship to energy expenditure, and provides an example of MET calculation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0672
content: Calculating maximal heart rate (HR max) is another method for establishing training intensity during cardiorespiratory exercise. The most commonly used formula for estimating HR max is (220 - age). This formula was never intended to be used as an instrument for designing cardiorespiratory fitness programs because maximal heart rate varies significantly among individuals of the same age. Estimating maximal heart rate from mathematical formulas can produce results that are ±10 to 12 beats per minute off the actual maximal heart rate. Accordingly, personal trainers should never use this, or any other formula, as an absolute.
summary: This chunk discusses using heart rate to determine training intensity, highlighting the limitations of using formulas to estimate maximal heart rate.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0673
content: Heart rate reserve (HRR), also known as the Karvonen method, is a method of establishing training intensity based on the difference between a client’s predicted maximal heart rate and their resting heart rate. Because heart rate and oxygen uptake are linearly related during dynamic exercise, selecting a predetermined training or target heart rate (THR) based on a given percentage of oxygen consumption is the most common and universally accepted method of establishing exercise training intensity. The heart rate reserve (HRR) method is defined as: THR = [(HR max - HR rest) x desired intensity] + HR rest.
summary: This chunk explains the heart rate reserve method for determining training intensity and emphasizes the importance of oxygen consumption in exercise prescription.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0674
content: A subjective rating of perceived exertion is a technique used to express or validate how hard a client feels he or she is working during exercise. When using the rating of perceived exertion (RPE) method, a person is subjectively rating the perceived difficulty of exercise. It is based on the physical sensations a person experiences during physical activity, including increased heart rate, increased respiration rate, increased sweating, and muscle fatigue. The client’s subjective rating should be reported based on the overall feelings of how hard he or she is working, including an overall sense of fatigue and not just isolated areas of the body, i.e., tired legs during treadmill testing. Although the RPE scale is a subjective measure, if clients report their exertion ratings accurately, RPE does provide a fairly good estimate of the actual heart rate during physical activity.
summary: This chunk describes the rating of perceived exertion (RPE) method for gauging exercise intensity and its relationship to physiological responses.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0675
content: Moderate-intensity activity is equal to “somewhat hard” (12 – 14) on the 6–20 Borg scale. Up until quite recently, the talk test has been an informal method used to gauge exercise training intensity during exercise. The belief has always been that if clients reach a point at which they are not able to carry on a simple conversation during exercise because they are breathing too hard, then they are probably exercising at too high of an intensity level. A number of studies have now confirmed that there is a correlation between the talk test, V.o2, ventilatory threshold (T vent), and heart rate during both cycle ergometer and treadmill exercise. Thus, it appears that the talk test can help personal trainers and clients monitor proper exercise intensity without having to rely on measuring heart rate or V.o2max.
summary: This chunk discusses the talk test as a method for monitoring exercise intensity and its correlation with physiological measures.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0676
content: Time refers to the length of time engaged in an activity or exercise training session and is typically expressed in minutes. According to the 2008 Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans, for health benefits, adults should accumulate 150 minutes of moderate-intensity aerobic activity every week or 75 minutes of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity every week or an equivalent mix of moderate- and vigorous-intensity aerobic activity.
summary: This chunk defines the importance of time spent exercising and outlines recommended weekly activity guidelines.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0677
content: Type refers to the mode or type of activity selected. For a mode of exercise to be considered aerobic, it should be rhythmic in nature, use large muscle groups, and be continuous in nature. Some examples of modes of exercise recommended to improve cardiorespiratory fitness include running or jogging, walking, exercising on cardio equipment, swimming, and cycling (indoors or outdoors).
summary: This chunk defines the type of exercise and provides examples of activities that improve cardiorespiratory fitness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0678
content: Enjoyment refers to the amount of pleasure derived from engaging in a specific exercise or activity. A client is more likely to continue with a fun and challenging program than a dull and boring one.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of enjoyment in exercise adherence.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0679
content: Type refers to the mode or type of activity selected. For a mode of exercise to be considered aerobic, it should be rhythmic in nature, use large muscle groups, and be continuous in nature. Some examples of modes of exercise recommended to improve cardiorespiratory fitness include running or jogging, walking, exercising on cardio equipment, swimming, and cycling (indoors or outdoors).
summary: This chunk defines aerobic exercise and provides examples.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0680
content: Enjoyment refers to the amount of pleasure derived from engaging in a specific exercise or activity. A client is more likely to continue with a fun and challenging program than a dull and boring one.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of enjoyment in exercise adherence.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0681
content: The 2008 Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans are the most current and comprehensive set of guidelines published by the US government to help Americans aged 6 and older improve their health through appropriate physical activity and exercise. The guidelines recommend adults engage in 150 minutes a week of moderate-intensity activity to improve overall health and fitness and reduce the risk of chronic diseases. If adults exceed 300 minutes a week of moderate-intensity activity or 150 minutes a week of vigorous-intensity activity, they will gain even more health benefits.
summary: This chunk outlines the 2008 Physical Activity Guidelines for Americans.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0682
content: The guidelines in Table 8.6 represent physical activity guidelines for all adults, especially those who are currently sedentary or have little previous experience with exercise. If clients are not able to achieve the suggested minimum guidelines for cardiorespiratory training of 150 minutes per week (or 30 minutes 5 times a week) of moderate-intensity aerobic activity on at least 5 days per week, they can break it up into shorter increments, for example 10 minutes at a time, until 150 minutes per week is met.
summary: This chunk provides specific recommendations for adults, including those who are new to exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0683
content: The FITTE guidelines allow for the development of a proper program and quantify its variables. For general health requirements, the recommended frequency of activity is preferably every day of the week, for small quantities of time. For improved fitness levels, the frequency is 3 to 5 days per week at higher intensities. For general health requirements, moderate intensity is preferred, of less than 60% V.o2max. Higher intensities of greater than 60% V.o2max are generally required for improvements in overall fitness and conditioning.
summary: This chunk describes the FITTE guidelines for exercise program design.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0684
content: Adults should accumulate 2 hours and 30 minutes (150 minutes) of moderate-intensity aerobic activity (i.e., brisk walking) every week or 1 hour and 15 minutes (75 minutes) of vigorous-intensity aerobic activity (i.e., jogging or running) every week or an equivalent mix of moderate- and vigorous-intensity aerobic activity.
summary: This chunk provides specific recommendations for weekly aerobic activity duration.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0685
content: Cardiorespiratory training falls under the principle of specificity. According to the principle of specificity, the body will adapt to the level of stress placed on it and will then require more or varied amounts of stress to produce a higher level of adaptation in the future.
summary: This chunk explains the principle of specificity in the context of cardiorespiratory training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0686
content: The purpose of stage training is to ensure that cardiorespiratory training programs progress in an organized fashion to ensure continual adaptation and to minimize the risk of overtraining and injury.
summary: This chunk describes the purpose of stage training in cardiorespiratory training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0687
content: Stage I is designed to help improve cardiorespiratory fitness levels in apparently healthy sedentary clients using a target heart rate of 65 to 75% of HR max or approximately 12 to 13 on the rating of perceived exertion scale (zone one). If using the talk test method to monitor training intensity, the client should be able to hold a conversation during the duration of the activity. In stage I, clients should start slowly and gradually work up to 30 to 60 minutes of continuous exercise in zone one. If the client has never exercised before, he or she might start in zone one for only 5 minutes or reduce the heart rate percentage to the general health activity recommendations discussed in Table 8.6. Stage I training helps a client to better meet the muscular endurance demands of the stabilization level of training in the OPT model.
summary: This chunk details Stage I of cardiorespiratory training, focusing on beginners and low-intensity exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0688
content: Stage II is designed for clients with low-to-moderate cardiorespiratory fitness levels who are ready to begin training at higher intensity levels. The focus in Stage II is on increasing the workload (speed, incline, level) in a way that will help the client alter heart rate in and out of zone one and zone two. Stage II training helps increase the cardiorespiratory capacity needed for the workout styles in the strength level of the OPT model. Stage II is the introduction to interval training in which intensities are varied throughout the workout.
summary: This chunk describes Stage II of cardiorespiratory training, focusing on increasing intensity and introducing interval training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0689
content: A typical Stage II workout starts with a 5 to 10 minute warm-up in zone one. During a Stage II workout, move into a 1-minute interval in zone two, gradually increasing the workload to raise the heart rate up to zone two within that minute. Once the heart rate reaches zone two of maximal heart rate, maintain it for the rest of that minute. After the 1-minute interval, return to zone one for 3 minutes. Repeat the 1-minute interval and 3-minute recovery if the client has time and can recover back into the zone one range. The most important part of the interval is to recover back to zone one between the intervals. During the first workout, questions may need to be asked frequently to determine if adjustments need to be made. If the client wasn’t able to reach the predicted zone two in 1 minute, then use the heart rate he or she was able to reach as their ‘85%.’ Take 9% off this number to get the lower end of the client’s readjusted zone. For example, if 150 beats per minute (bpm) was the predicted 85% of HR max, but the client was only able to work up to 145 bpm during the 1-minute push, 145 bpm should now be considered that client’s 85% HR max.
summary: This chunk provides a detailed description of a typical Stage II workout, including warm-up, interval structure, and recovery.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0690
content: The most important part of the interval is to recover back to zone one between the intervals.  If the client wasn’t able to reach the predicted zone two in 1 minute, then use the heart rate he or she was able to reach as their ‘85%. Take 9% off this number to get the lower end of the client’s readjusted zone. For example, if 150 beats per minute (bpm) was the predicted 85% of HR max, but the client was only able to work up to 145 bpm during the 1-minute push, 145 bpm should now be considered that client’s 85% HR max. Take 9% off 145% (9% of 145 is 13 beats; 145 - 13 = 132). So, 132 bpm is the individual’s 76% of HR max.
summary: This chunk describes how to adjust heart rate zones based on a client's initial performance during a 1-minute interval.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0691
content: If the client got into the readjusted zone two, and then reaching the zones was fine, work slowly to increase the client’s time in this zone. If the client’s heart rate goes above the predicted zone and he or she still can recover back to zone one at the end, add a couple of beats per minute to the zone and then work on increasing the time.
summary: This chunk outlines how to progressively increase the duration a client spends in a specific heart rate zone during training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0692
content: In stage II, it is important to alternate days of the week with stage I training. This means alternating sessions every workout. Start with stage I on Monday. Then, move to stage II on Wednesday and go back to stage I on Friday. The next week, start with stage II and so on. Rotate the stages to keep workouts balanced. This will become very important in stage III.
summary: This chunk explains the alternating schedule for stage I and stage II training, emphasizing the importance of balance and its relevance to stage III.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0693
content: The monthly plan is only a general guide and may be changed on the basis of the workout (if any) being performed on that day. As a general rule, intervals should start out relatively brief as previously demonstrated with a work-to-rest (hard-to-easy) ratio of 1:3 (e.g., 1-minute interval followed by a 3-minute recovery). Once fitness and overall conditioning improves, stage II programs can be progressed using 1:2 and eventually 1:1 work-to-rest ratios. Moreover, the duration of each of these intervals can be gradually increased in regular implements.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on interval training progression, emphasizing flexibility based on individual progress and the importance of gradually increasing work-to-rest ratios and interval duration.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0694
content: Stage III is for the advanced client who has a moderately high cardiorespiratory fitness level base and will use heart rate zones one, two, and three. The focus in this stage is on further increasing the workload (speed, incline, level) in a way that will help the client alter heart rate in and out of each zone. Stage III training increases the capacity of the energy systems needed at the power level of the OPT model.
summary: This chunk describes stage III training, which is designed for advanced clients with a high fitness level and focuses on progressively increasing workload to challenge their cardiovascular system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0695
content: A Stage III workout starts with a warm-up in zone one for up to 10 minutes. Then, increase the workload every 60 seconds until reaching zone three. This will require a slow climb through zone two for at least 2 minutes. After pushing for another minute in zone three, decrease the workload. This 1-minute break is an important minute to help gauge improvement. Drop the client’s workload down to the level he or she was just working in, before starting the zone 3 interval. During this minute, the heart rate will drop. As improvements are made during several weeks of training, the heart rate will drop more quickly. The faster the heart rate drops, the stronger the heart is getting.
summary: This chunk outlines a typical stage III workout structure, emphasizing the importance of monitoring heart rate recovery as a measure of progress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0696
content: If the client is not able to drop to the appropriate heart rate during the 1-minute break, assume that he or she is tired and about to overtrain. The solution is to stay in zone one or two for the rest of the workout. The bottom line is that the client is not rested enough to do that type of exercise on that day (which may be because of a hard workout the day before, not enough sleep, or poor nutrition). Monitoring heart rate is an excellent tool in avoiding overtraining. If the heart rate does drop to a normal rate, then overload the body again and go to the next zone, zone three, for 1 minute. After this minute, go back to zone one for 5–10 minutes and repeat if desired.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on recognizing and preventing overtraining by monitoring heart rate recovery and adjusting workout intensity accordingly.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0697
content: It is vital when training at this level to rotate all three stages. There will be a low-(stage I), medium-(stage II), and high-intensity day (stage III) to help minimize the risk of overtraining. The monthly plan is only a general guide and may change based on the workout (if any) that is being performed on that day. Intervals within zone 3 should start out relatively brief, 30 to 60 seconds. Once fitness and overall conditioning improves, stage III programs can be progressed similarly to stage II workouts, decreasing work-to-rest ratios and increasing the duration of high-intensity intervals. However, the frequency and duration of intervals in zone two and zone three should be client-specific based on their goals, needs, abilities, and tolerance to intense activity. Zone 1: HRmax (65%–75%), RPE = 12–13, Warm-up 5–10 min, Cool down 5–10 min
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of rotating training stages to prevent overtraining and provides guidance on progressing stage III workouts based on individual client needs and goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0698
content: The monthly plan is only a general guide and may change based on the workout (if any) that is being performed on that day.
summary: Monthly workout plans are flexible and can be adjusted based on daily workouts.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0699
content:
Zone 1: HRmax (65%–75%), RPE = 12–13, Warm-up 5–10 min, Cool down 5–10 min
Zone 2: HRmax (76%–85%), RPE = 14–16, Warm-up/Cool-down Zone 1: Recovery Zone 2: Lactate Threshold, 1 min
Zone 3: HRmax (86%–95%), RPE = 17–19, Warm-up/Cool-down Zone 1: Recovery Zone 2: Lactate Threshold Zone 3: Peak/Interval, 1 min
summary: Description of three heart rate zones (1-3) with corresponding percentages, RPE, and warm-up/cool-down durations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0700
content: Intervals within zone 3 should start out relatively brief, 30 to 60 seconds.
summary: Initial high-intensity intervals in zone 3 should be short (30-60 seconds).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0701
content: Once fitness and overall conditioning improves, stage III programs can be progressed similarly to stage II workouts, decreasing work-to-rest ratios and increasing the duration of high-intensity intervals.
summary: Progression of stage III workouts involves decreasing work-to-rest ratios and increasing high-intensity interval duration as fitness improves.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0702
content: However, the frequency and duration of intervals in zone two and zone three should be client-specific based on their goals, needs, abilities, and tolerance to intense activity.
summary: Interval frequency and duration in zones 2 and 3 should be individualized based on client factors.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0703
content: At the strength level, the warm-up and cool-down may be performed separately by the client once they have received proper instructions from the fitness professional.
summary: At the strength level, warm-up and cool-down can be performed separately with proper instruction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0704
content: This will allow for more time to be spent on the cardiorespiratory and circuit-training components.
summary: Separating warm-up and cool-down allows for more time dedicated to cardio and circuit training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0705
content: Advanced Client (Power Level) requires a 5–10 minute warm-up consisting of flexibility (self-myofascial release and dynamic stretching).
summary: Advanced clients require a 5-10 minute warm-up including self-myofascial release and dynamic stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0706
content: Advanced Clients (Power Level) require 5–10 minutes of Stage III cardiorespiratory training.
summary: Advanced clients require 5-10 minutes of stage III cardiorespiratory training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0707
content: Advanced Clients (Power Level) require 15–20 minutes of circuit weight training.
summary: Advanced clients require 15-20 minutes of circuit weight training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0708
content: Advanced Clients (Power Level) require 5–10 minutes of flexibility (self-myofascial release and static stretching).
summary: Advanced clients require 5-10 minutes of flexibility exercises (self-myofascial release and static stretching).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0709
content: Similar to the strength level, the warm-up and cool-down may be performed separately by the client as long as he or she has received proper instructions from the fitness professional.
summary: Warm-up and cool-down can be performed separately for advanced clients with proper instruction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0710
content: As any form of cardiorespiratory training involves movement, it must follow the same kinetic chain technique parameters as flexibility and resistance training exercises.
summary: Cardiorespiratory training should adhere to the same kinetic chain technique as flexibility and resistance exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0711
content: Selecting the appropriate form of cardiorespiratory training is also important for the beginner and should be approached as follows:
summary: Choosing the right cardio for beginners requires careful consideration.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0712
content:
Clients Who Possess a Rounded Shoulder and/or Forward Head Posture (Upper Crossed Syndrome) must be watched closely for the following kinetic chain deviations: N During use of stationary bicycles, treadmills, and elliptical trainers, watch closely for rounding of shoulders forward and a protruding head.
Clients Who Possess a Rounded Shoulder and/or Forward Head Posture (Upper Crossed Syndrome) must be watched closely for the following kinetic chain deviations: N On steppers and treadmills, watch for the grasping of the handles (with an oversupinated or overpronated hand position), which will cause elevated and protracted shoulders and a forward head. If possible, this equipment should be used without the assistance of the hands to increase the stabilization component, elevating the caloric expenditure and balance requirements.
Clients Who Possess a Rounded Shoulder and/or Forward Head Posture (Upper Crossed Syndrome) must be watched closely for the following kinetic chain deviations: N In settings in which a television is present, watch for excessive cervical extension (looking upward) or rotation of the head to view the television.
summary: Monitoring clients with upper crossed syndrome during cardio for proper posture and technique.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0713
content:
Clients Who Possess an Anteriorly Rotated Pelvis and Arched Lower Back (Lower Crossed Syndrome) must be watched closely for the following kinetic chain deviations: N Initial use of bicycles or steppers may not be warranted, as the hips are placed in a constant state of flexion, adding to a shortened hip flexor complex. If they are used, emphasize corrective flexibility techniques for the hip flexors before and after use.
Clients Who Possess an Anteriorly Rotated Pelvis and Arched Lower Back (Lower Crossed Syndrome) must be watched closely for the following kinetic chain deviations: N Treadmill speed should be kept to a controllable pace to avoid overstriding. The hips may not be able to properly extend and may cause the low back to overextend (arch), placing increased stress on the low back. Corrective flexibility for the hip flexors should be emphasized before and after use.
summary: Monitoring clients with lower crossed syndrome during cardio for proper hip and lower back mechanics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0714
content:
Clients Whose Feet Turn Out and/or Knees Move In (Pronation Distortion Syndrome) must be watched closely for the following kinetic chain deviations: N Use of the all cardio equipment that involves the lower extremities will require proper flexibility of the ankle joint. Emphasize foam rolling and static stretching for the calves, adductors, biceps femoris (short head), iliotibial (IT) band, and tensor fascia latae (TFL).
Clients Whose Feet Turn Out and/or Knees Move In (Pronation Distortion Syndrome) must be watched closely for the following kinetic chain deviations: N Using the treadmill and steppers that require climbing (or aerobics classes) may initially be too extreme for constant repetition, especially if clients are allowed to hold on to the rails and speed up the pace. If these modalities are used, emphasize the flexibility exercises mentioned above and keep the pace at a controllable speed.
summary: Monitoring clients with pronation distortion syndrome during cardio for proper ankle and lower extremity flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0715
content: Different cardiorespiratory training programs place different demands on the cardio-respiratory and muscular systems and ultimately affect a client’s adaptations and goals.
summary: Various cardio programs have different impacts on the cardiovascular and muscular systems, influencing client adaptations and goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0716
content: Stage training is a three-stage programming system that uses different heart rate training zones.
summary: Stage training utilizes three heart rate zones for programming.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0717
content:
Zone one consists of a heart rate of approximately 65 to 75% of predicted HR max and is intended for beginners and used as a recovery zone for more advanced clients.
Zone two consists of a heart rate of approximately 76 to 85% of predicted HR max, and is intended for individuals who have progressed to the strength level of the OPT model.
Zone three is at 86 to 95% of age-predicted HR max, and is intended for short bouts for individuals who have progressed to the power level of the OPT model.
summary: Description of the three heart rate zones (1-3) and their intended use for different client levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0718
content: These zones can be translated into three stages, which dictate in which zone and for what length of time cardiorespiratory activity should be performed.
summary: The three heart rate zones are used to create three stages of training, dictating the zone and duration of cardio activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0719
content: Circuit training programs consist of a series of resistance training exercises that an individual performs, one after the other, with minimal rest. Thus, they allow for comparable fitness results in shorter periods of time. It is also a good way to accomplish cardiorespiratory work during weight training.
summary: Circuit training involves performing resistance exercises consecutively with minimal rest, allowing for efficient fitness gains and incorporating cardio during weight training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0720
content: Because movement is involved, it is vital to monitor all kinetic chain checkpoints with clients who are performing cardiorespiratory activity.
summary: Monitoring kinetic chain checkpoints during cardio is crucial for clients.

The cool-down includes SMR: Calves, IT Band, Adductors Hold each tender area for 30 sec1 and Static Stretch: Calves, Hip Flexors, Adductors

content: Foster C, Porcari JP, Anderson J, et al. published a study in J Cardiopulm Rehabil Prev in 2008 on the talk test as a marker of exercise training intensity. Kaikkonen H, Yrlama M, Siljander E, Byman P, and Laukkanen R published a study in Scand J Med Sci Sports in 2000 on the effect of heart rate controlled low resistance circuit weight training and endurance training on maximal aerobic power in sedentary adults. Weltman A, Seip RL, Snead D, et al. published a study in Int J Sports Med in 1992 on exercise training at and above the lactate threshold in previously untrained women.
