id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0736
content: Core training has become a popular fitness trend and a common method of training used by personal trainers. The objective of core training is to uniformly strengthen the deep and superficial muscles that stabilize, align, and move the trunk of the body, especially the abdominals and muscles of the back. Historically, physical therapists prescribed core exercises for patients with low-back problems, and more recently core training has become popular among athletes to help improve sports performance. Core training is now commonly incorporated into exercise programs for clients at health clubs to help achieve goals ranging from a flatter midsection to a stronger lower back.
summary: This chunk describes the history, purpose, and current popularity of core training, highlighting its benefits for various populations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0737
content: A weak core is a fundamental problem inherent to inefficient movement that may lead to predictable patterns of injury (1–6). A properly designed core training program helps an individual gain neuromuscular control, stability, muscular endurance, strength, and power of the core. Personal trainers must have a basic understanding of functional anatomy to understand the principles of core training.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of core strength for movement efficiency and injury prevention, and the role of proper core training in achieving these benefits.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0738
content: The core is defined by the structures that make up the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex (LPHC), including the lumbar spine, the pelvic girdle, abdomen, and the hip joint (7–9). The core is where the body’s center of gravity (COG) is located and where all movement originates (10–13). A strong and efficient core is necessary for maintaining proper muscle balance throughout the entire human movement system (kinetic chain).
summary: This chunk defines the core and its anatomical components, highlighting its role as the body's center of gravity and the origin of all movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0739
content: Optimal lengths (or length-tension relationships), recruitment patterns (or force-couple relationships), and joint motions (or arthrokinematics) in the muscles of the LPHC establish neuromuscular efficiency throughout the entire human movement system. These factors allow for efficient acceleration, deceleration, and stabilization during dynamic movements, as well as the prevention of possible injuries (14, 15).
summary: This chunk explains how optimal core muscle function contributes to neuromuscular efficiency and movement control, reducing the risk of injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0740
content: The core musculature has been divided into the local stabilization system, global stabilization system, and the movement system. To maintain core stability, neuromuscular control of the local and global stabilization systems and the movement system is required, ensuring sequential coordinated activation of all systems at the right time with the right amount of force.
summary: This chunk introduces the three core muscle systems: local stabilization, global stabilization, and movement, emphasizing the importance of coordinated activation for stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0741
content: The local core stabilizers are muscles that attach directly to the vertebrae. These muscles consist primarily of type I (slow twitch) muscle fibers with a high density of muscle spindles (Table 9.1). Core stabilizing muscles are primarily responsible for intervertebral and intersegmental stability and work to limit excessive compressive, shear, and rotational forces between spinal segments. Another way to view the function of core stabilizing muscles is that they provide support from vertebra to vertebra. These muscles also aid in proprioception and postural control because of their high density of muscle spindles (16).
summary: This chunk describes the local core stabilizers, their muscle fiber type, function, and role in proprioception and postural control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0742
content: The primary muscles that make up the local stabilization system include the transverse abdominis, internal obliques, multifidus, pelvic floor musculature, and diaphragm. These muscles contribute to segmental spinal stability by increasing intra-abdominal pressure (pressure within the abdominal cavity) and generating tension in the thoracolumbar fascia (connective tissue of the low back), thus increasing spinal stiffness for improved intersegmental neuromuscular control (17–20).
summary: This chunk lists the primary muscles of the local stabilization system and explains their role in spinal stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0743
content: The muscles of the global stabilization system attach from the pelvis to the spine. These muscles act to transfer loads between the upper extremity and lower extremity, provide stability between the pelvis and spine, and provide stabilization and eccentric control. The primary muscles that make up the global stabilization system include the quadratus lumborum, psoas major, external obliques, portions of the internal oblique, rectus abdominis, gluteus medius, and adductor complex.
summary: This chunk describes the global stabilization system, its function, and the primary muscles involved.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0744
content: The movement system includes muscles that attach the spine and/or pelvis to the extremities. These muscles are primarily responsible for concentric force production and eccentric deceleration during dynamic activities. The primary muscles that make up the movement system include the latissimus dorsi, hip flexors, hamstring complex, and quadriceps. Collectively all of the muscles within each system provide dynamic stabilization and neuromuscular control of the entire core (LPHC). These muscles produce force (concentric contractions), reduce force (eccentric actions), and provide dynamic stabilization in all planes of movement during functional activities. In isolation, these muscles do not effectively achieve stabilization of the LPHC; rather it is through their synergistic interdependent functioning that they enhance stability and neuromuscular control.
summary: This chunk describes the movement system, its function, and the primary muscles involved, emphasizing the importance of synergistic muscle action for core stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0745
content: To better understand how these muscles work to stabilize the LPHC, it helps to view the systems from the inside out (local stabilization system → global stabilization system → movement system). Training the movement system muscles before training the muscles of the global and local stabilization systems would not make sense from both a structural and biomechanical standpoint. Some active individuals have developed strength, power, and muscular endurance in the movement system, which enables them to perform functional activities. Few people, however, have properly developed the local stabilization muscles required for intervertebral stabilization.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of training the core muscle systems in a specific order, highlighting the need for strong local stabilization muscles for effective core function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0746
content: The body’s core stabilization system has to be operating with maximal efficiency to effectively use the strength, power, and endurance that has been developed in the prime movers. If the movement system musculature of the core is strong and the local stabilization system is weak, the kinetic chain senses imbalance and forces are not transferred or used properly. This leads to compensation, synergistic dominance, and inefficient movements. Examples include performing a lunge, squat, or overhead press with excessive spinal extension. A weak core is a fundamental problem that causes inefficient movement and can lead to predictable patterns of injury.
summary: A strong core is essential for efficient force transfer and movement. Weakness in the core's stabilization systems can lead to compensation, inefficient movement, and injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0747
content: The core is defined by the structures that make up the LPHC, including the lumbar spine, the pelvic girdle, abdomen, and the hip joint. The core is the origin of all movement and where the center of gravity for the body is located. If the core is unstable during movement, it does not allow optimal stabilization, force reduction, force production, and transference to occur throughout the kinetic chain. An efficient core is necessary for maintaining proper muscle balance throughout the entire human movement system.
summary: The core, encompassing the lumbar spine, pelvis, abdomen, and hip joint, is the origin of movement and requires stability for efficient force transfer and muscle balance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0748
content: Optimal lengths (length-tension relationships), recruitment patterns (force-couple relationships), and joint motions (arthrokinematics) in the muscles of the LPHC establish neuromuscular efficiency throughout the entire human movement system, which allows for efficient acceleration, deceleration, and stabilization during dynamic movements, as well as the prevention of possible injuries.
summary: Proper muscle length, recruitment, and joint motion in the LPHC contribute to neuromuscular efficiency, enabling efficient movement and injury prevention.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0749
content: In isolation, these muscles do not effectively achieve stabilization of the LPHC; rather it is through their synergistic interdependent functioning that they enhance stability and neuromuscular control. To better understand how these muscles work to stabilize the LPHC, it helps to view the systems from the inside out (local stabilization system → global stabilization system → movement system). Training the movement system muscles before training the muscles of the global and local stabilization systems would not make sense from both a structural and biomechanical standpoint.
summary: Effective LPHC stabilization relies on the synergistic function of muscles, progressing from local to global to movement systems.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0750
content: The core musculature is divided into the local stabilization system, the global stabilization system, and the movement system. The local core stabilizers are muscles that attach directly to the vertebrae. These muscles help provide support from vertebra to vertebra. The global stabilizers are muscles that attach from the pelvis to the spine. These muscles act to transfer loads between the upper extremities and lower extremities and provide stability between the pelvis and spine. The movement system includes muscles that attach the spine and/or pelvis to the extremities. These muscles are primarily responsible for concentric force production and eccentric deceleration during dynamic activities.
summary: The core musculature is categorized into local stabilizers, global stabilizers, and movement system muscles, each with distinct functions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0751
content: Collectively, these systems work from the inside out (local stabilization system → global stabilization system → movement system). If the core’s movement system musculature is strong and the stabilization systems are weak, the human movement system senses imbalance and forces are not transferred or used properly. This all may result in compensation, synergistic dominance, and inefficient movements.
summary: The core's stabilization systems function hierarchically, with imbalances potentially leading to compensation and inefficient movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0752
content: Researchers have found that individuals with chronic LBP (~80% of U.S. adults) have decreased activation of certain muscles or muscle groups, including transverse abdominis, internal obliques, pelvic floor muscles, multifidus, diaphragm, and deep erector spinae. Individuals with chronic LBP also tend to have weaker back extensor muscles (33) decreased muscular endurance (34, 35). Trunk muscle weakness is an independent risk factor for developing LBP (36). Numerous studies support the role of core training in the prevention and rehabilitation of LBP.
summary: Chronic low back pain is often associated with decreased activation and strength of core muscles, highlighting the importance of core training for prevention and rehabilitation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0753
content: Core stabilization exercises restore the size, activation, and endurance of the multifidus (deep spine muscle) in individuals with LBP (37). Exercise programs that include specific core stabilization training tend to be more effective than manual therapy alone, traditional medical management alone, or other common exercise programs reported to reduce pain and improve function in those with acute and chronic LBP (3, 37, 38).
summary: Core stabilization exercises are beneficial for individuals with LBP, improving multifidus function and overall pain management compared to other treatment approaches.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0754
content: Clients and athletes with lower extremity pain (5), long-standing adductor (inner thigh) pain (39), hamstring strain (40), iliotibial band syndrome (runner’s knee) (41), and LBP (5) have a decreased chance of injury, less recurrence of injury, and improved performance measures after undergoing an active rehabilitation program aimed at improving strength and neuromuscular control of the core (LPHC) muscles.
summary: Strengthening and improving neuromuscular control of the core muscles can benefit individuals with various lower extremity pain conditions and reduce the risk of injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0755
content: A proper application of specific core training programs can reduce pain and help improve function and performance. Specific instructions on the neuromechanical activation of the local stabilization system (drawing in) (7, 42) and the global movement system (bracing) (16) have demonstrated preferential activation of these specific muscles during the core training continuum. The drawing-in maneuver and bracing will be discussed later in the next section.
summary: Targeted core training programs, including drawing-in and bracing techniques, can improve function and performance while reducing pain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0756
content: Traditional low-back hyperextension exercises without proper lumbo-pelvic-hip stabilization have been shown to increase pressure on the discs to 36. In a cross-sectional study with 14 men with a clinical diagnosis of back pain, Hungerford et al. (1) found delayed onset of the transverse abdominis, internal obliques, multifidus, and gluteus maximus during hip flexion on the support leg, suggesting an alteration in the strategy for lumbo-pelvic-hip stability. Ebenbichler et al. (2) found that single-leg stance balance in individuals with LBP is less efficient and effective than healthy controls. Hodges and Richardson (3) reported that slow speed of contraction of the transverse abdominis during arm and leg movements was well correlated with LBP. Hides et al. (4) demonstrated that recovery from acute back pain did not automatically result in restoration of the normal girth of the multifidus. Hides et al. (5) demonstrated multifidus atrophy in patients with LBP.
summary: Proper lumbo-pelvic-hip stabilization is crucial during exercises, as improper form can increase disc pressure and contribute to back pain. Studies have shown altered muscle activation and atrophy in individuals with LBP.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0757
content: In a cross-sectional study with 14 men with a clinical diagnosis of back pain, Hungerford et al. (1) found delayed onset of the transverse abdominis, internal obliques, multifidus, and gluteus maximus during hip flexion on the support leg, suggesting an alteration in the strategy for lumbo-pelvic-hip stability. Ebenbichler et al. (2) found that single-leg stance balance in individuals with LBP is less efficient and effective than healthy controls. Hodges and Richardson (3) reported that slow speed of contraction of the transverse abdominis during arm and leg movements was well correlated with LBP. Hides et al. (4) demonstrated that recovery from acute back pain did not automatically result in restoration of the normal girth of the multifidus. Hides et al. (5) demonstrated multifidus atrophy in patients with LBP.
summary: Research indicates that individuals with LBP exhibit altered muscle activation patterns and reduced muscle function, particularly in the core muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0758
content: Unsupported exercises can cause damage to the ligaments supporting the vertebrae. These exercises may lead to a narrowing of openings in the vertebrae that spinal nerves pass through (43–45).
summary: Unsuitable exercises can harm the spine by damaging ligaments and compressing spinal nerves.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0759
content: It is crucial for personal trainers to incorporate a systematic, progressive approach when training the core. The muscles that stabilize the spine (local stabilization systems) should be strengthened before the musculature that moves the spine and extremities (movement system).
summary: Effective core training requires a structured approach, prioritizing local stabilization muscles before movement-focused exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0760
content: Electromyography (EMG) is a procedure that measures the electrical conducting function of nerves in muscles. An EMG test is able to identify differences in muscle or muscle group activation when performing different movements or exercises.
summary: EMG is a tool used to assess muscle activation patterns during various movements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0761
content: Research has demonstrated that electromyogram (EMG) activity is increased during pelvic stabilization and transverse abdominis activation when an abdominal drawing-in maneuver is initiated before activity (31, 46, 47). The transverse abdominis, when properly activated, creates tension in the thoracolumbar fascia, contributing to spinal stiffness, and compresses the sacroiliac joint, increasing stability (48, 49). These findings have led other researchers to further understand and demonstrate the important role of the transverse abdominis on spinal stability and LBP.
summary: EMG studies highlight the importance of the transverse abdominis in spinal stability and its role in reducing LBP.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0762
content: To perform the drawing-in maneuver, pull in the region just below the navel toward the spine and maintain the cervical spine in a neutral position. Maintaining a neutral spine during core training helps to improve posture, muscle balance, and stabilization. If a forward protruding head is noticed during the drawing-in maneuver, the sternocleidomastoid (large neck muscle) is preferentially recruited, which increases the compressive forces in the cervical spine and can lead to pelvic instability and muscle imbalances as a result of the pelvo-ocular reflex. Because of this reflex, it is important to maintain the eyes level during movement (50). If the sternocleidomastoid muscle is hyperactive and extends the upper cervical spine, the pelvis rotates anteriorly to realign the eyes. This can lead to muscle imbalances and decreased pelvic stabilization (51).
summary: Proper technique for the drawing-in maneuver emphasizes neutral spine alignment and avoiding excessive neck extension to prevent muscle imbalances and pelvic instability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0763
content: Bracing is referred to as a co-contraction of global muscles, such as the rectus abdominis, external obliques, and quadratus lumborum. Bracing is also commonly referred to as a ‘bearing down’ or tightening of the global muscles by consciously contracting them. Research has shown that muscular endurance of global and local musculature, when contracted together, create the most benefit for those with LBP compared with traditional LBP training methods (51). Bracing focuses on global trunk stability, not on segmental vertebral stability, meaning that the global muscles, given the proper endurance training, will work to stabilize the spine.
summary: Bracing involves contracting global core muscles to provide overall trunk stability, which is beneficial for individuals with LBP.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0764
content: Both strategies (drawing-in maneuver and bracing) can be implemented in a core training program to help retrain motor control of local and global stabilization systems and movement musculature as well as to help retrain the strength and endurance of these muscles (7, 51, 52). Activation of the local stabilization system (drawing-in) (7, 52) and the global stabilization system (bracing) (51) has been demonstrated to preferentially activate these specific muscles during core training.
summary: Integrating both drawing-in and bracing techniques into core training programs can enhance motor control, strength, and endurance of both local and global stabilization muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0765
content: A core training program should regularly manipulate plane of motion, range of motion, modalities (tubing, stability ball, medicine ball, BOSU ball, Airex pad, etc.), body position, amount of control, speed of execution, amount of feedback, and specific acute training variables (sets, reps, intensity, tempo, frequency). When designing a core training program, the personal trainer should initially create a proprioceptively enriched (controlled yet unstable training environment), selecting the appropriate exercises to elicit a maximal training response. Core exercises performed in an unstable environment (such as with a stability ball) have been demonstrated to increase activation of the local and global stabilization system when compared with traditional trunk exercises.
summary: Effective core training programs should incorporate variety in exercises, planes of motion, and training variables to challenge and strengthen the core muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0766
content: The use of weight belts for apparently healthy adults engaging in a moderately intense exercise program is not recommended. Weight belts may raise an individual’s heart rate and systolic blood pressure and often give individuals a false sense of security and the misconception they can lift heavier loads. Personal trainers need to educate their clients as to appropriate exercise technique and proper activation of the core musculature (the body’s natural belt).
summary: Weight belts are generally not recommended for healthy individuals during moderate exercise, as they can create a false sense of security and potentially increase blood pressure. Proper core activation should be emphasized instead.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0767
content: Cosio-Lima et al. (1) in a randomized controlled trial with 30 subjects demonstrated increased abdominal and back extensor strength and single-leg balance improvements with a 5-week stability ball training program compared with conventional floor exercises. Mills and Taunton (2) in a randomized controlled trial with 36 subjects demonstrated that agility and balance were improved after a 10-week specific spinal stabilization training program compared with the control group performing an equivalent volume of traditional, nonspecific abdominal exercises. Vera-Garcia et al. (3) in a single-subject design with 8 subjects found that performing abdominal exercises on a labile surface increased activation levels, suggesting increased demand on the motor control system to help stabilize the spine. Hahn et al. (4) in a randomized controlled trial with 35 female subjects demonstrated that traditional floor exercises and stability ball exercises significantly increased core strength during a 10-week training period. O’Sullivan et al. (5) in a randomized clinical trial demonstrated that pain and function improve initially and at 1- and 3-year follow-up in patients with LBP undergoing specific stabilizing exercises.
summary: Research supports the effectiveness of stability ball training, specific spinal stabilization programs, and traditional core exercises in improving core strength, balance, and reducing LBP.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0768
content: Clients with chronic LBP activate their core muscles less and have a lower endurance for stabilization. Performing traditional abdominal and low-back exercises without proper spinal and pelvic stabilization may cause abnormal forces throughout the LPHC which may lead to tissue overload and cause damage.
summary: Individuals with chronic LBP often exhibit weakened core activation and endurance, emphasizing the importance of proper spinal and pelvic stabilization during exercises to prevent injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0769
content: Performing traditional abdominal and low-back exercises without proper spinal and pelvic stabilization may cause abnormal forces throughout the LPHC which may lead to tissue overload and cause damage. Performing the drawing-in maneuver or bracing can help stabilize the pelvis and spine during core training and other functional movements. Keeping the cervical spine in a neutral position during core training improves posture, muscle balance, and stabilization. The goal of core training is to develop optimal levels of neuromuscular efficiency, stability (intervertebral and lumbopelvic stability—local and global stabilization systems), and functional strength (movement system).
summary: Proper spinal and pelvic stabilization during core training is crucial to prevent injury and enhance effectiveness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0770
content: Clients with chronic LBP activate their core muscles less and have a lower endurance for stabilization. O’Sullivan et al. (5) in a randomized clinical trial demonstrated that pain and function improve initially and at 1- and 3-year follow-up in patients with LBP undergoing specific stabilizing exercises.
summary: Individuals with chronic low back pain (LBP) exhibit reduced core muscle activation and endurance, highlighting the importance of stabilizing exercises for pain management and functional improvement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0771
content: Vera-Garcia et al. (3) in a single-subject design with 8 subjects found that performing abdominal exercises on a labile surface increased activation levels, suggesting increased demand on the motor control system to help stabilize the spine. Hahn et al. (4) in a randomized controlled trial with 35 female subjects demonstrated that traditional floor exercises and stability ball exercises significantly increased core strength during a 10-week training period.
summary: Performing core exercises on unstable surfaces and incorporating stability ball exercises can enhance core muscle activation and strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0772
content: Neural adaptations become the focus of the program instead of striving for absolute strength gains. Increasing proprioceptive demand by using a multisensory environment and using multiple modalities (balls, bands, balance equipment) is more important than increasing the external resistance. The quality of movement should be stressed over quantity, and the focus of the program should be on function.
summary: Core training should prioritize neuromuscular adaptations, proprioception, and functional movement quality over maximal strength gains.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0773
content: The client begins at the highest level at which he or she is able to maintain stability and optimal neuromuscular control. The client progresses through the program once mastery of the exercises in the previous level has been achieved while demonstrating intervertebral stability and lumbopelvic stability.
summary: Core training progression is individualized based on the client's ability to maintain stability and should be advanced once mastery of previous exercises is achieved.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0774
content: A client has appropriate intervertebral stability when able to maintain the drawing-in position while performing various exercises. A client has appropriate lumbopelvic stability when able to perform functional movement patterns (squats, lunges, step-ups, single-leg movements, pressing, pushing, etc.) without excessive spinal motion (flexion, extension, lateral flexion, rotation, singly or in combination).
summary: Intervertebral and lumbopelvic stability are assessed based on the client's ability to maintain proper form during exercises and functional movements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0775
content: It is critical that the core training program is designed to achieve the following functional outcomes: intervertebral stability, lumbopelvic stability, and movement efficiency. There are three levels of training within the OPT™ model: stabilization, strength, and power.
summary: Core training programs should aim to improve intervertebral stability, lumbopelvic stability, and movement efficiency, progressing through stabilization, strength, and power phases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0776
content: Core-stabilization training (Phase 1) involves exercises with little motion through the spine and pelvis. These exercises are designed to improve neuromuscular efficiency and intervertebral stability, focusing on drawing-in and then bracing during the exercises. The client would traditionally spend 4 weeks at this level of core training. Sample exercises in this level include: Marching, Floor bridge, Floor prone cobra, Prone iso-ab.
summary: The first phase of core training focuses on stabilization exercises with minimal spinal and pelvic movement to enhance neuromuscular efficiency and intervertebral stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0777
content: Core-strength training (Phases 2, 3, and 4) involves more dynamic eccentric and concentric movements of the spine throughout a full range of motion while clients perform the activation techniques learned in core-stabilization training (drawing-in and bracing). The specificity, speed, and neural demands are also progressed in core-strength training. Clients would traditionally spend 4 weeks at the level of core training. Exercises in this level are designed to improve dynamic stabilization, concentric strength (force production), eccentric strength (force reduction), and neuromuscular efficiency of the entire kinetic chain. Exercises in core-strength training include ball crunch, back extensions, reverse crunch, and cable rotations.
summary: Core-strength training progresses to more dynamic movements, incorporating eccentric and concentric contractions, and focuses on improving dynamic stabilization, strength, and neuromuscular efficiency.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0778
content: To perform a ball crunch, lie supine on a stability ball (ball under low back) with knees bent at a 90-degree angle. Place feet flat on the floor with toes shoulders-width apart and pointing straight ahead. Allow back to extend over the curve of the ball. Cross arms across the chest or place hands behind ears or head. Slowly crunch the upper body forward, raising shoulder blades off the ball. Slowly lower the upper body over the ball, returning to the start position. To progress, perform as a long-lever exercise (arms raised overhead). Keep the chin tucked while performing the ball crunch to take stress off of the muscles of the cervical spine.
summary: Instructions for performing a ball crunch exercise, emphasizing proper form and progression.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0779
content: To perform a back extension, lie prone on a back-extension bench with legs straight and toes shoulders-width apart and pointing straight ahead. Place pads on thighs and cross arms over the chest or hands behind ears. Activate gluteal muscles, tuck chin, and retract shoulder blades. Slowly lower the upper body toward the ground to the end range. Raise the upper body to a neutral position, keeping the chin tucked and shoulder blades retracted and depressed.
summary: Instructions for performing a back extension exercise, highlighting proper form and muscle activation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0780
content: To perform a reverse crunch, lie supine on a bench with hips and knees bent at a 90-degree angle, feet in the air, and hands gripping a stable object for support. Lift hips off the bench while bringing the knees toward the chest. Slowly lower the hips back to the start position. Do not swing the legs when performing the reverse crunch. Once you have positioned the lower extremities during the setup, they should not move during the execution of the exercise.
summary: Instructions for performing a reverse crunch exercise, emphasizing controlled movement and avoiding leg swinging.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0781
content: To perform a reverse crunch, lie supine on a bench with hips and knees bent at a 90-degree angle, feet in the air, and hands gripping a stable object for support. Lift hips off the bench while bringing the knees toward the chest. Slowly lower the hips back to the start position. Do not swing the legs when performing the reverse crunch. Once you have positioned the lower extremities during the setup, they should not move during the execution of the exercise.
summary: Instructions for performing a reverse crunch, emphasizing controlled movement and proper form.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0782
content: Make sure that at the end position of the exercise, the ankle, knee, hip, shoulders, and ears are all in alignment. Do not hyperextend the low back.
summary: Important considerations for proper form during a reverse crunch to avoid injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0783
content: To perform a cable rotation, stand with feet shoulders-width apart, knees slightly flexed, and toes pointing straight ahead. Hold a cable with both hands directly in front of the chest, with arms extended and shoulder blades retracted and depressed. Rotate the body away from the weight stack using abdominals and gluteal muscles. Allow the back foot to pivot to achieve triple extension (plantar flexion, knee extension, hip extension). Slowly return to the start position.
summary: Instructions for performing a cable rotation, emphasizing proper form and muscle engagement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0784
content: To decrease stress to the low back, make sure to pivot the back leg into triple extension: hip extension, knee extension, and ankle plantarflexion (extension). This also ensures proper neuromuscular efficiency of the muscles that extend the lower extremities (gluteus maximus, quadriceps, gastrocnemius, and soleus).
summary: Tips for performing a cable rotation to minimize low back stress and maximize muscle activation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0785
content: Core-power training (Phase 5) exercises are designed to improve the rate of force production of the core musculature. These forms of exercise prepare an individual to dynamically stabilize and generate force at more functionally applicable speeds. Exercises in this level include rotation chest pass, ball medicine ball (MB) pullover throw, front MB oblique throw, and soccer throw.
summary: Description of core-power training and its purpose, including example exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0786
content: The Rotation Chest Pass exercise involves standing upright with feet shoulder-width apart and toes pointing straight ahead. During the Rotation Chest Pass, an individual holds a medicine ball weighing between 5 and 10% of their body weight. The Rotation Chest Pass requires the use of abdominal muscles and hips to rotate the body quickly and explosively 90 degrees. As the body turns during the Rotation Chest Pass, the back leg should pivot and go into triple extension (hip extension, knee extension, ankle plantarflexion). The medicine ball is thrown with the rear arm extending and applying force during the Rotation Chest Pass.
summary: Detailed instructions for performing the Rotation Chest Pass exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0787
content: The Ball Medicine Ball Pullover Throw exercise requires proper stabilization and strength before performing. Performing core-power exercises without proper stabilization and strength may lead to movement compensations, muscle imbalances, and eventually injury. It is important for an individual to have proper extensibility of the latissimus dorsi before performing the Ball Medicine Ball Pullover Throw to decrease stress to the low back and shoulders.
summary: Important considerations for performing the Ball Medicine Ball Pullover Throw, emphasizing the need for proper preparation and form.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0788
content: The Ball Medicine Ball Pullover Throw involves lying on a stability ball with knees bent at a 90-degree angle, feet flat on the floor, and toes pointing straight ahead. During the Ball Medicine Ball Pullover Throw, an individual holds a medicine ball weighing between 5 and 10% of their body weight overhead with arms extended.
summary: Instructions for performing the Ball Medicine Ball Pullover Throw exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0789
content: The Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise requires standing facing a wall or partner with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, and toes pointing straight ahead. During the Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw, an individual holds a medicine ball weighing between 5 and 10% of their body weight as illustrated.
summary: Instructions for performing the Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0790
content: The Soccer Throw exercise requires going as fast as possible while maintaining proper exercise technique. It may be easier to perform the Soccer Throw exercise using a D-ball or close to a wall for the medicine ball to bounce off of. The Soccer Throw involves standing holding a medicine ball weighing 5 to 10% of body weight overhead.
summary: Instructions for performing the Soccer Throw exercise, emphasizing speed and proper technique.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0791
content: The core musculature helps protect the spine from harmful forces that occur during functional activities. A core-training program is designed to increase stabilization, strength, power, muscle endurance, and neuromuscular control in the LPHC. Core training programs must be systematic, progressive, activity or goal-specific, integrated, and proprioceptively challenging.
summary: Importance of core training for spinal protection and overall fitness, outlining key program characteristics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0792
content: A proper core training program follows the same systematic progression as the OPT model: stabilization, strength, and power. In core-stabilization training (Phase 1), the emphasis is on stabilization of the LPHC. Core-stabilization training improves the function of the stabilization system. In core-strength training (Phases 2, 3, and 4), the spine moves dynamically through a full range of motion, with exercises that require greater specificity, speed, and neural demand. Core-strength training improves neuromuscular efficiency of the entire kinetic chain.
summary: Core training progression following the OPT model, outlining phases and their focus.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0793
content: Exercises of core-power training (Phase 5) improve the rate of force production in the musculature of the LPHC (movement system). Implementing a core training program requires that fitness professionals follow the progression of the OPT model.
summary: Core-power training as the final phase of core training progression, emphasizing force production and the importance of following the OPT model.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0794
content: Core-stabilization training improves the function of the stabilization system. Core-strength training (Phases 2, 3, and 4), the spine moves dynamically through a full range of motion, with exercises that require greater specificity, speed, and neural demand. Core-strength training improves neuromuscular efficiency of the entire kinetic chain.
summary: Core-stabilization training enhances stabilization system function, while core-strength training progresses through dynamic movements, improving neuromuscular efficiency.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0795
content: Exercises of core-power training (Phase 5) improve the rate of force production in the musculature of the LPHC (movement system). Implementing a core training program requires that fitness professionals follow the progression of the OPT model. For a client in the stabilization level of training (Phase 1), select core-stabilization exercises. For a different client in the strength level of training (Phases 2, 3, or 4), the fitness professional should select core-strength exercises. For an advanced client in the power level of training (Phase 5), select core-power exercises.
summary: Core training follows the OPT model, progressing from stabilization to strength to power exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0796
content:

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0797
content:
Hodges PW and Richardson CA published a study in 1996 titled 'Ineffi cient muscular stabilization of the lumbar spine associated with low back pain. A motor control evaluation of transversus abdominis' in the journal Spine.
Nadler SF, Malanga GA, Bartoli LA, Feinberg JH, Prybicien M, and Deprince M published a study in 2002 titled 'Hip muscle imbalance and low back pain in athletes: infl uence of core strengthening' in the journal Med Sci Sports Exerc.
O'Sullivan PB, Phyty GD, Twomey LT, and Allison GT published a study in 1997 titled 'Evaluation of specifi c stabilizing exercise in the treatment of chronic low back pain with radiologic diagnosis of spondylolysis or spondylolis-thesis' in the journal Spine.
Hewett TE, Paterno MV, and Myer GD published a study in 2002 titled 'Strategies for enhancing proprioception and neuromuscular control of the knee' in the journal Clin Orthop Relat Res.
Leetun DT, Ireland ML, Willson JD, Ballantyne BT, and Davis IM published a study in 2004 titled 'Core stability measures as risk factors for lower extremity injury in athletes' in the journal Med Sci Sports Exerc.
Nadler SF, Moley P, Malanga GA, Rubbani M, Prybicien M, and Feinberg JH published a pilot study in 2002 titled 'Functional defi cits in athletes with a history of low back pain' in the journal Arch Phys Med Rehabil.
Arokoski JP, Valta T, Airaksinen O, and Kankaanpaa M published a study in 2001 titled 'Back and abdominal muscle function during stabilization exercises' in the journal Arch Phys Med Rehabil.
Bergmark A published a study in 1989 titled 'Stability of the lumbar spine. A study in mechanical engineering' in the journal Acta Orthop Scand Suppl.
summary: A collection of research studies on core stability, low back pain, and athletic performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0798
content:

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0821
content: Balance is dependent on both internal and external factors to maintain the body’s center of gravity over its base of support. Balance is often thought of as only a static process, but balance is also a dynamic process involving multiple neurologic pathways. Maintenance of postural equilibrium (or balance) is an integrated process requiring optimal muscular balance (or length-tension relationships and force-couple relationships), joint dynamics (or arthrokinematics), and neuromuscular efficiency using visual, vestibular (inner ear), and proprioceptive inputs. Research has shown that specific kinetic chain imbalances (such as altered length-tension relationships, force-couple relationships, and arthrokinematics) can lead to altered balance and neuromuscular inefficiency (4–13).
summary: Balance is a dynamic process reliant on internal and external factors, involving muscular, joint, and neurological components. Kinetic chain imbalances can disrupt balance and neuromuscular efficiency.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0822
content: Alterations in the kinetic chain before, during, or after exercise can further affect movement quality and bring about flawed movement patterns. Flawed movement patterns alter the firing order of the muscles activated, which disturbs specific functional movement patterns and decreases neuromuscular efficiency (3, 14, 15). Prime movers may be slow to activate, whereas synergists and stabilizers substitute and become overactive (synergistic dominance). The combined effects of flawed movement patterns lead to abnormal joint stress, which affects the structural integrity of the kinetic chain and may lead to pain and joint dysfunction, and further decrease neuromuscular efficiency (6).
summary: Kinetic chain alterations can lead to flawed movement patterns, disrupting muscle activation and decreasing neuromuscular efficiency. This can result in joint stress, pain, and dysfunction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0823
content: Research has shown that joint dysfunction creates muscle inhibition (6, 16, 17). Joint dysfunction leads to joint injury, swelling, and interruption of sensory input from articular, ligamentous, and muscular mechanoreceptors to the central nervous system, which results in a clinically evident disturbance in proprioception (Figure 10.2 (18)). It has been demonstrated that sensory feedback to the central nervous system is altered after ankle sprains, ligamentous injuries to the knee, and low-back pain (6, 8, 9, 19–24).
summary: Joint dysfunction leads to muscle inhibition, pain, swelling, and altered proprioception due to disrupted sensory feedback.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0824
content: The consequences of joint dysfunction are important for personal trainers to understand because 80% of the adult U.S. population will experience an episode of low-back pain at some point in their lives (25, 26), and an estimated 80,000 to 100,000 anterior cruciate ligament (ACL) injuries (27) and another 11 million doctor visits for foot and ankle problems occur annually (28). Therefore, muscle imbalances, joint dysfunctions, pain, and swelling can lead to altered balance.
summary: Joint dysfunction is prevalent, impacting a large portion of the population. Understanding its consequences is crucial for personal trainers as it can lead to altered balance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0825
content: Because the majority of clients with whom personal trainers work may have decreased neuromuscular efficiency, it is imperative to understand balance and how to design a balance routine that caters to the needs of the client. Balance training should stress an individual’s limit of stability (or balance threshold). An individual’s limit of stability is the distance outside of the base of support that he or she can move into without losing control of his or her center of gravity. This threshold must be stressed in a multiplanar, proprioceptively enriched (unstable yet controlled) environment, using functional movement patterns to improve dynamic balance and neuromuscular efficiency.
summary: Personal trainers should understand balance and design routines to address client needs. Balance training should challenge an individual's limit of stability in a multiplanar, unstable environment using functional movements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0826
content: Training functional movements in a proprioceptively enriched environment with appropriate progressions (floor, balance beam, half foam roll, foam pad, balance disc), correct technique, and at varying speeds facilitates maximal sensory input to the central nervous system, resulting in the selection of the proper movement pattern. Personal trainers are encouraged to implement progressive, systematic training programs to develop consistent, long-term changes in their clients. Traditional training program designs often result in an incomplete training program, which does not challenge the proprioceptive mechanisms of the human movement system. Balance training fills this gap left by traditional training because it focuses on functional movement patterns in a multisensory, unstable environment (29, 30).
summary: Balance training should incorporate functional movements in a proprioceptively enriched environment with progressions and varying speeds. This helps develop consistent, long-term changes in clients by addressing the limitations of traditional training programs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0827
content: The design and implementation of balance exercises into a training program is critical for developing, improving, and restoring the synergy and synchronicity of muscle firing patterns required for dynamic balance and optimal neuromuscular efficiency (29–32). Balance is key to all functional movements and optimal force production, and may help avoid injuries. Balance does not work in isolation and is both static and dynamic. It relies on an integrated, dynamic process requiring optimal muscular relationships, joint dynamics, and neuromuscular efficiency. Faulty movement patterns can lead to reduced neuromuscular efficiency. Reduced neuromuscular efficiency may contribute to synergistic dominance. Synergistic dominance can cause joint dysfunction and pain.
summary: Integrating balance exercises into training programs is crucial for improving dynamic balance and neuromuscular efficiency. Balance is essential for functional movements, injury prevention, and relies on a dynamic interplay of muscular, joint, and neurological factors.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0829
content: Kibler WB, Chandler TJ, Livingston BP, and Roetert EP published a study titled 'Shoulder range of motion in elite tennis players. Eff ect of age and years of tournament play' in the American Journal of Sports Medicine in 1996;24(3):279-285. summary: This chunk contains research publications focusing on shoulder range of motion in tennis players, the effects of stability ball training, trunk muscle activity during unstable exercises, and lumbar spine range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0830
content: It relies on an integrated, dynamic process requiring optimal muscular relationships, joint dynamics, and neuromuscular efficiency. Faulty movement patterns can lead to reduced neuromuscular efficiency. Reduced neuromuscular efficiency may contribute to synergistic dominance. Synergistic dominance can cause joint dysfunction and pain. Joint dysfunction creates muscle inhibition. Muscle inhibition alters balance. Altered balance leads to tissue overload and injury. The majority of fitness clients have decreased neuromuscular efficiency and problems with balance.
summary: This chunk describes the interconnectedness of movement, highlighting how faulty movement patterns can cascade into issues like synergistic dominance, joint dysfunction, muscle inhibition, and ultimately, injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0831
content: Effective balance ability is necessary for both healthy and injured populations. Balance training programs can benefit both healthy and injured populations. Balance training should challenge an individual’s ability to stabilize outside the normal base of support. Training in a multisensory environment will increase the demand on the nervous system’s ability to activate the right muscles at the right time in the right plane of motion. Training should progress to include unstable environments in which an individual can still safely control movements.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of balance for all individuals and outlines key principles for effective balance training, including challenging stability and progressing to unstable environments.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0832
content: Balance training programs are frequently used to help prevent lower extremity injuries by improving balance ability in many types of individuals including those who are healthy and physically active. Research has shown that performing exercises that demand balance can reduce the rate of ankle sprains and other lower extremity injuries. Balance exercises are a frequent component of integrated ACL injury prevention programs. Integrated ACL injury prevention programs have shown promise in reducing the rate of ACL injuries. A recent systematic review suggested that integrated injury prevention programs that included balance exercises in addition to plyometric or strength exercises greatly influenced the ability to improve lower extremity biomechanics.
summary: This chunk focuses on the role of balance training in injury prevention, citing research demonstrating its effectiveness in reducing lower extremity injuries, particularly ACL injuries.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0833
content: Several studies have demonstrated success with improving balance ability after healthy, physically active individuals completed a balance training program. Balance training programs that are performed for at least 10 minutes a day, 3 times per week for 4 weeks, appear to improve both static and dynamic balance ability. Kovacs et al. (1) conducted a prospective randomized controlled trial with 44 competitive figure skaters. The study found a significant positive effect on postural control when balance training was incorporated into an off-ice training program compared to traditional training (flexibility and strength training alone). Emery et al. (2) concluded that a home-based balance training program with wobble boards resulted in improvements in both static and dynamic balance ability in adolescent individuals.
summary: This chunk presents evidence from various studies supporting the effectiveness of balance training programs in improving balance ability in different populations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0834
content: Gioftsidou et al. (3) performed a study comparing the effects of performing a single-leg balance training program either before or after soccer training sessions. The balance training program resulted in improvements in both treatment groups with no changes in the control group. Performing the balance training program after practice appears to be more beneficial than conducting the balance exercises before practice. Michell et al. (4) investigated the effect of using exercise sandals during an 8-week balance training program in subjects with stable ankles and functionally unstable ankles. Their results demonstrated that both subject groups were able to improve their postural stability regardless of whether or not the subjects used exercise sandals during the balance training program.
summary: This chunk delves into specific studies examining the timing and tools used in balance training, highlighting the positive outcomes regardless of the method.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0835
content: Emery et al. (5) found that a balance training warm-up and home-based balance program with wobble boards reduced the risk of acute injuries in high school basketball athletes. DiStefano et al. (6) implemented an integrated injury prevention program that included static and dynamic balance exercises to 10-year-old soccer athletes and found that the youth athletes were able to improve their dynamic balance ability. Soligard et al. (7) demonstrated that an integrated injury prevention program with balance exercises could reduce the risk of all injuries, overuse injuries, and severe injuries in adolescent female soccer athletes.
summary: This chunk showcases the application of balance training in injury prevention programs across various sports and age groups, emphasizing its effectiveness in reducing injury rates.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0836
content: The main goal of balance training is to continually increase the client’s awareness of his or her limit of stability (or kinesthetic awareness) by creating controlled instability. An example of controlled instability could range from having a 65-year-old client balance on one foot on the floor to having a 25-year-old athlete balance on one foot while standing on a balance disc. There are a number of training modalities on the market today that challenge one’s balance, and they all can be very useful tools.
summary: This chunk defines the core objective of balance training — enhancing kinesthetic awareness through controlled instability — and provides examples of varying difficulty levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0837
content: To ensure the safety and effectiveness of balance training, individuals must start in an environment they can safely control and go through a systematic progression (floor, balance beam, half foam roll, foam pad, and balance disc). Not following the proper progression can cause movement compensations and improper execution of the exercise, decreasing the effectiveness of the exercise and increasing the risk for injury. There are three levels of training within the National Academy of Sports Medicine’s OPT™ model—stabilization, strength, and power. A proper balance training program follows the same systematic progression. All three levels can be progressed or regressed by changing the surface, visual condition, and body position or movement that the exercise requires. Surfaces change in difficulty as an individual moves from stable surfaces (floor) to unstable surfaces (e.g., half foam roll, foam pad, balance disc).
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of a systematic progression in balance training, starting with stable surfaces and gradually increasing instability, and aligning it with the OPT™ model's three training levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0838
content: Balance-stabilization exercises involve little joint motion; instead they are designed to improve reflexive (automatic) joint stabilization contractions to increase joint stability. During balance-stabilization training, the body is placed in unstable environments so it learns to react by contracting the right muscles at the right time to maintain balance. Sample exercises in this level include: Single-leg balance, Single-leg balance reach, Single-leg hip internal and external rotation, Single-leg lift and chop, Single-leg throw and catch.
summary: Balance-stabilization exercises focus on reflexive joint stabilization in unstable environments.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0839
content: There are three levels of training within the National Academy of Sports Medicine’s OPT™ model—stabilization, strength, and power. All three levels can be progressed or regressed by changing the surface, visual condition, and body position or movement that the exercise requires. Surfaces change in difficulty as an individual moves from stable surfaces (floor) to unstable surfaces (e.g., half foam roll, foam pad, balance disc). Keeping the eyes open during an exercise is easier than having the eyes closed or moving the head around to look at various objects or performing a cognitive task simultaneously. Moving the contralateral limb, trunk, or arms also makes a balance exercise more challenging, whereas standing on two legs versus a single leg simplifies the exercise.
summary: The OPT™ model includes three levels of training (stabilization, strength, and power) that can be progressed by modifying exercise variables like surface, visual condition, and body position.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0840
content: A proper balance training program follows the same systematic progression. Caution should be used to change one variable at a time.
summary: Balance training programs should follow a systematic progression, changing one variable at a time.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0841
content: Balance-Strength Exercises involve dynamic eccentric and concentric movement of the balance leg, through a full range of motion. Balance-Strength Exercises require dynamic control in the mid-range of motion, with isometric stabilization at the end-range of motion. Sample Balance-Strength Exercises include single-leg squat, single-leg squat touchdown, single-leg Romanian deadlift, multiplanar step-up to balance, and multiplanar lunge to balance.
summary: Balance-Strength Exercises involve dynamic movement through a full range of motion, requiring both dynamic control and isometric stabilization.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0842
content: Not following the proper progression can cause movement compensations and improper execution of the exercise, decreasing the effectiveness of the exercise and increasing the risk for injury.
summary: Improper progression in exercise can lead to compensations, improper form, reduced effectiveness, and increased injury risk.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0843
content: The Single-Leg Squat preparation involves standing with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead, with hips in a neutral position. The Single-Leg Squat movement involves lifting one leg directly beside the balance leg, maintaining optimal alignment, and slowly squatting as if sitting in a chair, flexing at the hips, knees, and ankles. The Single-Leg Squat movement involves slowly standing upright and contracting gluteal muscles.
summary: The Single-Leg Squat involves a controlled movement from a standing position to a squat, focusing on maintaining proper alignment and engaging the gluteal muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0844
content: The Single-Leg Squat Touchdown preparation involves standing with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead, with hips in a neutral position. The Single-Leg Squat Touchdown movement involves lifting one leg directly beside the balance leg and slowly squatting as if sitting in a chair, reaching the hand opposite the balance leg toward the foot. The Single-Leg Squat Touchdown movement involves slowly standing upright using abdominal muscles and gluteal muscles. If individuals cannot touch their foot during the Single-Leg Squat Touchdown, they should first work on reaching to their knee, then to the shin, and then to the foot. During the Single-Leg Squat and Single-Leg Squat Touchdown, the knee should always stay in line with the toe and not move inside or outside the second and third toe to decrease stress to the knee.
summary: The Single-Leg Squat Touchdown is a variation that involves reaching towards the foot during the squat, progressing gradually as needed.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0845
content: The Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift preparation involves standing with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead, with hips in a neutral position. The Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift movement involves lifting one leg directly beside the balance leg, bending from the waist and slowly reaching the hand down toward the toes of the balance leg, keeping the spine in a neutral position and avoiding hunching over. The Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift movement involves slowly standing upright using abdominal muscles and gluteal muscles. One can use the same progression with the Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift as that performed in the Single-Leg Squat Touchdown: reaching to the knee, then to the shin, and then to the foot.
summary: The Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift involves a controlled hinge movement, focusing on maintaining a neutral spine and engaging the glutes and hamstrings.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0846
content: The Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance preparation involves standing in front of a box or platform with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead, with hips in a neutral position. The Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance movement involves stepping onto the box with one leg, keeping toes pointing straight ahead and the knee directly over the toes. The Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance movement involves pushing through the front heel and standing upright, balancing on one leg.
summary: The Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance involves stepping onto a platform with one leg, maintaining balance, and pushing through the heel to stand upright.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0847
content: Single-Leg Hip Rotation involves lifting one leg while maintaining optimal alignment, including level hips and shoulders. During Single-Leg Hip Rotation, slowly internally and externally rotate the hip of the lifted leg, holding each end position for a few seconds. When performing Single-Leg Hip Rotation, rotate through the hip of the balance leg versus the spine to decrease stress to the spine and enhance control of the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex.
summary: Single-Leg Hip Rotation involves controlled hip rotations while maintaining balance and proper alignment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0848
content: Single-Leg Lift and Chop involves lifting one leg while maintaining optimal alignment, including level hips and shoulders. During Single-Leg Lift and Chop, lift a medicine ball in a diagonal pattern until it is overhead.
summary: Single-Leg Lift and Chop involves lifting a medicine ball diagonally while maintaining balance and proper alignment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0849
content: Single-Leg Throw and Catch involves lifting one leg while maintaining optimal alignment, including level hips and shoulders. During Single-Leg Throw and Catch, toss a medicine ball to a partner or trainer while maintaining optimal postural alignment. To increase the demand of Single-Leg Throw and Catch, a trainer can toss the medicine ball at various heights and across the body. To increase the demand of Single-Leg Throw and Catch, increase the distance between both individuals. To increase the demand of Single-Leg Throw and Catch, increase the velocity of each throw.
summary: Single-Leg Throw and Catch involves throwing and catching a medicine ball while maintaining balance and proper alignment, with options to increase the challenge.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0850
content: The Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift movement involves slowly standing upright using abdominal muscles and gluteal muscles. One can use the same progression with the Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift as that performed in the Single-Leg Squat Touchdown: reaching to the knee, then to the shin, and then to the foot.
summary: The Single-Leg Romanian Deadlift targets the posterior chain and uses a progression similar to the Single-Leg Squat Touchdown.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0851
content: The Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance preparation involves standing in front of a box or platform with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead, with hips in a neutral position. The Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance movement involves stepping onto the box with one leg, keeping toes pointing straight ahead and the knee directly over the toes. The Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance movement involves pushing through the front heel and standing upright, balancing on one leg. The Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance movement involves holding for a few seconds, returning the lifted leg to the ground, followed by the opposite leg, keeping toes and knees aligned. As progressions for the Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance, use the same process and step up from the side (frontal plane) or turn 90 degrees (transverse plane).
summary: The Multiplanar Step-Up to Balance is a multi-planar exercise that involves stepping onto a box, balancing, and progressing through different planes of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0852
content: Stand with feet shoulders-width apart and pointing straight ahead. Hips should be in a neutral position. Lunge forward with toes pointed straight ahead and knee directly over the toes. Push off of the front foot through the heel onto the back leg and maintain balance on the back leg. As progressions, use the same process and lunge to the side (frontal plane) or turn 90 degrees (transverse plane). When performing a lunge, make sure the stride length is not too large, particularly if one has tight hip flexors. This can force the spine into excessive extension, increasing stress to the low back.
summary: The lunge is a lower body exercise that involves stepping forward and lowering the body, with progressions through different planes of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0853
content: Balance-power exercises are designed to develop proper deceleration ability to move the body from a dynamic state to a controlled stationary position, as well as high levels of eccentric strength, dynamic neuromuscular efficiency, and reactive joint stabilization. Stand with feet shoulders-width apart and pointing straight ahead. Hips should be in a neutral position. Lift one leg directly beside the balance leg. Hop forward (sagittal plane), landing on the opposite foot. Stabilize and hold for 3 to 5 seconds. Hop backward (sagittal plane), landing on the opposite foot in the starting position. Stabilize and hold for 3 to 5 seconds. As progressions, use the same process and hop side-to-side (frontal plane) or turning degrees (transverse plane). For all balance-power exercises, make sure the landing is soft (quiet) to ensure efficient acceptance of forces through the tissues and keep the knee in line with the second and third toes.
summary: Balance-power exercises improve deceleration, eccentric strength, and reactive joint stabilization through hopping movements in various planes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0854
content: Stand in front of a box or platform with feet shoulders-width apart and pointing straight ahead. Hips should be in a neutral position. Lift one leg directly beside the balance leg. Hop up and land on top of the box on one leg as illustrated. Hold for 3 to 5 seconds. As progressions, use the same format to hop in the frontal and transverse planes. The balance-power exercises require standing on a box or platform with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead. Hips should be in a neutral position during the balance-power exercises. In the balance-power exercises, lift one leg directly beside the balance leg. Hop off the box and land on the ground on one leg, keeping toes pointing straight ahead and the knee directly over the toes during the balance-power exercises. Hold the landing position for 3 to 5 seconds during the balance-power exercises. As progressions, use the same format to hop in the frontal and transverse planes during the balance-power exercises. Keep the knee in line with the toes when landing during balance-power exercises. Land as softly as possible during balance-power exercises.
summary: Balance-power exercises involve hopping onto and off a box, progressing through different planes of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0855
content: A well-designed balance training program ensures optimal neuromuscular efficiency of the entire human movement system. Balance training programs must be systematic and progressive, following specific program guidelines, proper exercise selection criteria, and detailed program variables. A properly designed balance training program follows the same systematic progression as the OPT model—stabilization, strength, and power levels of training. Balance-stabilization exercises do not involve much joint motion and improve joint stability. Balance-strength exercises involve the balancing leg moving dynamically through a full range of motion. Balance-strength exercises require greater specificity, speed, neural demand, and isometric stabilization at the end-range of motion. Balance-strength exercises improve neuromuscular efficiency of the entire human movement system.
summary: Balance training programs should be systematic and progressive, following the OPT model, and progressing from stabilization to strength to power.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0856
content: A properly designed balance training program follows the same systematic progression as the OPT model—stabilization, strength, and power levels of training. Balance-stabilization exercises do not involve much joint motion and improve joint stability. Balance-strength exercises involve the balancing leg moving dynamically through a full range of motion. Balance-strength exercises require greater specificity, speed, neural demand, and isometric stabilization at the end-range of motion. Balance-strength exercises improve neuromuscular efficiency of the entire human movement system. Balance-power exercises improve high levels of eccentric strength, dynamic neuromuscular efficiency, and reactive joint stabilization. Balance training program design includes the OPT Level, Phase(s), Exercise Number, Sets, Reps, Tempo, and Rest.
summary: This chunk describes the OPT model for balance training, outlining the progression from stabilization to strength to power, and detailing the characteristics of each level.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0857
content: Balance-stabilization exercises have 1–4 exercises, 1–3 sets, 12–20 reps, a slow tempo, and 0–90 seconds rest. Balance-strength exercises have 0–4 exercises, 2–3 sets, 8–12 reps, a medium tempo, and 0–60 seconds rest. Balance-power exercises have 0–2 exercises, 2–3 sets, 8–12 reps, a controlled tempo (hold landing position for 3–5 seconds), and 0–60 seconds rest.
summary: This chunk provides specific training parameters for each level of balance training: stabilization, strength, and power.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0858
content: Personal trainers should select balance-stabilization exercises for clients in the stabilization level of training (Phase 1). Personal trainers should select balance-strength exercises for clients in the strength level of training (Phase 2, 3, or 4). Personal trainers should select balance-power exercises for advanced clients in the power level of training (Phase 5). To fill in the balance training program template, refer to the Core/Balance/Plyometric section and Table 10.2 for exercise type, number of exercises, and acute variables specific to the training phase.
summary: This chunk outlines how to select appropriate balance exercises based on the client's training phase and provides guidance on program template completion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0880
content: Enhanced performance during functional activities emphasizes the ability of muscles to exert maximal force output in a minimal amount of time, also known as rate of force production.
summary: This chunk defines rate of force production as a key factor in enhanced performance during functional activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0881
content: Padua DA and DiStefano LJ conducted a systematic review on sagittal plane knee biomechanics and vertical ground reaction forces following ACL injury prevention programs. The review was published in Sports Health in 2009;1(2):165-173.
summary: This chunk describes a systematic review on knee biomechanics and ground reaction forces after ACL injury prevention programs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0882
content: DiStefano LJ, Padua DA, and Clark MA conducted a systematic literature review on the evidence supporting balance training in healthy individuals. The review was published in J Strength Cond Res. in 2009;23(9):2718-2731.
summary: This chunk describes a systematic review on the effectiveness of balance training in healthy individuals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0883
content: Enhanced performance during functional activities emphasizes the ability of muscles to exert maximal force output in a minimal amount of time, also known as rate of force production. Success in everyday activities and sport depends on the speed at which muscular force is generated.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of rate of force production for functional performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0884
content: Speed of movement and reactive neuromuscular control are a function of muscular development and neural control; the first is a function of training and the other of learning. Plyometric (reactive) training involves exercises that generate quick, powerful movements involving explosive concentric muscle contraction preceded by an eccentric muscle action.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between muscular development, neural control, and plyometric training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0885
content: There is a ‘cocking’ or loading phase described as an eccentric muscle action that dampens or slows the downward movement of the body (deceleration) followed immediately by an explosive concentric muscle contraction. These types of explosive muscular contractions can be seen in practical instances such as rebounding in basketball.
summary: This chunk describes the phases of a plyometric movement and provides an example.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0886
content: Good basketball players prepare to jump up for a loose ball by lowering their body slightly by flexing at the ankles, knees, and hips. They may even drop their arms to assist in takeoff. At a fairly shallow point, players will reverse this downward motion and rapidly project themselves from the ground extending their ankles, knees, hips, and arms upward.
summary: This chunk details the preparatory movements of a basketball player before a jump.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0887
content: The overall height that they will achieve is determined by their vertical velocity or how fast they leave the ground. This is the essence of a plyometric exercise and uses a characteristic of muscle known as the stretch-shortening cycle of the integrated performance paradigm.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between vertical velocity and plyometric exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0888
