content: Resistance training programs have traditionally focused on developing maximal strength in individual muscles, emphasizing one plane of motion (typically the sagittal plane). All muscles function eccentrically, isometrically, and concentrically in all three planes of motion (sagittal, frontal, and transverse) at different speeds. Training programs should be designed using a progressive approach that emphasizes the appropriate exercise selection, all muscle actions, and repetition tempos.
summary: Effective training programs should consider all muscle actions and planes of motion, progressing systematically.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0996
content: Muscle operates under the control of the central nervous system, so strength needs to be thought of not as a function of muscle, but as a result of activating the neuromuscular system. Strength gains can occur rapidly in beginning clients and can increase with a structured, progressive resistance training program. One factor in increased strength is an increase in the number of motor units recruited, especially early in a training program. Using heavier loads increases the neural demand and recruitment of more muscle fibers until a recruitment plateau is reached, after which further increases in strength are a result of fiber hypertrophy. Strength cannot be thought of in isolation. Strength is built on the foundation of stabilization requiring muscles, tendons, and ligaments to be prepared for the load that will be required to increase strength beyond the initial stages of training.
summary: Strength is a product of neuromuscular activation, and initial gains are primarily due to neural adaptations, followed by muscle hypertrophy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0997
content: Stabilization training is designed with the characteristics of type I slow-twitch muscle fibers in mind (slow-contracting, low tension output, and resistant to fatigue), while strength training is designed to match the characteristics of type II muscle fibers (quick-contracting, high tension output, prone to fatigue). Acute variables (sets, reps, intensities, etc.) are manipulated to take advantage of the specific characteristics of each fiber type. The majority of strength increases will occur during the first 12 weeks of resistance training from increased neural recruitment and muscle hypertrophy. Intermediate and advanced lifters will find it necessary to carry out a more demanding program in terms of training volume and intensity by following a sound periodized schedule.
summary: Training programs should consider the characteristics of different muscle fiber types and adjust variables accordingly.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0998
content: Power is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce the greatest possible force in the shortest possible time. This is represented by the simple equation of force multiplied by velocity. Power adaptations build on stabilization and strength adaptations and then apply them at more realistic speeds and forces seen in everyday life and sporting activities. The focus of power-resistance training is getting the neuromuscular system to generate force as quickly as possible (rate of force production).
summary: Power is the product of force and velocity, and is developed by applying strength and stabilization adaptations at higher speeds.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_0999
content: Power is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce the greatest possible force in the shortest possible time. This is represented by the simple equation of force multiplied by velocity. Power adaptations build on stabilization and strength adaptations and then apply them at more realistic speeds and forces seen in everyday life and sporting activities. The focus of power-resistance training is getting the neuromuscular system to generate force as quickly as possible (rate of force production). An increase in either force or velocity will produce an increase in power. Training for power can be achieved by increasing the weight (force), as seen in the strength adaptations, or increasing the speed with which weight is moved (velocity).
summary: Power is the product of force and velocity, and training for power involves increasing both force and velocity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1000
content: Power training allows for increased rate of force production by increasing the number of motor units recruited and the speed of contraction. The general adaptation syndrome and principle of specificity dictate that to maximize training for muscle fiber recruitment and rate of activation, both heavy and light loads must be moved as fast as possible in a controlled fashion. Early isokinetic work underscored the importance of speed of exercise, showing that training performed at high speeds led to better performance at the training speed and all movement speeds below the training speed.
summary: Power training increases rate of force production through motor unit recruitment and contraction speed, emphasizing both heavy and light loads moved quickly.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1001
content: Resistance training programs produce physiologic changes that result in a variety of strength adaptations. Strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce internal tension to overcome an external force. Traditionally, resistance training programs focused on developing maximal strength in individual muscles, whereas contemporary training programs emphasize appropriate exercise selection, all muscle actions, multiple planes of motion, and repetition tempos.
summary: Strength is the ability to produce internal tension to overcome external force, and modern training programs emphasize a holistic approach beyond isolated muscle development.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1002
content: Training adaptations can be divided into stabilization, muscular endurance, hypertrophy, strength, and power. Stabilization adaptations should be the starting point for clients new to resistance training. Stabilization adaptations are best achieved using high repetitions with low to moderate volume and low to moderate intensity in a postural position that challenges the stability of the body.
summary: Training adaptations encompass stabilization, muscular endurance, hypertrophy, strength, and power, with stabilization being foundational for beginners.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1003
content: Muscular endurance is the ability to produce and maintain force production for prolonged periods of time. Research has shown that resistance training protocols using high repetitions are the most effective way to improve muscular endurance. Muscular hypertrophy is the enlargement of skeletal muscle fibers in response to increased volumes of tension resulting from resistance training. Resistance training protocols that use low to intermediate repetition ranges with progressive overload lead to muscular hypertrophy.
summary: Muscular endurance is improved through high-repetition training, while hypertrophy results from low to intermediate repetition ranges with progressive overload.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1004
content: Strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce internal tension (in the muscles and connective tissues that pull on the bones) to overcome an external force. The specific form of strength produced is based on the type and intensity of training used by the client (principle of specificity). Generally, strength adaptations should use low to moderate repetition routines with moderate to high volume and moderate to high intensity. Heavier weights and higher volumes of training are used to improve the function of motor units, while placing stress on the muscles to increase size or strength.
summary: Strength is influenced by training type and intensity, with low to moderate repetitions, moderate to high volume, and moderate to high intensity generally promoting strength adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1005
content: Intermediate and advanced lifters will find it necessary to carry out a more demanding program in terms of training volume and intensity by following a sound periodized schedule.
summary: Intermediate and advanced lifters require more demanding training programs with periodized scheduling.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1006
content: There are numerous training systems that can be used to structure resistance training programs for a variety of effects. The Single-Set System uses 1 set per exercise. Single-set workouts are usually recommended to be performed two times per week to promote sufficient development and maintenance of muscle mass. The multiple-set system consists of performing multiple numbers of sets for each exercise. The resistance (load), sets, and repetitions performed are selected according to the goals and needs of the client. Multiple-set training can be appropriate for both novice and advanced clients, but has been shown to be superior to single-set training for more advanced clients. The increased volume (sets, reps, and intensity) is necessary for further improvement, but must be administered appropriately to avoid overtraining.
summary: Various training systems exist, including single-set and multiple-set approaches, each tailored to different client needs and experience levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1007
content: The single-set system has been shown to be just as beneficial for beginning-level clients. Single-set training systems are often negatively perceived for not providing enough stimuli for adaptation. Most beginning clients could follow a single-set program to allow for proper adaptive responses of the connective tissue and nervous system before engaging in more rigorous training systems. By encouraging clients to avoid lifting more than they can handle, synergistic dominance (synergists overcompensating for weak prime movers) and injury can be avoided.
summary: Single-set training is suitable for beginners, allowing for proper connective tissue and nervous system adaptation before progressing to more intense programs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1008
content: Research has shown that following a systematic, integrated training program and manipulating key training variables achieve optimal gains in strength, neuromuscular efficiency, hypertrophy, and performance.
summary: Systematic training programs with manipulated variables optimize gains in strength, neuromuscular efficiency, hypertrophy, and performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1009
content: By encouraging clients to avoid lifting more than they can handle, synergistic dominance (synergists overcompensating for weak prime movers) and injury can be avoided.  The resistance (load), sets, and repetitions performed are selected according to the goals and needs of the client.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of proper lifting technique and individualized training prescriptions to prevent injury and optimize performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1010
content: Multiple-set training can be appropriate for both novice and advanced clients, but has been shown to be superior to single-set training for more advanced clients. The increased volume (sets, reps, and intensity) is necessary for further improvement, but must be administered appropriately to avoid overtraining.
summary: This chunk discusses the benefits of multiple-set training and the importance of progressive overload while avoiding overtraining.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1011
content: The Multiple-Set System consists of performing multiple numbers of sets for each exercise. The Pyramid System involves a progressive or regressive step approach that either increases weight with each set or decreases weight with each set.
summary: This chunk describes two specific training systems: the Multiple-Set System and the Pyramid System.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1012
content: In the light-to-heavy system, the individual typically performs 10 to 12 repetitions with a light load and increases the resistance for each following set, until the individual can perform 1 to 2 repetitions, usually in 4 to 6 sets. This system can easily be used for workouts that involve only 2 to 4 sets or higher repetition schemes (12 to 20 repetitions).
summary: This chunk explains the light-to-heavy training system and its application in various workout structures.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1013
content: The heavy-to-light system works in the opposite direction, in which the individual begins with a heavy load (after a sufficient warm-up) for 1 to 2 repetitions, then decreases the load and increases the repetitions for 4 to 6 sets.
summary: This chunk describes the heavy-to-light training system, contrasting it with the light-to-heavy approach.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1014
content: The Superset System uses two exercises performed in rapid succession of one another. The first variation includes performing two exercises for the same muscle group back to back. An individual may perform the bench press exercise immediately followed by push-ups to fatigue the chest musculature.
summary: This chunk introduces the Superset System and its first variation, focusing on training the same muscle group consecutively.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1015
content: Completing two exercises in this manner will improve muscular endurance and hypertrophy because the volume of work performed is relatively high. This style of supersets can use two, three (a tri-set), or more exercises (a giant set) for the target muscle group. The greater the number of exercises used, the greater the degree of fatigue experienced and demands on muscular endurance.
summary: This chunk elaborates on the benefits of supersets for muscular endurance and hypertrophy, and discusses variations with multiple exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1016
content: The second variation of training consists of performing two exercises back to back that involve antagonist muscle groups (e.g., chest and back or quadriceps and hamstring complex). Performing supersets in this manner allows a significant load to be placed on the target muscle during each set because while the agonist is working, the antagonist is recovering, and vice versa.
summary: This chunk describes the second variation of supersets, focusing on training antagonist muscle groups for enhanced recovery and load.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1017
content: Supersetting typically involves sets of 8 to 12 repetitions with no rest between sets or exercises, however, any number of repetitions can be used. The superset system is popular among bodybuilders and may be beneficial for muscular hypertrophy and muscular endurance.
summary: This chunk provides details on typical superset parameters and its popularity among bodybuilders for muscle growth and endurance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1018
content: A drop-set is a resistance training system that is popular among bodybuilders. It is a technique that allows a client to continue a set past the point at which it would usually terminate. Drop-sets involve performing a set to failure, then removing a small percentage of the load (5–20%) and continuing with the set, completing a small number of repetitions (usually 2–4). This procedure can be repeated several times (typically 2 to 3 drops per set. A set to failure followed by three successive load decrements performed with no rest would be referred to as a triple drop.
summary: This chunk explains the drop-set technique, its application in bodybuilding, and variations like triple drops.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1019
content: Drop-sets are considered an advanced form of resistance training suitable for experienced lifters.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the advanced nature of drop-sets and their suitability for experienced lifters.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1020
content: The circuit-training system consists of a series of exercises that an individual performs one after the other, with minimal rest between each exercise. The typical acute variables for a circuit-training program include a low to moderate number of sets (1–3), with moderate to high repetitions (8–20) and short rest periods (15–60 seconds) between exercises; however, these variables can be manipulated to enhance the desired effect.
summary: This chunk defines circuit training and outlines its typical variables, emphasizing the flexibility in adjusting them for specific goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1021
content: Circuit training is a great training system for individuals with limited time and for those who want to alter body composition. The peripheral heart action system is another variation of circuit training that alternates upper body and lower body exercises throughout the circuit. This system of training distributes blood flow between the upper and lower extremities, potentially improving circulation.
summary: This chunk highlights the benefits of circuit training for time efficiency and body composition, and introduces the peripheral heart action system as a variation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1022
content: The number of exercises per sequence varies with the program’s goal. The individual performs 8 to 20 repetitions per exercise, depending on the desired adaptation and phase of training they are using in the OPT model. This system is very beneficial for incorporating an integrated, multidimensional program and for altering body composition.
summary: This chunk describes the exercise selection and repetition ranges within the circuit training system, emphasizing its adaptability and effectiveness for body composition changes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1023
content: A split-routine system involves breaking the body up into parts to be trained on separate days. Many bodybuilders and mass-dominant and strength athletes (football, shot put, etc.) use the split-routine system. Bodybuilders typically perform numerous exercises on the same day for the same body part to bring about optimal muscular hypertrophy.
summary: This chunk introduces the split-routine system, its common usage among bodybuilders and strength athletes, and the rationale behind training specific body parts on separate days.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1024
content: By breaking up the body into parts that can be trained on different days, more work can be performed for the allotted time per workout.
summary: This chunk explains the advantage of the split-routine system in maximizing training volume within a given time frame.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1025
content: The 5-Day Monday Chest workout schedule includes chest exercises on Monday, legs exercises on Tuesday, back exercises on Wednesday, shoulders exercises on Thursday, and arms exercises on Friday. The 6-Day Monday and Friday workout schedule includes chest, shoulders, and triceps exercises on Monday and Friday, legs exercises on Tuesday and Saturday, and back and biceps exercises on Wednesday and Sunday.
summary: This chunk provides examples of two different split-routine schedules: a 5-day and a 6-day plan.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1026
content: Vertical loading is a resistance training system used by NASM and follows the OPT model.
summary: This chunk identifies vertical loading as a resistance training system aligned with the NASM OPT model.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1027
content: Bodybuilders typically perform numerous exercises on the same day for the same body part to bring about optimal muscular hypertrophy. When training each body part more than once a week, recovery period, volume, and intensity should be accounted for.
summary: Bodybuilding training involves multiple exercises per body part per day, requiring careful consideration of recovery, volume, and intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1028
content: The 5-Day Monday Chest workout schedule includes chest exercises on Monday, legs exercises on Tuesday, back exercises on Wednesday, shoulders exercises on Thursday, and arms exercises on Friday. The 6-Day Monday and Friday workout schedule includes chest, shoulders, and triceps exercises on Monday and Friday, legs exercises on Tuesday and Saturday, and back and biceps exercises on Wednesday and Sunday.
summary: Two sample workout schedules are presented: a 5-day split and a 6-day split.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1029
content: Vertical loading is a resistance training system used by NASM and follows the OPT model. The OPT template for resistance training includes total body, chest, back, shoulders, biceps, triceps, and legs exercises. In a vertically loaded workout, the client performs the first exercise (total body) for the required repetitions and then moves to the next exercise (chest) for the next set of repetitions. The client continues to alternate exercises in a vertical loading workout until all exercises have been completed. Once completed, the client starts back at the first exercise (total body) and runs through the exercises again for the desired amount of sets. A vertically loaded workout can be performed in a circuit style by minimizing the rest periods between exercises. Vertical loading can be beneficial for allowing maximal recovery to each body part while minimizing the amount of time wasted on rest.
summary: Vertical loading is a NASM-utilized resistance training system that progresses exercises in a vertical fashion, allowing for efficient recovery and workout completion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1030
content: Horizontal loading refers to performing all sets of an exercise or body part before moving on to the next exercise or body part. For example, if performing three sets of a chest exercise and three sets of a back exercise, the client would perform all three sets of the chest exercise before moving on to the back exercise. Horizontal loading is the method most commonly used in health club environments and is appropriate for maximal strength and power training when longer rest periods are required between sets. The drawback to the horizontal loading system is the amount of time typically spent resting, which can often be more time than the actual workout itself. Horizontal loading can be a metabolic progression if rest periods are monitored and limited to 30 to 90 seconds between sets. If the same muscle groups are forced to work with minimal recovery, it can lead to faster development of metabolic and hypertrophy-related adaptations in the muscle.
summary: Horizontal loading involves completing all sets of an exercise before moving to the next, suitable for strength training but potentially time-consuming. Rest period management can enhance its metabolic benefits.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1031
content: The single-set system consists of performing one set of each exercise, usually of 8 to 12 repetitions. The single-set system has been shown to be beneficial for strength and hypertrophy gains in beginning clients. The multiple-set system of training consists of performing a multiple number of sets for each exercise, with resistance, sets, and repetitions adjusted according to the goals and needs of the client. The multiple-set system is superior to single-set training for more advanced clients.
summary: Two training systems are described: single-set, suitable for beginners, and multiple-set, more effective for advanced clients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1032
content: The pyramid system involves a progressive or regressive step approach that either increases weight with each set or decreases weight with each set. The superset system uses two exercises performed in rapid succession of one another. The superset system is popular among bodybuilders for muscular hypertrophy and muscular endurance. The circuit-training system programs consist of a series of exercises that an individual performs one immediately after the other, with minimal rest. The circuit-training system is good for clients with limited time and those who want to alter body composition. The peripheral heart action system is another variation of circuit training that alternates upper body and lower body exercises (of varied numbers) throughout the circuit.
summary: Various training systems are outlined: pyramid, superset, circuit, and peripheral heart action, each with specific applications and benefits.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1033
content: Begin with both feet shoulders-width apart pointing straight ahead and knees over the second and third toes. Position the ball in the low-back region as shown. Lower into a squat position, keeping lower extremities in proper alignment. Stand to a fully upright position while contracting the gluteal muscles and quadriceps. Once stabilized, curl and press the dumbbells overhead until both arms are fully extended. Slowly return to the start position and repeat. Decrease the range of motion.
summary: Instructions are provided for performing a squat and dumbbell overhead press exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1034
content: The squat begins with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead, with knees over the second and third toes. The individual performs a squat as low as can be safely controlled, keeping lower extremities in proper alignment during the squat. The individual activates gluteal muscles and stands to a fully upright position before any compensation occurs during the squat. Once stabilized, the individual curls and presses the dumbbells overhead until both arms are fully extended with palms facing away during the squat. The individual slowly returns the dumbbells to the start position and repeats during the squat.
summary: This describes the execution of a squat exercise with a dumbbell press component.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1035
content: The barbell clean exercise begins with the individual squatting down next to the bar, keeping feet flat on the floor.
summary: This describes the starting position for the barbell clean exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1036
content: Stand in front of a box with feet shoulders-width apart. Step onto the box with one leg, keeping foot and knee pointed straight ahead. Push through the heel and stand up straight, balancing on one leg. Flex the other leg at the hip and knee. Once balance has been established, curl and press the dumbbells overhead until both arms are fully extended. Slowly return the dumbbells to the starting position. Return the opposite leg to the ground and step off the box. Repeat on the other leg.
summary: This describes the execution of a single-leg box step-up with a dumbbell press.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1037
content: Lower into a squat position, keeping lower extremities in proper alignment. Stand to a fully upright position while contracting the gluteal muscles and quadriceps.
summary: This describes the basic movement pattern of a squat.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1038
content: When performing any form of a ball squat, try to use the ball to guide one through the squatting motion (sitting into a chair) versus relying on the ball for support (leaning back on the ball).
summary: This provides guidance on proper technique for a ball squat.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1039
content: The lunge to two-arm dumbbell press exercise begins with feet shoulder-width apart and dumbbells held at chest level with palms facing the body. During the lunge to two-arm dumbbell press, the individual lunges forward, coming to a stabilized position with the front foot pointing straight ahead and the knee directly over the second and third toes. Both knees should be bent at a 90-degree angle during the lunge to two-arm dumbbell press, with the front foot flat on the ground and the back foot having the heel lifted off the ground. The individual drives off the front foot and back into a standing position during the lunge to two-arm dumbbell press. The dumbbells are pressed overhead until the arms are fully extended during the lunge to two-arm dumbbell press.
summary: This describes the execution of a lunge to two-arm dumbbell press exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1040
content: When performing any squatting or lunging motion, the foot should stay straight and the knees should stay in line with the toes to ensure proper joint mechanics and optimal force generation.
summary: This emphasizes proper form for squats and lunges.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1041
content: The squat, curl, to two-arm press exercise begins with feet shoulder-width apart. The individual holds two dumbbells at shoulder level during the squat, curl, to two-arm press exercise.
summary: This describes the starting position for a squat, curl, to two-arm press exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1042
content: The two-arm push press exercise begins with feet shoulder-width apart and dumbbells held at shoulder level. The individual quickly drives the dumbbells up as if doing a shoulder press during the two-arm push press exercise. The individual drives the legs into a stagger stance position during the two-arm push press exercise, with the back leg in triple extension (plantar flexion, knee extension, hip extension) and the front leg bent slightly. The individual maintains optimal alignment on the return to the starting position during the two-arm push press exercise.
summary: This describes the execution of a two-arm push press exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1043
content: Perform alternating-arm exercises. Perform one-arm exercises. Perform single-leg exercises.
summary: This lists examples of unilateral exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1044
content: Perform exercises in the frontal plane. Perform exercises in the transverse plane.
summary: This suggests incorporating exercises in different planes of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1045
content: Decrease the range of motion.
summary: This describes a method for modifying exercise intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1046
content: When pressing overhead, make sure the low back does not arch. This may indicate tightness of the latissimus dorsi and weakness of the intrinsic core stabilizers.
summary: This provides a cue for maintaining proper form during overhead pressing.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1047
content: Omit the balance component.
summary: This describes a modification for a single-leg exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1048
content: One must establish proper stability and prime mover strength before progressing to power exercises.
summary: This emphasizes the importance of foundational strength before progressing to more advanced exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1049
content: The individual performs a squat as low as can be safely controlled, keeping lower extremities in proper alignment during the squat. The individual activates gluteal muscles and stands to a fully upright position before any compensation occurs during the squat. Once stabilized, the individual curls and presses the dumbbells overhead until both arms are fully extended with palms facing away during the squat. The individual slowly returns the dumbbells to the start position and repeats during the squat.
summary: This describes the execution of a squat with an added dumbbell overhead press.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1050
content: The barbell clean exercise begins with the individual squatting down next to the bar, keeping feet flat on the floor. The individual grasps the bar fully extended slightly wider than shoulder-width apart during the barbell clean exercise. The shoulders should be positioned over or slightly ahead of the bar, and the pelvis should be in a neutral position during the barbell clean exercise. The individual initiates the first pull by extending knees and hips, lifting the bar straight up (keeping the bar close to the shins) with elbows extended during the barbell clean exercise. The individual continues to move the bar upward by explosively extending hips, knees, and ankles (plantarflexion) in a jumping motion during the barbell clean exercise. At maximal plantarflexion, the individual elevates shoulders and flexes and pulls with the arms during the barbell clean exercise. The individual pulls the bar as high as possible before rotating elbows under the bar during the barbell clean exercise. The individual hyperextends wrists as the elbows move under the bar during the barbell clean exercise. The individual points elbows forward during the barbell clean exercise. The individual racks the bar across the front of the shoulders, keeping the torso erect during the barbell clean exercise. The individual flexes hips and knees to absorb the weight of the bar during the barbell clean exercise.
summary: This describes the step-by-step execution of the barbell clean exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1051
content: The Ball Dumbbell Chest Press involves lying on a stability ball in a bridge position with feet shoulder-width apart and toes pointing straight ahead. During the Ball Dumbbell Chest Press, dumbbells are held at chest level and pressed straight up and together by extending elbows and contracting the chest. The Ball Dumbbell Chest Press can be progressed to alternating-arm and single-arm variations. To ensure proper alignment during the Ball Dumbbell Chest Press, the ears, shoulders, hips, and knees should be in line.
summary: This describes the Ball Dumbbell Chest Press exercise, including progressions and alignment tips.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1052
content: The Push-Up involves beginning in a push-up position with the navel drawn in and gluteal muscles contracted. During a Push-Up, the pelvis should be kept in a neutral position while lowering the body toward the ground by flexing elbows and retracting and depressing shoulder blades. The Push-Up can be progressed to lower extremities on a ball, hands on medicine balls, and hands on a stability ball. The Push-Up can be regressed to on knees, hands on a bench with feet on the floor, and hands on a wall with feet on the floor. A common compensation during a Push-Up is low back arching, indicating weak intrinsic core stabilizers.
summary: This describes the Push-Up exercise, including progressions, regressions, and common compensation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1053
content: The Flat Dumbbell Chest Press involves lying on a flat bench with knees bent and feet flat on the floor. During the Flat Dumbbell Chest Press, dumbbells are held at chest level and pressed straight up and together by extending elbows and contracting the chest. The Flat Dumbbell Chest Press involves slowly returning dumbbells toward the body by flexing elbows and allowing shoulders to retract and depress. When performing chest presses, the range of motion at the shoulder joint is determined by the load and tissue extensibility.
summary: This describes the Flat Dumbbell Chest Press exercise, including technique and considerations for range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1054
content: The Barbell Bench Press involves lying on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and toes pointing straight ahead. During the Barbell Bench Press, a barbell is held with hands slightly wider than shoulder-width apart. The Barbell Bench Press involves slowly lowering the bar toward the chest by flexing elbows while avoiding back arching or head jutting. The Barbell Bench Press involves pressing the bar back up by extending arms and contracting the chest until elbows are fully extended.
summary: This describes the Barbell Bench Press exercise, including proper form and technique.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1055
content: The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass involves standing facing a wall or partner in a squared stance position. During the Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass, a medicine ball (5 to 10% of body weight) is held with both hands at chest level. The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass involves pushing and releasing the ball straight ahead as hard as possible by extending elbows and contracting the chest. The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass can be performed using tubing or cable if a medicine ball is unavailable. The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass should be performed quickly and under control without compensation.
summary: This describes the Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass exercise, including variations and execution guidelines.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1056
content: The Rotation Chest Pass exercise involves standing at a 90-degree angle from a wall or partner. In the Rotation Chest Pass, a medicine ball weighing 5 to 10% of body weight is held with both hands at chest level with elbows flexed. The Rotation Chest Pass movement involves using abdominal, hip, and gluteal muscles to rotate the body quickly and explosively toward the wall.
summary: This describes the Rotation Chest Pass exercise, focusing on the movement and muscle groups involved.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1057
content: The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass can be performed using tubing or cable if a medicine ball is unavailable. The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass should be performed quickly and under control without compensation.
summary: The Two-Arm Medicine Ball Chest Pass is a dynamic exercise that can be modified using tubing or cables.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1058
content: The Rotation Chest Pass exercise involves standing at a 90-degree angle from a wall or partner. In the Rotation Chest Pass, a medicine ball weighing 5 to 10% of body weight is held with both hands at chest level with elbows flexed. The Rotation Chest Pass movement involves using abdominal, hip, and gluteal muscles to rotate the body quickly and explosively toward the wall. During the Rotation Chest Pass, the back leg pivots and goes into triple extension (plantarflexion, knee extension, hip extension) as the body turns. The upper body pushes the medicine ball using the back arm to extend and apply force during the Rotation Chest Pass. The Rotation Chest Pass should be performed quickly and under control.
summary: The Rotation Chest Pass is a rotational exercise that targets the core and posterior chain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1059
content: The Standing Cable Row exercise involves standing facing a cable machine with feet pointing straight ahead. In the Standing Cable Row, the cables are held with arms extended at chest level. The Standing Cable Row movement involves rowing the cable by flexing elbows, retracting and depressing the shoulder blades with knees slightly flexed. During the Standing Cable Row, the head should not jut forward and the shoulders should not elevate. The Standing Cable Row progression options include seated, two-legs, alternating-arm, two-legs, one-arm, single-leg, two-arms, and single-leg.
summary: The Standing Cable Row is a compound exercise that targets the back muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1060
content: When performing rows, initiate the movement by retracting and depressing the shoulder blades (scapulae) and avoid elevating the shoulders.
summary: Proper form for rows emphasizes scapular retraction and depression.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1061
content: The Ball Dumbbell Row exercise begins in a prone position with a stability ball under the abdomen. In the Ball Dumbbell Row, feet are pointed down and legs are completely straight. Dumbbells are held in each hand and extended in front of the body during the Ball Dumbbell Row. The Ball Dumbbell Row movement involves contracting gluteal muscles and quadriceps and rowing the dumbbells by flexing elbows and retracting and depressing shoulder blades. The Ball Dumbbell Row progression options include kneeling over the ball and alternating-arm and one-arm. Performing exercises in a prone position can be uncomfortable, so for overweight individuals, seated or standing positions may be more appropriate.
summary: The Ball Dumbbell Row is a challenging exercise that targets the back muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1062
content: The seated cable row exercise involves sitting facing a cable machine with feet shoulder-width apart and pointing straight ahead. During the seated cable row, the individual holds the cables with arms extended and rows the cable by flexing elbows and pulling the handles toward the trunk. In the seated cable row, the thumbs should be brought toward the armpits while keeping the shoulder blades retracted and depressed. The head should not jut forward during the seated cable row. To increase the effectiveness of the seated cable row and decrease the risk of injury, the torso should be kept stationary throughout the exercise.
summary: The Seated Cable Row is a seated variation of the cable row.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1063
content: The seated lat pulldown exercise involves sitting upright with feet on the floor pointing straight ahead. During the seated lat pulldown, the handles are pulled toward the body by flexing elbows and depressing the shoulder blades. The individual should not arch back, allow the head to jut forward, or elevate the shoulders during the seated lat pulldown. The seated lat pulldown should be performed with the bar passing in front of the face approximately to shoulder height if using a bar instead of cables. Performing lat pulldowns with a bar behind the neck is not advised as it places stress on the shoulder joints and cervical spine.
summary: The Seated Lat Pulldown is a seated exercise that targets the back muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1064
content: The medicine ball pullover throw exercise involves placing a stability ball under the low back, bending knees at a 90-degree angle, and keeping feet flat with toes pointing straight ahead. The individual holds a medicine ball (5 to 10% of body weight) overhead with both hands, with arms extended, for the medicine ball pullover throw. The medicine ball pullover throw is performed by using the abdominals to quickly crunch forward and throw the medicine ball straight ahead while keeping elbows fairly straight. As the medicine ball releases during the medicine ball pullover throw, the arms continue pulling through to the sides of the body. The chin should be tucked throughout the medicine ball pullover throw exercise.
summary: The Medicine Ball Pullover Throw is a dynamic exercise that targets the core and upper back.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1065
content: The soccer throw exercise involves standing holding a medicine ball (5 to 10% of body weight) overhead. The soccer throw is performed by quickly throwing the medicine ball toward the floor, allowing the arms to follow through.
summary: The Soccer Throw is a dynamic exercise that targets the core and upper body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1066
content: Optimal extensibility through the latissimus dorsi musculature is important before performing back-power exercises to decrease stress to the shoulder and low back.
summary: Flexibility in the latissimus dorsi is important for safe back-power exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1067
content: Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption involves holding dumbbells at the side with palms facing the side of the body. During Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption, raise one foot off the floor.
summary: Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption is a unilateral exercise that targets the shoulder muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1068
content: The soccer throw exercise involves standing holding a medicine ball (5 to 10% of body weight) overhead. The soccer throw is performed by quickly throwing the medicine ball toward the floor, allowing the arms to follow through.
summary: The soccer throw is a dynamic exercise using a medicine ball, involving an overhead throw and follow-through.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1069
content: Optimal extensibility through the latissimus dorsi musculature is important before performing back-power exercises to decrease stress to the shoulder and low back.
summary: Flexibility in the latissimus dorsi muscles is crucial for safe back-power exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1070
content: Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption involves holding dumbbells at the side with palms facing the side of the body. During Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption, raise one foot off the floor. In Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption, raise both arms, thumbs up, at a 45-degree angle in front of the body until hands reach approximately eye level. Keep shoulder blades retracted and depressed throughout Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption. Do not allow the back to arch during Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption. Slowly return arms back to the sides of the body and repeat Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption. Regression options for Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption include two-legs and seated. Progression options for Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption include single-leg, alternating-arm, single-leg, single-arm, and proprioceptive modalities.
summary: Single-Leg Dumbbell Scaption is a shoulder exercise performed on one leg, with progressions and regressions available.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1071
content: Performing shoulder exercises in the scapular plane decreases the risk of the supraspinatus muscle becoming impinged between the head of the humerus and the coracoacromial arch of the scapula.
summary: Performing shoulder exercises in the scapular plane reduces the risk of supraspinatus impingement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1072
content: Seated Stability Ball Military Press involves sitting on a stability ball with toes pointed straight ahead and feet hips-width apart. Hold dumbbells at shoulder level during Seated Stability Ball Military Press. Press the dumbbells overhead until both arms are fully extended with palms facing away during Seated Stability Ball Military Press. Slowly return dumbbells to the start position and repeat Seated Stability Ball Military Press. Regression for Seated Stability Ball Military Press is seated on a bench. Progression options for Seated Stability Ball Military Press include alternating-arm, one-arm, and standing.
summary: Seated Stability Ball Military Press is a shoulder exercise performed on a stability ball, with progressions and regressions available.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1073
content: Performing exercises on a stability ball can be uncomfortable for some people. A health and fitness professional may need to hold the ball while an individual performs the exercise to provide support during Seated Stability Ball Military Press.
summary: Stability ball exercises may require support for some individuals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1074
content: Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press involves sitting on a bench with feet flat on the floor and toes pointing straight ahead. Hold dumbbells in each hand during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press. Press arms directly overhead by extending elbows during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press. Do not allow the head to jut forward or the back to arch during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press. Slowly return to the starting position and repeat Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press. When performing overhead presses, make sure the cervical spine stays neutral (head drawn back) during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press. Do not allow the head to migrate forward during Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press as this places excessive stress on the posterior neck muscles and cervical spine.
summary: Seated Dumbbell Shoulder Press is a shoulder exercise performed on a bench, emphasizing proper form and neck alignment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1075
content: The Seated Shoulder Press Machine exercise requires the user to sit with feet straight ahead, make adjustments for a proper fit, and select the desired weight. During the Seated Shoulder Press Machine exercise, the user should keep their chin tucked. The movement phase of the Seated Shoulder Press Machine exercise involves pressing the weight overhead until the arms are fully extended, holding the position, and then slowly returning the weight to the original position by flexing the elbows and allowing the shoulder blades to retract and depress.
summary: The Seated Shoulder Press Machine exercise involves pressing a weight overhead while maintaining proper form.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1076
content: The Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise requires the user to stand facing a wall or partner with feet shoulder-width apart, knees slightly bent, and toes pointing straight ahead. For the Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise, the user should hold a medicine ball weighing between 5 and 10% of their body weight. The movement phase of the Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise involves tossing the ball against a wall or to a partner with an underhand motion, using a scooping motion to catch the ball, and repeating as quickly as can be controlled. The Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise can be performed continuously to one side or by alternating sides. If a partner is unavailable for the Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw exercise, the user can perform it by tossing the medicine ball against a wall.
summary: The Front Medicine Ball Oblique Throw is a dynamic exercise using a medicine ball, targeting oblique muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1077
content: The Overhead Medicine Ball Throw exercise requires the user to stand with their back to a wall in a semi-squat position, holding a medicine ball as shown. The movement phase of the Overhead Medicine Ball Throw exercise involves jumping off the ground explosively, extending arms overhead, throwing the medicine ball off the wall, releasing the ball before the arms pass the ears, and landing in a controlled and stable manner, avoiding all compensations. The user should avoid hyperextension of the back during the Overhead Medicine Ball Throw exercise.
summary: The Overhead Medicine Ball Throw is a plyometric exercise involving a powerful throw against a wall.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1078
content: The Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise requires the user to stand on one foot and allow their arms to extend and hang to the sides of the body with a dumbbell in each hand. The movement phase of the Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise involves performing a biceps curl by performing elbow flexion and supination, and then slowly returning the dumbbells to their original position.
summary: Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl is a biceps exercise performed on one leg.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1079
content: The Overhead Medicine Ball Throw exercise requires the user to stand with their back to a wall in a semi-squat position, holding a medicine ball as shown. The movement phase of the Overhead Medicine Ball Throw exercise involves jumping off the ground explosively, extending arms overhead, throwing the medicine ball off the wall, releasing the ball before the arms pass the ears, and landing in a controlled and stable manner, avoiding all compensations. The user should avoid hyperextension of the back during the Overhead Medicine Ball Throw exercise.
summary: The Overhead Medicine Ball Throw is a dynamic exercise involving a jump, throw, and controlled landing, emphasizing explosive power and proper form.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1080
content: The Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise requires the user to stand on one foot and allow their arms to extend and hang to the sides of the body with a dumbbell in each hand. The movement phase of the Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise involves performing a biceps curl by performing elbow flexion and supination, and then slowly returning the dumbbells to their original position. The Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise can be regressed to a two-leg variation and progressed to an alternating-arm, single-arm, or proprioceptive modality variation. Keeping the scapulae retracted during the Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl exercise ensures proper scapular stability, placing more emphasis on the biceps musculature.
summary: The Single-Leg Dumbbell Curl is a unilateral exercise targeting the biceps, with variations for progression and regression.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1081
content: The Single-Leg Barbell Curl exercise requires the user to stand on one foot while holding a barbell in both hands. The movement phase of the Single-Leg Barbell Curl exercise involves performing a barbell curl by flexing both elbows, keeping the shoulder blades retracted, curling the bar up to chest level, and slowly lowering the bar back to the original position by extending the elbows. The Single-Leg Barbell Curl exercise can be regressed to a two-leg variation and progressed to a proprioceptive modality variation. To decrease stress on the elbow during the Single-Leg Barbell Curl exercise, the user should not grip the bar too close or too wide. The grip width should be determined by extending the elbows so the hands fall naturally to the sides, palms facing forward.
summary: The Single-Leg Barbell Curl is a unilateral exercise targeting the biceps, with variations for progression and regression, emphasizing proper grip width.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1082
content: The Seated Two-Arm Dumbbell Biceps Curl exercise requires the user to sit on a bench with feet flat on the floor, pointing straight ahead, and hold a dumbbell in each hand with arms at sides. The movement phase of the Seated Two-Arm Dumbbell Biceps Curl exercise involves performing a biceps curl by performing elbow flexion and supination, keeping the shoulder blades retracted throughout the exercise, and slowly returning the dumbbells to their original position. The Biceps Curl Machine exercise involves flexing elbows while keeping shoulder blades retracted. When performing biceps curls, it is important to maintain an upright posture and avoid excessive torso flexion or extension.
summary: Biceps curls can be performed seated or using a machine, emphasizing proper form and scapular retraction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1083
content: When performing stability ball exercises in a supine position, the head should comfortably rest on the ball to decrease stress on the cervical spine. To perform Supine Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions, lie on a stability ball with the ball between the shoulder blades, maintaining a bridge position by contracting gluteal muscles and keeping shoulders, hips, and knees level. Feet should be shoulder-width apart with toes pointing straight ahead during Supine Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions. To perform Prone Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions, lie in a prone position with the stability ball under the abdomen, keeping feet pointed down, legs straight, and the navel drawn in. Hold dumbbells in each hand with elbows bent and shoulder blades retracted and depressed during Prone Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions. To ensure optimal alignment during Prone Ball Dumbbell Triceps Extensions, maintain alignment of the ankles, knees, hips, elbows, shoulders, and ears.
summary: Stability ball exercises can target the triceps, emphasizing proper form and alignment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1084
content: To perform Cable Pushdowns, stand with feet shoulder-width apart, pointed straight ahead, and knees slightly flexed. Grasp a cable with elbows flexed and shoulder blades retracted and depressed during Cable Pushdowns. Contract triceps by pushing hands toward the ground until arms are fully extended during Cable Pushdowns. Using a rope when performing cable pushdowns allows the elbows to track through their natural path of motion.
summary: Cable pushdowns target the triceps, emphasizing proper form and elbow tracking.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1085
content: To perform Supine Bench Barbell Triceps Extensions, lie on a flat bench with feet flat and toes pointing straight ahead. Hold the barbell in both hands with elbows flexed during Supine Bench Barbell Triceps Extensions. Extend elbows until arms are straight during Supine Bench Barbell Triceps Extensions. Slowly lower the barbell toward the forehead by flexing the elbows during Supine Bench Barbell Triceps Extensions.
summary: Supine Bench Barbell Triceps Extensions target the triceps, emphasizing controlled movement and full range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1086
content: Keeping hands too close on the bar during barbell curls or triceps extensions can increase stress on the elbow. Ball squats are a great way to teach individuals how to squat properly, with the goal to have them eventually progress to squats without the stability ball. Stand with feet shoulders-width apart, toes pointing forward, and knees over second and third toes. Hold dumbbells to the side of the body. Rest back against a stability ball, which is placed on a wall. Ideally, keep feet under the knees. For individuals who lack ankle dorsiflexion (tight calves), place the feet slightly in front of the knees. Slowly begin to squat down, bending knees and flexing hips, keeping feet straight (as if sitting into a chair). Keep the knees in line with the toes and abdominals activated. Allow the pelvis to sit back under the ball while maintaining a neutral spine. Keep the chest up and put pressure through the heels. Do not rely solely on the ball for support. To rise back up, contract gluteal muscles and place pressure through the heels as knees are extended. Stand up straight until hips and legs are fully extended. Avoid compensation in the low back or lower extremities. Decrease range of motion
summary: Ball squats are a regression exercise for teaching proper squat technique, with modifications for ankle flexibility and range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1087
content: Stand in front of a box, with feet pointed straight ahead. Hold dumbbells to the side. Step onto the box with one leg, keeping foot pointed straight ahead and knee lined up over mid-foot. Push through heel and stand up straight, balancing on one leg. Flex the other leg at the hip and knee and dorsiflex the foot. Return 'floating' leg to the ground and step off the box, maintaining optimal alignment. Repeat on the other leg.
summary: Step-up exercise instructions: step onto a box, stand on one leg, step down, and repeat on the other leg.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1088
content: Omit balance. Decrease step height. Frontal plane step-up. Transverse plane step-up.
summary: Variations of the step-up exercise: omitting balance, decreasing step height, and performing frontal or transverse plane movements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1089
content: Lunges are excellent lower-extremity strengthening exercises; however, many individuals lack the flexibility and stabilization requirements to execute the exercise properly. Step-ups are a great way to regress the lunge until one develops proper flexibility and stabilization capabilities to perform the lunge correctly.
summary: Step-ups as a progression to lunges: they help develop the flexibility and stabilization needed for proper lunges.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1090
content: Stand in the machine. Make any adjustments necessary to fit the body. Place feet shoulders-width apart, with toes pointing forward and knees directly over second and third toes. Slowly perform a squat to a depth that can be safely controlled. Keep optimal alignment of the lower extremities throughout the movement.
summary: Instructions for using a squat machine: adjust the machine, position feet, and perform a controlled squat.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1091
content: Before any compensation occurs, activate gluteal muscles and apply pressure through heels extending hips and knees back to the starting position.
summary: Squat technique: activate glutes and push through heels to return to starting position.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1092
content: Make sure the feet are positioned on the platform hips-to shoulders-width apart, toes are pointed straight, and the knees track in line with the toes. This will decrease stress to the knees, hips, and low back.
summary: Proper foot placement in a squat machine: hips-to-shoulder width apart, toes straight, knees aligned with toes, to reduce stress on joints.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1093
content: Stand with feet shoulders-width apart, toes pointing straight ahead, and knees over second and third toes. Rest the barbell on shoulders, behind the neck, with hands grasping the bar wider than shoulders-width apart. Slowly begin to squat down, bending knees and flexing hips, keeping feet straight. Do not allow the knees to move inward. Keep the chest up and put pressure through the heels. Squat to a depth that can be safely controlled while maintaining ideal posture. To rise back up, contract gluteal muscles and place pressure through the heels as knees and hips are extended. Stand up straight until hips and legs are fully extended. Avoid compensation in the low back or lower extremities.
summary: Barbell back squat technique: proper setup, descent, ascent, and posture.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1094
content: Only squat as far down as can be controlled without compensating. As one develops more flexibility and stabilization strength, the range of motion can be increased, assuming no compensations occur.
summary: Squat depth: only go as deep as controlled, gradually increasing depth as flexibility and stability improve.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1095
content: Stand with feet shoulders-width apart and pointing straight ahead. Squat down slightly. Jump up into the air while extending arms overhead. Bring arms back to sides during landing. Land softly on the mid-foot in a controlled manner with feet and knees pointing straight ahead. Repeat as quickly as can be controlled.
summary: Jump squat instructions: squat, jump, land softly, and repeat.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1096
content: Allow the pelvis to sit back under the ball while maintaining a neutral spine. Keep the chest up and put pressure through the heels. Do not rely solely on the ball for support. To rise back up, contract gluteal muscles and place pressure through the heels as knees are extended. Stand up straight until hips and legs are fully extended. Avoid compensation in the low back or lower extremities.
summary: Stability ball squat technique: proper form and movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1097
content: Decrease range of motion. Holding on to a stable support. Squat without stability ball.
summary: Variations of the squat: decreasing range of motion, using support, and performing without a stability ball.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1098
content: Stand with feet shoulders-width apart and pointing straight ahead. Squat down slightly. Jump up into the air while extending arms overhead. Bring arms back to sides during landing. Land softly on the mid-foot in a controlled manner with feet and knees pointing straight ahead. Repeat as quickly as can be controlled.
summary: Instructions for performing a jump squat exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1099
content: Jump up off the ground and, while in the air, bring knees up to chest. Land softly on the midpoint of the feet, in a controlled manner, with feet straight and knees over mid-foot. Repeat, as quickly as can be controlled.
summary: Instructions for performing a jump with knee drive.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1100
content: When performing power exercises, make sure you land behind the ball of the foot (not on the ball of the foot or on the heel). This will ensure proper force distribution through the foot and lower extremity, improving force production capabilities.
summary: Landing technique for power exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1125
content: Richmond SR and Godard MP conducted a study on the effects of varied rest periods between sets to failure using the bench press in recreationally trained men, published in the Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research in 2004.
