McCall GE, Byrnes WC, Fleck SJ, Dickinson A, and Kraemer WJ investigated acute and chronic hormonal responses to resistance training designed to promote muscle hypertrophy in a study published in the Canadian Journal of Applied Physiology in 1999. Ratamess NA, Falvo MJ, Mangine GT, Hoffman JR, Faigenbaum AD, and Kang J examined the effect of rest interval length on metabolic responses to the bench press exercise, publishing their results in the European Journal of Applied Physiology in 2007.
summary: This chunk focuses on studies investigating the effects of rest intervals on various aspects of exercise performance and hormonal responses.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1126
content: The OPT model is a framework that helps guide program design and allows for a planned, systematic progression by preassigning specific acute variables for each of the five phases of training to elicit the desired adaptation. Acute variables are the most fundamental components of designing a training program. They determine the amount of stress placed on the body and, ultimately, what adaptations the body will incur. The body will specifically adapt to the demands placed on it (known also as the principle of specificity). The acute variables applied during an exercise program will dictate these demands and the adaptations achieved. The OPT model provides a framework to help guide program design and allows for a planned, systematic progression by preassigning specific acute variables for each of the five phases of training to elicit the desired adaptation. Personal trainers must be competent at designing training programs for a variety of clients, using acute variables and exercises in a structured, progressive manner. A structured, scientifically based program design model makes this process safer and more effective. The OPT model provides personal trainers with a proven system in which a client’s information can simply be plugged in and a program designed. Program design is creating a purposeful system or plan to achieve a specific goal. To do so, the personal trainer must understand acute variables and the OPT model and its phases, as well as how to apply it all.
summary: This chunk describes the OPT model, a framework for program design in personal training, emphasizing the importance of acute variables and their role in eliciting specific adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1127
content: A repetition is one complete movement of a particular exercise. Most repetitions will involve the three muscle actions: concentric, isometric, and eccentric (not necessarily in that order). These muscle actions can be seen in the example of a biceps curl. A single repetition includes raising the dumbbell up against the direction of resistance (a concentric contraction), pausing for any specified amount of time (an isometric hold), and then lowering the dumbbell with the direction of resistance back to its starting position (an eccentric action). Another example of these actions can be seen when performing a squat. Starting from a standing position, one repetition includes lowering the body (with the direction of resistance) toward the ground (eccentric), pausing for any specified amount of time (isometric), and then raising back up (against the direction of resistance) to the starting position (concentric). Repetitions are simply a means to count the number of movements performed in a given amount of time. They can therefore be a means to count the time the muscles are under tension (time under tension). For instance, during the squat exercise, if the eccentric movement portion is performed at a 4-second pace, followed by a 2-second isometric pause and a 1-second concentric movement pace, the amount of time that the muscles are under tension would be equal to 7 seconds per repetition. Each phase of training in the OPT model has specific goals and therefore requires a specific number of repetitions to achieve these goals. The number of repetitions performed in a given set is dependent on the client’s work capacity, intensity of the exercise, and the specific phase of training.
summary: This chunk defines repetitions in exercise, explaining the three muscle actions involved (concentric, isometric, and eccentric) and their role in time under tension. It also connects repetitions to the OPT model and its phases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1128
content: Personal trainers must keep in mind that all acute variables are interdependent. This means that the specific use of one will affect the others. For example, the more intense the exercise or heavier the load, the fewer the number of repetitions the individual can perform. Training in a specific repetition range yields specific adaptations.
summary: This chunk highlights the interdependence of acute variables in training, emphasizing that choices in one area will influence others, and specific repetition ranges lead to specific adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1129
content: The number of repetitions performed in a given set is dependent on the client’s work capacity, intensity of the exercise, and the specific phase of training. For example, the more intense the exercise or heavier the load, the fewer the number of repetitions the individual can perform. Training in a specific repetition range yields specific adaptations.
summary: Repetition selection depends on exercise intensity, client capacity, and training goals, as different rep ranges induce distinct adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1130
content: Muscular endurance and stabilization is best achieved by performing 12 to 20 repetitions at 50 to 70% of the one-repetition maximum (1RM). Muscular endurance and stabilization is best developed with 1 to 3 sets of 12 to 20 repetitions at 50 to 70% of 1RM intensity. Muscular endurance and stabilization is best developed with a slow repetition tempo.
summary: Muscular endurance and stabilization are optimized with 12-20 reps at 50-70% 1RM, using slow tempos.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1131
content: Hypertrophy (muscle growth) is best achieved using 6 to 12 repetitions at 75 to 85% of the 1RM. Hypertrophy adaptations are best stimulated by 3 to 5 sets of 6 to 12 repetitions at 75 to 85% of 1RM intensity level. Hypertrophy is best achieved with a moderate tempo.
summary: Hypertrophy is maximized with 6-12 reps at 75-85% 1RM, using moderate tempos.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1132
content: If maximal strength adaptations are desired, the repetition range is 1 to 5 at 85 to 100% of the 1RM. For maximal strength adaptation, 4 to 6 sets of 1 and 5 repetitions at an intensity of 85 to 100% of 1RM are recommended. Maximal strength adaptations are best achieved with a fast or explosive tempo that can be safely controlled.
summary: Maximal strength gains are achieved with 1-5 reps at 85-100% 1RM, using fast tempos.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1133
content: Power adaptations require 1 to 10 repetitions at 30 to 45% of 1RM, or approximately 10% of body weight. For power adaptations, 3 to 6 sets of between 1 and 10 repetitions at an intensity of 30 to 45% of 1RM (if using weights) or approximately 10% of body weight (if using medicine balls) are recommended. Power adaptations are best achieved with a fast or explosive tempo that can be safely controlled.
summary: Power development is optimized with 1-10 reps at 30-45% 1RM (or ~10% bodyweight), using fast tempos.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1134
content: The OPT model uses a specified repetition continuum to provide the desired adaptations in a systematic manner. The beginning phases of the OPT model consist of higher repetition schemes necessary to build proper connective tissue (tendons, ligaments) strength, stability, and muscular endurance. A common mistake of many advanced clients is to not use a planned training program that provides periods of low-repetition training alternated with periods of high-repetition training. Using the OPT model enables the personal trainer to use a systematic training approach to prevent overtraining and yield specific results by using planned intervals of training.
summary: The OPT model employs a progressive repetition continuum, starting with higher reps for foundational strength and progressing to lower reps for specific adaptations, preventing overtraining.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1135
content: A set is a group of consecutive repetitions. The quantities of the other acute variables (i.e., repetitions, training intensity, number of exercises, training level, and recoverability), as well as the training status of the client, determine the number of sets an individual performs. There is an inverse relationship between sets, repetitions, and intensity. The individual usually performs fewer sets when performing higher repetitions at a lower intensity (endurance adaptations) and more sets when performing lower repetitions at a higher intensity (strength and power adaptations).
summary: Sets are determined by repetitions, intensity, exercise selection, client experience, and recovery.  Sets generally decrease with higher reps/lower intensity and increase with lower reps/higher intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1136
content: Training intensity is defined as an individual’s level of effort compared with their maximal effort. The number of sets and repetitions for an exercise is determined by the specific training phase, an individual’s training goal, and the training status of the client. Intensity is determined by the number of sets and repetitions to be performed, which is based on the individual’s specific training goals. Muscular endurance and stabilization is best developed with a training intensity of 50 to 70% of 1RM. Hypertrophy is best achieved by training with 75 to 85% of 1RM. Maximal strength adaptations require training with 85 to 100% of 1RM. Power (high-velocity) adaptations are best attained with 30 to 45% of 1RM when using conventional weight training, or approximately 10% of body weight when using medicine balls.
summary: Training intensity is relative effort, influenced by training phase, goals, and experience.  Specific rep ranges and intensities target different adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1137
content: Training in an unstable environment can increase the training intensity because it requires greater motor unit recruitment. Changing other acute training variables such as rest periods and tempo also changes the training intensity. Intensity is a function of more than just external resistance.
summary: Training intensity is multifaceted, influenced by environment, rest periods, tempo, and more, not just external load.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1138
content: Repetition tempo refers to the speed with which each repetition is performed. Muscular endurance and stabilization is best developed with a slow repetition tempo. One example of a slow tempo would be a 4-second eccentric action, 2-second isometric hold, and 1-second concentric contraction (4/2/1). Hypertrophy is best achieved with a moderate tempo. One example of a moderate tempo would be a 2-second eccentric action, 0-second isometric hold, and 2-second concentric contraction (2/0/2).
summary: Repetition tempo impacts training adaptations. Slow tempos favor endurance, moderate tempos hypertrophy, and fast tempos power/strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1139
content: The OPT model places a major emphasis on the repetition tempo spectrum as it has a significant impact on the functional outcome of the stressed tissues.
summary: The OPT model prioritizes repetition tempo, recognizing its crucial role in tissue adaptation and functional outcomes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1140
content: Personal trainers must keep in mind that all acute variables are interdependent. This means that the specific use of one will affect the others.
summary: Acute training variables are interconnected, meaning changes in one will influence others.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1141
content: Hypertrophy is best achieved with a moderate tempo. One example of a moderate tempo would be a 2-second eccentric action, 0-second isometric hold, and 2-second concentric contraction (2/0/2).
summary: Moderate tempo (e.g., 2/0/2) is optimal for hypertrophy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1142
content: Maximal strength adaptations are best achieved with a fast or explosive tempo that can be safely controlled. Power adaptations are best achieved with a fast or explosive tempo that can be safely controlled.
summary: Fast, controlled tempos are best for maximal strength and power adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1143
content: The OPT model places a major emphasis on the repetition tempo spectrum as it has a significant impact on the functional outcome of the stressed tissues. By emphasizing eccentric and isometric muscle actions at slower velocities during the initial stabilization phases of training, more demand is placed on the connective tissue (as well as the stabilizing muscles) and better prepares the nervous system for functional movements.
summary: The OPT model emphasizes tempo's impact on training outcomes, with slower tempos benefiting connective tissue and stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1144
content: The rest interval is the time taken to recuperate between sets or exercises and has a dramatic effect on the outcome of the training program. Each exercise performed requires energy. The primary type of energy used during training depends on the training phase, intensity, exercise mode, and goal.
summary: Rest intervals significantly influence training outcomes, as they affect energy replenishment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1145
content: Muscular endurance and stabilization adaptations are best developed with relatively short rest periods; generally 0–90 seconds. However, the current work capacity of the client may dictate longer rest periods if needed. Hypertrophy is best achieved with relatively short rest periods often ranging from 0 to 60 seconds. However, the load, volume, and the current fitness level of the client may require longer rest periods.
summary: Rest periods for muscular endurance, stabilization, and hypertrophy are generally short (0-60 seconds), but may be adjusted based on individual factors.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1146
content: Maximal strength adaptations are best achieved with relatively long rest periods, generally 3–5 minutes, depending on the client’s level of fitness and intensity of the exercises. Power adaptations also require relatively long rest periods, generally 3–5 minutes, depending on the client’s level of fitness.
summary: Long rest periods (3-5 minutes) are optimal for maximal strength and power adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1147
content: Dynamic resistance training, as well as isometric training, can significantly reduce adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and phosphocreatine (PC) supplies. The ability to replenish these supplies is crucial for optimal performance and the desired adaptation. By adjusting the rest interval, energy supplies can be regained according to the goal of the training program.
summary: Rest intervals are crucial for replenishing ATP and PC, which are depleted during dynamic and isometric training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1148
content: 20 to 30 seconds will allow approximately 50% recovery of ATP and PC. 40 seconds will allow approximately 75% recovery of ATP and PC. 60 seconds will allow approximately 85 to 90% recovery of ATP and PC. 3 minutes will allow approximately 100% recovery of ATP and PC.
summary: Rest intervals of varying durations allow for different levels of ATP and PC recovery.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1149
content: The rest interval between sets determines to what extent the energy resources are replenished before the next set. The shorter the rest interval, the less ATP and PC will be replenished and consequently less energy will be available for the next set.
summary: Shorter rest intervals result in less ATP and PC replenishment, impacting energy availability for subsequent sets.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1150
content: With new clients, this can result in fatigue, which can lead to decreased neuromuscular control, force production, and stabilization by decreasing motor unit recruitment. Therefore, inadequate rest intervals can decrease performance and could lead to altered movement patterns, and even injury. As the client advances, this can be used as a means to increase the intensity of the workout and promote better adaptations, especially for stability, endurance, and hypertrophy.
summary: Inadequate rest intervals can lead to fatigue, decreased performance, and potential injury, especially for beginners. As clients progress, shorter rest periods can enhance adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1151
content: Conversely, if rest periods are too long between sets or exercises, the potential effects include decreased neuromuscular activity and decreased body temperature. If the beginner client is then asked to perform an intense bout of exercise, this could entail a potential increased risk of injury. For the advanced client, this may be necessary if heavy weight is being used repetitively.
summary: Excessive rest periods can negatively impact neuromuscular activity and body temperature, potentially increasing injury risk for beginners. Advanced clients may require longer rest periods for heavy lifting.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1152
content: The goal of the training program should establish the appropriate rest periods. There are several factors to consider when prescribing appropriate rest intervals. Individuals who are starting an exercise routine may respond better to longer rest periods until they adjust to the demands of their program. Longer rest periods also help to ensure proper exercise technique.
summary: Rest period prescription depends on individual factors and training goals. Beginners may benefit from longer rest periods for adaptation and technique.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1153
content: By reducing the amount of fatigue experienced by the client, that individual may be able to perform each exercise with greater precision. Individuals who are at advanced levels of training or higher fitness levels may respond better to shorter rest periods, but it is still dependent on the phase of training and the client’s goal.
summary: Rest periods should be adjusted based on individual fitness levels and training phases, with beginners potentially benefiting from longer rest periods and advanced individuals potentially responding better to shorter ones.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1154
content: Training volume is the total amount of work performed within a specified time. It is extremely important to plan and control training volume to prevent overtraining, as all training is cumulative. Training volume varies among individuals and is based on: training phase, goals, age, work capacity or training status, recoverability, nutritional status, injury history, and life stress.
summary: Training volume, the total work performed, should be carefully planned and controlled to prevent overtraining. It varies based on individual factors and training goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1155
content: For an individual to achieve optimal results from an integrated training program, the program must provide them with the appropriate planned training volume for extended periods. One of the most important training concepts to remember is that volume is always inversely related to intensity. An individual cannot safely perform high volumes of high-intensity exercises for any extended length of time.
summary: Consistent, appropriate training volume is essential for optimal results. Training volume and intensity are inversely related, meaning high volume and high intensity cannot be sustained for extended periods.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1156
content: It is extremely important to plan and control training volume to prevent overtraining, as all training is cumulative. Training volume varies among individuals and is based on: training phase, goals, age, work capacity or training status, recoverability, nutritional status, injury history, and life stress. For an individual to achieve optimal results from an integrated training program, the program must provide them with the appropriate planned training volume for extended periods.
summary: Training volume should be carefully planned and controlled to prevent overtraining and optimize results, considering individual factors and training goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1157
content: One of the most important training concepts to remember is that volume is always inversely related to intensity. An individual cannot safely perform high volumes of high-intensity exercises for any extended length of time. When working with loads exceeding 90% of an individual’s maximum, one rarely exceeds a workout volume of 30 repetitions (6 sets of 5 repetitions) per exercise. When working with loads of 60% of maximum, a client can easily perform a workout volume of 36 to 60 repetitions per exercise (3 sets of 12 to 20 repetitions). The exception is new clients who may only perform 12 to 20 total repetitions per exercise (1 set of each exercise).
summary: Training volume and intensity are inversely related, with higher intensity allowing for lower volume and vice versa.  Appropriate volume depends on the individual and the load.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1158
content: The training phase and the training goal dictate the repetitions, sets, intensity, rest, and tempo, and these combined dictate the volume. Research demonstrates that higher volume training produces cellular (hypertrophy, fat loss) adaptations. Conversely, high-intensity training with low training volumes produces greater neurologic (maximal strength, power) adaptations.
summary: Training volume and intensity are tailored to the specific training phase and goals, with higher volume favoring hypertrophy and lower volume favoring strength and power.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1159
content: Training frequency refers to the number of training sessions that are performed during a given period (usually 1 week). There is considerable debate concerning the adequate number of training sessions per body part per week necessary for optimal results. The number of training sessions per week per body part is determined by many factors, including training goals, age, general health, work capacity, nutritional status, recoverability, lifestyle, and other stressors. New clients may begin by training their entire body two times a week. Experienced bodybuilders with the specific goal of hypertrophy may have a training cycle in which they train with a split routine of six sessions per week, training each body part two times per week with a larger volume per session.
summary: Training frequency, the number of sessions per week, is individualized based on factors like training goals, experience level, and recovery capacity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1160
content: The specific training goal dictates the program design. Research on training frequency indicates that the optimal training frequency for improvements in strength is three to five times per week. Other research indicates that training at least one to two times per week is sufficient to maintain the physical, physiologic, and performance improvements that were achieved during other phases of training.
summary: Training frequency is influenced by the specific training goal, with higher frequency generally recommended for strength development.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1161
content: Training duration has two prominent meanings. The first meaning of training duration is the timeframe from the start of the workout to the finish of the workout. The second meaning of training duration is the length of time (number of weeks) spent in one phase (or period) of training.
summary: Training duration refers to both the length of a single workout and the duration of a training phase.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1162
content: The training duration for a workout is a function of the number of repetitions, number of sets, number of exercises, and the length of the rest intervals. Training programs that exceed 60 to 90 minutes (excluding warm-up/cool-down) are associated with rapidly declining energy levels. Rapidly declining energy levels cause alterations in hormonal and immune system responses that can have a negative effect on a training program and raise the risk of minor infections, especially upper respiratory infections.
summary: Workout duration should be optimized to avoid energy depletion and potential negative impacts on hormonal and immune function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1163
content: The training duration for a phase of training is dictated by the client’s level of physical ability, goal, and compliance to the program. Typically, a phase of training will last 4 weeks as this is the amount of time it generally takes for the body to adapt to a given stimulus. Afterward, the training stimulus will have to be raised to achieve further adaptations.
summary: Training phases typically last 4 weeks to allow for adaptation, after which the stimulus needs to be increased for continued progress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1164
content: Exercise selection is the process of choosing exercises for program design that allow for the optimal achievement of the desired adaptation. Exercise selection has a tremendous impact on the outcome of the training program. The human movement system is a highly adaptable system that readily adjusts to the imposed demands of training (principle of specificity). Therefore, exercises should be specific to the training goals.
summary: Exercise selection is crucial for achieving desired training outcomes, as the body adapts specifically to the imposed demands.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1165
content: The OPT model uses exercises from all components (core, balance, plyometric, and resistance training), which are categorized by the adaptation for which they are primarily used. Exercises used in Phase 1 of the OPT model (stabilization) are termed stabilization level exercises because they are used and progressed for the stabilization adaptation. Exercises used in Phases 2 to 4 are termed strength level exercises, and exercises used in Phase 5 are termed power level exercises.
summary: The OPT model utilizes exercises from various categories, categorized by the primary adaptation they target (stabilization, strength, power).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1166
content: Exercises can be broken down simplistically into three different types on the basis of the number of joints used, movements performed, and adaptation desired. Single joint exercises focus on isolating one major muscle group or joint (e.g., biceps curls, triceps pushdowns, calf raises). Multijoint exercises use the involvement of two or three joints (e.g., squats, lunges, step-ups, chest presses, rows). Total body exercises include multiple joint movements (e.g., step-up balance to overhead press, squat to two-arm press, barbell clean).
summary: Exercises can be classified as single-joint, multi-joint, or total-body, each targeting specific muscle groups and adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1167
content: The OPT model enables personal trainers to effectively select the appropriate exercise for each client.
summary: The OPT model provides a framework for selecting exercises tailored to individual client needs and goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1168
content: Exercises can be broken down simplistically into three different types on the basis of the number of joints used, movements performed, and adaptation desired. These types include single joint exercises, multijoint exercises, and total body exercises.
summary: Exercises are categorized into single joint, multijoint, and total body types based on their mechanics and training goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1169
content: Single joint exercises focus on isolating one major muscle group or joint (e.g., biceps curls, triceps pushdowns, calf raises).
summary: Single joint exercises target a specific muscle group by isolating a single joint.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1170
content: Multijoint exercises use the involvement of two or three joints (e.g., squats, lunges, step-ups, chest presses, rows).
summary: Multijoint exercises engage multiple joints and muscle groups simultaneously.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1171
content: Total body exercises include multiple joint movements (e.g., step-up balance to overhead press, squat to two-arm press, barbell clean).
summary: Total body exercises involve complex movements that engage multiple muscle groups and joints.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1172
content: The OPT model enables personal trainers to effectively select the appropriate exercise for each client. Completing a fitness assessment and reviewing the specific training goals will allow the fitness professional to implement these exercises into a properly planned, integrated training program.
summary: The OPT model guides exercise selection based on client assessments and goals, facilitating program design.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1173
content: To develop optimal stability, traditional exercises can be progressed to a more unstable environment, such as standing up (two-leg, staggered-stance, single-leg) or from a stable environment to an unstable environment (foam pad, stability ball, BOSU ball, etc.). Research has shown that exercises performed in unstable environments produce superior results for the goal of stabilization and training the core stabilizing muscles. NASM only recommends training in a proprioceptive (unstable) environment that can be safely controlled based on the client’s movement capabilities.
summary: Training in unstable environments enhances stability and core strength, progressing from stable to unstable surfaces as appropriate.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1174
content: Examples of stabilization exercises include: Chest press on a stability ball, Single-leg cable rows, Single-leg dumbbell shoulder press on a ½ foam roll, Single-leg squat.
summary: Examples of stabilization exercises include variations performed on unstable surfaces.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1175
content: To develop optimal strength, the use of total-body and multijoint exercises has been shown to be most beneficial. Examples of strength exercises include: Bench press, Rows (machine, seated cable, barbell), Shoulder press (seated barbell, seated dumbbell, machine), Squats/leg press.
summary: Strength development is optimized through total-body and multijoint exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1176
content: To develop optimal power, plyometrics, and explosive strength-training exercises can be performed during functional movement patterns. Examples of power exercises include: Overhead medicine ball throw, Medicine ball chest pass, Woodchop throw, Squat jump, Tuck jump, Two-arm push press, Barbell clean.
summary: Power is enhanced through plyometrics and explosive exercises mimicking functional movements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1177
content: All exercises, once selected, can be progressed or regressed in a systematic fashion. The Stabilization Continuum includes Floor, Sport beam, Half foam roll, Foam pad, Balance disc, Wobble board, and BOSU Ball. The Progression Continuum includes Floor, Sport beam, Half foam roll, Foam pad, Balance disc, Wobble board, and BOSU Ball.
summary: Exercises can be progressively or regressively modified using various stability and progression tools.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1178
content: The primary function of a personal trainer is to design safe and effective training programs for clients. Training programs should be individualized to meet the needs and goals of each client. The OPT model provides fitness professionals with the necessary tools to design programs.
summary: Personal trainers design individualized programs based on client needs and goals, utilizing the OPT model.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1179
content: Acute variables determine the amount of stress placed on the body and the adaptation it will incur. The acute variables to consider when designing a program include repetitions, sets, training intensity, repetition tempo, rest interval, training volume, training frequency, training duration, and exercise selection.
summary: Acute training variables, such as repetitions, sets, intensity, and rest intervals, influence training stress and adaptation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1180
content: The more intense the exercise, the fewer repetitions the individual should perform. Individuals usually perform fewer sets when performing higher repetitions at a lower intensity and more sets when performing lower repetitions at a higher intensity. Training intensity should be determined after sets and reps. Altering other variables such as environment stability, rest periods, and tempo changes the training intensity.
summary: Training intensity and volume are interrelated, with higher intensity typically requiring fewer repetitions and sets.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1181
content: Different times under tension yield specific results. By emphasizing eccentric and isometric muscle actions at slower velocities, more demand is placed on the connective tissue.
summary: Manipulating time under tension, particularly emphasizing eccentric and isometric phases, influences muscle and connective tissue adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1182
content: Rest interval has a dramatic effect on the outcome of the training program. By adjusting the rest interval, energy supplies can be regained according to the goal of the training program. The shorter the rest interval, the less ATP and PC will be replenished, and consequently less energy will be available for the next set.
summary: Rest intervals influence energy replenishment and training adaptations, with shorter intervals promoting higher intensity and metabolic stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1183
content: To avoid making rests too long or short, consider training experience, training intensity, tolerance to short rest periods, general fitness level, training goals, nutritional status, and recoverability.
summary: Rest interval selection should be individualized based on various factors like training experience, intensity, and recovery capacity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1184
content: Training volume should be planned and controlled to prevent overtraining. Volume is always inversely related to intensity.
summary: Training volume should be carefully managed to prevent overtraining, with a balance between intensity and volume.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1185
content: Optimal training frequency for improvements in strength is three to five times per week. Training at least one to two times per week is sufficient to maintain improvements achieved during other phases of training.
summary: Training frequency for strength gains is typically 3-5 times per week, while 1-2 times per week is sufficient for maintenance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1186
content: Programs exceeding 60 to 90 minutes can have a negative effect on a training program and raise the risk of minor infections, especially upper respiratory infections. Typically, a phase of training will last 4 weeks.
summary: Training sessions should ideally be within 60-90 minutes to minimize risk of infection, and training phases typically last 4 weeks.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1187
content: Exercises should be specific to the training goals and based on the principles of the exercise selection continuum. Periodization is a systematic approach to program design that uses the general adaptation syndrome and principle of specificity to vary the amount and type of stress placed on the body to produce adaptation and prevent injury.
summary: Exercise selection should align with training goals, and periodization systematically varies training stress to optimize adaptation and prevent injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1188
content: Training at least one to two times per week is sufficient to maintain improvements achieved during other phases of training. Programs exceeding 60 to 90 minutes can have a negative effect on a training program and raise the risk of minor infections, especially upper respiratory infections.
summary: Maintaining fitness gains requires minimal training frequency, while excessively long workouts can be detrimental to health.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1189
content: Typically, a phase of training will last 4 weeks. Periodization is a systematic approach to program design that uses the general adaptation syndrome and principle of specificity to vary the amount and type of stress placed on the body to produce adaptation and prevent injury. Periodization (or planned fitness training) varies the focus of a training program at regularly planned periods of time (weeks, months, and so forth) to produce optimal adaptation.
summary: Training phases typically last 4 weeks, and periodization is a structured approach to program design that varies training stress for optimal adaptation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1190
content: The objectives of periodization are to divide the training program into distinct periods (or phases) of training and to train different forms of strength in each period (or phase) to control the volume of training and to prevent injury. Periodization has been shown to be an effective form of program design for many fitness-related goals. Periodization provides for the repeated use of different forms of training at specific times in an annual training program to elicit different adaptations in the body (stabilization, strength, and power).
summary: Periodization aims to structure training phases, vary training types, and control volume to optimize adaptation and prevent injury.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1191
content: By intentionally cycling through different periods (or phases) of training, the acute variables are manipulated to adjust the volume of training. By controlling the volume of training as a function of time in any given program, periodization allows for maximal levels of adaptation, while minimizing overtraining. Overtraining will lead to fatigue and eventual injury.
summary: Periodization manipulates training volume and intensity to maximize adaptation and minimize overtraining risks.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1192
content: Making gradual increases in volume and decreases in intensity was the most effective program for increasing muscular endurance. A 9-month periodized resistance-training program was superior for enhancing strength and motor performance in collegiate women tennis players. Making program alterations on a daily basis was more effective in eliciting strength gains than doing so every 4 weeks.
summary: Research supports the effectiveness of periodization for various fitness goals, including muscular endurance, strength, and motor performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1193
content: Planned, integrated strength-training programs led to superior physical, physiologic, and performance improvements compared with nonperiodized training programs. Planned variations in an integrated training program were essential because they enabled continuous adaptations to occur during a training period and prevented injury. An evaluation of the body of research comparing periodized with nonperiodized training demonstrated a superior strength and power adaptation with periodized training.
summary: Periodized training consistently outperforms nonperiodized training in terms of physical, physiological, and performance improvements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1194
content: Planned fitness training (or periodization) shifts the focus of a training program at regularly planned intervals of time to vary stress placed on the body to produce adaptation and prevent injury. A training plan clarifies what forms of training will be used, how long it will take, how often it will change, and what specific exercises will be performed.
summary: Periodization involves strategically varying training stress to promote adaptation and prevent injury, while a training plan outlines the specific details of the program.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1195
content: An annual plan organizes the training program for a 1-year period to show when the client is in which phase. The annual plan is further broken down into periods of training called monthly plans, which detail the specific days of each workout, showing the client exactly what type of training will be required each day of the month. Weekly plans are the specific workouts and exercises that the client will do for that week.
summary: Training plans are structured hierarchically, with annual plans providing the long-term framework, monthly plans outlining weekly workouts, and weekly plans detailing specific exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1196
content: The different periods (or phases) of training seen in a traditional periodization model include a preparatory period (termed anatomic adaptation), a hypertrophy period, a maximal strength period, and a power period. The OPT model simplifies these phases into stabilization, strength, and power.
summary: Traditional periodization models often include distinct phases like preparatory, hypertrophy, maximal strength, and power, while the OPT model simplifies these into stabilization, strength, and power.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1197
content: The OPT model seen in a phase-specific model of training includes five different phases of training.
summary: The OPT model utilizes five distinct phases within its training approach.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1198
content: The annual plan is further broken down into periods of training called monthly plans, which detail the specific days of each workout, showing the client exactly what type of training will be required each day of the month. Weekly plans are the specific workouts and exercises that the client will do for that week.
summary: This chunk describes the structure of a training plan, outlining annual, monthly, and weekly components.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1199
content: The different periods (or phases) of training seen in a traditional periodization model include a preparatory period (termed anatomic adaptation), a hypertrophy period, a maximal strength period, and a power period. The OPT model simplifies these phases into stabilization, strength, and power.
summary: This chunk compares traditional periodization models with the OPT model, highlighting their key phases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1200
content: The OPT model seen in a phase-specific model of training includes five different phases of training. The OPT model should be thought of as a staircase guiding clients through different levels of adaptation. The first level of training in the OPT model focuses on the main adaptation of stabilization (or anatomic adaptation) and is designed to prepare the body for the demands of higher levels of training that may follow. This period is crucial for all beginners. It is also necessary to cycle back through this level after periods of strength and power training to maintain a high degree of core and joint stability. In addition, it allows the body to actively rest from more intense bouts of training.
summary: This chunk introduces the OPT model, emphasizing its five phases and the importance of the initial stabilization phase.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1201
content: The focus of stabilization training includes: improving muscle imbalances, improving stabilization of the core musculature, preventing tissue overload by preparing muscles, tendons, ligaments, and joints for the upcoming imposed demands of training, improving overall cardiorespiratory and neuromuscular condition, and establishing proper movement patterns and exercise technique. This period prepares clients for the demands of the Strength Endurance Phase (Phase 2).
summary: This chunk details the goals and benefits of the stabilization training phase.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1202
content: The primary means of progressing (or increasing the intensity of training) in this period is by increasing the proprioceptive demands of the exercises. This form of training has been shown to be extremely effective for increasing neuromuscular efficiency in the healthy (80), elderly (97), and unhealthy populations (98–101). Another important component of stabilization training is that it may help to ensure activity-specific strength adaptations (such as standing on one leg to kick a ball, climbing up stairs, or simply walking) (79).
summary: This chunk explains how progression occurs in the stabilization phase and highlights its effectiveness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1203
content: The stabilization period of training in the OPT model consists of one phase of training: Stabilization Endurance Training. Stabilization Endurance Training is designed to create optimal levels of stabilization strength and postural control. Although this phase is the first phase of training in the OPT model, as previously stated, it will also be important to cycle back through this phase of training between periods of higher-intensity training seen in Phases 2 through 5. This will allow for proper recovery and maintenance of high levels of stability that will ensure optimal adaptations for strength, power, or both.
summary: This chunk defines the Stabilization Endurance Training phase and its importance within the OPT model.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1204
content: The primary focus when progressing in this phase is on increasing the proprioception (controlled instability) of the exercises, rather than just the load. This phase of training focuses on: increasing stability, increasing muscular endurance, increasing neuromuscular efficiency of the core musculature, and improving intermuscular and intramuscular coordination. In addition to increasing proprioceptive demand, acute variables can be progressed by increasing the volume (sets, reps) and intensity (load, exercise selection, planes of motion), and by decreasing rest periods. A client in this category will generally stay in this phase of training for a 4-week duration.
summary: This chunk outlines the progression and focus of the Stabilization Endurance Training phase.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1205
content: The second level of training in the OPT model focuses on the main adaptation of strength, which includes strength endurance, hypertrophy, and maximal strength. This period of training is a necessary progression from stabilization for anyone who desires to increase caloric expenditure, muscle size, muscle strength, and bone mineral density. The focus of the strength period of training is to increase the ability of the core musculature to stabilize the pelvis and spine under heavier loads, through more complete ranges of motion. The focus of the strength period of training is to increase the load-bearing capabilities of muscles, tendons, ligaments, and joints. The focus of the strength period of training is to increase the volume of training. The focus of the strength period of training is to increase metabolic demand by taxing the ATP-PC and glycolysis energy systems to induce cellular changes in muscle (weight loss or hypertrophy). The focus of the strength period of training is to increase motor unit recruitment, frequency of motor unit recruitment, and motor unit synchronization (maximal strength).
summary: This chunk describes the second level of the OPT model, focusing on strength adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1206
content: The strength period of training in the OPT model consists of three phases: Phase 2 (Strength Endurance Training), Phase 3 (Hypertrophy Training), and Phase 4 (Maximal Strength Training). Strength endurance is a hybrid form of training that promotes increased stabilization endurance, hypertrophy, and strength. This form of training entails the use of super-set techniques in which a more stable exercise (such as a bench press) is immediately followed with a stabilization exercise with similar biomechanical motions (such as a stability ball push-up). For every set of an exercise/body part performed according to the acute variables, there are actually two exercises or two sets being performed. High amounts of volume can be generated in this phase of training. A client in this category will generally stay in this phase of training for a 4-week duration.
summary: This chunk details the three phases within the strength period of the OPT model.

Back exercises are performed for 3 sets of 10 reps with a Medium tempo and 60 seconds rest. Shoulder exercises are performed for 3 sets of 10 reps with a Medium tempo and 60 seconds rest. Biceps exercises are optional. Triceps exercises are optional. Legs exercises are performed for 3 sets of 10 reps with a Medium tempo and 60 seconds rest.
summary: This chunk outlines a 3-day hypertrophy training program with specific exercise sets, reps, tempo, and rest periods.

content: Bench Press is an example resistance exercise. Lat Pulldown is an example resistance exercise. Shoulder Press Machine is an example resistance exercise. id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1212
content: Optional exercises are available for Speed, Agility, and Quickness. Treadmill (optional) is performed for 1 set. SMR: Calves, IT-Band, Lats is performed for 1 set. id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1213
content: SMR: Calves, IT-Band, Lats is performed for 1 set. Active Stretch: Calves, Hip Flexors, Lats is performed for 1 set. Treadmill is performed for 30 seconds. Brisk walk to slow jog is performed for 1 set. Hold each stretch for 1–2 seconds.
summary: This chunk details additional warm-up and stretching routines.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1214
content: The maximal strength training phase focuses on increasing the load placed on the tissues of the body. Maximal intensity improves recruitment of more motor units. Maximal intensity improves rate of force production.
summary: This chunk describes the maximal strength training phase and its benefits on motor unit recruitment and force production.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1215
content: The different levels of training seen in a traditional periodization model include anatomical adaptation, hypertrophy, maximal strength, and power. In the OPT model, these are simplified into stabilization, strength, and power.
summary: This chunk describes the different levels of training in traditional periodization and the OPT model.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1216
content: The first level, stabilization, is crucial for all beginners as it is designed to prepare the body for the demands of higher levels of training. For advanced clients, the stabilization level allows for active rest from more intense bouts of training. Stabilization training involves low-intensity, more realistic speeds and forces that the body will encounter in everyday life and in sports.
summary: This chunk explains the importance and nature of the stabilization training level.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1217
content: The second level, strength, is designed to increase strength endurance, muscle size, and maximal strength. The strength period of training in the OPT model consists of three phases: Phase 2: Strength Endurance Training; Phase 3: Hypertrophy Training; and Phase 4: Maximal Strength Training.
summary: This chunk describes the strength training level and its phases within the OPT model.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1218
content: The maximal strength training phase focuses on increasing the load placed on the tissues of the body. Maximal intensity improves recruitment of more motor units. Maximal intensity improves rate of force production. Maximal intensity improves motor unit synchronization. Maximal strength training has also been shown to help increase the benefits of power training used in Phase 5.
summary: This chunk details the goals and benefits of maximal strength training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1219
content: Because the goal of this phase of training is primarily maximal strength, the personal trainer will want to increase intensity (load) and volume (sets). Rest periods may need to increase as the client trains with heavier loads. A client in this category will generally stay in this phase of training for a 4-week duration before cycling back through Phase 1 or 2 or progressing on to Phase 5.
summary: This chunk outlines the training prescription and duration for the maximal strength phase.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1220
content: Power is simply defined as force multiplied by velocity (P = F × V). Any increase in either force or velocity will produce an increase in power. This is accomplished by either increasing the load (or force) as in progressive strength training, or increasing the speed with which you move a load (or velocity). The combined effect is a better rate of force production in daily activities and sporting events.
summary: This chunk defines power and explains how to increase it.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1221
content: To develop optimal levels of power, individuals should train both with heavy loads (85 to 100%) and light loads (30 to 45%) at high speeds. The 85 to 100% refers to the intensity for traditional strength training exercises. These exercises and loads increase power by increasing the force side of the power equation (force multiplied by velocity), whereas the 30 to 45% intensity range is used for “speed” exercises, such as speed squats in which the squats are performed as fast as possible with a lighter load. The 10% intensity is used as an indicator for medicine ball training that will require the throwing or release of a medicine ball. These last two forms of training affect the velocity side of the power equation (force multiplied by velocity).
summary: This chunk details the training intensities and exercises used for power development.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1222
content: The power level of training in the OPT model consists of one phase of training: Phase 5 (Power Training). The power training phase focuses on both high force and velocity to increase power. This is accomplished by combining a strength exercise with a power exercise for each body part (such as performing a barbell bench press superset with a medicine ball chest pass). By using both heavy loads with explosive movement and low resistance with a high velocity, power outputs can be enhanced.
summary: This chunk describes the power training phase in the OPT model.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1223
content: Because the goal of this phase of training is primarily power, the personal trainer will want to progress by increasing volume (sets), intensity (load), and velocity. A client in this category will generally stay in this phase of training for a 4-week duration before cycling back through Phase 1 or 2.
summary: This chunk outlines the training progression and duration for the power phase.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1224
content: Hold each stretch for 1–2 seconds. 10 reps are performed.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1230
content:
The third level of training, power, is designed to increase the rate of force production.
To develop optimal levels of power, it has been shown that individuals must train both with heavy loads and light loads at high speeds.
The power level consists of one phase of training: Phase 5: Power Training.
This phase focuses on both high force and velocity to increase power and lasts about 4 weeks.
summary: Description of the power training phase.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1231
content:
Program design was defined as creating a purposeful system or plan to achieve a goal.
Periodization is the scientific basis that allows fitness professionals to strategically plan and design programs without the risk of placing improper stresses on the body.
The OPT model is a proven, easy-to-use system of periodization that can be used to create programs for clients with various goals.
summary: Explanation of program design and periodization.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1232
content:
The goal of reducing body fat requires clients to follow the simple principle of burning more calories than they consume.
The best way to increase the calories burned is to move more.
Weight training provides an extremely potent means to burn calories when it is combined with cardiorespiratory training by maintaining, or even increasing, lean muscle tissue.
More activity and greater amounts of lean body mass result in more calories burned during exercise and throughout the day.
summary: Strategies for reducing body fat.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1233
content:
Resistance training also provides the added benefit of increased muscle strength.
Muscle hypertrophy can be defined as the chronic enlargement of muscles.
summary: Benefits of resistance training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1234
content: The best way to increase the calories burned is to move more. More activity and greater amounts of lean body mass result in more calories burned during exercise and throughout the day. Weight training provides an extremely potent means to burn calories when it is combined with cardiorespiratory training by maintaining, or even increasing, lean muscle tissue. Resistance training also provides the added benefit of increased muscle strength.
summary: Increasing activity levels and lean body mass elevates calorie expenditure, and weight training, combined with cardio, maximizes calorie burn and muscle strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1235
content: Muscle hypertrophy can be defined as the chronic enlargement of muscles. Training programs need to be progressed with higher volumes (more sets, reps, and intensity) to force muscles to regenerate their cellular makeup and produce increased size.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy is achieved through progressive overload, increasing training volume to stimulate muscle growth.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1236
content: The goal of improving general sports performance requires the client to increase overall proprioception, strength, and power output (or rate of force production). The training will need to be progressed from stabilization through power phases of training.
summary: Improving general sports performance necessitates enhancing proprioception, strength, and power output through a training progression from stabilization to power phases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1237
content: The client will start in January in Phase 1 to ensure proper muscle balance and endurance of the stabilization muscles. Clients will remain in Phase 1 for approximately 4 weeks before moving into Phase 2. Phase 1 is vital for the client as it will prepare the connective tissues and muscles for the higher demands of training required for this goal. Without proper preparation, injury may be imminent.
summary: Phase 1 focuses on muscle balance and stabilization, preparing the body for subsequent training phases and minimizing injury risk.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1238
content: Clients will remain in Phase 1 for approximately 4 weeks before moving into Phase 2. Phase 2 will promote greater strength endurance and more volume to prepare the client for the greater demands of Phases 3 and 4.
summary: Phase 2 builds upon Phase 1, increasing strength endurance and training volume in preparation for more demanding phases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1239
content: Phase 3 is specific for maximal hypertrophy and will place larger volumes of stress through the body to force cellular changes that result in muscle hypertrophy. Phase 4 is used to increase the strength capacity to allow the client to train with heavier weights in the future.
summary: Phase 3 prioritizes muscle hypertrophy through high-volume training, while Phase 4 focuses on increasing strength capacity for future training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1240
content: Returning to Phase 1 will allow the client proper recovery time before entering back into Phases 2 through 4. Cardiorespiratory training can be performed each month to ensure the cardiorespiratory system is efficient and to promote optimal tissue recovery.
summary: Periodic return to Phase 1 allows for recovery, while monthly cardio sessions support cardiovascular health and tissue repair.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1241
content: This plan demonstrates a 3-day-per-week workout plan, with scheduled workouts on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. Cardio can be done on the workout days (or any other day during the week, depending on the client’s schedule).
summary: The plan outlines a 3-day workout schedule with flexibility for cardio integration.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1242
content: Figure 14.10 illustrates the monthly plan for February (Phase 2). This monthly plan demonstrates a 3-day-per-week workout plan, with scheduled workouts on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. This monthly plan could easily be performed four times per week, with a split routine for the body parts. Cardio can be done on the workout days (or any other day during the week, depending on the client’s schedule).
summary: February's plan focuses on Phase 2, offering a 3-day or 4-day workout option with flexibility for cardio.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1243
content: Figure 14.11 illustrates the monthly plan for March (Phase 3). This plan demonstrates a 4-day-per-week workout plan (split routine) with scheduled workouts on Mondays, Tuesdays, Thursdays, and Fridays. Cardio can be done on the workout days (or any other day during the week, depending on the client’s schedule).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1244
content: Figure 14.12 illustrates the monthly plan for June (Phase 4). This plan demonstrates a 4-day-per-week workout plan (split routine) with scheduled workouts on Mondays, Tuesdays, Thursdays, and Fridays. Cardio can be done on the workout days (or any other day during the week, depending on the client’s schedule).
summary: June's plan focuses on Phase 4, utilizing a 4-day split routine with cardio flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1245
content: During April and May the client will repeat Phases 2 and 3 respectively. Figure 14.13 shows the annual plan. The client will start January in Phase 1 to ensure proper muscle balance and endurance of the stabilization muscles. Clients will remain there for approximately 4 weeks before moving on to Phase 2. Phase 1 is vital for this client, as it will prepare the connective tissues and muscles for the higher demands of training to follow.
summary: The annual plan cycles through Phases 2 and 3 in April and May, starting with Phase 1 in January for foundational muscle balance and preparation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1246
content: However, for the typical client, Phases 1, 2, and 5 will be the most important. Because Phase 3 is dedicated to maximal hypertrophy, it will not be necessary for the goal of general performance. Phase 4 can be used in moderation to help increase the initial strength levels required to optimize the adaptation in Phase 5, if necessary.
summary: For general performance goals, Phases 1, 2, and 5 are most crucial, with Phase 3 (hypertrophy) and Phase 4 (strength) potentially incorporated selectively.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1247
content: The training will need to be progressed from stabilization through power phases of training. The following program is a general representation of how the OPT model is used for clients with the goal of improving general performance. The client can be cycled through the entire OPT model, depending on the needs and wants of the client.
summary: Training progression follows the OPT model, starting with stabilization and progressing to power phases, adaptable to individual client needs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1248
content: The client will start January in Phase 1 to ensure proper muscle balance and endurance of the stabilization muscles. Clients will remain there for approximately 4 weeks before moving on to Phase 2. Phase 1 is vital for this client, as it will prepare the connective tissues and muscles for the higher demands of training to follow. Without proper preparation, injury will be imminent for the athletic client.
summary: The initial phase focuses on muscle balance, endurance, and connective tissue preparation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1249
content: The remainder of the annual plan shows the client cycling through Phases 1, 2, and 5. Phase 2 will promote greater overall strength endurance to prepare the client for the greater demands of Phase 5. As previously mentioned, Phase 4 can be used to increase the strength capacity of the client, but is not vitally necessary for general performance.
summary: The annual plan cycles through phases focusing on strength endurance and power.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1250
content: This is a hybrid form of periodization known as undulating periodization. Undulating periodization allows the client to train at various intensities during the course of a week, eliciting multiple adaptations once a certain level of fitness is achieved (106). In this program, stabilization (Phase 1), strength (Phase 2), and power (Phase 5) are all being trained together.
summary: The program utilizes undulating periodization, training various intensities throughout the week.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1251
content: Figure 14.13 shows the annual plan. Figure 14.14 illustrates the monthly plan for January. The February plan demonstrates a 3-day-per-week workout plan with scheduled workouts on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. The February plan could easily be four times a week with a split routine for the body parts. Figure 14.15 illustrates the monthly plan for February. Figure 14.16 illustrates the monthly plan for March. The March plan demonstrates a 3-day-per-week workout plan with scheduled workouts on Mondays, Wednesdays, and Fridays. In March, Phases 1, 2, and 5 are all used in the same week.
summary: The program outlines monthly and annual plans with specific workout schedules.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1252
content: Cardio can be done on the workout days (or any other day during the week, depending on the client’s schedule). It is important not to perform high-intensity cardiorespiratory exercise the same day as a high-intensity OPT workout. Rather a high-intensity OPT workout (Phase 5) should be paired with a low-intensity cardio program (Stage 1). Conversely, a high-intensity cardio program (Stage III) should be paired with a low-intensity OPT workout (Phase 1).
summary: Cardio should be scheduled strategically to avoid conflicting with high-intensity OPT workouts.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1253
content: The resistance-training section of the OPT template can be completed. The importance of the OPT system is that it provides a systematic format to follow. When filling in the resistance-training portion of the OPT template, just simply choose which phase of training the client will work in. All of the major acute variables are already predetermined. Sets, reps, intensity, tempo, and rest intervals are already given. In the exercises box, simply choose an exercise that fits the desired body part as well as the guidelines of the specific phase of training. For example, Phase 2, strength endurance, consists of a strength exercise, followed by a stabilization exercise. In the chest section, a bench press followed by a stability ball push-up would be appropriate exercise selections.
summary: The OPT system provides a structured template for resistance training, outlining specific sets, reps, intensity, and exercise selections based on the chosen training phase.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1254
content: Using information from chapter 13 (resistance training), the fitness professional can choose a particular system of training (such as using a circuit-training or vertical-loading method) to increase the intensity of the workout. If the client works out 2 to 6 days a week, a split routine may be used with varying body parts. The possibilities are endless and only limited by creativity.
summary: Fitness professionals can customize the program by incorporating different training systems and split routines based on client needs and preferences.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1255
content: Phase 4 can be used in moderation to help increase the initial strength levels required to optimize the adaptation in Phase 5, if necessary.
summary: Phase 4 can be incorporated to enhance strength levels before progressing to Phase 5.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1256
content: The OPT model is a planned fitness training system that can be used to create programs for clients with various goals. The OPT model can be used to reduce body fat, increase lean body mass, and improve general performance. To reduce body fat, clients must burn more calories than they consume. To increase lean body mass, clients must work with higher volumes to increase muscle size. To improve general performance, clients must increase overall proprioception, strength, and rate of force production.
summary: The OPT model is a versatile training system for various goals, addressing fat loss, muscle gain, and overall performance improvement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1257
content: Personal trainers must be able to apply the information in the OPT model to multiple situations and a variety of clients. The most important thing, however, is to follow the physiologic guidelines of the OPT model.
summary: Effective application of the OPT model requires adaptability to diverse client needs and adherence to its physiological principles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1258
content: Clients will work in Phase 1 for 4 weeks to ensure proper muscle balance and endurance of the stabilization muscles. The remainder of the annual plan for fat-loss clients shows cycling back and forth between Phases 1 and 2 (metabolic demand and more volume for increased caloric expenditure). Cardiorespiratory training should be performed each month to improve cardiorespiratory system efficiency and increase caloric expenditure. Phase 3 is optional for fat-loss clients.
summary: A typical fat-loss program utilizes the OPT model's phases, emphasizing stabilization, strength endurance, and cardiorespiratory training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1259
content: Clients will work in Phase 1 for 4 weeks to ensure proper muscle balance and endurance of the stabilization muscles. The remainder of the annual plan for lean body mass gain shows the client cycling through Phase 1 (recovery time), Phase 2 (greater strength endurance and more volume), Phase 3 (larger volumes of stress for hypertrophy), and Phase 4 (increased strength capacity with even higher volumes of training and more hypertrophy). Cardiorespiratory training can be performed each month to ensure the cardiorespiratory system is efficient and promoting optimal tissue recoverability.
summary: A lean body mass gain program utilizes the OPT model's phases, focusing on recovery, strength endurance, hypertrophy, and strength capacity, with integrated cardiorespiratory training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1260
content: The training for general performance will use the entire OPT model, although for the typical client, Phases 1, 2, and 5 will be the most important. The client will work in Phase 1 for 4 weeks to ensure proper muscle balance and endurance of the stabilization muscles. The remainder of the annual plan for general performance shows the client cycling through Phases 1, 2, and 5. After the first 4 months, undulating periodization is used, and stabilization (Phase 1), strength (Phase 2), and power (Phase 5) are used in the same month and week. Cardiorespiratory training can be performed each month as well.
summary: A general performance program emphasizes stabilization, strength, and power, utilizing the OPT model's phases and undulating periodization with consistent cardiorespiratory training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1261
content: For example, Phase 2, strength endurance, consists of a strength exercise, followed by a stabilization exercise. In the chest section, a bench press followed by a stability ball push-up would be appropriate exercise selections.
summary: Phase 2 of the OPT model focuses on strength endurance, combining strength exercises with stabilization exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1262
content: Using information from chapter 13 (resistance training), the fitness professional can choose a particular system of training (such as using a circuit-training or vertical-loading method) to increase the intensity of the workout. If the client works out 2 to 6 days a week, a split routine may be used with varying body parts. The possibilities are endless and only limited by creativity.
summary: Fitness professionals can customize workouts using various training systems and split routines based on client frequency and goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1286
content: The squat is a compound exercise that primarily targets the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings. A typical squat prescription for strength development is 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.
summary: The squat is a compound exercise targeting the quadriceps, glutes, and hamstrings, typically prescribed for strength development with 3-5 sets of 5-8 reps at 70-85% of 1RM.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1287
content: The deadlift is a posterior chain exercise targeting the erector spinae, glutes, and hamstrings. Conventional deadlifts place greater demand on the erector spinae compared to sumo deadlifts.
summary: The deadlift targets the posterior chain, with conventional and sumo variations differing in erector spinae demand.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1288
content: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body during training.
summary: Progressive overload involves gradually increasing training stress over time.
