id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1434
content: Resistance training can be added to any exercise program designed to promote weight loss, but sustained long-term aerobic endurance activities remain a priority. Circuit-style resistance training produces nearly identical caloric expenditure rates compared to walking at a fast pace in the same time span. Resistance training increases lean body mass, which leads to a higher metabolic rate and improved body composition.
summary: Both aerobic and resistance training are valuable for weight loss, with resistance training further enhancing metabolism and body composition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1435
content: The same exercise training guidelines for healthy adults can be used for obese clients. Health and movement assessments should be performed to establish initial program design goals and parameters for obese clients.
summary: Exercise guidelines for obese clients are similar to those for healthy adults, but personalized assessments are crucial for program design.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1436
content: Resistance training can be added to any exercise program designed to promote weight loss, but sustained long-term aerobic endurance activities remain a priority. Resistance training increases lean body mass, which leads to a higher metabolic rate and improved body composition. Although resistance training typically burns fewer calories than aerobic exercise, it preserves lean body mass, which is important for maintaining metabolism and improving body composition. Resistance training should be part of any exercise regimen to promote weight loss. Similar resistance training guidelines used for adults of normal weight should be used in the obese population, focusing on correct form and breathing and technique.
summary: Resistance training is crucial for weight loss, preserving lean mass, and improving body composition. It should be incorporated alongside aerobic exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1437
content: Circuit-style resistance training produces nearly identical caloric expenditure rates compared to walking at a fast pace in the same time span.  For effective weight loss, a reasonable energy expenditure goal using aerobic exercise should be 200 to 300 kcal per session, with a minimum weekly goal of 1,250 kcal combined exercise and physical activity. Walking is often recommended if the risk of orthopedic injury is low. If the benefits of walking, particularly adherence, exceed the observed or perceived risk of an orthopedic injury, walking may be a primary exercise recommendation. Walking is often both a preferred activity for many obese clients and one that is easily engaged in and adhered to.
summary: Circuit training and walking are effective for calorie expenditure and weight loss. Walking is particularly suitable for obese clients due to its accessibility and adherence potential.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1438
content: Health and movement assessments should be performed to establish initial program design goals and parameters for obese clients. Fitness assessments, such as pushing, pulling, and squatting assessments, can be used for obese individuals. Resistance training exercises for assessment or training may be best performed with cables, exercise tubing, or body weight from a standing or seated position for obese clients. A single-leg balance assessment may be more appropriate than a single-leg squat for obese clients. Flexibility exercises should also be performed from a standing or seated position. Using the standing hip flexor (rather than the kneeling hip flexor stretch), standing hamstring stretch, wall calf stretch, and seated adductor stretch would be advised for obese clients.
summary: Assessments and exercise modifications are crucial for obese clients, considering their limitations and ensuring safe and effective training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1439
content: Core and balance training is important for obese clients because they lack balance and walking speed, both of which are important to exercise. Personal trainers must use caution when placing an obese client in a prone or supine position because these obese individuals are prone to both hypotensive and hypertensive responses to exercise. Having obese clients perform exercises in a standing position may be more appropriate and more comfortable for them. Performing prone iso-abs (planks) on an incline or a standing medicine ball rotation may be best suited for certain obese clients versus supine crunches. Resistance training exercises may need to be started in a seated position and progressed to a standing position for obese clients.
summary: Core and balance training are essential for obese clients, and exercise modifications should prioritize their safety and comfort.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1440
content: Obesity is the fastest growing health problem in the United States. Personal trainers must be prepared and willing to work with obese clients. Once BMI is determined, realistic weight-loss and exercise goals can be discussed. When designing programs for overweight and obese clients, adherence to the type and amount of exercise should be considered.  The same exercise training guidelines for healthy adults can be used for obese clients.  Obesity is a unique chronic disease because it also affects a person’s sense of emotional well-being and self-esteem. Obesity can alter the emotional and social aspects of a person’s life as much as it does the physical aspects.
summary: Personal trainers should be equipped to work with obese clients, considering their unique physical and emotional needs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1441
content: Personal trainers should ensure that the client is breathing correctly during resistance training exercises and avoids straining during exercise or squeezing exercise bars too tightly, which can cause an increase in blood pressure.  Machines are often not designed for obese individuals and may require a significant amount of mobility to get in and out. Dumbbells, cables, or exercise tubing exercises work quite well instead of machines for obese clients. The use of self-myofascial release should be done with caution as many obese clients will not feel comfortable rolling or lying on the floor. It may be a good idea to recommend that certain exercises be performed in the privacy of their own home or in a location within a health club that offers privacy for obese clients. Personal trainers must make sure of all the situations in which the obese client will exercise, including positions, locations in the training facility that offer greater privacy, and choice of exercise equipment.
summary: Exercise modifications and equipment choices should be tailored to obese clients' comfort and safety.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1442
content: Low-impact or step aerobics (such as treadmill walking, rowing, stationary cycling, and water activity) is recommended.  It is recommended that obese clients engage in weight-supported exercise (such as cycling or swimming) to decrease orthopedic stress.
summary: Low-impact aerobic activities are recommended for obese clients to minimize orthopedic stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1443
content: Personal trainers must be very aware of the psychosocial aspects of obesity when training obese clients to ensure that the client feels socially and emotionally safe. Proper exercise selections and positions are very important to the client’s sense of well-being.
summary: Personal trainers should prioritize the emotional well-being of obese clients, creating a safe and supportive training environment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1444
content: For effective weight loss, a reasonable energy expenditure goal using aerobic exercise should be 200 to 300 kcal per session, with a minimum weekly goal of 1,250 kcal combined exercise and physical activity. Resistance training should be part of any exercise regimen to promote weight loss. Although resistance training typically burns fewer calories than aerobic exercise, it preserves lean body mass, which is important for maintaining metabolism and improving body composition.
summary: Guidelines for weight loss through exercise emphasize both aerobic and resistance training, highlighting the importance of calorie expenditure and lean body mass preservation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1445
content: Aerobic exercise frequency should be at least 5 days per week. Aerobic exercise intensity should be 60–80% of maximum heart rate. The ‘Talk Test’ can be used to determine exertion. Aerobic exercise duration should be 40–60 minutes per day, or 20- to 30-minute sessions twice each day. Stage I cardiorespiratory training should progress to stage II (intensities may be altered to 40–70% of maximal heart rate if needed). Low-impact or step aerobics (such as treadmill walking, rowing, stationary cycling, and water activity) is recommended.
summary: Recommended aerobic exercise guidelines include frequency, intensity, duration, and examples of suitable activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1446
content: Resistance training should consist of 1–3 sets of 10–15 repetitions on 2–3 days per week. Phases 1 and 2 of the program should be performed in a circuit-training manner (higher repetitions such as 20 may be used). Exercises should be performed in a standing or seated position. Assessment exercises include push, pull, squat, single-leg balance (if tolerated), and flexibility.
summary: Guidelines for resistance training include sets, repetitions, frequency, exercise types, and considerations for individual abilities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1447
content: Clients may have other chronic diseases; in such cases a medical release should be obtained from the individual’s physician.
summary: Important safety precaution: obtain medical clearance before exercise for clients with pre-existing conditions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1448
content: Diabetes is a metabolic disorder in which the body does not produce enough insulin (type 1) or the body cannot respond normally to the insulin that is made (type 2). There are two primary forms of diabetes: type 1 (insulin-dependent diabetes) and type 2 (non–insulin-dependent diabetes). Although type 2 is referred to as non–insulin-dependent diabetes, some individuals with type 2 diabetes cannot manage their blood glucose levels and do require additional insulin. Type 2 diabetes is strongly associated with an increase in childhood and adult-onset obesity. Type 1 diabetes is typically diagnosed in children, teenagers, or young adults.
summary: Definition and types of diabetes, highlighting the distinction between type 1 and type 2, and their associated risk factors.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1449
content: With type 1 diabetes, specialized cells in the pancreas called beta cells stop producing insulin, causing blood sugar levels to rise, resulting in hyperglycemia (high levels of blood sugar). To control this high level of blood sugar, the individual with type 1 diabetes must inject insulin to compensate for what the pancreas cannot produce. Exercise increases the rate at which cells utilize glucose, which may mean that insulin levels may need to be adjusted with exercise. If the individual with type 1 diabetes does not control his or her blood glucose levels (via insulin injections and dietary carbohydrates) before, during, and after exercise, blood sugar levels can drop rapidly and cause a condition called hypoglycemia (low blood sugar), leading to weakness, dizziness, and fainting.
summary: Explanation of type 1 diabetes, insulin dependence, blood sugar regulation, and exercise considerations for individuals with this condition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1450
content: Type 2 diabetes is associated with obesity, particularly abdominal obesity. The incidence and prevalence of adult type 2 diabetes in the United States has increased sharply in recent years. There is a significant public health concern about the rising incidence of type 2 diabetes in children, associated with both the increase in abdominal obesity and decrease in voluntary physical activity. Individuals with type 2 diabetes usually produce adequate amounts of insulin; however, their cells are resistant to the insulin (the insulin present cannot transfer adequate amounts of blood sugar into the cell). This condition can lead to hyperglycemia (high blood sugar). Chronic hyperglycemia is associated with a number of diseases associated with damage to the kidneys, heart, nerves, eyes, and circulatory system.
summary: Description of type 2 diabetes, its prevalence, risk factors, and the physiological mechanisms underlying the condition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1451
content: Although individuals with type 2 diabetes do not experience the same fluctuations in blood sugar as those with type 1, it is still important to be aware of the symptoms, particularly for individuals with type 2 diabetes who use insulin medications. The most important goals of exercise for individuals with either type of diabetes are glucose control and for those with type 2 diabetes, weight loss. Exercise training is effective with both goals because it has a similar action to insulin by enhancing the uptake of circulating glucose by exercising skeletal muscle. Research has shown that exercise improves a variety of glucose measures, including tissue sensitivity, improved glucose tolerance, and even a decrease in insulin requirements. Exercise has a substantial positive effect on the prevention of type 2 diabetes.
summary: Exercise benefits for individuals with both types of diabetes, focusing on glucose control, weight management, and the physiological mechanisms involved.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1452
content: There are specific exercise guidelines and recommendations to follow when working with a diabetic population.
summary: Emphasis on the need for tailored exercise guidelines for individuals with diabetes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1453
content: The most important goals of exercise for individuals with either type of diabetes are glucose control and for those with type 2 diabetes, weight loss. Exercise training is effective with both goals because it has a similar action to insulin by enhancing the uptake of circulating glucose by exercising skeletal muscle. Research has shown that exercise improves a variety of glucose measures, including tissue sensitivity, improved glucose tolerance, and even a decrease in insulin requirements. Exercise has a substantial positive effect on the prevention of type 2 diabetes.
summary: Exercise is crucial for managing diabetes, improving glucose control, and aiding weight loss in type 2 diabetes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1454
content: There are specific exercise guidelines and recommendations to follow when working with a diabetic population. Strategies to prevent hypoglycemic and hyperglycemic events during or after exercise as well as when to defer exercise based on resting blood glucose levels or symptoms should be included in exercise guidelines for diabetic populations. In most cases, excluding other health-related problems, the exercise management goals for individuals with diabetes are similar to those for physical inactivity and excess body weight.
summary: Exercise guidelines for diabetics should address blood sugar management, potential hypo/hyperglycemia, and overall fitness goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1455
content: Walking is a highly preferred form of exercise for obese clients. Care must be taken when recommending walking to clients with diabetes to prevent blisters and foot microtrauma that could result in foot infection. Special care should also be taken with respect to giving advice to clients with diabetes regarding carbohydrate intake and insulin use, not only before exercise but afterward, to reduce the risk of a hypoglycemic or hyperglycemic event.
summary: Walking is a good exercise option for obese diabetics, but precautions should be taken to prevent foot injuries and manage blood sugar levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1456
content: Exercise guidelines for clients with diabetes are similar to those advised for obese adults, as many clients with type 2 diabetes are obese. Daily exercise is recommended for more stable glucose management and maximal caloric expenditure for clients with diabetes. Low-impact exercise activities can reduce the risk of injury.
summary: Exercise recommendations for diabetics align with those for obese adults, emphasizing daily activity and low-impact options.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1457
content: Resistance training is advised as part of an overall exercise plan for clients with diabetes.
summary: Resistance training is beneficial for individuals with diabetes as part of a comprehensive exercise program.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1458
content: Diabetes impairs the body’s ability to either produce or use insulin effectively. Type 1 diabetes (insulin-dependent diabetes) is typically found in younger individuals. If individuals with type 1 diabetes do not control their blood glucose levels (via insulin injections and dietary carbohydrates) before, during, and after exercise, blood sugar levels can rise or fall rapidly and cause hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia, with the latter leading to weakness, dizziness, and fainting. Type 2 diabetes (non–insulin-dependent diabetes) is associated with obesity, particularly abdominal obesity. Individuals with type 2 diabetes usually produce adequate amounts of insulin; however, their cells are resistant to the insulin, which can lead to hyperglycemia.
summary: Diabetes affects insulin production and utilization, with type 1 being insulin-dependent and type 2 involving insulin resistance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1459
content: Exercise is effective for glucose control and weight loss. Exercise recommendations generally follow those advised for obese adults, as many clients with type 2 diabetes are obese, and daily exercise is recommended for more stable glucose management and caloric expenditure.
summary: Exercise benefits both glucose control and weight loss in diabetics, aligning with recommendations for obese individuals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1460
content: Weight-bearing activities may need to be avoided at least initially to prevent blisters and foot microtrauma that could result in foot infections. Carbohydrate intake or insulin use should be stressed before exercise as well as afterward to reduce the risk of postexercise hyperglycemic or hypoglycemic events.
summary: Diabetic exercise plans should consider foot health and blood sugar management before and after activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1461
content: Follow exercise guidelines for obese adults, using lower-impact exercise modalities. Special care should be given to self-myofascial release, and this may be contraindicated for anyone with a loss of protective sensation in the feet and legs.
summary: Diabetic exercise programs should prioritize low-impact activities and consider potential contraindications for self-myofascial release.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1462
content: Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model are appropriate for this population, but plyometric training may be inappropriate.
summary: Exercise programming for diabetics should focus on foundational phases and avoid high-impact plyometrics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1463
content: Blood pressure is defined as the pressure exerted by the blood against the walls of the blood vessels, especially the arteries. It varies with the strength of the heartbeat, the elasticity of the arterial walls, the volume and viscosity of the blood, and a person’s health, age, and physical condition.
summary: Blood pressure is influenced by various factors, including heart rate, blood vessel elasticity, and individual health.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1464
content: Hypertension, or high blood pressure, is a common medical disorder in which arterial blood pressure remains abnormally high (resting systolic ≥140 or diastolic ≥90 mm Hg). A client is considered to have hypertension (HTN) if they have had two or more resting blood pressure measurements made on separate days that are ≥140 or ≥90 mm Hg or if they are currently taking medication to control blood pressure.
summary: Hypertension is defined as persistently elevated blood pressure, diagnosed by repeated measurements or medication use.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1465
content: Recent guidelines state that individuals with resting blood pressure measurements between 120/80 and 135/85 mm Hg are considered to be prehypertensive and should be encouraged to lower their blood pressure through appropriate lifestyle modifications.
summary: Prehypertension is defined as blood pressure between 120/80 and 135/85 mm Hg, prompting lifestyle changes to prevent hypertension.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1466
content: Most people think that normal blood pressure is 120/80 mm Hg, but new guidelines published by the American Heart Association have changed the definition of normal blood pressure to “less than 120 and 80 mm Hg.”
summary: The definition of normal blood pressure has been updated to less than 120/80 mm Hg.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1467
content: Some of the most common causes of hypertension include smoking, a diet high in fat (particularly saturated fat), and excess weight.
summary: Common risk factors for hypertension include smoking, unhealthy diet, and obesity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1468
content: The health risks of hypertension are well known and include increased risk for stroke, cardiovascular disease, chronic heart failure, and kidney failure.
summary: Untreated hypertension significantly increases the risk of serious health problems like stroke, heart disease, and kidney failure.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1469
content: One of the most common and traditional methods of controlling hypertension is through antihypertensive medications. Although medications have been proven to be highly effective, comprehensive lifestyle changes, including regular physical activity, diet, and smoking cessation, have also been shown to reduce blood pressure, potentially eliminating the need for medications.
summary: Medication and lifestyle modifications are both effective in managing hypertension.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1470
content: Consistently elevated arterial blood pressure, which, if sustained at a high enough level, is likely to induce cardiovascular or end-organ damage.
summary: Chronic hypertension can lead to damage to blood vessels and vital organs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1471
content: Research has shown that exercise can have a modest impact on lowering elevated blood pressure by an average of 10 mm Hg for both systolic and diastolic blood pressure (37–39). Exercise may help the body to produce more appropriate responses to physical activity or other physiologic stressors.
summary: Regular exercise can modestly lower blood pressure and improve the body's response to stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1472
content: Low to moderately intense cardiorespiratory exercise has been shown to be just as effective as high-intensity activity in reducing blood pressure.
summary: Both moderate and high-intensity exercise can effectively lower blood pressure.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1473
content: Although medications have been proven to be highly effective, comprehensive lifestyle changes, including regular physical activity, diet, and smoking cessation, have also been shown to reduce blood pressure, potentially eliminating the need for medications. It is important to emphasize the importance of an overall plan to reduce blood pressure that includes exercise, diet, weight loss (if appropriate), and compliance with any medical treatment plans if clients are under the care of a physician. Personal trainers are encouraged to stress the importance of taking any prescribed medications with all of their clients.
summary: Lifestyle modifications, including exercise, diet, and medication adherence, are crucial for managing blood pressure.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1474
content: Consistently elevated arterial blood pressure, which, if sustained at a high enough level, is likely to induce cardiovascular or end-organ damage. Accepted blood pressure contraindications for exercise include an SBP of 200 mm Hg and a DBP of 115 mm Hg. Always check with any other lower guidelines the fitness facility may have in place.
summary: High blood pressure can lead to cardiovascular complications, and exercise should be avoided if blood pressure exceeds certain thresholds.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1475
content: Research has shown that exercise can have a modest impact on lowering elevated blood pressure by an average of 10 mm Hg for both systolic and diastolic blood pressure (37–39). Exercise may help the body to produce more appropriate responses to physical activity or other physiologic stressors. Low to moderately intense cardiorespiratory exercise has been shown to be just as effective as high-intensity activity in reducing blood pressure.
summary: Exercise effectively lowers blood pressure, with moderate-intensity options being equally beneficial as high-intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1476
content: Personal trainers are also encouraged to learn how to accurately assess both resting and exercise blood pressure with all of their clients, but especially those clients with hypertension. Classes to learn how to take blood pressure measurements are available through local organizations such as the American Heart Association and the American Red Cross, or through local hospitals.
summary: Personal trainers should be proficient in measuring blood pressure and encourage clients to learn this skill as well.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1477
content: It is important to monitor the body position of clients with hypertension at all times throughout an exercise training session. Body position can have a dramatic effect on blood pressure response before, during, and after exercise in clients with hypertension. Supine or prone positions (especially when the head is lower in elevation than the heart) can often increase blood pressure, and, as such, these positions may be contraindicated.
summary: Body positioning significantly impacts blood pressure in individuals with hypertension, requiring careful monitoring and consideration during exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1478
content: It is important to note that both hypotensive and hypertensive responses to exercise are possible in clients with hypertension, especially when taking into account the effects of medication, body position, and exercise selection. When assessing clients with hypertension, personal trainers should follow the guidelines in chapter 6.
summary: Exercise responses can vary in individuals with hypertension, necessitating careful assessment and adherence to specific guidelines.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1479
content: Use of a single-leg balance (or squat) assessment can also be beneficial, if tolerated by the client. If possible, all other exercises should be performed in a seated or standing position. Clients may use the full flexibility continuum; however, static and active stretching may be the easiest and safest. Foam rolling may be contraindicated as it requires lying down.
summary: Exercise selection for clients with hypertension should prioritize seated or standing positions, with modifications for balance and stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1480
content: Cardiorespiratory endurance training should focus on stage I and progress only after a physician’s approval. Resistance training should be performed in a seated or standing position. Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model will be appropriate for this population, but should be progressed slowly. The programs should be performed in a circuit-style or Peripheral Heart Action (PHA) training system to distribute blood flow between the upper and lower extremities.
summary: Exercise programming for clients with hypertension should be gradual, focusing on stages I and II of the OPT model with a circuit-style approach.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1481
content: Personal trainers should always ensure that clients with hypertension try and breathe normally and avoid the Valsalva maneuver or overgripping. A maneuver in which a person tries to exhale forcibly with a closed glottis (windpipe) so that no air exits through the mouth or nose as, for example, in lifting a heavy weight. The Valsalva maneuver impedes the return of venous blood to the heart.
summary: Clients with hypertension should be instructed to avoid the Valsalva maneuver and maintain normal breathing patterns during exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1482
content: A program of continuous, lower-intensity (50–85% of work capacity) aerobic exercise is initially recommended. Frequency and duration parameters should be at a minimum 3–5 days per week, 20–45 minutes per day, with additional increases in overall volume of exercise if weight loss is also desired. Despite medication, clients may arrive with preexercise hypertension. Resistance exercise should consist of a Peripheral Heart Action or circuit-training style. Avoid Valsalva maneuvers (holding breath), emphasize rhythmic breathing and a program design for muscular fitness (e.g., 1–3 sets of 8–10 exercises, 10–20 reps, 2–3 days per week).
summary: Initial exercise programs for clients with hypertension should focus on continuous, moderate-intensity aerobic exercise, with gradual progression and adherence to specific training guidelines.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1483
content: Hypertension frequently is associated with other comorbidities, including obesity, cardiovascular disease, and diabetes. Screening for comorbidities is important. Exercise should target a weekly caloric goal of 1,500–2,000 kcal, progressing as tolerable, to maximize weight loss and cardio protection.
summary: Clients with hypertension often have other health conditions, necessitating comprehensive screening and exercise programs tailored to address multiple health concerns.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1484
content: Some medications, such as /H9252-blockers, for hypertension will attenuate the heart rate at rest and its response to exercise. For clients taking medications that will influence heart rate, do not use predicted maximal heart rate or estimates for the exercise. Instead, use actual heart rate response or the Talk Test.
summary: Medications for hypertension can affect heart rate, requiring alternative methods for exercise intensity monitoring.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1485
content: Personal trainers are encouraged to stress the importance of taking any prescribed medications with all of their clients. It is important to evaluate their client’s heart rate response to exercise, as measured during a submaximal exercise test or even during a simple assessment of heart rate during a comfortable exercise load. Individuals with hypertension frequently take medications that can alter the heart rate response to exercise, in most cases blunting the heart rate response to exercise, thus invalidating prediction equations or estimates of training heart rate.
summary: Personal trainers should emphasize medication adherence and assess heart rate response to exercise, considering potential medication effects.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1486
content: Screening for comorbidities is important. Exercise should target a weekly caloric goal of 1,500–2,000 kcal, progressing as tolerable, to maximize weight loss and cardio protection.
summary: Exercise prescription for weight loss and cardio protection should consider comorbidities and a weekly caloric goal of 1,500–2,000 kcal.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1487
content: Some medications, such as /H9252-blockers, for hypertension will attenuate the heart rate at rest and its response to exercise. For clients taking medications that will influence heart rate, do not use predicted maximal heart rate or estimates for the exercise. Instead, use actual heart rate response or the Talk Test.
summary: Medications for hypertension can affect heart rate response to exercise, necessitating the use of actual heart rate or the Talk Test instead of predicted values.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1488
content: Accepted blood pressure contraindications for exercise include an SBP of 200 mm Hg and a DBP of 115 mm Hg. Always check with any other lower guidelines the fitness facility may have in place. SBP, systolic blood pressure; DBP, diastolic blood pressure.
summary: Exercise contraindications for hypertension include SBP of 200 mm Hg and DBP of 115 mm Hg, with additional facility-specific guidelines to consider.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1489
content: Basic Exercise Guidelines for Individuals with Hypertension: Mode: Stationary cycling, treadmill walking, rowers. Frequency: 3–7 days per week. Intensity: 50–85% of maximal heart rate. Stage I cardiorespiratory training progressing to stage II (intensities may be altered to 40–70% of maximal heart rate if needed). Duration: 30–60 minutes.
summary: Exercise guidelines for individuals with hypertension include preferred modes, frequency, intensity, duration, and progression stages.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1490
content: Assessment: Push, pull, OH squat. Single-leg balance (squat if tolerated). Flexibility: Static and active in a standing or seated position. Resistance training: 1–3 sets of 10–20 repetitions 2–3 days per week. Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model.
summary: Assessment and training program components for individuals with hypertension include push/pull assessments, single-leg balance, flexibility, and resistance training within the OPT model phases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1491
content: Tempo should not exceed 1 second for isometric and concentric portions (e.g., 4/1/1 instead of 4/2/1). Use circuit or PHA weight training as an option, with appropriate rest intervals. Special considerations: Avoid heavy lifting and Valsalva maneuvers—make sure client breathes normally. Do not let client overgrip weights or clench fists when training. Modify tempo to avoid extended isometric and concentric muscle action. Perform exercises in a standing or seated position. Allow client to stand up slowly to avoid possible dizziness. Progress client slowly.
summary: Exercise technique and progression considerations for individuals with hypertension include tempo modifications, circuit training, avoiding heavy lifting and Valsalva maneuvers, and gradual progression.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1492
content: OH, overhead squats; PHA, Peripheral Heart Action. A normal blood pressure is considered ≤120/80 mm Hg. Hypertension is defined as a blood pressure ≥140/90 mm Hg. A blood pressure between 120/80 and 135/85 mm Hg is considered prehypertensive.
summary: Definitions of normal, prehypertensive, and hypertensive blood pressure are provided, along with abbreviations for overhead squats and Peripheral Heart Action.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1493
content: Hypertension can be controlled through cardio-respiratory exercise, diet, and other lifestyle changes. Clients with hypertension are encouraged to take all prescribed medications as directed by their physician. Individuals with hypertension should engage in low-intensity aerobic exercise. Individuals with hypertension may want to avoid high-intensity, high-volume resistance training.
summary: Management of hypertension includes lifestyle modifications, medication adherence, and exercise recommendations for low-intensity aerobic activity and caution with high-intensity resistance training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1494
content: Personal trainers are encouraged to measure and pay close attention to the heart rate response to exercise of clients with hypertension, instead of relying on estimates or equations. Monitoring body position is very important when working with clients with hypertension. Supine or prone positions (especially when the head is lower in elevation than the heart) may be contraindicated for clients with hypertension. The majority of exercises for clients with hypertension should be performed in a seated or standing position.
summary: Exercise prescription for clients with hypertension emphasizes heart rate monitoring, body position awareness, and exercise selection favoring seated or standing positions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1495
content: The full flexibility continuum can be used for clients with hypertension, but static and active stretching may be easiest and safest. Self-myofascial release may be contraindicated depending on body position for clients with hypertension. Cardiorespiratory training for clients with hypertension should focus on stage I and progress only with a physician’s approval. Plyometric training should be used with care for clients with hypertension. Resistance training for clients with hypertension should be performed in a seated or standing position.
summary: Flexibility, self-myofascial release, and training progression considerations for clients with hypertension include prioritizing static and active stretching, cautious use of plyometrics, and seated or standing resistance training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1496
content: The full flexibility continuum can be used for clients with hypertension, but static and active stretching may be easiest and safest. Clients should stay with static and active stretching in a standing or seated position because they may be the easiest and safest to perform.
summary: Stretching recommendations for clients with hypertension prioritize safety and ease, favoring static and active stretching in seated or standing positions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1497
content: Self-myofascial release may be contraindicated depending on body position for clients with hypertension.
summary: Self-myofascial release should be approached cautiously for clients with hypertension due to potential contraindications based on body position.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1498
content: Cardiorespiratory training for clients with hypertension should focus on stage I and progress only with a physician’s approval. Large muscle group activities, such as stationary cycling, treadmill walking, or rowing are recommended.
summary: Cardiorespiratory training for clients with hypertension should begin at stage I intensity and progress gradually with medical supervision. Large muscle group activities are preferred.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1499
content: Plyometric training should be used with care for clients with hypertension. Plyometric training would not be recommended for this population in the initial months of training.
summary: Plyometric training should be approached cautiously for clients with hypertension and avoided during the initial stages of their program.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1500
content: Resistance training for clients with hypertension should be performed in a seated or standing position. Use of a single-leg balance (or squat) exercise can also be beneficial, if tolerated by the client. If possible, all other exercises should be performed in a seated or standing position.
summary: Resistance training for clients with hypertension should be performed in seated or standing positions, prioritizing exercises that are well-tolerated and safe.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1501
content: Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model are appropriate for clients with hypertension. Programs for clients with hypertension should be performed in a circuit style or using the Peripheral Heart Action (PHA) training system. The programs should be performed in a circuit-style or PHA training system (see chapter 13).
summary: The OPT model phases 1 and 2 are suitable for clients with hypertension, and training programs should be structured using circuit training or the PHA system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1502
content: Coronary heart disease (CHD) remains the leading cause of death and disability for both men and women. There was a significant (29.2%) reduction in death rates from CHD between 1996 and 2006.
summary: CHD is a leading cause of death and disability, but death rates have significantly decreased in recent decades.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1503
content: CHD is caused by atherosclerosis (plaque formation), which leads to narrowing of the coronary arteries and ultimately angina pectoris (chest pain), myocardial infarction (heart attack), or both. The primary cause of CHD is poor lifestyle choices, primarily cigarette smoking, poor diet, and physical inactivity.
summary: CHD is caused by atherosclerosis, leading to coronary artery narrowing and potentially chest pain or heart attack. Lifestyle factors like smoking, diet, and inactivity are major contributors.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1504
content: The emphasis on treating CHD is centered on improving the health of the internal lining of the coronary artery, called plaque ‘stabilization.’ The other primary focus on the treatment of CHD is through medical management, including pharmaceuticals, as well as aggressive lifestyle intervention, including eating better, getting more exercise, smoking cessation, and stress reduction.
summary: CHD treatment focuses on stabilizing plaque in coronary arteries and managing the condition through medication and lifestyle changes like diet, exercise, smoking cessation, and stress reduction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1505
content: Increasing numbers of individuals with diagnosed as well as undiagnosed heart disease are seeking the advice of health and fitness professionals, including personal trainers, to get advice on exercise training.
summary: Individuals with heart disease are increasingly seeking guidance from fitness professionals regarding exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1506
content: The risk of serious cardiovascular complications, even death, is low in exercise programs that include clients with CHD. The risk of exercise for clients with CHD is likely low because they should be well screened and monitored by their physician and fitness staff.
summary: Exercise programs for clients with CHD generally pose a low risk of serious complications due to careful screening, monitoring, and physician guidance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1507
content: Personal trainers must be aware of the presence of clients with heart disease and help design effective exercise programs with the knowledge that exercise can pose a risk for clients with heart disease. Personal trainers must have a clear understanding about the client’s disease, medication use, and the upper safe limit of exercise—and any other restrictions—imposed by the client’s physician. Personal trainers must not compromise on obtaining this information, and client participation must not proceed until the information is received.
summary: Personal trainers must be knowledgeable about heart disease and exercise risks, obtain thorough client information, and ensure client safety before commencing exercise programs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1508
content: Clients must be able to find and monitor their own pulse rate or use an accurate monitor to stay below their safe upper limit of exercise. The heart rate response to exercise in this population can vary considerably from age-predicted formulas, and will often be lower.
summary: Clients with CHD should be able to monitor their heart rate and stay within their safe exercise limits. Heart rate responses may differ from typical age-predicted formulas.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1509
content: Although signs and symptoms should always supersede all else as a sign to decrease or stop exercising, signs and symptoms vary greatly among individuals with CHD, so careful monitoring of heart rate, rating of perceived exertion (RPE), and signs of worsening CHD like angina become increasingly important. Using rate of perceived exertion to assess exercise intensity is another useful tool.
summary: Close monitoring of heart rate, RPE, and symptoms is crucial for clients with CHD, as exercise responses can vary widely. RPE is a valuable tool for assessing intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1510
content: Clients with stable coronary artery disease (and especially those who have participated in a cardiac rehabilitation program) should know or be taught information on the importance and benefits of exercise, which include a lower risk of mortality (death), increased exercise tolerance, muscle strength, reduction in angina and heart failure symptoms, and improved psychological status and social adjustment.
summary: Clients with stable CHD, particularly those who have undergone cardiac rehabilitation, should understand the benefits of exercise, including reduced mortality risk, improved exercise tolerance, and enhanced psychological well-being.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1511
content: There is evidence that heart disease may be slowed (or even reversed) when a multifactor intervention program of intensive education, exercise, counseling, and lipid-lowering medications are used, as appropriate. Personal trainers must be careful to not overstate the benefits of exercise as a singular intervention and must emphasize to clients the importance of a multidisciplinary approach to heart disease.
summary: A comprehensive approach involving education, exercise, counseling, and medication can effectively manage heart disease. Personal trainers should emphasize the importance of a multidisciplinary approach and avoid overstating the benefits of exercise alone.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1512
content: Exercise is critically important and can be safely incorporated into a comprehensive treatment plan for heart disease. Personal trainers should follow the guidelines in chapter 6 for assessment of clients with CHD.
summary: Exercise is a vital component of heart disease management and can be safely integrated into treatment plans. Personal trainers should adhere to guidelines for assessing clients with CHD.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1513
content: Core exercises in the standing position are preferred. Some examples of core exercises include performing prone iso-abs (planks) on an incline, standing torso cable iso-rotation, or cobras in a standing position (two-leg or single-leg).
summary: Standing core exercises are recommended, with examples including incline planks, standing cable rotations, and standing cobra poses.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1514
content: Resistance training should be performed in a seated or standing position. Most exercises should be performed in a seated or standing position. Resistance training should be performed in a seated or standing position.
summary: Resistance training exercises should be performed in a seated or standing position.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1515
content: Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model will be appropriate for this population. Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model are appropriate for clients with heart disease.
summary: The OPT model phases 1 and 2 are suitable for this population.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1516
content: The programs should be performed in a circuit-style or PHA training system (see chapter 13). Programs for clients with heart disease should be performed in a circuit style or using the PHA training system.
summary: Exercise programs should utilize circuit or PHA training systems.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1517
content: The health and fitness professional should always ensure that the client is breathing normally and is not straining to exercise or overgripping (squeezing too tightly) the exercise equipment, as this can increase blood pressure. Avoid heavy lifting and Valsalva maneuvers—make sure the client breathes normally. Do not let the client overgrip weights or clench fists when training. Modify tempo to avoid extended isometric and concentric muscle action.
summary: Exercise professionals should ensure clients breathe properly and avoid overexertion or gripping too tightly.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1518
content: Large muscle group activities, such as stationary cycling, treadmill walking, or rowing are recommended.  Aerobic low-intensity exercise is recommended with a weekly caloric expenditure goal of 1,500 to 2,000 kcal.
summary: Recommended exercises include large muscle group activities and low-intensity aerobic exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1519
content: The frequency of exercise should be 3–5 days/week. The duration of exercise should be 5–10 minutes warm-up, followed by 20–40 minutes of exercise, followed by a 5- to 10-minute cool-down.
summary: Exercise frequency should be 3-5 days per week, with a duration of 5-10 minutes warm-up, 20-40 minutes of exercise, and 5-10 minutes cool-down.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1520
content: The intensity of exercise should be 40–85% of maximal heart rate reserve. The Talk Test may also be more appropriate as medications may affect heart rate.
summary: Exercise intensity should be 40-85% of maximal heart rate reserve, with the Talk Test as a possible alternative.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1521
content: Assessment exercises include push, pull, OH squat, and single-leg balance (squat if tolerated). Flexibility exercises should be static and active in a standing or seated position.
summary: Assessment exercises include push, pull, overhead squat, and single-leg balance, while flexibility exercises should be static and active in a standing or seated position.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1522
content: Resistance training should consist of 1–3 sets of 10–20 repetitions 2–3 days per week. Tempo should not exceed 1 second for isometric and concentric portions (e.g., 4/1/1 instead of 4/2/1). Use circuit or PHA weight training as an option, with appropriate rest intervals.
summary: Resistance training should involve 1-3 sets of 10-20 repetitions, 2-3 days per week, with a tempo not exceeding 1 second for isometric and concentric portions. Circuit or PHA training can be used with appropriate rest intervals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1523
content: Coronary heart disease is the leading cause of death and disability for both men and women. Coronary heart disease (CHD) is caused by atherosclerosis (plaque formation) which leads to narrowing of the coronary arteries. Narrowing of the coronary arteries can lead to angina pectoris (chest pain), myocardial infarction (heart attack), or both.
summary: Coronary heart disease is a leading cause of death and disability, caused by plaque buildup in coronary arteries, potentially leading to chest pain or heart attack.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1524
content: The cardiovascular complication rate is low in exercise programs. Personal trainers must be aware of the presence of clients with heart disease and help design effective exercise programs. Personal trainers must have a clear understanding about a client’s disease, medication use, and upper safe limit of exercise imposed by the client’s physician. Participation in exercise must not proceed until the information about a client’s disease, medication use, and upper safe limit of exercise imposed by the client’s physician is received.
summary: Exercise programs generally have a low risk of cardiovascular complications. Personal trainers should be aware of clients with heart disease and design appropriate programs, ensuring they understand the client's medical history and exercise limitations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1525
content: Resistance training should not be started until the client has been exercising without any problems for at least 3 months. Cardiorespiratory training should focus on stage I and progress only with the physician’s approval. Plyometric training would not be recommended for clients with heart disease in the initial months of training.
summary: Resistance training should be delayed until after 3 months of consistent exercise, and cardiorespiratory training should progress gradually with physician approval. Plyometric training is not recommended initially for clients with heart disease.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1526
content: Osteopenia is a condition in which bone mineral density (BMD) is lower than normal and is considered a precursor to osteoporosis. Osteoporosis is a disease of bones in which BMD is reduced, bone microstructure is disrupted, and the actual proteins in bone are altered.
summary: Osteopenia is a condition of reduced bone mineral density, often preceding osteoporosis, which is a more severe bone disease characterized by reduced bone density, altered microstructure, and protein changes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1527
content: Resistance training should be performed in a seated or standing position.  Remember to have clients from this population exercise while seated or in a standing position.
summary: Exercise positioning recommendations for clients, emphasizing seated or standing positions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1528
content: Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model are appropriate for clients with heart disease. Programs for clients with heart disease should be performed in a circuit style or using the PHA training system.
summary: Suitable OPT phases and training styles for clients with heart disease.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1529
content: Osteopenia is a condition in which bone mineral density (BMD) is lower than normal and is considered a precursor to osteoporosis. Osteoporosis is a disease of bones in which BMD is reduced, bone microstructure is disrupted, and the actual proteins in bone are altered.
summary: Definitions of osteopenia and osteoporosis, highlighting their differences.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1530
content: There are two types or classes of osteoporosis: type 1 (primary) and type 2 (secondary). Primary osteoporosis is associated with normal aging and is attributable to a lower production of estrogen and progesterone, both of which are involved with regulating the rate at which bone is lost. Secondary osteoporosis is caused by certain medical conditions or medications that can disrupt normal bone reformation, including alcohol abuse, smoking, certain diseases, or certain medications.
summary: Types of osteoporosis, explaining their causes and distinctions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1531
content: Both types of osteoporosis are treatable and can occur in both men and women. Although the vast majority of clients with osteopenia and osteoporosis will be women, men can, in fact, have either of these diseases. Type 1 osteoporosis is most prevalent in postmenopausal women because of a deficiency in estrogen (usually secondary to menopause).
summary: Prevalence and impact of osteoporosis on both men and women, emphasizing type 1's association with menopause.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1532
content: Type 1 osteoporosis is characterized by an increase in bone resorption (removal of old bone) with a decrease in bone remodeling (formation of new bone), which leads to a decrease in bone mineral density. Osteoporosis commonly affects the neck of the femur and the lumbar vertebrae.
summary: Characteristics of type 1 osteoporosis and its common sites of impact.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1533
content: The neck of the femur and the lumbar vertebrae are considered part of the core and are located in the region of the body where the majority of all forces come together. A decrease in bone mineral density places the core in a weakened state and, thus, more susceptible to injury.
summary: Importance of the neck of the femur and lumbar vertebrae for core strength and vulnerability to injury with osteoporosis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1534
content: The risk of hip fractures doubles every 5 years in postmenopausal women older than the age of 50. In the United States today, an estimated 10 million individuals already have osteoporosis and nearly 34 million are estimated to have low bone mass. Osteoporosis affects more than 25 million people each year, resulting in approximately 1.5 million hip fractures.
summary: Prevalence and impact of osteoporosis, highlighting hip fracture risks.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1535
content: Of these 1.5 million hip fractures, only 20% of the patients return to a normal functional status.
summary: Limited recovery rates following hip fractures.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1536
content: Peak bone mass is the highest amount of bone mass a person is able to achieve during his or her lifetime. New bone formation (remodeling) occurs as the result of stress placed on the musculoskeletal system. To maintain consistent bone remodeling, people must remain active enough to ensure adequate stress is being placed on their bodies.
summary: Importance of peak bone mass and the role of stress in bone remodeling.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1537
content: This is especially important for adolescents and young adults trying to reach peak bone mass. Other risk factors for osteoporosis include a lack of physical activity, smoking, excess alcohol consumption, and low dietary calcium intake.
summary: Importance of physical activity and other lifestyle factors for bone health.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1538
content: Resistance training can increase bone mineral density. Resistance training has been shown to improve bone mineral density by no more than 5%. Some researchers believe that a 5% increase in bone mineral density is not enough to prevent fractures. It has been estimated that a 20% increase in bone mineral density is necessary to offset fractures.
summary: Effectiveness of resistance training for bone density, highlighting the need for greater increases to prevent fractures.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1539
content: Training that focuses on the prevention of falls, rather than strength alone, is more advantageous for the elderly. Exercise regimens that combine resistance training to increase bone mineral density with flexibility, core, and balance training to enhance proprioception might better facilitate the needs of the elderly.
summary: Exercise recommendations for the elderly, emphasizing fall prevention and multi-faceted training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1540
content: When using the OPT model with elderly clients, personal trainers must follow precautionary measures. If the client demonstrates the ability to move fairly well without assistance, the movement assessments may be followed (Overhead Squat, Single-Leg Squat or Balance, Push, Pull). If the client is not able to get around very well, it is a good idea to use more stable, machine-based equipment during the assessment process and often times during the exercise program.
summary: Adaptations to the OPT model for elderly clients, emphasizing safety and equipment choices.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1541
content: Follow the kinetic chain checkpoints as closely as possible with this population, but realize that there may be degenerative changes in their posture that cannot be corrected. Try and get clients to their own ideal position, not a general ideal position.
summary: Guidance on kinetic chain checkpoints and individualization for elderly clients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1542
content: Flexibility should be limited to static and active stretching. The use of self-myofascial release may be contraindicated for this population. Cardiorespiratory training should be done on a treadmill with handrail support.
summary: Specific exercise recommendations for elderly clients, including limitations on stretching and support during cardio.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1543
content: Try and get clients to their own ideal position, not a general ideal position. Remember to have clients from this population exercise while seated or in a standing position. Exercises should be progressed if possible toward free sitting (no support) or standing. Progressing exercises to the standing position will help increase stress to the hips, thighs, and back as well as increase the demand for balance.
summary: This chunk focuses on exercise positioning and progression for clients, emphasizing individualization and gradual advancement towards standing exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1544
content: Flexibility should be limited to static and active stretching. The use of self-myofascial release may be contraindicated for this population. Care should be taken with crunches or movements with a lot of spinal flexion. Avoid excessive spinal loading on squat and leg press exercises. Make sure the client is breathing in a normal manner and avoid holding breath as in a Valsalva maneuver.
summary: This chunk outlines specific exercise modifications and precautions for clients, emphasizing spinal safety and proper breathing techniques.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1545
content: Cardiorespiratory training should be done on a treadmill with handrail support. The frequency of cardiorespiratory training should be 2–5 days per week. The intensity of cardiorespiratory training should be 50–90% of maximal heart rate. Cardiorespiratory training should progress from stage I to stage II. The duration of cardiorespiratory training should be 20–60 minutes per day or 8- to 10-minute bouts. Progression to stage II cardiorespiratory training should be based on the physician’s advice and the client’s ability.
summary: This chunk details the parameters for cardiorespiratory training, including frequency, intensity, duration, and progression guidelines.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1546
content: Resistance training should consist of 1–3 sets of 8–20 repetitions at up to 85% on 2–3 days per week. Resistance training should be performed in a seated or standing position. Exercise training programs for clients in this population may be performed in a circuit-style or PHA training system (see chapter 13), focusing on hips, thighs, back, and arms.
summary: This chunk outlines the structure and focus of resistance training for clients, emphasizing proper form and exercise selection.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1547
content: Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model should be mastered before moving on. Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model will be appropriate for this population. If clients within this population are not progressed appropriately (following the OPT model), they may get injured and have a setback in their progress.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of following the OPT model for progression and injury prevention.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1548
content: Weight-bearing activities may be more beneficial to increasing bone mineral density. It appears that the load (rather than the number of repetitions) is the determining factor in bone formation (45). Research has indicated that higher intensities (75–85%) are needed to stimulate bone formation. It generally takes about 6 months of consistent exercise at relatively high intensities before any effect on bone mass is realized.
summary: This chunk highlights the role of weight-bearing activities and exercise intensity in promoting bone health.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1549
content: Stabilization training is also important to counter a lack of balance that can lead to falls and hip fractures.  Clients in this population, who can move fairly well without assistance, can perform many of the movement assessments discussed in chapter 6 (overhead squat, single-leg squat, pushing, pulling). Those not able to get around very well should use more stable, machine-based equipment to assess movement quality and often during the exercise program.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of balance training and exercise selection based on client mobility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1550
content: Osteopenia is a condition in which bone mineral density is lower than normal and is considered a precursor to osteoporosis. Osteoporosis is a disease of bones in which bone mineral density is reduced, bone microstructure is disrupted, and the actual proteins in bone are altered. Physical inactivity, smoking, excess alcohol consumption, and low dietary calcium intake are factors that contribute to the risk of osteoporosis. Clients should be encouraged to increase dietary intake of calcium, lower alcohol intake, and quit smoking. If a client has a diagnosis of osteoporosis, a physician must dictate to what degree he or she can engage in weight-bearing activities or resistance training. Exercise is designed to increase bone density and reduce the potential risk of future fractures. Exercise training programs that combine resistance training to increase bone mineral density with flexibility, core, and balance training to enhance proprioception are highly recommended for this population as long as they have been cleared by their physician and the preassessment indicates they can.
summary: This chunk provides a definition and explanation of osteopenia and osteoporosis, outlining risk factors and the role of exercise in prevention and management.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1551
content: Example core exercises in the standing position include performing prone iso-abs on an incline or cobras in a standing position. Other examples of core exercises include performing a standing cable torso iso-rotation or medicine ball rotations.
summary: This chunk provides examples of core exercises suitable for clients in a standing position.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1552
content: Exercise is designed to increase bone density and reduce the potential risk of future fractures. Exercise training programs that combine resistance training to increase bone mineral density with flexibility, core, and balance training to enhance proprioception are highly recommended for this population as long as they have been cleared by their physician and the preassessment indicates they can. Clients in this population, who can move fairly well without assistance, can perform many of the movement assessments discussed in chapter 6 (overhead squat, single-leg squat, pushing, pulling). Those not able to get around very well should use more stable, machine-based equipment to assess movement quality and often during the exercise program.
summary: Exercise benefits bone density and should be tailored to individual ability, incorporating resistance training, flexibility, core, and balance exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1553
content: If a client has a diagnosis of osteoporosis, a physician must dictate to what degree he or she can engage in weight-bearing activities or resistance training. Kinetic chain checkpoints should be followed, taking into consideration that degenerative deformations in posture may not be able to be corrected. Exercises should be performed in a seated or standing position. Flexibility exercises should be limited to static and active stretching in a seated or standing position. Self-myofascial release may be contraindicated for this population.
summary: Exercise for osteoporosis requires physician guidance, focusing on safe positions and avoiding aggressive techniques.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1554
content: Cardiorespiratory training should focus on stage I (with a walking program, if tolerated). Progression to stage II cardiorespiratory training should be based on a physician’s advice and client’s ability. Care should be taken with crunches or movements with a lot of spinal flexion. Plyometric training would not be recommended for this population. Resistance training should be performed in a seated or standing position. Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model are appropriate. Six months of consistent exercise at high intensities, progressed appropriately, will be required for training to have an effect on bone mass. Programs should be performed in a circuit style or using the PHA training system, focusing on hips, thighs, back, and arms and progressing exercises to the standing position.
summary: Cardio and resistance training should be tailored to the individual, prioritizing safety and gradual progression.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1555
content: Arthritis is an inflammatory condition that mainly affects the joints of the body. Arthritis is the leading cause of disability among U.S. adults, and is also associated with significant activity limitation, work disability, reduced quality of life, and high healthcare costs. Currently an estimated 21.6% of the adult U.S. population (46.4 million individuals) have arthritis. Two of the most common types of arthritis are osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis. Osteoarthritis is caused by degeneration of cartilage within joints. This lack of cartilage creates a wearing on the surfaces of articulating bones, causing inflammation and pain at the joint. Some of the most commonly affected joints are in the hands, knees, hips, and spine. Rheumatoid arthritis is a degenerative joint disease in which the body’s immune system mistakenly attacks its own tissue (in this case, tissue in the joint or organs). This can cause an inflammatory response in multiple joints, leading to pain and stiffness. The condition is systemic and may affect both a variety of joints and organ systems. Joints most commonly affected by this condition include the hands, feet, wrists, and knees. It is usually characterized by morning stiffness, lasting more than a half hour, which can be both acute and chronic, with eventual loss of joint integrity.
summary: Arthritis is a prevalent condition affecting millions, with osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis being the most common types.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1556
content: It is important for personal trainers to understand the difference between rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis, and be aware of the signs and symptoms of an acute rheumatoid arthritis exacerbation. In the presence of an arthritic flare-up, even flexibility exercises may need to be postponed. Personal trainers should also monitor the progress of clients with arthritis to assess the effects of the exercise program on joint pain. Pain persisting for more than 1 hour after exercise is an indication that the exercise should be modified or eliminated from the routine. Exercises of higher intensity or involving high repetitions are to be avoided to decrease joint aggravation. In that regard, a low-volume circuit program or multiple session format is suitable for clients with arthritis. Health and fitness professionals need to be aware of the medications being taken by clients with arthritis. Clients taking oral corticosteroids, particularly over time, may have osteoporosis, increased body mass, and, if there is a history of gastrointestinal bleeding, anemia. Steroids also increase fracture risk. Research indicates that people exhibiting osteoarthritis have a decrease in strength and proprioception. Research has shown that individuals with arthritis have a decreased ability to balance while
summary: Personal trainers should understand arthritis types, monitor client progress, and modify exercises to minimize joint stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1557
content: Arthritis is an inflammatory condition that mainly affects the joints of the body. Two of the most common types of arthritis are osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis. Rheumatoid arthritis results, in particular, in early morning stiffness. Avoid early morning exercise for clients with rheumatoid arthritis.
summary: Arthritis, particularly rheumatoid arthritis, affects joints and may cause morning stiffness, necessitating avoidance of early morning exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1558
content: Health and fitness professionals need to be aware of the medications being taken by clients with arthritis. Medications may significantly influence bone and muscle health. Clients taking oral corticosteroids, particularly over time, may have osteoporosis, increased body mass, and, if there is a history of gastrointestinal bleeding, anemia. Steroids also increase fracture risk.
summary: Health professionals should be aware of medication influences on clients with arthritis, as some medications can negatively impact bone and muscle health.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1559
content: Research indicates that people exhibiting osteoarthritis have a decrease in strength and proprioception. Research has shown that individuals with arthritis have a decreased ability to balance while. Tolerance to exercise may be influenced by acute arthritic flare-ups. Awareness of the signs and symptoms that may be associated with acute arthritic flare-ups should dictate a cessation or alteration of training, and joint pain persisting for more than 1 hour should result in an altered exercise format.
summary: Individuals with osteoarthritis often experience decreased strength, proprioception, and balance. Exercise tolerance can be affected by flare-ups, requiring adjustments based on pain levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1560
content: Maximal oxygen uptake and ventilatory threshold are frequently lower as a result of decreased exercise associated with pain and joint inflammation.
summary: Reduced exercise due to pain and inflammation can lead to lower maximal oxygen uptake and ventilatory threshold.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1561
content: Multiple sessions or a circuit format, using a treadmill, elliptical trainer, or arm and leg cycles, are a better alternative than higher-intensity, single-modality exercise formats. The usual principles for aerobic exercise training apply (60–80% peak work capacity, 3–5 days per week). Duration of exercise should be an accumulated 30 minutes, following an intermittent or circuit format, 3–5 days per week.
summary: For individuals with arthritis, multiple sessions or circuit training with low-impact equipment are recommended, adhering to general aerobic exercise guidelines.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1562
content: Incorporate functional activities in the exercise program whenever possible. Performing exercises in the seated and standing positions can increase functional capacity and balance. Core and balance exercises are important for this population to increase levels of joint stability and balance.
summary: Exercise programs should incorporate functional activities, focusing on balance and core strength to improve joint stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1563
content: Evaluate for the presence of comorbidities, particularly osteoporosis. Resistive exercise training is recommended, as tolerable, using pain as a guide. Start with a very low number of repetitions and gradually increase to the number usually associated with improved muscular fitness (e.g., 10–12 reps, before increasing weight, 1 set of 8–10 exercises, 2–3 days per week.)
summary: Assess for comorbidities like osteoporosis and recommend resistive exercise, starting with low repetitions and gradually increasing intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1564
content: Basic exercise guidelines for individuals with arthritis include treadmill walking, stationary cycling, rowers, and low-impact or step aerobics. The frequency of exercise for individuals with arthritis is 3–5 days per week. The intensity of cardiorespiratory training for individuals with arthritis is 60–80% of maximal heart rate. Stage I cardiorespiratory training may progress to stage II (may be reduced to 40–70% of maximal heart rate if needed). The duration of cardiorespiratory training for individuals with arthritis is 30 minutes.
summary: Recommended exercises for individuals with arthritis include low-impact activities, performed 3-5 days a week at 60-80% of maximum heart rate for 30 minutes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1565
content: Assessments for individuals with arthritis include push, pull, overhead squat, single-leg balance or single-leg squat (if tolerated). Flexibility exercises for individuals with arthritis include SMR and static and active stretching. Resistance training for individuals with arthritis consists of 1–3 sets of 10–12 repetitions 2–3 days per week. Individuals with arthritis may use a circuit or PHA training system.
summary: Assessments and exercise programs for individuals with arthritis should include strength, balance, flexibility, and resistance training, tailored to their capabilities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1566
content: Special considerations for individuals with arthritis include avoiding heavy lifting and high repetitions, staying in pain-free ranges of motion, and only using SMR if tolerated by the client. There may be a need to start out with only 5 minutes of exercise and progressively increase, depending on the severity of conditions.
summary: Exercise for individuals with arthritis should prioritize pain-free movement, avoid heavy lifting, and gradually increase duration and intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1567
content: Plyometric training is not recommended for arthritic clients.
summary: Plyometric training is contraindicated for individuals with arthritis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1568
content: Phase 1 of the OPT model will be used for this population with modified repetitions (10 to 12) to avoid heavy, repetitive joint loading that increases stress to the affected joints.
