summary: The OPT model, specifically Phase 1, is adapted for individuals with arthritis, using modified repetitions to minimize joint stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1569
content: Osteoarthritis is caused by degeneration of cartilage in joints. Rheumatoid arthritis is a systemic, degenerative joint disease in which the body’s immune system mistakenly attacks its own tissues in the joint or organs, causing an inflammatory response leading to pain and stiffness. Improving muscle strength and enhancing flexibility through exercise can decrease arthritis symptoms.  Symptoms of arthritis (such as joint pain and stiffness) are heightened through inactivity as a result of muscle atrophy and lack of tissue flexibility.
summary: This chunk describes the two main types of arthritis, their causes, and how exercise can help manage symptoms.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1570
content: Individuals with arthritis may use a circuit or PHA training system. Resistance training for individuals with arthritis consists of 1–3 sets of 10–12 repetitions 2–3 days per week. There may be a need to start out with only 5 minutes of exercise and progressively increase, depending on the severity of conditions.  Exercises that cause pain to persist for more than 1 hour after exercise should be modified or eliminated from the routine.
summary: This chunk outlines exercise prescription guidelines for individuals with arthritis, emphasizing gradual progression and pain management.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1571
content: Special considerations for individuals with arthritis include avoiding heavy lifting and high repetitions, staying in pain-free ranges of motion, and only using SMR if tolerated by the client. Flexibility exercises for individuals with arthritis include SMR and static and active stretching. Flexibility exercises should improve muscle strength and enhance flexibility. Static and active forms of stretching can be used and may be better tolerated from a seated or standing position. The use of self-myofascial release can be used, if tolerated.
summary: This chunk details specific exercise modifications and considerations for individuals with arthritis, focusing on pain management and flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1572
content: Clients with osteoarthritis have a decrease in strength and proprioception and a loss of knee-extensor strength in some cases. Functional capacity and balance can be increased by progressing exercises so that they are performed in the seated position (without support) to a standing position. Core and balance exercises will be very important.
summary: This chunk highlights common physical limitations associated with osteoarthritis and emphasizes the importance of functional exercises for improving balance and core strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1573
content: Personal trainers need to be aware of the medications being taken by clients, especially oral corticosteroids and steroids.  Cardiorespiratory training should begin in stage I and may progress to stage II or stage III, depending on the client’s capabilities and a physician’s advice. Plyometric training is not recommended. Phase 1 will be used for this population with modified repetitions (10 to 12) to avoid heavy, repetitive joint loading that increases stress to the affected joints.  Cardiorespiratory training for this population is very important, but may have to start with 5 minutes of stage I training, progressing up to 30 minutes, 3 to 5 days per week.
summary: This chunk provides exercise prescription guidelines for individuals with arthritis, emphasizing medication awareness, progression, and avoidance of high-impact activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1574
content: Cancer is the second leading cause of death in the United States with more than one-half million deaths annually, behind cardiovascular disease. It has been estimated that American men have about a 44% probability and women have a 38% probability of developing cancer during their lifetime. Because of better detection and treatment strategies, those living with cancer have improved quality of life and life spans. In recent years there has been a variety of studies that have documented the positive benefits of exercise in the treatment of cancer, including improved aerobic and muscular fitness, retention of lean body mass, less fatigue, improved quality of life, and positive effects on mood and self-concept.
summary: This chunk provides general information about cancer prevalence, mortality, and the positive impact of exercise on cancer patients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1575
content: Because cancer is not a single disease, but a collection of diseases that share the same description (with respect to cell division, accumulation, and death), its signs and symptoms vary widely. Medications used by clients with cancer can result in substantial adverse effects, including peripheral nerve damage, cardiac and pulmonary problems, skeletal muscle myopathy (muscle weakness and wasting), and anemia, as well as frequent nausea. In addition, the combination of the disease and its treatments frequently result in a diminished quality of life. Exercise is an important intervention for clients recovering from cancer. Exercise can improve exercise tolerance, reduce the cellular risks associated with cancer, and also improve quality of life.
summary: This chunk discusses the heterogeneity of cancer, its treatment side effects, and the importance of exercise for cancer survivors.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1576
content: Exercise at low to moderate intensities for moderate durations appears to have a more positive effect on the immune system when compared with higher intensities for longer durations (55). Research indicates that moderate to high levels of physical activity seem to be associated with decreased incidence and mortality rates for certain forms of cancer (56). Exercise programs for clients recovering from cancer should follow the OPT model. Assessment procedures for individuals with cancer can follow the guidelines in chapter 6. The specific push, pull, and overhead squat assessments should be representative of the client’s ability level. A single-leg balance assessment would also be advised if a single-leg squat cannot be performed by the client. Flexibility should include static and active stretching. Self-myofascial release can be used if no complications exist that would prevent its use. Self-myofascial release is not recommended for clients receiving chemotherapy or radiation treatments.
summary: This chunk outlines exercise recommendations and assessment procedures for cancer survivors, emphasizing the importance of the OPT model and individualized programming.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1577
content: Cancer is the second leading cause of death in the United States, although the number of individuals living with cancer has increased substantially. There are several positive benefits of exercise in the treatment of cancer. Exercise is an important intervention in terms of improving exercise tolerance, reducing cellular risks associated with cancer, and improving quality of life.
summary: Exercise offers numerous benefits for cancer patients, including improved exercise tolerance, reduced cancer risks, and enhanced quality of life.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1578
content: Medications used by clients with cancer can result in substantial adverse effects. The combination of the disease and its treatments frequently result in a diminished quality of life. The adverse effects of the treatments for cancer can be substantially greater than the treatments prescribed for most other chronic diseases.
summary: Cancer treatments, including medications, can significantly impact quality of life due to adverse effects that often exceed those of other chronic diseases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1579
content: For clients recovering from cancer, exercise that follows the OPT model at low to moderate intensities for moderate durations has a positive effect on the immune system. Aerobic exercise should be done at low-moderate intensity (40–50% of peak capacity), 3–5 days per week, using typical aerobic modes (treadmill, elliptical trainer, cycle, depending on patient preference). In particular, avoid higher-intensity training during periods of cancer treatment.
summary: Low to moderate intensity exercise, following the OPT model, is beneficial for cancer recovery, boosting the immune system.  Avoid high-intensity training during treatment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1580
content: Excessive fatigue may result in overall diminished activity. Use intermittent bouts of exercise to accumulate 20–30 minutes of total aerobic exercise.
summary: Manage fatigue by using intermittent exercise bouts to achieve 20-30 minutes of aerobic activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1581
content: Resistance training can be performed (1 set of 8–10 exercises, 10–15 repetitions to fatigue, 2–3 days per week).
summary: Resistance training involves 1 set of 8-10 exercises, performed with 10-15 repetitions to fatigue, 2-3 days per week.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1582
content: Assess and provide intervention for decreased range of motion and balance. A single-leg balance assessment would also be advised if the client is capable.  Flexibility should include static and active stretching. Self-myofascial release can be used if no complications exist that would prevent its use such as chemotherapy or radiation treatments.
summary: Assess and address range of motion and balance limitations. Include static and active stretching, and consider self-myofascial release if no contraindications exist.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1583
content: Cardiorespiratory training for this population is very important and may have to start with 5 minutes of stage I training, progressing up to 30 minutes, 3 to 5 days per week. Stage II or stage III training may be used on agreement of the client’s physician. Core and balance exercises are essential for this population.
summary: Cardiorespiratory training is crucial, starting gradually and progressing to 30 minutes, 3-5 days per week. Stage II or III training may be used with physician approval. Core and balance exercises are essential.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1584
content: Clients should be progressed slowly using the stabilization, strength, and power continuums. Plyometric training is not recommended until the client has progressed to three phase 1 workouts per week. Resistance training for this population will include phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model. Other phases may be used as the client progresses and are approved by his or her physician.
summary: Progress clients gradually through stabilization, strength, and power phases. Plyometrics are introduced after consistent phase 1 workouts. Resistance training follows phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model, with additional phases as appropriate.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1585
content: The physiologic differences during exercise between men and women have been well documented. The majority of observed disparities in athletic performance between men and women are explained by differences in body structure, muscle mass, lean to fat body mass ratio, and, to a lesser extent, blood chemistry. When measures are adjusted for body composition, both physiologic and performance parameters narrow considerably or completely vanish.
summary: Physiological differences between men and women during exercise are largely due to body composition, with performance disparities narrowing when adjusted for body composition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1586
content: There has been substantial research documenting the beneficial effects of exercise during pregnancy on the physiology and health of both the mother and developing fetus.
summary: Exercise during pregnancy offers numerous benefits for both the mother and the developing fetus.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1587
content: The physiologic differences during exercise between men and women have been well documented. The majority of observed disparities in athletic performance between men and women are explained by differences in body structure, muscle mass, lean to fat body mass ratio, and, to a lesser extent, blood chemistry. When measures are adjusted for body composition, both physiologic and performance parameters narrow considerably or completely vanish.
summary: Athletic performance differences between men and women are primarily due to body composition, but physiological parameters converge when adjusted for body composition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1588
content: There has been substantial research documenting the beneficial effects of exercise during pregnancy on the physiology and health of both the mother and developing fetus. Fears that the fetus may be harmed by increased blood circulation, thermoregulatory changes, or decreased oxygen supply can be minimized with appropriate precautions. The general consensus is that most recreational pursuits are appropriate for all pregnant women.
summary: Exercise during pregnancy is beneficial for both mother and fetus, with precautions minimizing potential risks.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1589
content: Those already engaged in an exercise program before pregnancy may continue with moderate levels of exercise until the third trimester, when a logical reduction in activity is recommended. The gradual growth of the fetus can alter the posture of pregnant women, making flexibility and core training important, particularly core-stabilization exercises to improve strength of the pelvic floor musculature.
summary: Pregnant women can continue moderate exercise until the third trimester, focusing on flexibility and core strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1590
content: As the mother-to-be progresses to the more advanced stages of pregnancy (second and third trimesters, or after 12 weeks), performing exercises in a prone (on stomach) or supine (on back) position, as well as uncontrolled twisting motions of the torso, is not advised. Certain resistance exercises such as hip abduction and hip adduction machines are not advised.
summary: Exercise modifications are necessary in later pregnancy, avoiding prone, supine, and uncontrolled twisting positions, and certain resistance exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1591
content: Changes also occur in the cardiovascular system, decreasing work capacity and leading to necessary alterations in the cardiorespiratory program and the increased importance of proper hydration during aerobic activity. Personal trainers should be aware that women in the childbearing period are vulnerable to nausea, dizziness, and fainting.
summary: Pregnancy affects cardiovascular function, requiring adjustments to exercise programs and increased hydration, while trainers should be aware of potential dizziness and fainting.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1592
content: Pregnant women should immediately stop exercising if they experience any of these symptoms along with any abdominal pain (or contractions), excessive shortness of breath, or bleeding or leakage of amniotic fluid.
summary: Pregnant women should stop exercising immediately if experiencing dizziness, nausea, fainting, abdominal pain, shortness of breath, bleeding, or amniotic fluid leakage.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1593
content: During the postpartum period, there may be a tendency to rush an exercise program in an effort to return to pre-pregnancy physiologic and morphologic status. Pregnancy may persist for a month to a month and a half. Ideally, postnatal women should be encouraged to reeducate posture, joint alignment, muscle imbalances, stability, motor skills, and recruitment of the deep core stabilizers such as the transverse abdominis, internal oblique, and pelvic floor musculature.
summary: Postpartum exercise should be gradual, focusing on posture, joint alignment, muscle imbalances, stability, and core strengthening.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1594
content: A return to a more vigorous program should be deferred and entered into gradually. Personal trainers should follow the assessment guidelines in chapter 6, using seated and standing assessments. A single-leg squat assessment may be performed if a woman is capable. If a woman is not capable of a single-leg squat, a single-leg balance assessment may be more appropriate.
summary: Postpartum exercise progression should be gradual, with assessments tailored to individual capabilities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1595
content: Flexibility exercises should be performed in seated and standing positions, especially in the second and third trimesters. Static and active stretching should be used, and self-myofascial release may also be used as tolerated. Self-myofascial release should not be performed on varicose veins that are sore, or on areas where there is swelling (such as the calves). During more advanced stages of pregnancy (second and third trimesters), performing self-myofascial release exercises in prone or supine positions is not advised.
summary: Flexibility exercises, including stretching and self-myofascial release, are important during pregnancy, with modifications for later stages.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1596
content: Cardiorespiratory training should consist primarily of stage I and only enter stage II on a physician’s advice. Women who have not exercised before pregnancy can begin with 15 minutes of continuous aerobic activity and gradually progress to 30 minutes of low to moderate aerobic activity. Plyometric training is not advised for this population after the first trimester.
summary: Cardiorespiratory training should be gradual, starting with low intensity and avoiding plyometrics after the first trimester.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1597
content: Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model may be used in the first trimester; however, in the second and third trimesters the use of only phase 1 is advised. Appropriate precautions can minimize the risks of exercise during pregnancy, including increased blood circulation, thermoregulatory changes, or decreased oxygen supply.
summary: Exercise programming should adapt to pregnancy stages, with Phase 1 of the OPT model recommended for later trimesters.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1598
content: Most recreational pursuits are appropriate for all pregnant women, and moderate levels of exercise are encouraged until the third trimester. In the postpartum period, a return to a more vigorous program should be entered into gradually. Postpartum exercise should be similar to pregnancy guidelines, as physiologic changes that occur during pregnancy may persist for up to 6 weeks.
summary: Exercise recommendations for pregnant and postpartum women emphasize moderation and gradual progression.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1599
content: Contraindications include persistent bleeding (2nd to 3rd trimester), medical documentation of incompetent cervix or intrauterine growth retardation, pregnancy-induced hypertension, preterm rupture of membrane, or preterm labor during current or prior pregnancy. Screen carefully for potential contraindications to exercise.
summary: Exercise should be avoided in pregnant women with certain medical conditions, requiring careful screening for contraindications.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1600
content: Decreased oxygen is available for aerobic exercise. Low-moderate intensity aerobic exercise (40–50% of peak work capacity) should be performed 3–5 days per week, emphasizing non–weight-bearing exercise (e.g., swimming, cycling), although certain treadmill or elliptical training modes may be preferred and are appropriate. Posture can affect blood flow to the uterus during vigorous exercise. Avoid supine exercise, particularly after the first trimester. Even in the absence of exercise, pregnancy may increase metabolic demand by 300 kcal per day to maintain energy balance.
summary: Aerobic exercise should be low-moderate intensity, non-weight bearing, and avoided in supine positions after the first trimester. Pregnancy increases metabolic demand even without exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1601
content: Decreased oxygen is available for aerobic exercise.  Even in the absence of exercise, pregnancy may increase metabolic demand by 300 kcal per day to maintain energy balance.  Advise adequate caloric intake to offset exercise effect.
summary: Pregnancy increases metabolic demand, and exercise can further reduce oxygen availability.  Adequate caloric intake is crucial.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1602
content: Posture can affect blood flow to the uterus during vigorous exercise. Avoid supine exercise, particularly after the first trimester.
summary: Pregnancy exercise should avoid supine positions and consider posture's impact on blood flow.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1603
content: Low-moderate intensity aerobic exercise (40–50% of peak work capacity) should be performed 3–5 days per week, emphasizing non–weight-bearing exercise (e.g., swimming, cycling), although certain treadmill or elliptical training modes may be preferred and are appropriate.
summary: Aerobic exercise prescription for pregnant individuals: low-moderate intensity, 3-5 days/week, emphasizing non-weight-bearing activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1604
content: There are no published guidelines for resistance, flexibility, or balance training specific to pregnancy exercise. Provided exercise intensity is below the aerobic prescription of 40–50% of peak work capacity, with careful attention to special considerations and contraindications described, adding these components may be helpful.
summary: Limited guidelines exist for resistance, flexibility, and balance training during pregnancy.  These components may be beneficial if intensity is controlled.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1605
content: For resistance training, if cleared by the physician, a circuit-training format is recommended, 1–3 sets of 12–15 reps per exercise, emphasizing breathing control and rest, as needed, between sets.
summary: Resistance training during pregnancy should be circuit-based, with 1-3 sets of 12-15 reps, emphasizing breathing control and rest.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1606
content: Advise clothing that will dissipate heat easily during exercise.
summary: Clothing for exercise during pregnancy should facilitate heat dissipation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1607
content: Mode: Low-impact or step aerobics that avoid jarring motions, treadmill walking, stationary cycling, and water activity.
summary: Recommended exercise modes for pregnancy include low-impact options like walking, cycling, and water activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1608
content: Frequency: 3–5 days per week. Intensity: Stage I and only enter stage II on a physician’s advice. Duration: 15–30 minutes per day. There may be a need to start out with only 5 minutes of exercise and progressively increase to 30 minutes, depending on the severity of conditions.
summary: Exercise frequency, intensity, and duration recommendations for pregnancy: 3-5 days/week, stage I intensity, 15-30 minutes per day, with gradual progression.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1609
content: Assessment: Push, pull, overhead squat, single-leg squat or balance.
summary: Assessments for pregnant individuals include push, pull, overhead squat, and single-leg exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1610
content: Flexibility: Static, active stretching and SMR. Resistance training: 2–3 days per week, using light loads at 12–15 repetitions.
summary: Flexibility training should include static and active stretching, and resistance training should be 2-3 days/week with light loads and 12-15 reps.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1611
content: Phases: 1 and 2 of the OPT model are advised (use only phase 1 after the first trimester). Special considerations: Avoid exercises in a prone (on stomach) or supine (on back) position after 12 weeks of pregnancy. Avoid SMR on varicose veins and areas of swelling.
summary: Exercise phases and considerations for pregnancy: OPT phases 1 and 2 (phase 1 only after 1st trimester), avoid prone/supine positions after 12 weeks, and avoid SMR on varicose veins or swelling.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1612
content: Plyometric training is not advised in the second and third trimesters.
summary: Plyometric training is contraindicated in the second and third trimesters of pregnancy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1613
content: Personal trainers should follow the assessment guidelines in chapter 6, using seated or standing assessments. A single-leg squat assessment may be performed depending on the trimester. In the second and third trimester, a single-leg balance assessment may be more appropriate.
summary: Assessment guidelines for pregnant individuals: seated or standing assessments, single-leg squat depending on trimester, single-leg balance in later trimesters.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1614
content: Flexibility exercises should be performed in the seated or standing position, especially in the second and third trimesters. Static and active stretching should be used. Self-myofascial release can be used as long as the client avoids using the foam roll on varicose veins or anywhere there is swelling.
summary: Flexibility exercises during pregnancy should be seated or standing, utilizing static and active stretching. SMR is permitted with precautions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1615
content: Cardiorespiratory training should consist primarily of stage I. Plyometric training is not advised for this population in the second and third trimesters. Phases 1 and 2 of the OPT model may be used in the first trimester. In the second and third trimesters only phase 1 is advised.
summary: Cardiorespiratory training should be primarily stage I, and plyometrics are contraindicated in later trimesters. OPT phases 1 and 2 are appropriate in the first trimester, with only phase 1 in later trimesters.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1616
content: Smoking has progressively declined during the last few decades; however, it is still one of the leading preventable causes of death and a primary risk factor for the development of chronic lung diseases. Chronic lung disease is largely broken into two major categories, obstructive and restrictive.
summary: Smoking remains a major health risk, contributing to chronic lung diseases categorized as obstructive or restrictive.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1617
content: In restrictive lung disease or disorders, lung tissue may be fibrotic and, thus, dysfunctional (as in the case of pulmonary fibrosis or asbestosis). In restrictive lung disease the ability to expand the lungs may be decreased as a result of any number of causes (such as fractured ribs, a neuromuscular disease, or even obesity).
summary: Restrictive lung diseases involve impaired lung expansion due to fibrosis or other factors like fractured ribs, neuromuscular conditions, or obesity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1618
content: In chronic obstructive lung disease, the lung tissue may be normal, but airflow is restricted. The major obstructive lung diseases include asthma, chronic bronchitis, and emphysema. These diseases are characterized by chronic inflammation (caused primarily by smoking, although in the case of asthma may be caused by environmental irritants) and airway obstruction via mucus production.
summary: Obstructive lung diseases, like asthma, bronchitis, and emphysema, involve airflow restriction despite normal lung tissue.  Inflammation and mucus production are key features.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1619
content: Cystic fibrosis is another disease that is characterized by excessive mucus production, but is instead a genetic disorder.
summary: Cystic fibrosis is a genetic disorder characterized by excessive mucus production, distinct from obstructive lung diseases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1620
content: In restrictive lung disease the ability to expand the lungs may be decreased as a result of any number of causes (such as fractured ribs, a neuromuscular disease, or even obesity). In restrictive disease or disorders, lung tissue may be fibrotic and the ability to expand the lungs may be decreased owing to any number of causes.
summary: Restrictive lung disease is characterized by decreased lung expansion due to various factors, including fibrosis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1621
content: In chronic obstructive lung disease, the lung tissue may be normal, but airflow is restricted. In obstructive lung disease, the lung tissue may be normal, but airflow is restricted. These diseases are characterized by chronic inflammation (caused primarily by smoking, although in the case of asthma may be caused by environmental irritants) and airway obstruction via mucus production.
summary: Obstructive lung diseases involve airflow restriction, often due to inflammation and mucus buildup.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1622
content: The major obstructive lung diseases include asthma, chronic bronchitis, and emphysema. Cystic fibrosis is another disease that is characterized by excessive mucus production, but is instead a genetic disorder.
summary: Asthma, chronic bronchitis, emphysema, and cystic fibrosis are examples of obstructive lung diseases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1623
content: The impairments during exercise are similar with regard to both restrictive and obstructive lung disease. Problems include decreased ventilation and decreased gas exchange ability (resulting in decreased aerobic capacity and endurance and in oxygen desaturation). Clients with lung disease experience fatigue at low levels of exercise and often have shortness of breath (or dyspnea).
summary: Both restrictive and obstructive lung diseases impair exercise performance due to decreased ventilation and gas exchange, leading to fatigue and shortness of breath.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1624
content: Those with emphysema are frequently underweight and may exhibit overall muscle wasting with hypertrophied neck muscles (which are excessively used to assist in labored breathing). Those with chronic bronchitis may be the opposite: overweight and barrel-chested.
summary: Individuals with emphysema often experience muscle wasting and shortness of breath, while those with chronic bronchitis may be overweight and have a barrel chest.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1625
content: In general, exercise for these clients is similar to what would be appropriate for the general population. Exercise can improve functional capacity and decrease the symptoms of dyspnea in this population, among many other physiological and psychological benefits.
summary: Exercise recommendations for individuals with lung disease are generally similar to those for the general population, with potential benefits for functional capacity and dyspnea.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1626
content: The use of lower body cardiorespiratory and resistance training exercises seem to be best tolerated. Upper extremity exercises place an increased stress on the secondary respiratory muscles that are involved in stabilizing the upper extremities during exercise.
summary: Lower body exercises are generally better tolerated than upper body exercises for individuals with lung disease.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1627
content: Therefore, caution should be used when designing programs for this population to ensure adequate rest intervals. The use of the Peripheral Heart Action (PHA) training system would be advised.
summary: Exercise programs for individuals with lung disease should include adequate rest intervals and consider the use of PHA training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1628
content: Health and fitness professionals working with clients who have lung disease should inquire about inspiratory muscle training to see whether it might augment the general exercise program.
summary: Inspiratory muscle training may be beneficial for individuals with lung disease.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1629
content: For more information, read the comprehensive guidelines regarding the exercise assessment and training of individuals with lung disease published by the American Association of Cardiovascular and Pulmonary Rehabilitation (59).
summary: Refer to the guidelines published by the American Association of Cardiovascular and Pulmonary Rehabilitation for comprehensive information on exercise assessment and training for individuals with lung disease.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1630
content: Lung disease frequently is associated with other comorbidities, including cardiovascular disease. A decrease in the ability to exchange gas in the lungs may result in oxygen desaturation and marked dyspnea at low workloads.
summary: Lung disease often co-occurs with cardiovascular disease and can lead to oxygen desaturation and shortness of breath.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1631
content: If possible and properly trained, ascertain the level of oxygen saturation using a pulse oximeter. Pulse oximetry values should be above 90% and certainly above 85%. Values below this level are a contraindication to continued exercise, regardless of symptoms.
summary: Pulse oximetry should be used to monitor oxygen saturation during exercise, with values below 85% indicating a need to stop exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1632
content: Chronic deconditioning results in low aerobic fitness and decreased muscular performance. The aerobic exercise prescription should be guided by the client’s shortness of breath. Workloads of 40–60% of peak work capacity, 3–5 days per week, 20–45 minutes as tolerable, may be achievable.
summary: Exercise prescriptions for individuals with lung disease should consider their shortness of breath and aim for workloads of 40-60% of peak capacity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1633
content: Intermittent exercise with frequent rest breaks may be necessary to achieve sufficient overall exercise duration. Upper extremity exercise may result in an earlier onset of dyspnea and fatigue than expected, when compared with lower extremity exercise.
summary: Intermittent exercise with rest breaks may be necessary, and upper body exercises may be more taxing than lower body exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1634
content: Upper extremity exercise should be programmed carefully and modified, based on fatigue. Resistance training can be helpful; use conservative guidelines.
summary: Upper body exercises should be carefully programmed and modified based on fatigue, and resistance training should be approached conservatively.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1635
content: Circuit training in a PHA format is recommended (8–10 exercises, 1 set of 8–15 reps per exercise), emphasizing breathing control and rest as needed between sets. Clients may have significant muscle wasting and be of low body weight (with a BMI <18).
summary: Circuit training with PHA format is recommended, emphasizing breathing control and rest. Clients may be underweight and require dietary recommendations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1636
content: If the client is very thin, be certain to recommend adequate caloric intake to offset exercise effects. Clients may be using supplemental oxygen.
summary: Clients with lung disease may require dietary recommendations to address potential weight loss. Some clients may use supplemental oxygen.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1637
content: Trainers may not adjust oxygen flow during exercise; it is considered a medication. If a client experiences unusual dyspnea or has evidence of oxygen desaturation during exercise, stop exercise and consult with the client’s physician.
summary: Trainers should not adjust oxygen flow during exercise, as it is considered medication. Exercise should be stopped if unusual dyspnea or oxygen desaturation occurs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1638
content: Chronic lung disease is largely broken into two major categories, obstructive and restrictive. In restrictive disease or disorders, lung tissue may be fibrotic and the ability to expand the lungs may be decreased owing to any number of causes. In obstructive lung disease, the lung tissue may be normal, but airflow is restricted. Both types cause decreased ventilation and decreased gas exchange ability. Clients with lung disease are often short of breath (dyspnea) and fatigue at low levels of exercise. Exercise can improve functional capacity and decrease the symptoms of dyspnea. In some clients, inspiratory muscle training can specifically improve the work associated with breathing.
summary: Chronic lung disease, categorized as obstructive or restrictive, impairs ventilation and gas exchange, leading to dyspnea and fatigue. Exercise can improve function and reduce symptoms.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1639
content: In general, exercise for this population is similar to what would be appropriate for the general population. Lower body cardiorespiratory and resistance training exercises seem to be best tolerated. Adequate rest intervals need to be maintained. The Peripheral Heart Action training system is advised.
summary: Exercise for clients with lung disease generally follows general guidelines, emphasizing lower body exercises, adequate rest, and the Peripheral Heart Action system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1640
content: Intermittent claudication is the name for the manifestation of the symptoms caused by peripheral arterial disease (PAD). The term peripheral vascular disease is also commonly used to describe the activity-induced symptoms that characterize this disease. Essentially, intermittent claudication is characterized by limping, lameness, or pain in the lower leg during mild exercise resulting from a decrease in blood supply (oxygen) to the lower extremities. Peripheral arterial disease is characterized by narrowing of the major arteries that are responsible for supplying blood to the lower extremities. The primary limiting factor for exercise in the client with PAD is leg pain.
summary: Intermittent claudication, a symptom of peripheral arterial disease (PAD), involves leg pain during exercise due to reduced blood flow. PAD is characterized by narrowed arteries supplying the lower extremities, with leg pain being the primary exercise limitation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1641
content: If the client has a diagnosis of PAD, the symptoms are likely to be present. PAD patients frequently have coexisting coronary artery disease or diabetes. For clients with coexisting coronary artery disease, do not exceed the established heart rate upper limit (usually, this limit is established from a walking test, in which leg pain is the limiting factor). Switching modalities so that leg pain will not limit exercise may result in a higher—and possibly inappropriate—cardiac workload. If possible, a continuous format of exercise using walking is preferred.
summary: Clients with PAD often experience symptoms, and may have coexisting conditions like coronary artery disease. Exercise should be tailored to avoid exceeding heart rate limits and avoid modalities that mask leg pain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1642
content: Exercise duration should strive to be 20–30 total minutes, with continuous bouts of 10 minutes or greater, 3–5 days per week working up to every day. Smoking significantly worsens PAD and exercise tolerance. Strongly recommend smoking cessation. If a client continues to smoke, do not allow smoking for at least 1 hour before exercise. PAD frequently results in decreased aerobic capacity and endurance. Focus on aerobic exercise activities, with an emphasis on walking.
summary: Exercise for PAD clients should aim for 20-30 minutes, 3-5 days a week, with a focus on walking and smoking cessation. PAD often reduces aerobic capacity and endurance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1643
content: Resistance training may improve overall physical function, but may not address limitations of PAD. Resistance exercise should be complementary to but not substituted for aerobic exercise. A circuit-training format is recommended (e.g., 8–10 exercises, 1–3 sets of 8–12 reps per exercise, progressing up to 12–20 reps). An intermittent format of exercise may be necessary, with intensity guided by pain tolerance. Typical guidelines suggest exercise into moderate to severe discomfort, rest until subsided, and repeat until total exercise time is achieved (20–30 minutes).
summary: Resistance training can improve function but may not address PAD limitations. It should complement aerobic exercise, ideally using a circuit format. Exercise intensity should be guided by pain tolerance, with rest periods as needed.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1644
content: Always screen for comorbidities. Intermittent claudication exercises may still be associated with deconditioning. The personal trainer should consult with the client’s physician concerning intermittent claudication. If pain continues during exercise, the personal trainer must refer the client to a licensed physician immediately. The personal trainer should develop a training regimen that attempts to improve physical function in the face of limiting factors. In the case of peripheral vascular disease or deconditioning, the use of an intermittent format of exercise, with rest as necessary between exercise bouts, is similar.
summary: Trainers should screen for comorbidities, consult with physicians regarding PAD, and immediately refer clients with persistent pain. Training should focus on improving function despite limitations, using an intermittent format as needed.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1645
content: PAD is associated with coronary heart disease and diabetes. Health and fitness professionals should be aware of other existing comorbidities and be suspicious that these comorbidities may still exist, undiagnosed.
summary: PAD often coexists with coronary heart disease and diabetes. Professionals should be aware of potential undiagnosed comorbidities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1646
content: If a client experiences unusual dyspnea or has evidence of oxygen desaturation during exercise, stop exercise and consult with the client’s physician.
summary: If a client experiences unusual shortness of breath or oxygen desaturation during exercise, stop immediately and consult a physician.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1647
content: If pain continues during exercise, the personal trainer must refer the client to a licensed physician immediately.  Health and fitness professionals should be aware of other existing comorbidities and be suspicious that these comorbidities may still exist, undiagnosed. Physician clearance for exercise is necessary for the client with PAD.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of medical referral for persistent pain during exercise and the need for physician clearance, especially for clients with PAD.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1648
content: PAD is associated with coronary heart disease and diabetes. The primary limiting factor is leg pain. When there is increased activity of the leg muscles, PAD results in symptoms in which oxygen supply does not meet demand. Exercise for PAD should induce symptoms, causing a stimulus that increases local circulation. Intermittent claudication is the name for the manifestation of the symptoms caused by PAD.
summary: This chunk describes PAD, its association with other conditions, its symptoms, and the rationale for exercise in managing PAD.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1649
content: The personal trainer should develop a training regimen that attempts to improve physical function in the face of limiting factors. In the case of peripheral vascular disease or deconditioning, the use of an intermittent format of exercise, with rest as necessary between exercise bouts, is similar. Exercise in an intermittent format, with rest as necessary between exercise bouts, is recommended.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of personalized training regimens, particularly for clients with PAD or deconditioning, emphasizing an intermittent exercise approach.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1650
content: Exercise programming should follow the OPT methodology, using the suggested assessment process in chapter 6. The number of repetitions for movement assessments may have to be decreased depending on the client’s abilities. It will be important to make clients feel comfortable and competent with the assessment process to ensure compliance.
summary: This chunk outlines the recommended exercise programming framework (OPT methodology) and emphasizes the need for client-centered assessments and a supportive environment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1651
content: Static and active stretching should be used for this population. Guidelines for self-myofascial release are not known at this time, and it is suggested that it not be used in this population, unless approved by a licensed physician. Phase 1 of the OPT model is suggested.
summary: This chunk provides specific recommendations for stretching and self-myofascial release, suggesting Phase 1 of the OPT model for this population.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1652
content: Repetitions may need to start at 8 to 12 and slowly progress to 12 to 20. Exercise bouts may initially start with 5 to 10 minutes of activity and progress slowly to 20 to 30 minutes. Treadmill walking is preferred, also stationary cycling, steppers, and elliptical trainers.
summary: This chunk details the progression of repetitions and exercise duration, suggesting preferred exercise modalities for this population.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1653
content: Frequency is 3–5 days per week working up to every day. Intensity is 50–85% of maximal heart rate. Duration is work up to 20–30 minutes.
summary: This chunk outlines the recommended frequency, intensity, and duration for exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1654
content: Assessment includes push, pull, overhead squat, single-leg squat or balance. Flexibility includes static and active stretching. Resistance training is 1–3 sets of 8–12 repetitions 2–3 days per week, and slowly increasing up to 12–20 reps. Allow for sufficient rest between exercises. Workout may start with 5–10 minutes of activity. Slowly progress the client.
summary: This chunk provides specific details about assessments, flexibility, resistance training, and progression strategies.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1655
content: A study published by ScienceDaily on February 18, 2009, found an alarming increase in the prevalence of chronic low-back pain.
summary: This chunk presents a finding about the prevalence of chronic low-back pain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1656
content: Self-myofascial release is not recommended. Phase 1 of the OPT model should be used. Repetitions may need to start at 8 to 12 and slowly progress to 12 to 20. Exercise bouts may initially start with 5 to 10 minutes of activity and progress slowly to 20 to 30 minutes.
summary: This chunk outlines initial exercise recommendations, emphasizing a gradual progression in repetitions and exercise duration.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1657
content: A study published by ScienceDaily on February 18, 2009, found an alarming increase in the prevalence of chronic low-back pain. Ogden, Carroll, McDowell, and Flegal (2007) reported that there was no statistically significant change in obesity prevalence among adults in the United States between 2003-2004.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1658
content: Goble, Coxon, Wenderoth, Van Impe, and Swinnen (2009) researched proprioceptive sensibility in the elderly, focusing on degeneration, functional consequences, and plastic-adaptive processes. Hertling and Kessler (2006) authored the book 'Management of Common Musculoskeletal Disorders: Physical Therapy Principles and Methods'. Wescott and Baechle (1999) wrote the book 'Strength Training for Seniors'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1659
content: Humphries (2010) wrote an article titled 'Strength training for bone, muscle and hormones' for ACSM Current Comments. Johnston, De Lisio, and Parise (2008) explored the relationship between resistance training, sarcopenia, and the mitochondrial theory of aging. Roth, Ferrell, and Hurley (2000) examined the role of strength training in the prevention and treatment of sarcopenia.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1660
content: Evans and Rosenberg (1992) authored the book 'Biomarkers'. Meredith, Zackin, Frontera, and Evans (1987) studied body composition and aerobic capacity in young and middle-aged endurance-trained men.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1661
content: Drewnowski and Darmon (2005) investigated the economics of obesity, focusing on dietary energy density and energy cost. Slemenda, Heilman, Brandt, et al. (1998) found that reduced quadriceps strength relative to body weight may be a risk factor for knee osteoarthritis in women.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1662
content: Ogita, Stam, Tazawa, Toussaint, and Hollander (2000) researched oxygen uptake in one-legged and two-legged exercise. Lagally, Cordero, Good, Brown, and McCaw (2009) studied the physiological and metabolic responses to a continuous functional resistance exercise workout.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1663
content: Catenacci, Ogden, Stuht, et al. (2008) examined physical activity patterns in the National Weight Control Registry. Vaidya (2006) explored the psychosocial aspects of obesity.
summary: This chunk investigates physical activity patterns and the psychosocial factors associated with obesity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1664
content: According to the American Diabetes Association (2010), diabetes statistics are available on their website. The American Diabetes Association (2002) published guidelines on diabetes mellitus and exercise in Diabetes Care.
summary: This chunk highlights resources and guidelines related to diabetes and exercise from the American Diabetes Association.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1665
content: The Joint National Committee of Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Pressure (1997) released the sixth report on the prevention, detection, evaluation, and treatment of high blood pressure. Tsai, Yang, Wang, et al. (2004) investigated the beneficial effects of regular endurance exercise training on blood pressure and quality of life in patients with hypertension. Ketelhut, Franz, and Scholze (2004) examined the effectiveness of regular exercise as an approach in antihypertensive therapy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1666
content: The American Heart Association (2007) published 'Heart Disease and Stroke Statistics Update'. Ornish, Scherwitz, Billings, et al. (1998) conducted the Lifestyle Heart Trial, which showed that intensive lifestyle changes could reverse coronary heart disease. Pischke, Weidner, Scherwitz, and Ornish (2008) studied the long-term effects of lifestyle changes on well-being and cardiac variables among patients with coronary heart disease.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1667
content: Banks, Reeves, Beral, Balkwill, Liu, and Roddam (2009) analyzed the relationship between hip fracture incidence and age, menopausal status, and age at menopause. The National Osteoporosis Foundation (2010) provided fast facts on osteoporosis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1668
content: Layne and Nelson (1999) reviewed the effects of progressive resistance training on bone density. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (2006) reported on the prevalence of doctor-diagnosed arthritis and arthritis-attributable activity limitation in the United States (2003-2005.)
summary: This chunk focuses on the effects of exercise on bone density and the prevalence of arthritis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1669
content: Slemenda, Heilman, Brandt, et al. (1998) found that reduced quadriceps strength relative to body weight may be a risk factor for knee osteoarthritis in women. Slemenda, Brandt, Heilman, et al. (1997) studied quadriceps weakness and osteoarthritis of the knee. Hurley, Scott, Rees, and Newham (1997) examined sensorimotor changes and functional performance in patients with knee osteoarthritis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1670
content: O’Reilly, Jones, Muir, and Doherty (1998) investigated the effect of quadriceps weakness on pain and disability in patients with knee osteoarthritis. Pai, Rogers, Patton, Cain, and Hanke (1998) compared static and dynamic predictions of protective stepping following waist-pull perturbations in young and older adults. summary: This article focused on clinical aspects of peripheral arterial disease.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1682
content: A basic understanding of nutrition is vital to the safety and success of a personal trainer’s clients. An individually planned nutrition strategy can enhance the results from the stimulus of exercise. An individually planned nutrition strategy can improve health and athletic performance. An individually planned nutrition strategy can reduce the risk of disease and illness. An individually planned nutrition strategy can increase energy levels. An individually planned nutrition strategy can favorably alter body composition in clients.
summary: This section emphasizes the importance of nutrition knowledge for personal trainers and the benefits of individualized nutrition plans for clients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1683
content: The information presented in this chapter provides an understanding of the basics of nutrition and how nutrition can help apparently healthy clients achieve their personal health and fitness goals. It is important that personal trainers have a network of qualified health-care professionals (physicians, dieticians, eating-disorder specialists, and other health-care professionals) in their area to which they can refer clients with health or medical-related problems.
summary: This section highlights the role of nutrition in achieving fitness goals and the importance of referring clients to appropriate healthcare professionals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1684
content: Personal trainers should be familiar with the concepts of nutrition. Integrating nutritional strategies with exercise will help clients achieve their desired outcomes. It is important to recognize and respect the scope of practice of each professional field.
summary: This section stresses the importance of nutrition knowledge for personal trainers and the need to collaborate with other professionals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1685
content: The professional, legally qualified to practice in the field of nutrition, is a Registered Dietician (RD). The Registered Dietician is a specialized food and nutrition expert with extensive training who meets specified criteria. RDs receive their degrees at an accredited college or university with coursework approved by the Commission on Accreditation for Dietetics Education (CADE). RDs then complete a CADE-accredited program, typically 6 to 12 months in length, with focused practice and study in clinical and community nutrition, and food-service management. Graduate study is often combined. After completing the supervised practice program, candidates must pass the national examination to receive their RD credential. Individuals must complete continuing education requirements to maintain an active RD credential.
summary: This section defines the qualifications and training required to become a Registered Dietician (RD).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1686
content: The practice of nutrition, more formally called dietetics, is governed by national credentialing programs and state licensing laws. Currently, 46 states have specific laws that explicitly define the scope of practice for nutrition and dietetics professionals. Performing nutrition and dietetics duties without a license could be considered illegal.
summary: This section explains the legal framework governing the practice of nutrition and dietetics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1687
content: Some health-care professionals (other than Registered Dieticians) often make nutrition recommendations. These health-care professionals are usually licensed by the state as a nurse or physician and are thus protected regarding state laws and regulations. Personal trainers are not licensed health-care professionals (unless they have additional training and education) but often give nutrition advice to their clients.
summary: This section clarifies the scope of practice for different healthcare professionals regarding nutrition advice.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1688
content: It is important for all personal trainers to be aware of the standards of practice and scope of practice guidelines that pertain to them. A general understanding of basic nutrition and weight management should exist to educate clients and provide general guidance in this area. It is likely that a personal trainer will be the first person approached with nutrition questions. Being confident about the nature of the relationship between nutrition, fitness, and weight control is essential. Providing individual nutrition assessment, meal plans, or recommendations for nutritional therapy are best left to an RD or other qualified licensed professionals.
summary: This section emphasizes the importance of personal trainers understanding their scope of practice regarding nutrition and referring clients to qualified professionals for specialized advice.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1689
content: It is important for all personal trainers to be aware of the standards of practice and scope of practice guidelines that pertain to them. A general understanding of basic nutrition and weight management should exist to educate clients and provide general guidance in this area. It is likely that a personal trainer will be the first person approached with nutrition questions. Being confident about the nature of the relationship between nutrition, fitness, and weight control is essential.
summary: Personal trainers should understand their scope of practice, including basic nutrition, as clients often seek their guidance on these topics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1690
content: Providing individual nutrition assessment, meal plans, or recommendations for nutritional therapy are best left to an RD or other qualified licensed professionals. The skills and abilities required to calculate, counsel, or prescribe an individualized nutrition or weight-management plan exceed the training and expertise of the personal trainer (2). This becomes especially important if the client has health and medical concerns such as obesity, diabetes, heart disease, allergies, or hypertension.
summary: Personal trainers should refer clients seeking in-depth nutrition counseling to qualified professionals like registered dietitians.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1691
content: By 2015, public health experts expect that 75% of all US adults will be either overweight or obese (3). The desire for a quick solution for weight loss has fostered numerous myths about exercise and diet and has led to the overcommercialization of quick weight-loss methods, fad diets, and ineffective exercise programs and devices (4–6). It is important for personal trainers to understand what the common myths and inaccuracies about weight loss and diets are, and to be able to educate clients regarding safe and effective diet and weight-loss methods. The majority of common diet and weight-loss myths usually hinder more than help a client’s progress and may result in serious negative health consequences if they are not dispelled.
summary: The prevalence of obesity and weight loss myths necessitates that personal trainers educate clients on safe and effective weight management strategies.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1692
content: A calorie is a unit of energy defined as the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of 1 gram of water 1 degree Celsius. A Calorie or kilocalorie (kcal) is equal to 1,000 calories. Estimated total energy expenditure (TEE), also referred to as total daily energy expenditure (TDEE), is defined as the amount of energy (calories) spent, on average, in a typical day. TEE is the sum total of three different energy components: Resting metabolic rate (RMR), the thermic effect of food (TEF), and energy expended during physical activity.
summary: This chunk defines key terms related to energy balance: calorie, kilocalorie, total daily energy expenditure (TEE), resting metabolic rate (RMR), thermic effect of food (TEF), and energy expenditure during physical activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1693
content: RMR represents the minimal amount of energy required to sustain vital bodily functions such as blood circulation, respiration, and temperature regulation. RMR typically accounts for 70% of TEE. TEF is the amount of energy expended above RMR as a result of the processing of food (digestion) for storage and use. TEF typically accounts for approximately 6–10% of TEE. Energy expended during physical activity accounts for approximately 20% of TEE.
summary: This chunk details the three components of total daily energy expenditure (TEE): resting metabolic rate (RMR), thermic effect of food (TEF), and energy expenditure during physical activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1694
content: Eat fewer calories than are expended and there will be a reduction in weight. Consume more calories than are expended and there will be an increase in weight.
summary: This chunk explains the fundamental principle of energy balance: weight loss occurs when calorie expenditure exceeds calorie intake, and weight gain occurs when calorie intake exceeds calorie expenditure.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1695
content: The environment in which we live today provides a constant supply of palatable food (increasing energy intake) while promoting a sedentary lifestyle (reducing energy expenditure), which have played a significant role in the rising obesity rates in the United States.
summary: This chunk highlights the environmental factors contributing to the obesity epidemic: readily available calorie-dense foods and decreased physical activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1696
content: RMR accounts for approximately 70% of total daily energy expenditure in the sedentary person, although it may vary among individuals. RMR can be affected by a wide variety of factors including age, sex, genetics, hormonal changes, body size, body composition, temperature, altitude, illness, medication, food and caffeine intake, and cigarette smoking.
summary: This chunk describes resting metabolic rate (RMR) and the factors influencing its variability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1697
content: Constant factors that cannot be altered are age, sex, and genetics. Slight increases in fat-free mass can have a gradual increase in RMR; thus, exercise is said to have a positive effect on RMR indirectly if fat-free mass (FFM) is increased or decreased.
summary: This chunk focuses on the non-modifiable factors influencing RMR (age, sex, genetics) and the indirect effect of exercise on RMR through changes in fat-free mass.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1698
content: Factors that temporarily alter RMR include hormonal changes, exercise, environmental temperature, altitude, caffeine intake, and cigarette smoking.
summary: This chunk lists the temporary factors that can influence resting metabolic rate (RMR).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1699
content: Chronic or acute illness as well as hormonal changes and certain types of medications can influence RMR. Hormonal changes can increase or decrease RMR.
summary: This chunk explains how illness, hormonal fluctuations, and medications can affect resting metabolic rate (RMR).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1700
content: Thyroid hormones influence many metabolic functions throughout the body, including fat and carbohydrate metabolism and growth. Thyroid hormones have a constant effect on energy expenditure and affect every cell in the body. High concentrations of thyroid hormones tend to cause an increase in RMR, whereas lower than normal levels tend to cause a decrease in RMR.
summary: This chunk describes the role of thyroid hormones in regulating metabolism and energy expenditure.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1701
content: According to the American Association of Clinical Endocrinologists (AACE), 27 million Americans have thyroid-related disorders, but more than half remain undiagnosed. Another endocrine disorder, diabetes, is the seventh leading cause of death in the United States. The total cost of diagnosed diabetes in the United States in 2007 was $174 billion.
summary: This chunk highlights the prevalence of thyroid disorders and diabetes in the United States, emphasizing their significant health and economic impact.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1702
content: Certain medications have also been shown to alter RMR. Certain cardiovascular medications can reduce RMR from 4% to 12%, chemotherapy can reduce RMR 6% to 11%, long-term use of human growth hormone has been shown to increase RMR by 12%, and thyroid medications used in hypothyroidism can increase RMR as much as 17%.
summary: This chunk lists examples of medications that can influence resting metabolic rate (RMR).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1703
content: Energy expenditure can be elevated between 10 and 90 minutes after exercise depending on the intensity and duration. Regular chronic exercise may affect RMR slightly.
summary: This chunk explains the post-exercise elevation in energy expenditure and the potential long-term effect of regular exercise on resting metabolic rate (RMR).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1704
content: The total cost of diagnosed diabetes in the United States in 2007 was $174 billion.
summary: The total cost of diagnosed diabetes in the US in 2007 was $174 billion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1705
content: Certain medications have also been shown to alter RMR. Certain cardiovascular medications can reduce RMR from 4% to 12%, chemotherapy can reduce RMR 6% to 11%, long-term use of human growth hormone has been shown to increase RMR by 12%, and thyroid medications used in hypothyroidism can increase RMR as much as 17%.
summary: Various medications can influence resting metabolic rate (RMR), with some decreasing it and others increasing it.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1706
content: Energy expenditure can be elevated between 10 and 90 minutes after exercise depending on the intensity and duration. Regular chronic exercise may affect RMR slightly. Changes in FFM from exercise training can increase or decrease RMR.
summary: Exercise and changes in fat-free mass (FFM) can impact energy expenditure and resting metabolic rate (RMR).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1707
content: To avoid declines in resting metabolism individuals should be encouraged to avoid starvation diets that could lead to wasting of skeletal muscle and instead be encouraged to build and maintain muscle for active living. Maintaining muscle mass is particularly important during aging because some of the decline in RMR associated with age is caused by a decline of muscle.
summary: Building and maintaining muscle mass is crucial for preserving resting metabolic rate (RMR), especially as we age.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1708
content: The meal is called the thermic effect of food (TEF) and comprises approximately 6% to 10% of total energy expenditure depending on the frequency and energy content of the meal. Any physical activity requires energy expenditure above resting metabolism. Even sedentary people who only engage in modest daily physical activities consisting of personal care and other necessary daily movement expend energy above their RMR.
summary: The thermic effect of food (TEF) and physical activity contribute to total energy expenditure (TEE).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1709
content: Participating in sports and exercise can significantly increase energy expenditure above that of resting metabolism. Physical activity can account for 20% or more of an individual’s TEE, although the exact percentage can vary greatly depending on an individual’s current fitness level and the type, intensity, and duration of physical activity or exercise engaged in.
summary: Physical activity significantly increases energy expenditure, accounting for a substantial portion of total energy expenditure (TEE).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1710
content: Physical activity can be influenced more dramatically than RMR and TEF, which for the most part are relatively constant. One of the most common ways to estimate total energy expenditure is to first estimate RMR, then multiply RMR by an appropriate activity factor. There are several different prediction equations used to estimate RMR. Such simplified equations can provide a reasonable estimate of TEE in most cases.
summary: Total energy expenditure (TEE) can be estimated by considering resting metabolic rate (RMR), the thermic effect of food (TEF), and physical activity levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1711
content: The primary function of protein is to build and repair body tissues and structures. Protein is also involved in the synthesis of hormones, enzymes, and other regulatory peptides. Additionally, protein can be used for energy if calories or carbohydrate are insufficient in the diet.
summary: Protein's primary roles include building and repairing tissues, synthesizing hormones and enzymes, and providing energy when necessary.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1712
content: Proteins are made up of amino acids linked together by peptide bonds. The body uses approximately 20 amino acids to build its many different proteins. Just as specific words are formed by certain sequences of letters, arranging the amino acids in different sequences yields the body’s myriad of proteins.
summary: Proteins are composed of amino acids linked together, with different sequences creating diverse protein structures.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1713
content: There are two general classes of amino acids: essential and nonessential. The body cannot manufacture eight essential amino acids, therefore they must be obtained from the food supply or an exogenous source. The body can manufacture nonessential amino acids from dietary nitrogen and fragments of carbohydrate and fat.
summary: Amino acids are classified as essential (must be obtained from diet) or nonessential (synthesized by the body).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1714
content: Arginine and histidine are considered semiessential amino acids because the body cannot manufacture them at a rate that will support growth, especially in children.
summary: Arginine and histidine are semiessential amino acids, meaning the body cannot produce enough to support growth, particularly in children.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1715
content: Proteins must be broken down into constituent amino acids before the body can use them to build or repair tissue or as an energy substrate. The fate of amino acids after digestion and absorption by the intestines depends on the body’s homeostatic needs, which can range from tissue replacement or tissue addition to a need for energy.
summary: Proteins are broken down into amino acids, which are then utilized for various bodily functions, including tissue repair and energy production.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1716
content: As ingested proteins enter the stomach, they encounter hydrochloric acid (HCl), which uncoils (or denatures) the protein so that digestive enzymes can begin dismantling the peptide bonds. The enzyme pepsin begins to cleave the protein strand into smaller polypeptides (strands of several amino acids) and single amino acids.
summary: Protein digestion begins in the stomach with the action of hydrochloric acid and the enzyme pepsin.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1717
content: As these protein fragments leave the stomach and enter the small intestine, pancreatic and intestinal proteases (or protein enzymes) continue to dismantle the protein fragments. The resulting dipeptides, tripeptides, and single amino acids are then absorbed through the intestinal wall into the enterocytes and released into the blood supply to the liver.
summary: Further protein breakdown occurs in the small intestine, with the resulting amino acids being absorbed into the bloodstream.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1718
content: Once in the bloodstream, the free-form amino acids have several possible fates: they can be used for protein synthesis (building and repairing tissues or structures), immediate energy, or potential energy (fat storage).
summary: Absorbed amino acids can be used for protein synthesis, immediate energy, or stored as fat.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1719
content: The body has a constant need for energy, and the brain and nervous system, in particular, have a constant need for glucose. If carbohydrate or total energy intake is too low, the body has the ability to use amino acids (from dietary or body proteins) to provide energy.
summary: The body can utilize amino acids for energy when carbohydrate intake is insufficient.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1720
content: The amino acids are first deaminated (or stripped of the amine group), allowing the remaining carbon skeleton to be used for the production of glucose or ketones to be used for energy. The removed amine group produces ammonia, a toxic compound, which is converted to urea in the liver and excreted as urine by the kidneys.
summary: During amino acid breakdown for energy, the amine group is removed and converted to urea for excretion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1721
content: If protein intake exceeds the need for synthesis and energy needs are met, then amino acids from dietary protein are deaminated, and their carbon fragments may be stored as fat. Among Americans, protein and caloric intakes are typically well above requirements, allowing protein to contribute significantly to individuals’ fat stores.
summary: Excess protein intake can contribute to fat storage.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1722
content: Dietary protein is the delivery vehicle for amino acids. Meats, fruits, vegetables, grains, dairy products, and even supplements supply us with the valuable building blocks of protein we need. If a food supplies all of the essential amino acids in appropriate ratios, it is called a complete protein. If a food source is low or lacking in one or more essential amino acids, it is called an incomplete protein. The essential amino acid that is missing or present in the smallest amount is called the limiting factor of that protein. Because the process of protein synthesis works on an all-or-none principle, all amino acids must be present at the site of protein manufacture, or synthesis will be reduced to the point at which the cell runs out of the limiting amino acid.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of complete and incomplete proteins, emphasizing the importance of all essential amino acids for protein synthesis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1723
content: If carbohydrate or total energy intake is too low, the body has the ability to use amino acids (from dietary or body proteins) to provide energy. The amino acids are first deaminated (or stripped of the amine group), allowing the remaining carbon skeleton to be used for the production of glucose or ketones to be used for energy. The removed amine group produces ammonia, a toxic compound, which is converted to urea in the liver and excreted as urine by the kidneys. If protein intake exceeds the need for synthesis and energy needs are met, then amino acids from dietary protein are deaminated, and their carbon fragments may be stored as fat.
summary: This chunk describes how the body utilizes amino acids for energy production when carbohydrate intake is insufficient and the potential for amino acid conversion to fat.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1724
content: Among Americans, protein and caloric intakes are typically well above requirements, allowing protein to contribute significantly to individuals’ fat stores. The major sources of complete proteins are animal sources, dairy, and meats. Sources of incomplete protein include grains, legumes, nuts, seeds, and other vegetables. Barley, cornmeal, oats, buckwheat, pasta, rye, wheat, beans, lentils, dried peas, peanuts, chickpeas, soy products, sesame seeds, sunflower seeds, walnuts, cashews, pumpkin seeds, and other nuts are the main sources of incomplete proteins.
summary: This chunk discusses typical protein intake in America, highlighting the sources of complete and incomplete proteins.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1725
content: Incomplete proteins can be combined to make available all of the essential amino acids and form a complete protein. Protein quality improves when a small amount of complete protein like a dairy food is combined with plant-based foods and when incomplete proteins from plant-based foods such as rice and beans are mixed together.
