summary: This chunk explains how to combine incomplete proteins to create complete protein sources.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1726
content: There are several factors that affect protein requirements, including an individual’s daily exercise and physical activity levels, daily caloric consumption, body-composition goals, and sports-performance goals. Both anaerobic and aerobic exercise can affect protein requirements in different ways. Exercise increases the oxidation of amino acids as well as the rate of protein turnover in lean body mass during recovery. Because different types of exercise have specific effects, an individual participating in both types of exercise may have a need for protein greater than someone involved in only one.
summary: This chunk outlines the factors influencing protein requirements, emphasizing the role of exercise type and intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1727
content: Because protein can be used for tissue repair and synthesis as well as for energy, protein requirements will increase as total energy intake decreases. As total caloric intake is reduced, energy needs may no longer be satisfied by carbohydrate and fat intake alone, necessitating that protein be used to provide energy. The goal is to satisfy the majority of energy needs with carbohydrate and fat, saving protein for tissue repair and growth. This is why carbohydrates are often referred to as protein sparing.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between energy intake, protein utilization, and the concept of carbohydrate sparing.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1728
content: Individuals interested in general fat loss or muscle hypertrophy have erroneously mimicked the acceptable use of a high-protein diet by a physique competitor. Under the proper circumstances, high-protein diets, when used temporarily, can be effective. For clients pursuing body-fat reduction, body-fat loss goals require that a caloric deficit be maintained until the goal is reached. During a negative energy balance, amino acids are used to assist in energy production, a term referred to as gluconeogenesis. Anaerobic or aerobic exercise depletes glycogen, increasing gluconeogenesis. The increase in gluconeogenesis is supported by the release of branched-chain and other amino acids from structural proteins to maintain glucose homeostasis during exercise. A hypocaloric diet establishes less-than-optimal glycogen stores, and when this is combined with increased glycogen demand during exercise, protein’s energy utilization is increased.
summary: This chunk discusses the potential benefits and drawbacks of high-protein diets for fat loss, emphasizing the importance of caloric balance and exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1729
content: The ability of a protein to satisfy these essential amino acid requirements can be quantified in several ways. Terms used to rate dietary protein include protein efficiency ratio (PER), net protein utilization (NPU), and biologic value (BV). BV is a measure frequently used when discussing protein sources in popular media and by supplement manufacturers. Essentially, BV is a measure of protein quality, or how well it satisfies the body’s essential amino acid needs. A protein source with a higher score provides an amino acid profile that is more closely related to the needs of the human body.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of protein quality and the different methods used to quantify it, focusing on biologic value (BV).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1730
content: One is led to believe that consuming specially prepared high-BV proteins will allow an individual who is already consuming adequate protein to build muscle to a greater degree, or more quickly. However, consuming protein above requirements will not force the body to unleash a previously untapped muscle-building capacity. Instead, if individuals exclusively consume very high BV proteins, their amino acid requirements would be met with less protein. Conversely, if individuals choose a diet composed of mostly lower BV protein sources, the total protein requirements will increase.
summary: This chunk debunks the myth that consuming high-BV proteins leads to greater muscle growth and clarifies the relationship between protein intake and requirements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1731
content: For clients pursuing body-fat reduction, body-fat loss goals require that a caloric deficit be maintained until the goal is reached. During a negative energy balance, amino acids are used to assist in energy production, a term referred to as gluconeogenesis. Anaerobic or aerobic exercise depletes glycogen, increasing gluconeogenesis. The increase in gluconeogenesis is supported by the release of branched-chain and other amino acids from structural proteins to maintain glucose homeostasis during exercise. A hypocaloric diet establishes less-than-optimal glycogen stores, and when this is combined with increased glycogen demand during exercise, protein’s energy utilization is increased.
summary: Body-fat reduction requires a caloric deficit, which can lead to increased gluconeogenesis and protein utilization during exercise and a hypocaloric diet.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1732
content: The amount of lean body mass lost in persons in a negative energy balance can be reduced by increasing the amount of protein in the diet, leading to a more rapid return to nitrogen balance. A number of studies show that an increase in protein utilization during a hypocaloric diet will produce effects that can be exacerbated by exercise.
summary: Increasing protein intake during a calorie deficit can help preserve lean body mass and mitigate the negative effects of exercise on protein utilization.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1733
content: Bodybuilders during positive energy balance (off-season) should follow the same protein recommendations as strength athletes. During negative energy balance (used to create competition-level body-fat percentages), protein requirements may dramatically increase. To reach competitive body-fat levels, calorie intake is continually lowered while exercise (such as cardiorespiratory training, weight training, and posing) is increased. Competitive levels of body fat are generally unhealthy and impossible to maintain for prolonged periods.
summary: Protein requirements for bodybuilders vary depending on their training phase, with higher needs during competition preparation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1734
content: Each component of this regimen may have additive effects on protein requirements. The body’s survival mechanisms, related to increases in energy expenditure and decreases in food supply, are probably highly active during this period, forcing a continued reduction in food intake to achieve the goal. Because of its anabolic requirements, protein intake cannot be lowered. In fact, protein intake may have to be increased in the final weeks before competition. During this period, the body must have the option to use available food either for energy or muscle support. The body does not have a choice with dietary carbohydrate or fat, making them the only dispensable calories. Therefore, protein intake could be dramatically increased to theoretically lessen the obligatory loss of lean tissue during drastic training measures. It is quite common to see clients consuming the majority of their calories from protein in the final weeks before competition.
summary: During competition preparation, protein intake should be prioritized to support muscle maintenance and minimize lean tissue loss despite drastic calorie restriction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1735
content: During the off-season, athletes return to normal food intake (or protein at anabolic requirements and energy needs met primarily with carbohydrates and fats) and normal energy balance. This return to normal eating habits enables greater muscular gains than would be achieved by maintaining a high-protein intake year-round.
summary: Off-season training allows for a return to normal eating habits, prioritizing carbohydrate and fat intake for optimal muscle growth.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1736
content: In fact, it appears that carbohydrate (1 g/kg or 0.5 gram per pound), not protein, consumed within an hour after heavy resistance training inhibits muscle-protein breakdown, resulting in a positive protein balance.
summary: Carbohydrate intake after resistance training is crucial for inhibiting muscle protein breakdown and promoting a positive protein balance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1737
content: Skeletal muscle is approximately 72% water, 22% protein, and 6% fat, glycogen, and minerals. 1 pound of muscle tissue contains approximately 100 grams of protein. Theoretically, an athlete would have to ingest an extra 14 grams of protein per day.
summary: Muscle composition is primarily protein, highlighting the importance of adequate protein intake for muscle growth and repair.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1738
content: Most experts believe the single most important factor in gaining lean mass (along with resistance training, of course) is consuming adequate calories. To ensure the body has sufficient energy for lean mass accretion, consume an additional 200 to 400 calories daily (3 to 5 calories per kg or 1.5 to 2.5 calories per pound per day) above maintenance requirements in addition to consuming a little extra protein (approximately 2 ounces of lean meat).
summary: Adequate calorie intake is essential for muscle growth, along with sufficient protein and resistance training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1739
content: Protein’s role in satiety is an important consideration. Protein activates specific satiety mechanisms and may be more satiating than fat and carbohydrate. Protein-induced suppression of food intake in animals and humans is greater than its energy content alone, which suggests that protein has a direct effect on satiety. In studies of rats and humans, a preload of protein suppressed their food intake for several hours and to a greater extent than a similar energy load of fat and carbohydrate.
summary: Protein is more satiating than fat and carbohydrates, potentially aiding in weight management and adherence to dietary plans.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1740
content: Individuals seeking fat loss may benefit from the satiating properties of protein to feel full and energized throughout the day. This can assist clients in program adherence.
summary: Protein's satiating effects can help individuals feel fuller for longer, potentially improving adherence to fat loss programs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1741
content: The Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) for protein is 0.8 g/kg per day. The Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Range for protein intake for an adult is 10% to 35% of total caloric intake. Protein recommendations range from 10% to 35% of total caloric intake, which allows not only for differences in goals and activity but also for bioindividuality in terms of satiety and performance. Some people respond better to slightly higher or lower protein intakes, which may help with adherence to the amount of calories required to reach and maintain goals. Individuals eating lower amounts of protein may need supplementation. In relation to total caloric intake, protein intake should still fall approximately within the above ranges of grams per kilogram. A small person losing fat (or hypocaloric) and exercising using strength and aerobic training may have a high percentage of protein (around 25%) but still fall in the appropriate range of absolute protein (1.2 to 1.7 g/kg per day).
summary: Protein recommendations vary based on individual needs, goals, and activity levels, with a general range of 10% to 35% of total caloric intake.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1742
content: Protein recommendations range from 10% to 35% of total caloric intake, which allows not only for differences in goals and activity but also for bioindividuality in terms of satiety and performance. Some people respond better to slightly higher or lower protein intakes, which may help with adherence to the amount of calories required to reach and maintain goals. Individuals eating lower amounts of protein may need supplementation. In relation to total caloric intake, protein intake should still fall approximately within the above ranges of grams per kilogram.
summary: Protein intake recommendations vary based on individual needs and goals, typically ranging from 10% to 35% of total calories.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1743
content: A small person losing fat (or hypocaloric) and exercising using strength and aerobic training may have a high percentage of protein (around 25%) but still fall in the appropriate range of absolute protein (1.2 to 1.7 g/kg per day). A high-protein diet is typically defined as one that consists of more than 35% of total caloric intake from protein, or three times the protein RDA for athletes. Chronic consumption of a high-protein diet is generally associated with a higher intake of saturated fat and low fiber intake, both of which are risk factors for heart disease and some types of cancer. Because the kidneys are required to work harder to eliminate the increased urea produced, caution should always be taken when recommending high-protein intakes to people with a history of kidney problems such as renal insufficiency or kidney stones.
summary: High-protein diets, exceeding 35% of calories, can pose health risks due to potential saturated fat and low fiber intake, and may strain kidney function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1744
content: The effects of high-protein diets on bone health have been debated in the literature with reports that a high-protein intake can increase urinary calcium losses. Early researchers in this area speculated that bone was the source of elevated urinary calcium excretion during a high-protein diet. Kerstetter et al. (59) found that consuming a low-protein diet (0.7 g/kg) caused an increase in two hormones that work together to increase blood calcium levels. The elevated hormone levels suggest that a low-protein diet may decrease calcium absorption. A follow-up evaluation showed that 18% of calcium consumed is absorbed during a low-protein diet (0.7 g/kg), and absorption increases to 26% during a high-protein (2.1 g/kg) diet. Therefore, it appears that an increase in intestinal calcium absorption as people follow a high-protein diet likely accounts for a majority of observed increases in calcium.
summary: Research on the impact of high-protein diets on bone health is inconclusive, with some studies suggesting potential calcium loss while others indicate increased absorption.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1745
content: There is a need for greater fluid consumption when consuming large quantities of protein. Protein requires approximately seven times the water for metabolism than carbohydrate or fat. Low-carbohydrate consumption typically accompanies high-protein diets (especially for weight loss), which can lead to decreased glycogen stores, inhibition of performance, and possible dehydration. Therefore, the main concern with high-protein diets is dehydration because the urea nitrogen cycle processes dietary nitrogen and water is eliminated via the urinary system. Because dehydration of as little as 3% can impair performance, athletes and active individuals ingesting extra protein should weigh themselves regularly to ensure they are properly hydrated.
summary: High-protein diets can lead to dehydration due to increased urea production and potential low carbohydrate intake, emphasizing the need for adequate fluid consumption.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1746
content: One gram of protein yields 4 calories. Protein must be broken down completely (into constituent amino acids) before it can be used. Amino acids from protein are used by the body for the following: synthesizing body-tissue protein, providing glucose for energy (many can be converted to glucose), providing nitrogen in the form of amine groups to build nonessential amino acids, and contributing to fat stores. Amino acids are not used to build protein under the following conditions: not enough available energy from carbohydrate and fat, consistently low or lacking essential dietary amino acids owing to the exclusive consumption of incomplete proteins, and an excess of necessary protein. The following conditions are necessary for the body to synthesize endogenous protein: availability of all essential and nonessential amino acids in proper amounts, an adequate supply of exogenous protein (supplying amine groups, which synthesize the nonessential amino acids), and adequate energy-yielding carbohydrate and fat (sparing the protein).
summary: Protein provides 4 calories per gram and is broken down into amino acids, which are used for various bodily functions, including muscle protein synthesis, energy production, and nonessential amino acid synthesis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1747
content: The recommended protein intake for athletes and exercisers is 1.2 to 1.7 g/kg depending on goal, activity, protein source, and total caloric intake. Typically, protein intake falls in a range of 10 to 35% of total caloric intake. Chronic high-protein intake (greater than three times the RDA) diets can lead to a higher intake of saturated fat and low fiber intake, increased urea production, decreased glycogen stores, and possible dehydration. Protein requirements can be affected by anaerobic and aerobic exercise, total energy intake, caloric intake, and carbohydrate intake. During a negative energy balance (or caloric deficit), amino acids are used to assist in energy production (or gluconeogenesis), wherein protein requirements may dramatically increase. Protein intake may also be adjusted to aid in satiety in individuals seeking fat loss, who may benefit from protein by feeling full and energized throughout the day.
summary: Protein requirements for athletes and exercisers vary based on factors like activity level, goals, and caloric intake, typically ranging from 1.2 to 1.7 g/kg body weight.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1748
content: The recommended dietary allowance for protein is 0.8 g/kg per day. However, this may vary among athletes from 1.2 to 1.7 g/kg per day. Protein supplementation is not typically recommended in general use among athletes. No substantial evidence exists that either using protein supplements to replace food or increasing protein intake above requirements will enhance performance or adult skeletal muscle hypertrophy. However, supplemental protein may be useful to quickly get amino acids into the blood before and after weight training, to replace whole-food proteins for weight loss, in situations when whole food is not available, and for bodybuilders, wrestlers, or other weight-conscious athletes preparing for competition.
summary: While the recommended protein intake is 0.8 g/kg per day, athletes may benefit from 1.2 to 1.7 g/kg, but supplementation is generally not necessary for performance enhancement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1749
content: Carbohydrates are compounds containing carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. Carbohydrates are generally classified as sugars, starches, and fiber. A sugar, as it would appear on a food label, is any monosaccharide or disaccharide. A monosaccharide is a single sugar unit. Many monosaccharides are connected to make starches, which are the storage form of carbohydrates in plants. Glycogen is the storage form of carbohydrates in humans.
summary: Carbohydrates are composed of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen and are categorized as sugars, starches, and fiber, with monosaccharides being the basic building blocks.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1750
content: Carbohydrates are generally classified as sugars, starches, and fiber. A sugar, as it would appear on a food label, is any monosaccharide or disaccharide. A monosaccharide is a single sugar unit. Many monosaccharides are connected to make starches, which are the storage form of carbohydrates in plants. Glycogen is the storage form of carbohydrates in humans. Examples of monosaccharides include glucose, fructose, and galactose. Disaccharides are two sugar units. Examples of disaccharides include sucrose, lactose, and maltose. Polysaccharides are long chains of monosaccharide units linked together and found in foods that contain starch and fiber.
summary: This chunk defines the basic classifications of carbohydrates: sugars (monosaccharides and disaccharides), starches, and fiber, explaining their structures and roles in the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1751
content: Complex carbohydrates include starch found in plants, seeds, and roots. Complex carbohydrates are primarily starch and fiber, and the starch is digested to glucose. Dietary fiber is a part of the plant that cannot be digested by human gut enzymes and passes through the small intestine and colon. Dietary fiber is expelled as fecal material or fermented and used as food by the gut bacteria.
summary: This chunk focuses on complex carbohydrates, differentiating them from simple sugars and highlighting the role of dietary fiber in digestion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1752
content: Carbohydrates are a chief source of energy for all body functions and muscular exertion. Carbohydrates help regulate the digestion and utilization of protein and fat. The principal carbohydrates present in food occur in the form of simple sugars, starches, and cellulose. Simple sugars, such as those in honey and fruits, are very easily digested. Double sugars, such as table sugar, require some digestive action but are not nearly as complex as starches. Starches require prolonged enzymatic action to be broken down into simple sugars for utilization. Cellulose, commonly found in the skins of fruits and vegetables, is largely indigestible by humans and contributes little energy value to the diet.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of carbohydrates as an energy source and discusses the different types of carbohydrates and their digestibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1753
content: Cellulose provides the bulk necessary for intestinal motility and aids in elimination. The rate at which ingested carbohydrate raises blood sugar and its accompanying effect on insulin release is referred to as the glycemic index (GI). The GI for a food is determined when the particular food is consumed by itself on an empty stomach. Mixed meals of protein, other carbohydrate, and fat can alter the glycemic effect of single foods.
summary: This chunk focuses on the role of cellulose in digestion and introduces the concept of the glycemic index (GI).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1754
content: Foods lower on the glycemic index are good sources of complex carbohydrates, as well as being high in fiber and overall nutritional value. Soluble fiber is dissolved by water and forms a gel-like substance in the digestive tract. Soluble fiber has many benefits, including moderating blood glucose levels and lowering cholesterol. Good sources of soluble fiber include oats and oatmeal, legumes (peas, beans, lentils), barley, and many uncooked fruits and vegetables (especially oranges, apples, and carrots).
summary: This chunk explains the benefits of low-GI foods and highlights the role of soluble fiber in health.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1755
content: Insoluble fiber does not absorb or dissolve in water. Insoluble fiber passes through the digestive tract close to its original form. Insoluble fiber offers many benefits to intestinal health, including a reduction in the risk and occurrence of colorectal cancer, hemorrhoids, and constipation. Most insoluble fiber comes from the bran layers of cereal grains.
summary: This chunk describes the properties and benefits of insoluble fiber.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1756
content: The recommended intake of fiber is set at 38 g per day for young men and 25 g per day for young women (73). Fiber provides bulk in the diet, thus increasing the satiety value of foods. Some fibers also delay the emptying of the stomach, further increasing satiety (78). Fiber prevents constipation and establishes regular bowel movements. Fiber may reduce the risks of heart and artery disease by lowering blood cholesterol. Fiber regulates the body’s absorption of glucose (diabetics included), perhaps because fiber is believed to be capable of controlling the rate of digestion and assimilation of carbohydrates.
summary: This chunk outlines the recommended fiber intake and discusses the various health benefits associated with fiber consumption.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1757
content: High-fiber meals have been shown to exert regulatory effects on blood glucose levels for up to 5 hours after eating. Carbohydrate availability is vital for maximal sports performance.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the long-lasting effects of high-fiber meals on blood sugar regulation and highlights the importance of carbohydrates for athletic performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1758
content: Fiber prevents constipation and establishes regular bowel movements. Fiber may reduce the risks of heart and artery disease by lowering blood cholesterol. Fiber regulates the body’s absorption of glucose (diabetics included), perhaps because fiber is believed to be capable of controlling the rate of digestion and assimilation of carbohydrates. High-fiber meals have been shown to exert regulatory effects on blood glucose levels for up to 5 hours after eating.
summary: Fiber offers various health benefits, including digestive regularity, heart health, and blood sugar control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1759
content: Carbohydrate availability is vital for maximal sports performance. When performing high-intensity, short-duration activity (anaerobic), muscular demand for energy is provided for and dependent on muscle glycogen. During endurance exercise (aerobic) muscle glycogen provides approximately 50% of energy needs during moderate-intensity aerobic exercise (60% of maximal oxygen consumption [V̇O2 max]). During high-intensity aerobic exercise (>79% of V̇O2 max), muscle glycogen yields nearly all of the energy needs. As the duration of exercise increases, available glucose and glycogen diminish, increasing the reliance on fat as a fuel source. If the workout contributes to a caloric deficit, the body will draw on its fat stores to make up for the deficit.
summary: Carbohydrate availability, particularly muscle glycogen, is crucial for exercise performance, especially during high-intensity activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1760
content: The limiting factor for exercise performance is carbohydrate availability. Maximal fat utilization cannot occur without sufficient carbohydrate to continue Krebs cycle activity. When an endurance athlete 'hits the wall,' it is the result of fatigue caused by severely lowered liver and muscle glycogen. This occurs even though there is sufficient oxygen being delivered to the muscles and an abundance of potential energy from fat stores.
summary: Carbohydrate availability directly impacts exercise performance, and depletion can lead to 'hitting the wall'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1761
content: A diet containing between 6 and 10 g/kg per day of carbohydrate (2.7 to 4.5 g/lb) is recommended. According to the Institute of Medicine, the Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Range for carbohydrate intake for an adult is 45 to 65% of total caloric intake. Complex carbohydrates (such as whole grains and fresh fruits and vegetables) should constitute the majority of calories because of their nutrient-dense nature.
summary: Recommended carbohydrate intake for adults ranges from 45-65% of total calories, with complex carbohydrates being prioritized.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1762
content: High-carbohydrate diets increase the use of glycogen as fuel, whereas a high-fat diet increases the use of fat as fuel. A high-fat diet results in lower glycogen synthesis. For the endurance athlete, a carbohydrate-rich diet will build glycogen stores and aid in performance and recovery.
summary: Dietary carbohydrate intake directly influences fuel utilization, with high-carbohydrate diets promoting glycogen storage and benefiting endurance athletes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1763
content: Although some studies show an increase in performance associated with the consumption of a high-fat diet, these improvements are seen in exercise performed at a relatively low intensity (less than 70% of V̇O2 max). As the intensity of exercise increases, performance of high-intensity exercise will ultimately be impaired.
summary: While high-fat diets may improve performance at low intensities, they negatively impact high-intensity exercise performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1764
content: It is recommended that the individual consume a high-carbohydrate meal 2 to 4 hours before exercising for more than an hour. This will allow time for appropriate gastric emptying before exercise. If this is not feasible because of time constraints, a liquid meal such as a meal-replacement formula may be used. One advantage to such formulas is their quick gastric emptying time.
summary: Pre-exercise nutrition should prioritize high-carbohydrate meals 2-4 hours before exercise, with liquid options available for time constraints.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1765
content: Some research recommends a carbohydrate intake of 1 to 4.5 g/kg, between 1 and 4 hours before exercise, respectively. In this study, the group ingesting 4.5 g/kg of carbohydrate 4 hours before exercise saw performance improved by 15%. To avoid gastrointestinal distress, smaller meals should be consumed closer to the exercise session.
summary: Carbohydrate intake timing and amount before exercise can influence performance, with optimal timing and dosage varying based on individual needs and tolerance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1766
content: Muscle-glycogen stores become depleted during endurance exercise of greater than a 90-minute duration. Carbohydrate loading, also called glycogen supercompensation, is a technique used to increase muscle glycogen before an endurance event. Carbohydrate loading can nearly double muscle glycogen stores, increasing endurance potential.
summary: Carbohydrate loading is a technique used to maximize muscle glycogen stores, enhancing endurance performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1767
content: Historically, the weeklong carbohydrate loading program includes 4 days of glycogen depletion through a low-carbohydrate diet that is approximately 10% of calories and exhaustive exercise. The historical carbohydrate loading program is followed by 3 days of rest and a high-carbohydrate diet that is approximately 90% of calories. The historical carbohydrate loading method has drawbacks including periods of hypoglycemia, irritability, increased susceptibility to injury, and difficulty in compliance.
summary: Traditional carbohydrate loading involves a week-long process of glycogen depletion followed by repletion, but it can have drawbacks.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1768
content: A 1981 study proposed a revised carbohydrate loading method that accomplishes the same goal with greater ease of compliance and fewer side effects. Maximizing muscle glycogen before an event may improve power, performance, output, and speed by postponing muscle glycogen depletion. Glycogen loading before exercise does not always improve performance. Some athletes experience extreme gastrointestinal distress including diarrhea when attempting glycogen loading.
summary: Revised carbohydrate loading methods aim to achieve similar benefits with improved compliance and fewer side effects, but individual responses may vary.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1769
content: The historical carbohydrate loading method has drawbacks including periods of hypoglycemia, irritability, increased susceptibility to injury, and difficulty in compliance. A 1981 study proposed a revised carbohydrate loading method that accomplishes the same goal with greater ease of compliance and fewer side effects. Glycogen loading before exercise does not always improve performance. Some athletes experience extreme gastrointestinal distress including diarrhea when attempting glycogen loading. Meals for glycogen loading should contain familiar foods that are relatively low in fat and fiber to minimize gastrointestinal distress. Leg muscles become heavier with the addition of glycogen and water, causing some athletes to feel heavy and sluggish. Athletes may experience a slight weight gain with glycogen loading. Athletes should experiment with glycogen loading before using it for competition.
summary: Carbohydrate loading, while potentially beneficial for performance, has drawbacks and requires careful consideration and experimentation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1770
content: Maximizing muscle glycogen before an event may improve power, performance, output, and speed by postponing muscle glycogen depletion. For exercise lasting more than 1 hour, carbohydrate feedings during exercise can help supply glucose to working muscles whose glycogen stores are dwindling. Carbohydrate feedings during exercise also maintain blood glucose levels, increasing time to exhaustion by 20 to 60 minutes. Endurance athletes should consume between 30 and 60 g of carbohydrate every hour during exercise. Popular sports beverages are perfect for supplying carbohydrates during exercise and replacing fluid losses. The replacement of carbohydrate and water has individual benefits that together are additive. One study showed that performance during 1 hour of intense cycling was improved by 12% with the consumption of 1,330 mL (53 oz) of water containing 79 g of carbohydrate.
summary: Carbohydrate intake before and during exercise can enhance performance and delay fatigue.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1771
content: Sports beverages including potassium and sodium help replace electrolytes, whereas carbohydrates provide energy. Sports beverages containing 6% to 8% carbohydrate are recommended for exercise lasting longer than 1 hour.
summary: Sports beverages are a valuable tool for replenishing carbohydrates and electrolytes during exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1772
content: Repeated days of strenuous exercise take a toll on an individual’s glycogen stores. A high-carbohydrate intake helps to replenish glycogen stores; however, the timing of carbohydrate ingestion can also be important to maximizing recovery. Consuming 1.5 g per kg of carbohydrate within 30 minutes of completing exercise is recommended to maximize glycogen replenishment. Delaying carbohydrate intake by even 2 hours can decrease total muscle glycogen synthesis by 66%. The postworkout environment may hasten glycogen repletion as a result of increased blood flow to the muscles and an increased sensitivity of the cells to the effects of insulin. Additional meals of 1.5 g per kg of carbohydrate every 2 hours are recommended to completely restore muscle glycogen.
summary: Optimal carbohydrate intake timing is crucial for maximizing muscle glycogen replenishment after exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1773
content: Carbohydrate should generally make up the highest percentage of macronutrient calories when one is attempting fat loss or muscle gain. Carbohydrates provide variety, valuable nutrients, and volume to the diet. The satiating value of complex carbohydrate is especially important when one is in a caloric deficit for the goal of fat loss. For most moderately active adults, a carbohydrate intake of between 45 and 65% is recommended, which will provide sufficient food volume and the fuel necessary for energy and productive workouts.
summary: Carbohydrates play a vital role in both fat loss and muscle gain, providing energy, satiety, and nutritional value.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1774
content: There is no need for one to reduce carbohydrate percentage to lose fat. There is recent evidence from examining the glycemic response to specific foods, suggesting that a diet that is centered around low glycemic index carbohydrates may be useful in the prevention of obesity, coronary artery disease, colon cancer, and breast cancer.
summary: Carbohydrate intake does not need to be restricted for fat loss, and low glycemic index carbohydrates may offer additional health benefits.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1775
content: Weight loss or gain is primarily related to total caloric intake, not the macronutrient profile of the diet. The weight lost on a low-carbohydrate diet can be attributed to two factors: low caloric intake and loss of fat-free mass (FFM). If an individual begins dropping carbohydrate-rich foods from his or her diet, it is inevitable that caloric intake will drop as a result. Added to the caloric reduction are dwindling glycogen stores. For every gram of glucose taken out of glycogen, it brings with it 2.7 g of water. This loss of muscle glycogen (including water) can be quite significant in the first week of a low-carbohydrate diet, and adds to the pounds lost on the scale. This is how low-carbohydrate fad diets can promise dramatic weight loss in such a short period. Long-term success in weight loss is associated with a realistic eating style, not one that severely limits or omits one of the macronutrients.
summary: Weight management is primarily driven by caloric balance, and restrictive diets may lead to unintended losses of muscle mass.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1776
content: The percentage of calories consumed from fat has dropped from 36% (NHANES II 1976–1980) to 34% of total energy intake (106). When total fat intake (grams per person per day) is measured, fat intake has remained quite constant for the past several years (107). The data from NHANES III also show an increase in total energy intake.
summary: Dietary fat intake has slightly decreased over time, while total energy intake has increased.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1777
content: The percentage of calories consumed from fat has dropped from 36% (NHANES II 1976–1980) to 34% of total energy intake (106). When total fat intake (grams per person per day) is measured, fat intake has remained quite constant for the past several years (107). The data from NHANES III also show an increase in total energy intake. In the early 1900s, the percentage of carbohydrates consumed as energy intake was higher and consumption of fat lower than it is today, without the prevalence of obesity we now experience (108). Only during the last two decades has there been a significant increase in obesity. The data support two primary variables responsible for this dramatic rise in obesity: an increased energy intake and a reduction in energy expenditure (108, 109).
summary: This chunk discusses historical trends in macronutrient consumption and their relationship to the rise in obesity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1778
content: At the turn of the century, carbohydrate intake as a percentage of total energy was higher, fat as a percentage was lower, and obesity was not the problem it is today. Currently, total fat intake is higher, carbohydrate is lower, and obesity has reached epidemic proportions (111, 112). In addition, energy intake has increased and energy expenditure has decreased.
summary: This chunk compares macronutrient intake and obesity prevalence at the turn of the century to the present day.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1779
content: One gram of carbohydrate yields 4 calories. Carbohydrates provide the body with nutrition that fat and protein cannot (from complex carbohydrates). Carbohydrates provide satiety by keeping glycogen stores full and adding bulk to the diet. Carbohydrates provide proper cellular fluid balance, maximizing cellular efficiency. Carbohydrates provide proper blood sugar levels, if there is a consistent intake of low-glycemic carbohydrates. Carbohydrates spare protein for building muscle. The body needs carbohydrates because they are the perfect and preferred form of energy. The body constantly needs to replace carbohydrates, causing a craving that must be satisfied. Parts of the central nervous system rely exclusively on carbohydrate.
summary: This chunk outlines the various benefits of carbohydrates for the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1780
content: Carbohydrates are a chief source of energy for all body functions and muscular exertion. They are compounds containing carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen and are generally classified as sugars (simple), starches (complex), and fiber. A monosaccharide is a single sugar unit (such as glucose, fructose, or galactose), many of which are connected to make starches and glycogen. Disaccharides (two sugar units) include sucrose, lactose, and maltose. Polysaccharides are foods that contain starch and fiber. These foods are often called complex carbohydrates. Carbohydrates help to regulate the digestion and utilization of protein and fat.
summary: This chunk defines carbohydrates and their different classifications.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1781
content: GI is the rate at which ingested carbohydrate raises blood sugar and affects insulin release. Foods lower on the glycemic index are good sources of complex carbohydrates, as well as being high in fiber and overall nutritional value. When total caloric intake exceeds output, any excess carbohydrate, dietary fat, or protein may be stored as body fat until needed.
summary: This chunk explains the glycemic index and its implications for carbohydrate consumption.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1782
content: Fiber is one of the greatest contributions made by dietary complex carbohydrate. Higher intakes of dietary fiber are associated with a lower incidence of heart disease and certain types of cancer. In addition, fiber provides many other benefits including satiety, intestinal health, and regulation of the body’s absorption of glucose.
summary: This chunk highlights the benefits of dietary fiber.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1783
content: The availability of carbohydrate is vital for fitness enthusiasts and optimal sports performance because the demand for energy is provided for and dependent on muscle glycogen. Duration and intensity of exercise affect the amount of glycogen used for energy. Maximal fat utilization cannot occur without sufficient carbohydrate. A diet containing between 6 and 10 g/kg/day of carbohydrate (2.7 to 4.5 g per pound) is recommended.
summary: This chunk discusses the importance of carbohydrates for fitness and athletic performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1784
content: This is how low-carbohydrate fad diets can promise dramatic weight loss in such a short period. Long-term success in weight loss is associated with a realistic eating style, not one that severely limits or omits one of the macronutrients.
summary: This chunk addresses the potential drawbacks of fad diets that severely restrict carbohydrates.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1785
content: It is estimated that more than 75% of the American adult population does not partake, on a daily basis, in 30 minutes of low-to-moderate physical activity (110).
summary: This chunk highlights the prevalence of physical inactivity in the American adult population.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1786
content: In addition, fiber provides many other benefits including satiety, intestinal health, and regulation of the body’s absorption of glucose. Fruits, whole grains, and vegetables are all excellent sources of fiber.
summary: Fiber offers various benefits, including satiety, intestinal health, glucose regulation, and is abundant in fruits, whole grains, and vegetables.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1787
content: The availability of carbohydrate is vital for fitness enthusiasts and optimal sports performance because the demand for energy is provided for and dependent on muscle glycogen. Duration and intensity of exercise affect the amount of glycogen used for energy.
summary: Carbohydrates are crucial for fitness and sports performance as they fuel muscle glycogen, with glycogen usage influenced by exercise duration and intensity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1788
content: Maximal fat utilization cannot occur without sufficient carbohydrate. A diet containing between 6 and 10 g/kg/day of carbohydrate (2.7 to 4.5 g per pound) is recommended. Complex carbohydrates (such as whole grains and fresh fruits and vegetables) should constitute the majority of calories because of their nutrient-dense (providing B vitamins, iron, and fiber) nature.
summary: Carbohydrates are essential for optimal fat utilization, with recommended daily intake ranging from 6 to 10 g/kg/day. Complex carbohydrates, rich in nutrients, should form the bulk of calorie intake.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1789
content: It is recommended that the individual consume a high-carbohydrate meal 2 to 4 hours before exercising for more than an hour. For exercise lasting more than 1 hour, endurance athletes should consume between 30 and 60 g of carbohydrate every hour (which may consist of sports beverages).
summary: Pre-exercise carbohydrate intake is crucial for prolonged exercise, with recommendations for high-carbohydrate meals 2-4 hours before workouts exceeding one hour and ongoing carbohydrate consumption during endurance activities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1790
content: In endurance exercise of greater than a 90-minute duration, carbohydrate loading can be used to increase muscle glycogen before an endurance event. After exercise, consuming 1.5 g per kg of carbohydrate within 30 minutes is recommended. Additional meals of 1.5 g per kg of carbohydrate every 2 hours are recommended to completely restore muscle glycogen.
summary: Carbohydrate loading can enhance muscle glycogen stores before endurance events exceeding 90 minutes. Post-exercise, immediate carbohydrate replenishment (1.5 g/kg within 30 minutes) and subsequent meals every 2 hours are recommended for complete glycogen restoration.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1791
content: For fat loss or muscle gain, carbohydrates should generally make up the highest percentage of macronutrient calories. According to the Institute of Medicine, the Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Range for carbohydrate intake for an adult is 45 to 65% of total caloric intake. There is no need to reduce carbohydrate percentage and carbohydrate-rich whole foods to lose fat.
summary: Carbohydrates should be prioritized for fat loss or muscle gain, comprising the largest proportion of macronutrient calories. The recommended daily intake for adults is 45-65% of total calories, and reducing carbohydrates is unnecessary for fat loss.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1792
content: America’s increasing problem of obesity is not a direct result of carbohydrate intake, but rather one of energy imbalance. Carbohydrate recommendations should be estimated after protein and fat requirements are met.
summary: Obesity is primarily attributed to energy imbalance, not carbohydrate intake. Carbohydrate recommendations should be determined after fulfilling protein and fat requirements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1793
content: Lipids are a group of compounds that include triglycerides (fats and oils), phospholipids, and sterols. Of the lipids contained in food, 95% are fats and oils. In the body, 99% of the stored lipids are also triglycerides.
summary: Lipids encompass triglycerides (fats and oils), phospholipids, and sterols. Dietary lipids primarily consist of fats and oils, while stored body lipids are predominantly triglycerides.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1794
content: Structurally, triglycerides are three fatty acids attached to a glycerol backbone. Lipids (or fats) may play a role in the treatment and prevention of heart disease, hypertension, arthritis, and cancer.
summary: Triglycerides consist of three fatty acids linked to a glycerol molecule. Lipids may contribute to the prevention and treatment of various health conditions, including heart disease, hypertension, arthritis, and cancer.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1795
content: Trans-fatty acids are the result of hydrogenation, which is the process of adding hydrogen to unsaturated fatty acids to make them harder at room temperature and increase food shelf-life. Trans-fatty acids have been shown to increase LDL cholesterol and decrease HDL cholesterol.
summary: Trans-fatty acids are produced through hydrogenation, a process that alters unsaturated fatty acids to increase their solidity and shelf life. They elevate LDL cholesterol and lower HDL cholesterol.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1796
content: One gram of fat yields approximately 9 calories when oxidized. Fats act as carriers for the fat-soluble vitamins A, D, E, and K.
summary: Fat provides 9 calories per gram upon oxidation. Fats are essential for transporting fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, and K).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1797
content: Vitamin D aids in the absorption of calcium, making it available to body tissues, particularly to the bones and teeth. Fats are also important for the conversion of carotene to vitamin A.
summary: Vitamin D facilitates calcium absorption, crucial for bone and teeth health. Fats are also vital for converting carotene into vitamin A.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1798
content: Fats are involved in cellular membrane structure and function. Fats are precursors to hormones. Fats are involved in cellular signals. Fats are involved in the regulation and excretion of nutrients in the cells.
summary: Fats play diverse roles in cellular processes, including membrane structure, hormone production, cellular signaling, and nutrient regulation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1799
content: Fats surround, protect, and hold in place organs, such as the kidneys, heart, and liver. Fats insulate the body from environmental temperature changes and preserve body heat.
summary: Fats provide physical protection and insulation for organs, shielding them from damage and regulating body temperature.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1800
content: Fats prolong the digestive process by slowing the stomach’s secretions of hydrochloric acid, creating a longer-lasting sensation of fullness after a meal. Fats initiate the release of the hormone cholecystokinin (CCK), which contributes to satiety.
summary: Fats contribute to satiety by slowing digestion and promoting the release of cholecystokinin (CCK), a hormone that signals fullness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1801
content: Digestion of dietary fat starts in the mouth, moves to the stomach, and is completed in the small intestine. In the intestine, fat interacts with bile to become emulsified so that pancreatic enzymes can break triglycerides down into two fatty acids and a monoglyceride.
summary: Fat digestion begins in the mouth, continues in the stomach, and is finalized in the small intestine. Bile emulsifies fat, allowing pancreatic enzymes to break down triglycerides into fatty acids and monoglycerides.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1802
content: Absorption of these constituents occurs through the intestinal wall. Dietary fats are reassembled into triglycerides in the intestinal wall.
summary: Absorbed fatty acids and monoglycerides are reassembled into triglycerides within the intestinal wall.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1803
content: Fats initiate the release of the hormone cholecystokinin (CCK), which contributes to satiety. Dietary fats stimulate the release of CCK, a hormone that signals satiety. Fats slow the digestion of foods (and thus the nutrient content in the bloodstream), assisting in blood sugar stabilization. Reducing blood sugar fluctuations can contribute to satiety.  Satiety is achieved by more than just total caloric intake. A low-volume, high-calorie contribution of fat may not satisfy other peripheral satiation mechanisms (chewing, swallowing, stomach distention), leading to hyperphagia (or overeating).
summary: Dietary fats promote satiety through hormonal mechanisms, slowed digestion, and blood sugar stabilization.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1804
content: Digestion of dietary fat starts in the mouth, moves to the stomach, and is completed in the small intestine. In the intestine, fat interacts with bile to become emulsified so that pancreatic enzymes can break triglycerides down into two fatty acids and a monoglyceride. Absorption of these constituents occurs through the intestinal wall. Dietary fats are reassembled into triglycerides in the intestinal wall. Triglycerides are released into the lymph in the form of a lipoprotein called chylomicron. Chylomicrons from the lymph move to the blood.
summary: Dietary fat digestion and absorption occur in a multi-step process involving the mouth, stomach, small intestine, lymph, and blood.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1805
content: The triglyceride content of chylomicron is removed by the enzyme lipoprotein lipase (LPL). Released fatty acids are taken up by the tissues. Triglycerides are constantly cycled in and out of tissues, including muscles, organs, and adipose.
summary: Chylomicrons deliver triglycerides to tissues, where they are broken down and reassembled, contributing to energy storage and utilization.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1806
content: The Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Range for fat intake for an adult is 20 to 35% of total caloric intake. Athletes are recommended to consume 20 to 25% of total calories from fat. There appears to be no health or performance benefit to consuming less than 15% of energy from fat.
summary: Dietary fat recommendations vary based on individual needs, with general guidelines suggesting 20-35% of total calories from fat for adults and 20-25% for athletes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1807
content: Higher-fat diets are not conducive to successful weight loss or maintenance and appear to increase the ease with which the body converts ingested calories to body fat. Fat has a lower thermic effect than other macronutrients. The thermic effect of a food (TEF) is the rise in metabolic rate that occurs after the food is ingested. Typically, TEF amounts to 10% of ingested calories. As fat percentage in the diet increases, the amount of heat given off (TEF) decreases. As carbohydrate percentage in the diet increases, so does the TEF.
summary: Higher fat diets may hinder weight loss and promote fat storage due to their lower thermic effect compared to carbohydrates.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1808
content: It is metabolically inexpensive to convert dietary fat to body-fat stores. Only 3% of the calories in fat are required to store it as fat. It takes 23% of the calories in carbohydrate to convert it to body fat.
summary: Storing dietary fat as body fat requires significantly fewer calories compared to storing carbohydrates.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1809
content: Clients must be satiated by the amount of calories necessary to allow fat loss or energy balance. The goal is to keep the client’s diet within the guidelines for health.
summary: Client dietary plans should prioritize satiety while adhering to healthy calorie and macronutrient guidelines for weight management.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1810
content: Long-chain triglycerides (LCT), which make up the majority of dietary fatty acids (16 to 18 carbons), must go through the process of digestion and absorption described earlier before they can be utilized. Medium-chain triglycerides (MCT) are more rapidly absorbed. MCT do not require incorporation into chylomicrons for transport, but can enter the systemic circulation directly through the portal vein, providing a readily available, concentrated source of energy.
summary: Different types of triglycerides (LCT and MCT) have varying digestion and absorption rates, influencing their utilization by the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1811
content: MCT could benefit endurance performance by supplying an exogenous energy source in addition to carbohydrate during exercise and increase plasma free fatty acids (FFA), sparing muscle glycogen. Several studies of well-trained endurance athletes have shown that MCT supplementation does not improve performance. MCT ingestion does not alter fat metabolism, spare muscle glycogen, or improve performance (128, 129). Goedecke et al. (130) found that ingestion of MCT with ultraendurance cyclists actually compromised performance.
summary: While MCTs offer potential benefits for endurance performance, research suggests they may not consistently improve performance outcomes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1812
content: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of symptoms characterized by obesity, insulin resistance, hypertension, and dyslipidemia, leading to an increased risk of cardiovascular disease. Syndrome X is usually associated with obesity (especially abdominal), a high-fat diet, and a sedentary lifestyle (131–134).
summary: Metabolic syndrome, often linked to obesity, high-fat diets, and inactivity, increases the risk of cardiovascular disease.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1813
content: Several studies of well-trained endurance athletes have shown that MCT supplementation does not improve performance. MCT ingestion does not alter fat metabolism, spare muscle glycogen, or improve performance (128, 129). Goedecke et al. (130) found that ingestion of MCT with ultraendurance cyclists actually compromised performance. Medium-chain triglycerides are not recommended as supplements for the goal of improving endurance exercise.
summary: MCT supplementation does not improve endurance performance and may even hinder it.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1814
content: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of symptoms characterized by obesity, insulin resistance, hypertension, and dyslipidemia, leading to an increased risk of cardiovascular disease. Syndrome X is usually associated with obesity (especially abdominal), a high-fat diet, and a sedentary lifestyle (131–134). A common denominator associated with these factors is high levels of circulating free fatty acids (FFA).
summary: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of conditions linked to obesity, high-fat diets, and sedentary lifestyles, characterized by high circulating free fatty acids.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1815
content: In the presence of high FFA concentrations, the body will favor their use as energy, decreasing glucose oxidation and glycogen synthesis and inhibiting glucose transport (131). The result is chronically elevated levels of blood sugar levels, a condition called hyperglycemia. During states of hyperglycemia, insulin will also be elevated, leading to the conversion of the excess blood sugar to other products such as glycoproteins and fatty acids.
summary: High free fatty acids lead to decreased glucose utilization, resulting in chronically elevated blood sugar levels and increased insulin production.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1816
content: Although there is some evidence that there may be a genetic component that contributes to insulin resistance (IR), the condition itself will not allow for weight gain without an energy intake in excess of expenditure (7, 135, 136). In fact, obesity itself is a risk factor for development of IR, not the other way around (137). If one constantly overeats, excess calories are stored as fat, which causes fat cells to increase in size. The growing fat cell itself becomes insulin resistant, and the resulting prevalence of FFA will cause the body to favor the use of fat for energy at the expense of glucose (138).
summary: Insulin resistance is a consequence of excess calorie intake, leading to fat cell growth and further exacerbating insulin resistance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1817
content: This becomes a vicious cycle, with the overweight condition leading to IR, which in turn leads to impaired glucose use. Blood sugar levels rise, insulin levels rise, and cholesterol, triglycerides, and blood pressure rise as well. The impaired ability of glucose to enter muscle cells keeps glycogen stores lower, which can increase appetite, motivating the individual to eat more, increasing fat stores, exacerbating IR, and so on.
summary: Insulin resistance creates a vicious cycle of elevated blood sugar, increased insulin, and impaired glucose utilization, leading to further weight gain and worsening insulin resistance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1818
content: High-fat diets are strongly associated with obesity, and thus insulin resistance and diabetes (138–140). Eating fat does not make one fat (the same applies to carbohydrate) unless it is consumed in excess of energy requirements. It is easier to consume excess energy (or be hyperphagic) on a high-fat diet, owing to fat’s high caloric density. When large quantities of high-caloric-dense foods are consumed in combination with excess calories and a sedentary lifestyle, it is easy to envision an abundance of fatty acids floating around in the bloodstream.
summary: High-fat diets, when combined with excess calories and sedentary behavior, contribute to obesity and insulin resistance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1819
content: It is much more likely that a high-fat diet leads to excess consumption of calories, obesity, IR, and eventually non–insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus than it is that carbohydrates cause IR and, as a result, obesity. The only solution is a diet containing the appropriate amount of energy, high in fibrous vegetables or starchy carbohydrates, and exercise.
summary: Excess calorie consumption, rather than fat itself, is the primary driver of obesity and insulin resistance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1820
content: A study of type 2 diabetics, those with insulin resistance, and people of normal weight found that 3 weeks of a high-carbohydrate, low-fat diet and an exercise program significantly lowered insulin levels.
summary: A high-carbohydrate, low-fat diet combined with exercise effectively reduces insulin levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1821
content: One gram of fat yields 9 calories. Fat is generally insoluble in water. Fat is present in all cells: high in adipose and nerve tissue, low in epithelial and muscle tissue. Fatty acids can be saturated, polyunsaturated, and monounsaturated.
summary: Fat is a concentrated energy source, insoluble in water, and present in varying amounts throughout the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1822
content: The body needs fats for energy, structure and membrane function, precursors to hormones, cellular signals, and regulation of uptake and excretion of nutrients in the cells. The Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Range for fat intake for an adult is 20 to 35% of total caloric intake (20 to 25% for athletes). There appears to be no health or performance benefit to consuming less than 15% of energy from fat.
summary: Fat is essential for various bodily functions, and a minimum intake of 15% of calories from fat is recommended.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1823
content: A high polyunsaturated-to-saturated fat ratio is desirable. More than 35% of fat intake leads to overeating (lack of food volume) and often slows metabolism. Lipids are a group of compounds that include triglycerides (fats and oils), phospholipids, and sterols.
summary: A balanced fat intake, prioritizing polyunsaturated fats, is important for health and metabolism.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1824
content: Most lipids in food and in the body are triglycerides, which are three fatty acids (saturated or unsaturated) attached to a glycerol backbone. Polyunsaturated fatty acids provide important essential fatty acids. Saturated fatty acids and trans-fatty acids are implicated as a risk factor for heart disease because they raise bad cholesterol levels (LDL), whereas unsaturated fats are associated with increases in good cholesterol (HDL) and decreased risk of heart disease.
summary: Triglycerides are the primary form of fat in the body, and unsaturated fats are generally healthier than saturated and trans fats.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1825
content: Lipids are the most concentrated source of energy in the diet. Fat is digested and absorbed quite slowly.
summary: Fat is a highly concentrated energy source that is digested and absorbed slowly.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1826
content: Polyunsaturated fatty acids provide important essential fatty acids. Saturated fatty acids and trans-fatty acids are implicated as a risk factor for heart disease because they raise bad cholesterol levels (LDL), whereas unsaturated fats are associated with increases in good cholesterol (HDL) and decreased risk of heart disease.
summary: This chunk discusses the different types of fats and their impact on heart health.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1827
content: Lipids are the most concentrated source of energy in the diet. Fat is digested and absorbed quite slowly.
summary: This chunk describes the role of lipids as an energy source and their digestion process.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1828
content: Medium-chain triglycerides are not recommended as supplements for the goal of improving endurance exercise.
summary: This chunk provides a specific recommendation regarding medium-chain triglycerides and endurance exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1829
content: Metabolic syndrome is a cluster of symptoms characterized by obesity and insulin resistance. Obesity itself is a risk factor for development of insulin resistance, not the other way around. Insulin resistance alone will not allow for weight gain without an energy intake in excess of expenditure.
summary: This chunk explains the relationship between obesity, insulin resistance, and weight gain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1830
content: Sedentary men should consume on average 3.0 L (approximately 13 cups) of water per day. Sedentary women should consume on average 2.2 L (approximately 9 cups) of water per day. Those participating in a fat-loss program should drink an additional 8 ounces of water for every 25 pounds they carry above their ideal weight. Water intake should also be increased if an individual is exercising briskly or residing in a hot climate.
summary: This chunk provides guidelines for daily water intake based on activity level and body weight.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1831
content: Water is vital to life itself; it constitutes approximately 60% of the adult human body by weight. One can only survive for a few days without water. Consuming an adequate amount of water will benefit the body in the following ways: endocrine gland function improves, fluid retention is alleviated, liver functions improve, increasing the percentage of fat used for energy, natural thirst returns, metabolic functions improve, nutrients are distributed throughout the body, body-temperature regulation improves, and blood volume is maintained.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of water for human health and its various benefits.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1832
content: The body cannot adapt to dehydration, which impairs every physiologic function. A fluid loss of even 2% of body weight will adversely affect circulatory functions and decrease performance levels. Thirst alone is a poor indicator of how much water is needed. Athletes consistently consume inadequate fluid volume, managing to replace approximately 50% of sweat losses.
summary: This chunk highlights the negative consequences of dehydration and the importance of adequate fluid intake for athletes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1833
content: Consume 14 to 22 ounces of fluid 2 hours before exercise. Drink 6 to 12 ounces of fluid for every 15 to 20 minutes of exercise. If exercise exceeds 60 minutes, use of a sports drink (containing up to 8% carbohydrate) can replace both fluid and dwindling muscle glycogen stores. When exercising for less than 60 minutes, water is the experts’ choice for fluid replacement.
summary: This chunk provides specific recommendations for fluid intake before, during, and after exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1834
content: The goal is to replace sweat and urine losses. Ingest 16 to 24 ounces of fluid for every pound of body weight lost after an exercise bout, especially if rapid rehydration is necessary, as in twice-a-day training.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of replacing fluid losses after exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1835
content: For fat loss, make small decreases in food and beverage calories and increase physical activity. For fat loss, distribute protein, carbohydrate, and fat throughout the day and at each meal. For fat loss, consume less than 10% of calories from saturated fat. For fat loss, choose whole grains and fiber-rich fruits and vegetables over refined grains and simple sugars. For fat loss, limit alcohol consumption. For fat loss, schedule no fewer than four and as many as six meals a day. For fat loss, avoid empty calories and highly processed foods. For fat loss, drink plenty of water (minimum of 9 to 13 cups per day). For fat loss, have clients weigh and measure food for at least 1 week.
summary: This chunk provides a comprehensive list of recommendations for fat loss.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1836
content: For lean body mass gain, eat four to six meals a day. For lean body mass gain, spread protein intake throughout the day. For lean body mass gain, ingest protein and carbohydrate within 90 minutes of a workout to increase recovery and protein synthesis. For lean body mass gain, do not neglect the importance of carbohydrate and fat.
summary: This chunk outlines strategies for maximizing lean body mass gain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1837
content: Carbohydrates are necessary nutrients. Carbohydrates provide energy for the body, metabolism of fats, spare muscle proteins, and provide essential fiber, vitamins, and minerals.
summary: This chunk highlights the essential role of carbohydrates in the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1838
content: For lean body mass gain, spread protein intake throughout the day. For lean body mass gain, ingest protein and carbohydrate within 90 minutes of a workout to increase recovery and protein synthesis. For lean body mass gain, do not neglect the importance of carbohydrate and fat.
summary: Strategies for maximizing lean body mass gain include distributing protein intake, consuming protein and carbohydrates post-workout, and ensuring adequate carbohydrate and fat intake.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1839
content: Carbohydrates are necessary nutrients. Carbohydrates provide energy for the body, metabolism of fats, spare muscle proteins, and provide essential fiber, vitamins, and minerals. Selecting carbohydrates that are moderate- to low-glycemic foods and high in fiber can help with satiety, blood sugar regulation, and energy balance. Overconsumption of sugar, refined processed carbohydrates, and high-glycemic foods could lead to uncontrolled spikes in blood sugar, low energy, and increased appetite.
summary: Carbohydrates are essential nutrients with various functions, including energy provision, fat metabolism, and nutrient delivery. Choosing complex carbohydrates and limiting refined sugars promotes health and weight management.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1840
content: To avoid hunger, it is advised to choose unprocessed, whole-food carbohydrate sources such as vegetables, starchy vegetables, whole fruit, and grains to provide fiber, vitamins, and minerals for healthy weight loss. Carbohydrate is imperative to glycogen repletion before, during, and after exercise for strength, power, aerobic and anaerobic performance, and conditioning. The Acceptable Macronutrient Distribution Range for carbohydrate intake for an adult is 45 to 65% of total caloric intake.
summary: Whole, unprocessed carbohydrates are crucial for satiety, nutrient intake, and athletic performance. Carbohydrates should comprise 45-65% of daily caloric intake.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1841
content: Weight gain is a result of eating more calories than you burn on a regular basis, not when you eat. The body does not have an enzyme with a watch that after 7 p.m. preferentially stores items, especially carbohydrates, as fat. As long as you do not exceed your calorie limit, weight gain will not occur.
summary: Weight gain is determined by caloric intake exceeding expenditure, regardless of meal timing. The body does not store fat preferentially at night.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1842
content: Neither a low-fat and high-carbohydrate or a high-protein and low-carbohydrate diet is superior for weight loss. Weight loss is achieved when calories consumed are less than calories expended. One study examined weight loss in obese subjects using four popular weight-loss diets: the Atkins diet, the Zone, Weight Watchers, and the Ornish plan. The weight-loss average among subjects was the same after 1 year regardless of the diet program.
summary: Weight loss is primarily driven by caloric deficit, not specific macronutrient ratios. Different diets may yield similar results over time.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1843
content: The more restrictive diets such as the Atkins very low carbohydrate, high-fat, and high-protein diet and the Ornish plan high-carbohydrate and very low fat diet had higher dropout rates. For those who completed the study, weight loss after a year was approximately 5 to 7 pounds regardless of the diet plan. The more restricted the diet, e.g., Atkins and Ornish, the harder it was for subjects to adhere to (i.e., the higher the dropout rate).
summary: More restrictive diets often have higher dropout rates, suggesting that sustainability is crucial for long-term weight management.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1844
content: Because of individual responses, it is not appropriate to recommend “one” weight-loss plan for the entire population. The primary goal in weight loss is to improve health by lowering body fat while maintaining or increasing the proportion of FFM and muscle tissue. If FFM and muscle can be increased during weight loss, it is easier to maintain RMR and fat loss. Maintaining muscle is also important to strength and the ability to perform physical activities of daily living.
summary: Personalized weight loss plans are essential, prioritizing health improvements and preserving muscle mass for metabolic and functional benefits.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1845
content: The majority of daily caloric expenditure is not in the time spent exercising but in the total energy expenditure during 24 hours. Approximately 3,500 calories equals a pound of body fat, so to lose 1 to 2 pounds per week, a client must maintain an average caloric deficit of 500 to 1,000 calories per day. However, a person may burn 250 calories from exercise and spend the rest of the day participating in sedentary activities. Calories that are not used for energy production are stored as fat.
summary: Calorie expenditure throughout the day, not just exercise, determines weight loss. A 500-1000 calorie deficit per day is generally recommended for healthy weight loss.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1846
content: Therefore, a person can eat 100 calories a day more than what their body needs to maintain, and in the course of 35 days, theoretically they will gain a pound of fat. Even a mere 10 extra calories a day over daily maintenance needs could add up to a 1 pound of weight gain over 350 days! Most nutrition experts do not recommend an energy intake any lower than 1,200 calories and even that may be too low for an active or very large person.
summary: Even small daily caloric surpluses can lead to weight gain over time. Calorie intake should be individualized and not fall below recommended minimums.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1847
content: Very low calorie diets (VLCD) should be followed only under the supervision of a medical professional. VLCD formulas are commercially prepared formulas designed to promote rapid weight loss in obese patients. These formulas, usually liquid shakes or bars, replace all food intake for several weeks or months. VLCD formulas need to contain appropriate levels of vitamins and micronutrients to ensure that patients meet their nutritional requirements. People on a VLCD consume about 800 calories per day or less.
summary: VLCDs are medically supervised weight loss programs that require careful monitoring due to their restrictive nature.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1848
content: VLCD formulas are not the same as meal replacements sold at grocery stores or pharmacies, which are meant to substitute for one or two meals a day.
summary: VLCDs differ from meal replacements in their intended use and calorie restriction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_1849
content: VLCD formulas are commercially prepared formulas designed to promote rapid weight loss in obese patients. These formulas, usually liquid shakes or bars, replace all food intake for several weeks or months. VLCD formulas need to contain appropriate levels of vitamins and micronutrients to ensure that patients meet their nutritional requirements. People on a VLCD consume about 800 calories per day or less. VLCD formulas are not the same as meal replacements sold at grocery stores or pharmacies, which are meant to substitute for one or two meals a day.
