Exercise helps prevent, postpone, and treat many chronic health problems, and regular physical activity has been shown to reduce symptoms of many emotional health problems (6–8). Stress reduction is one of the primary reasons people stick to an exercise program. Ask clients about their stress levels and suggest that exercise might be beneficial. After personal trainers have established a good first impression and discussed health concerns, it is now important to talk with their clients about their fitness goals. It is important to understand what fitness improvements clients hope to achieve and to clarify what clients mean by feeling better, being stronger, improving appearance, and being fit. These kinds of phrases are used frequently but mean different things to different people.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of discussing health concerns in detail, building rapport, and understanding a client's fitness goals and motivations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2044
content: Stress reduction is one of the primary reasons people stick to an exercise program. Ask clients about their stress levels and suggest that exercise might be beneficial.
summary: Exercise can reduce stress, motivating clients to stick with a program.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2045
content: After personal trainers have established a good first impression and discussed health concerns, it is now important to talk with their clients about their fitness goals. It is important to understand what fitness improvements clients hope to achieve and to clarify what clients mean by feeling better, being stronger, improving appearance, and being fit. These kinds of phrases are used frequently but mean different things to different people. It is important to clarify with clients what they mean rather than trying to interpret what they mean. It is also important to have clients verbalize their goals, not only to clarify their goals for the personal trainer, but also to clarify their goals for themselves.
summary: Personal trainers should discuss fitness goals with clients, clarifying their meaning and encouraging verbalization.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2046
content: It is important for personal trainers to have an understanding of how to set goals and be able to work with their clients around setting goals. Clients often come up with unrealistic goals, and as a personal trainer, it is important to be able to recognize what is unrealistic for a client. Unrealistic goals lead to demanding exercise programs that may be well beyond a client’s ability, lifestyle, time limitations, or initial fitness level. This will often result in a client dropping out of an exercise program or possible injury. Personal trainers should use experience and expertise when helping their client to set goals. These goals should be Specific, Measurable, Attainable, Realistic, and Timely (known as SMART goals). Encourage clients to set goals that include physical skills, mental skills, and psychological benefits of exercise. Personal trainers should suggest that clients record their goals on paper and learn to refer to them frequently.
summary: Personal trainers should guide clients in setting SMART goals that are realistic and encompass various aspects of well-being.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2047
content: Personal trainers should work with their clients to break down goals into small steps so their clients can accomplish them in a fairly short period of time. Accomplishing goals in a short period will help clients to feel successful (9). There are two types of goals: process and product. It is most important to help a client focus on process goals. A client has more control over process goals than product goals. Tracking success in the process can be very motivating for a client. Something as simple as completing an exercise session represents the completion of a goal when the goal is participation.
summary: Break down goals into smaller steps, focusing on process goals for client motivation and success.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2048
content: It is important for personal trainers to determine when they will revisit with their clients to discuss their goals. Because every client is different, some clients progress more quickly than others, so it is important to discuss with each client when and how goals will be revisited and reevaluated. It is important to let clients know that everyone progresses differently and that is okay. It is also important to let clients know that it is okay to revise goals, add goals, or delete goals if necessary.
summary: Regularly revisit and adjust goals based on individual client progress and needs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2049
content: To set up a well-rounded exercise plan it is important to understand a client’s health concerns and fitness goals as well as to review previous exercise experiences. In discussing previous exercise experiences with a client, it is important to talk about not only the positive experiences but the negative experiences; what works and what does not work. Personal trainers should have a conversation with their clients about what types of things have worked in the past and what factors may have helped them stick with an exercise program. Some clients may have to go back and have a discussion from when they were younger or when they were a child. A client may not have current exercise history but asking a client to remember being physically active when he or she was a child can elicit positive memories and helps a client to remember that he or she has been successful. Personal trainers are encouraged to talk with their clients about their positive experiences. Usually those positive experiences can be replicated into a new experience. If a client struggles to find something positive from past exercise experiences try asking the following questions: Did you ever feel good after working out? Did you ever notice having more energy during the day? Did you sleep better at night? Did you feel more relaxed? Were you able to think more clearly? Some follow-up questions might be: Was exercise more successful at a certain time of the day? Was exercise more successful because of attending a class? Have you tried listening to music during exercise? Is it easier to stick to an exercise program when exercising with other people? What made past exercise experiences positive? What led to your quitting?
summary: Understanding a client's past exercise experiences, both positive and negative, is crucial for creating a successful program.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2050
content: As personal trainers talk about previous exercise experiences with their clients there are usually a lot of reasons why a past exercise program did not work. Some of these reasons may be excuses, but others may be barriers that are difficult for a client to overcome. It is important for clients to have this information as an understanding of what factors could inhibit a new exercise program. By anticipating problems personal trainers can help their clients be more emotionally prepared to cope. At this point in the conversation clients may mention several important factors that led to them dropping out of exercise: the exercise program was too difficult, they get injured, if the exercise program takes too much time, or if life gets too complicated.
summary: Identifying past barriers to exercise helps anticipate and address potential challenges in a new program.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2051
content: It is important for clients to have this information as an understanding of what factors could inhibit a new exercise program. Personal trainers should keep in mind that clients may drop out of exercise programs if they get injured, if the exercise program takes too much time, or if life gets too complicated. Clients may drop out of exercise programs if they get injured, if the exercise program takes too much time, or if life gets too complicated. Personal trainers should check in with clients frequently about whether or not they are feeling injured, if the exercise program takes too much time, or if life gets too complicated.
summary: Understanding potential barriers to exercise adherence is crucial for trainers to anticipate client challenges and provide support.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2052
content: Personal trainers should have a good sense of their client’s health concerns, fitness goals, and past positive and negative exercise experiences. The basics of an exercise plan should be developed during the initial session with a client. Clients should be able to clearly see that a personal trainer has taken all of the information from their conversation to develop their program. Personal trainers should gather input from the client about what activities they want included in their exercise program.
summary: Initial client consultations involve gathering information, setting goals, and collaboratively developing a personalized exercise plan.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2053
content: If a client is interested in developing a strength program, it is important to take them through all training modalities: strength machines, free weights, balls, bands, medicine balls, and kettlebells. Personal trainers should take the client through a detailed fitness assessment process to determine objective information such as body composition, cardiorespiratory fitness, and movement quality to further aid in program design.
summary: Comprehensive strength training programs incorporate various modalities, and fitness assessments inform program design.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2054
content: Personal trainers should perform fitness assessments where the client has some sense of privacy and out of the viewing eyes of other gym patrons. Fitness assessments may need to be performed on a following day if dealing with time constraints.
summary: Fitness assessments should be conducted in a private and convenient manner for the client.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2055
content: Trainers should talk to their clients about whether they are ready to begin, whether they have everything they need, whether they know where they are going, and whether they need more information before the end of the first meeting. Personal trainers should have a conversation with clients who are new to a fitness facility about how to fit in. It may be important to discuss the different kinds of members attending the facility with clients who are new to a fitness facility. Clients will feel more comfortable knowing that there are other people who are similar to them.
summary: Initial consultations should address client readiness, logistics, and social integration within the fitness facility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2056
content: It is important to discuss what clothing is appropriate to wear for exercise with clients who are new to a fitness facility. Many people are not educated on appropriate workout clothing. Having the knowledge that there are other people similar to them and understanding the appropriate workout clothing can help curb any social physique anxiety that clients may have.
summary: Addressing appropriate workout attire and social norms can alleviate client anxieties and promote comfort.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2057
content: Another important item to discuss with any new clients is the effect of other exercisers on their behavior. People increase their effort and performance when others are watching them, a principle called social facilitation. People want to create an impression that they are just as fit as the people exercising around them and may be reluctant to admit that a workout is more strenuous for them than for the person next to them. In self-reported studies, people report lower ratings of perceived exertion (RPE) when they exercise next to a person who gives the impression that the exercise is very easy than when they are alone.
summary: Clients should be informed about social facilitation and its potential influence on their exercise experience.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2058
content: At this point in the conversation clients may mention several important factors that led to them dropping out of exercise: the exercise program was too difficult, they  Some of these reasons may be excuses, but others may be barriers that are difficult for a client to overcome. By anticipating problems personal trainers can help their clients be more emotionally prepared to cope.
summary: Understanding client dropout reasons helps trainers anticipate challenges and provide emotional support.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2059
content: Personal trainers should understand the impact of verbal and nonverbal communication on their successes and failures. Effective communication skills are a learned behavior. Building the relationship with a client starts with the initial conversation, whether that is over the phone or in person. It is important to start building a positive relationship with new clients through proper communication skills regardless of the mode of communication. Explain important policies, procedures, and expectations so clients understand what is expected of them. Be sensitive to clients’ feelings and connect emotionally to them (express empathy). Communicate consistently according to your personality and training style.
summary: Effective communication, including verbal and nonverbal cues, is crucial for building rapport and client trust.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2060
content: Listen carefully, keep the conversation friendly and open, and be as empathetic as possible during the initial session with a client. When talking about health concerns, educate about the dangers of a sedentary lifestyle and the specific health benefits associated with exercise during the initial session with a client. Help clients identify goals that are realistic for them during conversations about exercise goals. Build self-confidence by helping clients identify areas of success but also discuss things that did not work, ensuring clients those factors will be considered when developing their exercise program. Allow clients to be involved in finalizing their exercise program and help them to anticipate what their first day will be like.
summary: Initial client interactions should prioritize active listening, empathy, goal setting, and client empowerment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2061
content: Effective communication is often the difference between success and failure for the relationship between a personal trainer and his or her client.
summary: Strong communication is fundamental to a successful trainer-client relationship.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2062
content: Building the relationship with a client starts with the initial conversation, whether that is over the phone or in person. It is important to start building a positive relationship with new clients through proper communication skills regardless of the mode of communication. Explain important policies, procedures, and expectations so clients understand what is expected of them. Be sensitive to clients’ feelings and connect emotionally to them (express empathy). Communicate consistently according to your personality and training style. Use a positive communication approach that includes encouragement, support, and positive reinforcement. Greet your client with a hello and a smile.
summary: Building a strong client relationship begins with initial communication, emphasizing empathy, clear expectations, and consistent, positive interaction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2063
content: Nonverbal communication reflects what someone is thinking or feeling in their body language. Much nonverbal communication is shown in the face, such as lip movements or changes in the eyes. Other forms of nonverbal communication include physical appearance, posture, gestures, and body position. Humans are programmed to notice small nonverbal changes in other people. The negative effects of nonverbal communication can cause a client to not return.
summary: Nonverbal cues, including facial expressions, posture, and gestures, significantly impact client perception and should be carefully considered.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2064
content: Verbal messages must be clear to be received and interpreted correctly. For clear verbal communication, messages need to be delivered at the right time and place. The listener needs to clarify what they have heard from the speaker, and the speaker needs to approve or reclarify with the listener. Figure 19.2 illustrates a four-step model of communication between a speaker and a listener. The speaker may choose words or phrases that are not completely accurate. A speaker's communication can vary in terms of tone, inflection, and enunciation. What the listener actually hears may be all, some, or very little of what the speaker intended to communicate. The way a listener interprets the words they heard may be different than how the speaker interprets those same words.
summary: Effective verbal communication requires clarity, appropriate timing, active listening, and awareness of potential interpretation differences.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2065
content: Active listening is more than having good communication skills. Active listening is about having an attitude and genuine interest in seeking a client's perspective and getting to know them. Active listening requires paying attention, avoiding distractions, looking the speaker in the eye, and providing feedback only when the speaker has finished. Active listening in communication is not about convincing a client to do something, selling a program, or providing the right information.
summary: Active listening involves genuine interest, focused attention, and understanding the client's perspective rather than simply conveying information.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2066
content: Personal trainers should seek to be respectful and genuinely care for clients, and desire to form partnerships or collaborative relationships with them. A personal trainer's goal should be to build a relationship with their client. It is arguably more important to communicate understanding than to provide the right information. Simple communication strategies can be effective in helping clients feel supported, understood, and comfortable in forming a new relationship. These same simple communication strategies minimize disagreements that may make the relationship with a client uncomfortable. Positive connections with clients give personal trainers the opportunity to be supportive of their client’s pursuit of exercise.
summary: Building a strong client relationship prioritizes understanding, respect, and open communication, fostering a supportive and collaborative partnership.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2067
content: Lifestyle change seldom occurs all at once and without struggle. Gradual lifestyle change is often accompanied by a pattern of backsliding, stopping, and starting over. Collaborative relationships with clients can and will provide the means to be supportive throughout the process.
summary: Lifestyle changes are often gradual and challenging, requiring ongoing support and understanding from the trainer.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2068
content: Closed-ended (directive) questions can be answered with one word. Open-ended (nondirective) questions allow clients to give more information. Open-ended questions cannot be answered with a number, a place, or yes or no. Open-ended questions require clients to elaborate. Open-ended questions are very important for building collaborative relationships with a client because they invite discussion. Open-ended questions invite the client to express personal fears, barriers, failures, and successes.
summary: Open-ended questions encourage client elaboration and foster a collaborative, supportive environment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2069
content: Reflections are conversational techniques that express the purported meaning of what was just heard.
summary: Reflections are a communication technique used to demonstrate understanding and encourage client expression.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2070
content: Open-ended questions cannot be answered with a number, a place, or yes or no. Open-ended questions require clients to elaborate. Open-ended questions are very important for building collaborative relationships with a client because they invite discussion. Open-ended questions invite the client to express personal fears, barriers, failures, and successes.
summary: Open-ended questions are crucial for building rapport and understanding clients' perspectives.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2071
content: Reflections are conversational techniques that express the purported meaning of what was just heard. Reflections are rarely used in conversations but they are both subtle and powerful. Reflections express caring and communicate understanding. Reflection is an opportunity to make sure that what a client says is accurately understood, and it gives a client an opportunity to ensure they said what they thought or felt. Reflections are powerful because they show that active listening is occurring.
summary: Reflections are a powerful active listening technique that demonstrate understanding and encourage clarification.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2072
content: Summaries are a series of reflections. If a trainer has done a good job reflecting with clients, then summaries should be pretty simple. Summaries draw all the important points of the conversation together and again allow clients to clarify either what they have said or how someone has interpreted what they have said. Summaries show greater depth of listening throughout an entire conversation.
summary: Summaries synthesize key points from a conversation, ensuring accurate understanding and demonstrating attentive listening.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2073
content: Affirmations show appreciation for clients and their strengths. It is important to genuinely affirm something the client personally values. Affirming a client’s workout clothes, sneakers, or heart rate monitor might seem appropriate, but generally, people will feel more validated by positive comments about their thoughts, plans, or skills. Compliments typically have an evaluative judgment implicit within them. Compliments usually begin with 'I' statements. Changing 'I' statements to 'you' statements relocates the affirmation from an external advantage point to an internal client attribute. An affirmation communicates an appreciation of clients for who they are.
summary: Affirmations express genuine appreciation for clients' qualities and efforts, fostering a sense of value and encouragement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2074
content: Personal trainers often times have the knowledge of what information is important and helpful for a client, but it is equally important to recognize as well that they do not always have the right information to share. Clients are paying personal trainers to share information with them. Before sharing information with a client, it is important to ask permission to share that information. Asking permission to give advice softens the trainer’s role as an authority figure, and supports a partnership in the decision-making process. Imposing information on a client is likely to provoke resistance. Sometimes trainers may have information that they think will be beneficial for a client’s success; however, it is important to ask permission, particularly if clients have not asked for the information.
summary: Effective communication involves sharing information responsibly, respecting client autonomy, and fostering a collaborative approach.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2075
content: Provide information by using examples of work with other clients. Give permission for clients to disagree with or disregard advice. Provide a menu of options.
summary: Sharing information effectively involves providing examples, respecting client autonomy, and offering choices.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2076
content: A personal trainer’s effectiveness depends on his or her ability to communicate with all types of people, clients, and colleagues. Personal trainers get paid to give good advice. The need to give a lot of good advice has gotten in the way of being able to listen and the ability to get to know clients and what their needs are. Research has proven that what is more important than advice is how people engage and connect with clients.
summary: Effective personal training prioritizes building relationships and understanding client needs over simply providing advice.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2077
content: Engagement involves building a foundation with clients, listening and being able to understand their needs and wants and who they are overall as a person. Connecting with a client involves deepening all of the skills necessary for engagement including active listening, asking questions, reflecting, and affirming a client’s beliefs.
summary: Engagement and connection with clients are essential for successful training relationships.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2078
content: Social networks can strongly influence behavior and beliefs. People who are trying to change their exercise behavior and who have a strong social support fare better. Social support can include family, friends, coworkers, personal trainers, and other people who provide encouragement, support, accountability, and companionship. Social support is one of the strongest predictors of exercise adherence.
summary: Social support plays a crucial role in exercise adherence and behavior change.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2079
content: During the first session with a new client, personal trainers should discuss the amount and type of social support the client may need or have.
summary: Assessing and discussing social support is important during the initial client consultation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2080
content: Social networks can strongly influence behavior and beliefs. People who are trying to change their exercise behavior and who have a strong social support fare better. Social support can include family, friends, coworkers, personal trainers, and other people who provide encouragement, support, accountability, and companionship. Social support is one of the strongest predictors of exercise adherence. During the first session with a new client, personal trainers should discuss the amount and type of social support the client may need or have.
summary: Social support is crucial for exercise adherence, and trainers should assess and discuss a client's support network during initial consultations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2081
content: When talking about prior exercise successes, people may mention or attribute their past exercise successes to a workout buddy, a family member, or a friend. Some questions that might be asked include: Were other people involved in your past successes? Did family and friends help you stick to your exercise program? Who was the most important person that added to your exercise success? Inevitably exercise adherence and success can be partially attributed to social support. Some clients may have a strong social support network whereas other clients may have a limited number of connections.
summary: Past exercise successes often involve social support, and trainers should assess a client's existing network during consultations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2082
content: For clients who have a strong social support network, have a conversation with them about how to use the social support they have available. For clients who have a limited social support network or no social support network, trainers should help clients find the social support they need to adhere to an exercise program. There are usually several opportunities within a fitness facility that will assist personal trainers in helping clients find areas of social support. One very simple way of helping a client find social support is to introduce them to other staff and members at the fitness facility. It is important for a client new to exercise to know people in their environment.
summary: Trainers should help clients leverage existing social support or find new support within the fitness community.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2083
content: Trainers can also design small-group training classes and encourage their existing clients to invite friends, coworkers, or family members. Lastly, provide informal orientation sessions for groups of new members, helping to connect them. Although people with a strong social support fare better with exercise adherence, many people do enjoy exercising alone. Generally these people use exercise to get away from people, to spend some time alone, to think, or to destress and relax. It is important to respect their need for solitude and privacy. People who like to exercise alone may have strong social support at work or home that enables them to exercise regularly.
summary: Trainers can facilitate social connections through group classes and orientations, while respecting individual preferences for solo exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2084
content: Helping clients to determine the kind and amount of social support they need to adhere to exercise is obtained in a conversation about past successful and unsuccessful exercise experiences. It is difficult for people to connect their successful exercise experiences to social support, just as it may be difficult for some clients to connect their unsuccessful exercise experiences to lack of social support. It is important to help clients make that connection.
summary: Discussions about past exercise experiences can help clients identify the role of social support in their successes and failures.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2085
content: There are various kinds of support that help clients reach their health and fitness goals, and each of these support mechanisms has a significant impact on a client’s successes or failures. Some of these support mechanisms include instrumental support, emotional support, informational support, and companionship support. Instrumental support is the tangible and practical factors necessary to help a person adhere to exercise or achieve exercise goals. Some examples include transportation to a fitness facility, a babysitter, or a spotter for weightlifting at the gym. Components of instrumental support that are unsupported actually turn into barriers. It is practical to have a conversation with a client around these factors.
summary: Different types of support (instrumental, emotional, informational, companionship) contribute to exercise adherence, and trainers should discuss these with clients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2086
content: Emotional support is expressed through encouragement, caring, empathy, and concern. Praising a client for his or her efforts, encouraging a client to work harder, and sympathizing with a client when he or she complains about sore muscles are all examples of emotional support. Emotional support enhances self-esteem and reduces anxiety. Affirmations affirm intent and effort. Affirmations should be genuine and affirm something of value to clients.
summary: Emotional support, including affirmations, can boost client morale and motivation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2087
content: Informational support includes the directions, advice, or suggestions given to clients about how to exercise and the feedback regarding their progress. Informational support can come from formal sources such as personal trainers, group exercise instructors, or other health and fitness professionals or it can come from informal sources such as family and friends who share their own exercise experiences.
summary: Informational support encompasses guidance on exercise techniques and progress feedback from various sources.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2088
content: Companionship support includes the availability of family, friends, and coworkers with whom clients can exercise. Companionship during exercise produces positive feelings and may distract people from negative exercise-related feelings such as fatigue, pain, and boredom. Companionship also provides a sense of camaraderie and accountability. The positive effects of a supportive spouse or partner on exercise behavior have been consistently demonstrated in published research.
summary: Companionship support during exercise enhances motivation and reduces negative feelings, with a strong emphasis on the benefits of a supportive partner.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2089
content: Informational support can come from formal sources such as personal trainers, group exercise instructors, or other health and fitness professionals or it can come from informal sources such as family and friends who share their own exercise experiences.
summary: Informational support for exercise can come from both formal professionals and informal sources like friends and family.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2090
content: Companionship support includes the availability of family, friends, and coworkers with whom clients can exercise. Companionship during exercise produces positive feelings and may distract people from negative exercise-related feelings such as fatigue, pain, and boredom. Companionship also provides a sense of camaraderie and accountability.
summary: Companionship support during exercise comes from having workout buddies and offers emotional benefits and accountability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2091
content: The positive effects of a supportive spouse or partner on exercise behavior have been consistently demonstrated in published research. Healthy married adults who joined a fitness program with their spouse had significantly better attendance and were less likely to drop out of the program than married people who joined without their spouse.
summary: A supportive spouse or partner can significantly improve exercise adherence and program completion rates.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2092
content: When family members pressure or make loved ones feel guilty about exercise, that person may actually respond by exercising less. This is known as behavioral reactants. Individuals who feel controlled by others and pressured to exercise may do the opposite. Negative support of exercise may include overprotectiveness. Family members may be overprotective of people with chronic disease or disability. Parents may be overprotective of their children’s participation in physical activities that are perceived to have a high risk of injury.
summary: Negative support, such as pressure or overprotectiveness, can hinder exercise behavior.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2093
content: Other forms of negative support may include family members providing a sense of guilt because exercise is taking away from family time and family obligations.
summary: Negative support can also come from guilt-inducing messages about exercise interfering with family commitments.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2094
content: Parental support at a young age is important for a developing child. It is important for a child to grow up understanding the importance of exercise and the negative impact of inactivity. Children need parents who model healthy and appropriate physical activity.
summary: Early parental support is crucial for instilling a lifelong appreciation for exercise and healthy habits.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2095
content: It provides a sense of routine and familiarity for children that can last a lifetime. Besides being important for parents to model healthy and appropriate physical activity, it is important for children to be exposed to a variety of physical activities and for parents to empower their children with the ability to be able to choose the appropriate physical activities for themselves.
summary: Parental modeling and exposure to diverse activities help children develop a positive relationship with exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2096
content: An exercise leader plays a role similar to that of a personal trainer. A big part of this job is engaging, connecting, and getting to know the members in the class.
summary: Exercise leaders, like personal trainers, build rapport and connections with class members.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2097
content: Group exercise instructors should introduce themselves, ask and remember members’ names, acknowledge new class participants and ensure they are properly set up for class, introduce new class participants to veteran class members, talk to members about their goals and expectations, and check in with members after class.
summary: Effective group exercise instructors prioritize member engagement and individual attention.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2098
content: Compassionate exercise leaders generally have a positive social influence on exercisers and may contribute to increases in their self-confidence, enjoyment, and motivation to exercise.
summary: Compassionate exercise leaders positively impact participants' self-esteem, enjoyment, and motivation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2099
content: Group exercise participants who experienced a socially supportive leadership style reported greater self-efficacy, more energy and enthusiasm, less social physique anxiety, more enjoyment of the class and the ability to try new things, and greater confidence. These results indicate that a socially supportive group exercise instructor can have positive effects on participants in a single exercise class.
summary: Socially supportive group exercise instructors lead to increased self-efficacy, enjoyment, and confidence in participants.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2100
content: As role models, it is important that group exercise instructors use their influence to promote healthy and balanced attitudes and behaviors toward exercise. Instructors who exercise even when they are ill or exercise excessively may not be sending the appropriate message.
summary: Group exercise instructors should model healthy exercise habits and avoid promoting unhealthy extremes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2101
content: A sense of cohesion in a group exercise setting is related to whether or not an individual adheres to an exercise program. Some principles shown to increase group cohesiveness include having a group distinctiveness and giving group members a sense of belonging.
summary: Group cohesion, fostered through distinctiveness and belonging, contributes to exercise adherence.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2102
content: The instructor's role is clearest in the largest and smallest classes. In large classes participants expect a group-oriented approach to teaching whereby the instructor addresses and reinforces the group as a whole. In a small class participants expect and usually receive a more individually oriented approach to teaching whereby the instructor addresses and reinforces individual members of the group.
summary: Instructional approach adapts to class size, with larger classes benefiting from a group-oriented approach and smaller classes from individual attention.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2103
content: Research has shown that in a medium-sized group exercise class it may be unclear whether a group- or individual-oriented approach is best. Research suggests that there may be discrepancies between what participants expect and what approach is used by the instructor in a medium-sized class. Instructors may also be unsure about which approach is best in a medium-sized class and use both styles inconsistently. Inconsistency in approach in a medium-sized class could contribute to dissatisfaction with the class and the instructor.
summary: In medium-sized classes, instructors may face challenges in balancing group and individual attention, potentially leading to participant dissatisfaction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2104
content: The characteristics of the people in a group exercise class can affect the exercise experience of everyone in the group. Some characteristics that affect an individual's experience include sex, assimilation, and enthusiasm. The gender makeup of a group exercise class is one group composition factor that can affect an exerciser's experience. Women often report feeling uncomfortable in an environment that is made up of primarily men.
summary: Group composition, including gender dynamics, can influence the exercise experience of individuals within a class.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2105
content: In a study with group exercise participants who were enthusiastic, encouraging of one another, and socially interactive, most participants reported greater enjoyment and stronger intentions to join an exercise group. Feeling similar to other group members in a group exercise setting can affect comfort level and motivation. People would prefer to exercise in an environment with people who are of similar body type and similar ability. Feeling less competent in a group exercise setting can diminish an exerciser's self-confidence and motivation.
summary: A supportive and cohesive group exercise environment enhances enjoyment, motivation, and intentions to participate.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2106
content: Some characteristics that affect an individual's experience include sex, assimilation, and enthusiasm. The gender makeup of a group exercise class is one group composition factor that can affect an exerciser's experience. Women often report feeling uncomfortable in an environment that is made up of primarily men. Some health clubs have decided to offer classes for women only.
summary: Group composition, particularly gender, can influence individual exercise experiences and comfort levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2107
content: Inconsistency in approach in a medium-sized class could contribute to dissatisfaction with the class and the instructor. It is important for the group exercise instructor to develop a positive, socially supportive leadership style and for the class to create a cohesive group environment to help create a more comfortable group exercise experience that may prevent exercisers from quitting.
summary: Instructor consistency and a supportive class environment are crucial for participant satisfaction and retention.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2108
content: Some first-time exercisers reported concerns about embarrassing themselves and being evaluated by other members in the group. For this group, encouragement and attention from other group members elicited feelings of self-consciousness and anxiety. For people who are new to exercise it may be important to help them focus on mastering the exercise skills before introducing them to an enthusiastic group.
summary: First-time exercisers may benefit from a less intense and more supportive group environment initially.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2109
content: Sticking to a regular exercise schedule is not easy, especially for somebody new to exercise. There are plenty of potential barriers and obstacles for an individual to overcome, such as a busy schedule, a poor social support network, and low self-confidence. The most frequent reason given for not exercising is lack of time (19). Usually a closer look at an exerciser’s schedule reveals that what people perceive as a lack of time is not in reality. One technique that exercise psychologists use with clients is a time journal. A time journal allows clients to keep track of what they do during the day for 3 or 4 days to gain a general sense of how the exerciser uses his or her time. In most cases the problem lies in how the exerciser prioritizes the day. For example, in many cases free time is used to watch television, to surf the Web, or to send e-mails. Being able to bring awareness to how much time an exerciser spends participating in these types of activities is important and can shed light on where to cut back to make time for physical activity (19, 20).
summary: Common barriers to exercise include time constraints, social support, and self-confidence. Time management strategies and awareness of time allocation can help overcome these obstacles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2110
content: When done properly goal setting is designed to help increase motivation, to build self-esteem, and for an exerciser to see and feel successful. Unrealistic goals, on the other hand, lower motivation, decrease self-esteem, and do not provide the exerciser a sense of success. It is not uncommon for someone to try and make up for missing weeks or years of exercise by engaging in unrealistic and intense exercise programs that lead to unnecessary injury. It is important for personal trainers to help their clients define what is realistic. It is very difficult for people who are newer to exercise to understand what is realistic and what is not realistic. After weeks, months, or years, something has triggered your client to start exercising but that does not mean that he or she had any sense of how to start, what to do, or what is necessary to sustain exercise for a lifetime of health. In fact, many people in this position start off way too fast with far too many unrealistic goals. For example, a goal to lose a large amount of weight in a relatively short time is unrealistic and unhealthy. It is your responsibility to talk to your client about setting up a plan for losing weight in a realistic and healthy way (4).
summary: Effective goal setting is crucial for motivation and adherence. Realistic goals, tailored to individual needs and capabilities, are essential for long-term success.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2111
content: Social support is probably the most important type of social influence on exercise. Social support is the perceived physical and emotional comfort that a person receives from others (18). There is evidence to support the motivational and adherence value. There are various types of social support: instrumental, emotional, informational, and companionship. Personal trainers need to define what type and amount of social support their clients need to exercise that will help support their current exercise program and their adherence to exercise.
summary: Social support plays a vital role in exercise motivation and adherence. Understanding different types of support and tailoring them to individual needs is essential.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2112
content: Social physique anxiety is a concern with body image. In an exercise setting, social physique anxiety includes a sense of difference in body type and attire preferences.
summary: Social physique anxiety, particularly regarding body image and attire, can be a factor in exercise settings.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2113
content: There is evidence to support the motivational and adherence value. There are various types of social support: instrumental, emotional, informational, and companionship. Personal trainers need to define what type and amount of social support their clients need to exercise that will help support their current exercise program and their adherence to exercise.
summary: Social support is important for exercise adherence. Personal trainers should assess and provide appropriate support to clients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2114
content: Social physique anxiety is a concern with body image. In an exercise setting, social physique anxiety includes a sense of difference in body type and attire preferences. Exercisers who experience social physique anxiety overemphasize the difference between their body type and the body types of others when in a fitness facility. Their perception is that they are more overweight and more out of shape than most others and then less able (capable) to participate in exercise. People in a situation where fitness facilities design exercise programs for people of similar body types do not feel comfortable being singled out.
summary: Social physique anxiety is a barrier to exercise, particularly for those concerned about body image and perceived differences from others.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2115
content: Studies done on women in a group exercise setting and the clothes they wore revealed that women with higher social physique anxiety stood further away from the instructor and wore less revealing clothing. For these women, standing at the back of a group exercise class and wearing long, baggy exercise attire are strategies that they might use to ward off other people seeing and evaluating their bodies. Social physique anxiety is a real problem for many people, particularly those who are new to exercise.
summary: Women with social physique anxiety may exhibit behaviors like standing further back in class and wearing more concealing clothing.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2116
content: As a personal trainer, you should try to work with your client to normalize their body type. One suggestion is introducing your client to people of similar body types who have become comfortable in a similar exercise environment. For a client who is dealing with social physique anxiety, it is vital to talk to your client about appropriate attire for an exercise environment and where to buy that clothing. A client new to exercise may not know what attire is appropriate for exercise nor will they know where to buy the appropriate attire.
summary: Personal trainers can help clients with social physique anxiety by normalizing body types, suggesting appropriate attire, and connecting them with others.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2117
content: Access to facilities, classes, personal trainers, and instructors may be limited based on physical location or lack of adequate financial resources. Sometimes convenience of the physical location of a fitness facility is based on an individual’s perception rather than the actual proximity of the facility. Examine all options available to clients, and if exercising at home will help some clients, come up with a program that is affordable and easy to do at home outside of an exercise facility.
summary: Accessibility to exercise resources can be limited by location, finances, and perception. Trainers should explore options and create programs that fit individual needs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2118
content: There are many barriers to exercise. Some of those barriers to exercise include time, unrealistic goals, lack of social support, social physique anxiety, and convenience of exercising. Some of these barriers are real and some are perceived. For clients to adhere to exercise, they need to know their barriers, understand how those barriers affect their exercise, and have a plan to overcome them. Talk to clients about what gets in the way of their being able to exercise and help them find ways of dealing with those things that get in the way.
summary: Exercise adherence is hindered by various barriers, both real and perceived. Trainers should help clients identify and overcome these barriers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2119
content: Starting and continuing an exercise program can be a challenging yet rewarding experience. Many individuals who attempt a new exercise regimen drop out within the first 6 months. Personal trainers should become aware of behavioral and cognitive factors that can improve exercise adherence and lasting lifestyle changes for their clients.
summary: Exercise adherence is often challenging, with many individuals dropping out within the first six months. Trainers should focus on behavioral and cognitive factors to improve adherence.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2120
content: Behavioral strategies aim to change a client’s behaviors and actions to improve exercise adherence and maintain a physically active lifestyle. Behavioral strategies include self-management, goal setting, and self-monitoring.
summary: Behavioral strategies, including self-management, goal setting, and self-monitoring, can improve exercise adherence.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2121
content: Self-management refers to individuals managing their own behaviors, thoughts, and emotions. Self-management skills improve an individual’s ability to look at his or her behaviors, thoughts, and emotions and to change whatever is not working. Self-management of behaviors might include setting up an initial exercise program that is realistic and easy to follow. Managing individual behaviors, thoughts, and feelings incorporates looking at how individuals see life and the way they talk to themselves.
summary: Self-management involves managing behaviors, thoughts, and emotions to improve exercise adherence. It includes setting realistic goals and addressing negative self-talk.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2122
content: Effective self-management skills maximize self-control energy, channeling it into the most important areas. Self-management skills increase the likelihood that desired changes in behavior will occur. These skills help clients change their environments in ways that support their exercise program adherence and help clients cope with difficulties that might otherwise short-circuit plans to exercise regularly. Self-management skills are rarely taught, and it is generally not until clients have had an experience in which they need self-management training that they look for help.
summary: Effective self-management skills are crucial for exercise adherence. They help individuals channel their energy, overcome obstacles, and maintain consistency.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2123
content: Goal setting is a process that involves assessing one’s current level of fitness or performance; creating a specific, measurable, realistic, and challenging goal for one’s future level of fitness or performance; and detailing the actions to be taken to achieve that goal. Initial goal-setting sessions with new clients should be focused on coming up with long-term goals first. Clients' goals should be an extension of their values to maintain focus, passion, and inspiration. Using the following questions may help clients determine their long-term goals: What do I want to accomplish in 6 months? What do I want to accomplish in the next year? What do I want to accomplish in the next 5 years? What is my dream accomplishment? Long-term goals are made up of many short-term goals.
summary: Goal setting is a key component of exercise adherence. It involves identifying long-term aspirations, breaking them down into smaller, achievable goals, and aligning them with personal values.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2124
content: Goal setting is a process that involves assessing one’s current level of fitness or performance; creating a specific, measurable, realistic, and challenging goal for one’s future level of fitness or performance; and detailing the actions to be taken to achieve that goal. Initial goal-setting sessions with new clients should be focused on coming up with long-term goals first. Clients' goals should be an extension of their values to maintain focus, passion, and inspiration. Using the following questions may help clients determine their long-term goals: What do I want to accomplish in 6 months? What do I want to accomplish in the next year? What do I want to accomplish in the next 5 years? What is my dream accomplishment? Long-term goals are made up of many short-term goals.
summary: Goal setting involves assessing current fitness, creating specific, measurable, achievable, realistic, and time-bound goals, and outlining action steps. It starts with long-term goals, which are broken down into short-term ones.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2125
content: Short-term goals provide a focused path, motivation, and confidence when the client sees the benefit of achieving each short-term goal. Short-term goals help to focus the client's attention on the present. Clients should narrow down their goals to the most important ones to avoid feeling overwhelmed. Clients' short-term goals should include both physical and mental skills.
summary: Short-term goals provide focus, motivation, and a sense of accomplishment, helping clients stay present and prioritize their objectives.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2126
content: Effective goal setting stems from the acronym SMART. SMART goals are specific, measurable, attainable, realistic, and timely. A specific goal is clearly defined so that anyone could understand the intended outcome. Goals should contain a detailed description of what is to be accomplished, when it is to be accomplished by, and the action(s) that will be taken to accomplish it. Goals need to be quantifiable to assess progress. Attainable goals are challenging but not extreme. Goals that are too easily accomplished do not stretch a client or make them grow as a person because they are not challenging enough. A realistic goal is one that an individual is both willing and able to work towards. A goal is probably realistic if the individual truly believes that it can be accomplished. A goal should always have a specific date of completion. The date should be realistic but not too distant in the future.
summary: SMART goals are specific, measurable, attainable, realistic, and time-bound, ensuring clarity, progress tracking, and a balance between challenge and feasibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2127
content: The last piece of a personal trainer's goal-setting plan should include developing an action plan for how a client will reach their goals. To develop an action plan, answer these questions: What is my objective (goal)? What is the target date to complete my goal? What resources are needed to accomplish my goal (money, time, equipment, people)? What do I need to accomplish on a daily basis to reach my goal? What is the current status of my goal? What are some possible difficulties I may have to overcome to reach my objective?
summary: Developing an action plan involves outlining specific steps, resources, and timelines to achieve the client's goals, addressing potential challenges.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2128
content: Part of the goal-setting process is regularly reviewing a client's goals and the difficulties they may have in reaching their goals. A client's priorities and circumstances may change, and it is important to be able to adjust goals when circumstances change.
summary: Goal-setting is an ongoing process that requires regular review and adjustments based on evolving priorities and circumstances.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2129
content: Goals direct attention to important elements of the skill being performed. Learning what is within a client's control. Goals provide empowerment. Goals provide satisfaction. Goals provide self-confidence. Goals increase focus. Goals increase motivation.
summary: Goals enhance focus, motivation, and self-confidence by providing direction, a sense of control, and a path to achievement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2130
content: Common problems with goal setting include failure to convince exercisers of the importance, setting only outcome-oriented goals, failure to set SMART goals, setting too many goals, not knowing which goals are the priority, failure to adjust goals, and lack of support.
summary: Common pitfalls in goal setting include neglecting the importance of goals, focusing solely on outcomes, not using the SMART framework, setting too many goals, lacking prioritization, failing to adapt goals, and insufficient support.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2131
content: Self-monitoring is usually done in the form of a daily written record of the behavior that a client is trying to change. Clients who are new to exercise may choose to keep an exercise log. An exercise log can have a variety of forms: journal, calendar, or a workout card. It is important for a client to find an effective, individual method of self-monitoring. A trainer may suggest that clients include not only time, exercises, calories expended, and perceived heart rate but also how much sleep they are getting, the kind of food they are eating, and how they are feeling emotionally. This log gives clients the opportunity to look at progress over time. Being able to see progress builds self-confidence and self-esteem, which leads to exercise adherence. The exercise log can be used as a form of accountability.
summary: Self-monitoring, often through an exercise log, helps clients track progress, build self-confidence, and enhance accountability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2132
content: It is important for a client to find an effective, individual method of self-monitoring. A trainer may suggest that clients include not only time, exercises, calories expended, and perceived heart rate but also how much sleep they are getting, the kind of food they are eating, and how they are feeling emotionally. This log gives clients the opportunity to look at progress over time. Being able to see progress builds self-confidence and self-esteem, which leads to exercise adherence. The exercise log can be used as a form of accountability. If clients know you are going to ask them to show their exercise log each week, it can help keep them motivated. Self-monitoring encourages clients to be honest about their activity, which encourages them to stick to an exercise program. Logging a workout serves as a reward.
summary: Self-monitoring through exercise logs helps clients track progress, build confidence, and stay accountable, ultimately improving adherence.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2133
content: Self-monitoring helps clients identify challenging situations and barriers to exercise.
summary: Self-monitoring allows clients to recognize obstacles and difficulties in their exercise journey.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2134
content: Cognitive strategies aim to change a client’s thoughts and attitudes toward exercise and physical activity. Cognitive strategies include positive self-talk, psyching up, and imagery. For someone new to exercise there is a high degree of negativity and negative thoughts around exercise. Some negative thoughts include I can’t do this, it’s too hard, it takes too much time, and it’s too painful. Helping your client to develop positive self-talk is essential. Help your clients become aware of their negative thought process by making a list of any negative thoughts they might have around exercise. Next, help your clients come up with a list of positive thoughts they might use with regard to exercise. Train your clients to notice negative thoughts, stop those negative thoughts, and translate those negative thoughts into something positive from the list of positive thoughts you generated. Another alternative is to generate a list of positive, motivating key words that your clients can use as an awareness tool and in place of negative thoughts to keep them motivated.
summary: Cognitive strategies, including positive self-talk, psyching up, and imagery, help clients overcome negative thoughts and develop a more positive attitude towards exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2135
content: For someone new to exercise it is difficult to be motivated and get psyched up to exercise. It is valuable to give your clients the knowledge and skills to be able to develop the necessary positive energy to exercise. We want new exercisers to feel excited, positive, engaged, and energetic before exercise. These feelings will promote a desire for your client to work out. The dialogue about getting psyched up to exercise should include any techniques your clients currently might use to get psyched up for other situations in their lives. It is important to use outside examples of how your clients get psyched up for work, shopping, or other projects. Using that information with regard to exercise can help make the transition much smoother.
summary: Motivating new exercisers involves helping them develop positive energy and excitement for workouts by drawing on techniques they use in other areas of their lives.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2136
content: Personal trainers should help clients create a list of things that motivate them to exercise. Examples of motivators include positive thoughts, keywords, imagery, specific foods, and music. Not all motivators work in all situations, so trainers should help clients fine-tune what works best for them in different situations, on different days, and at different times. Trainers should continue to have conversations with clients about their motivation levels and what is working and what is not working.
summary: Trainers should work with clients to identify and personalize their motivators, recognizing that what works best can vary depending on the situation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2137
content: Imagery is a form of stimulation that involves all the senses and occurs in the mind. Exercise imagery is the process of creating internalized experiences to support or enhance exercise participation. Imagining oneself exercising with confidence, relaxation, and positive outcomes can foster positive behavior change and goal attainment. Clients can use imagery to manage pain and discomfort and create images of highly focused actions. When developing imagery with a client, trainers should consider if the client has had a similar past positive experience. If a client has a past positive experience, trainers should use that experience as the basis for creating a piece of imagery. It is best to draw from a client's own past experiences to give them a sense of control and feelings associated with overcoming challenges. Trainers should work with clients to write down as much information as they can remember about a past positive experience, including thoughts, feelings, sounds, tastes, smells, and sights. The more vivid the image, the more real it will feel. If a client does not have a past positive experience, trainers can help them develop one by using television shows, videos, or role models. Trainers should understand how these external sources have impacted the client and their rationale for using them.
summary: Exercise imagery, drawing on past positive experiences or external sources, can be a powerful tool for enhancing motivation, managing discomfort, and improving performance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2138
content: It is best to draw from a client's own past experiences to give them a sense of control and feelings associated with overcoming challenges. Trainers should work with clients to write down as much information as they can remember about a past positive experience, including thoughts, feelings, sounds, tastes, smells, and sights. The more vivid the image, the more real it will feel.
summary: Utilizing past positive experiences for motivation

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2139
content: If a client does not have a past positive experience, trainers can help them develop one by using television shows, videos, or role models. Trainers should understand how these external sources have impacted the client and their rationale for using them. Once a trainer has worked with a client to develop a positive, successful piece of imagery, it is time to figure out the best times to use it. Clients can use imagery at any time, particularly when they may not be feeling motivated or positive about exercising. Many clients find it helpful to record their imagery and listen to it in the car or before going to bed.
summary: Developing and utilizing imagery for motivation

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2140
content: There are numerous techniques for helping clients enhance their exercise adherence. The most important aspect of helping clients enhance exercise adherence is to help them find the most appropriate technique for them. Behavioral strategies for enhancing exercise adherence include goal setting and self-monitoring. Cognitive strategies for enhancing exercise adherence include positive self-talk, helping a client to get psyched up for exercise, and visualization. These strategies can be used alone or in combination with each other.
summary: Techniques for enhancing exercise adherence

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2141
content: The psychological benefits of exercise are well documented (6–8). Some proposed psychological benefits of exercise include promoting a positive mood, reducing stress, improving sleep, and reducing depression and anxiety. Many times people who exercise report feeling good after a workout. Feeling good means different things for different people, but it mainly signifies a positive mood encompassing feelings of satisfaction. Clients will often feel energized after exercise, which leads to a more positive outlook on life. Exercise also promotes feelings of relaxation and stress reduction, alertness, and improved ability to concentrate and focus, all leading to a positive mood.
summary: Psychological benefits of exercise

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2142
content: Stress is the outcome of challenging situations in our lives. There are physical symptoms of stress such as headaches and stomachaches, and there are emotional symptoms including frustration, pressure, and uncertainty. Exercise has been shown to be effective at reducing stress and can lead to immediate and long-term results (3). Many people report feeling less angry and irritable as a result of regular exercise. Anger is most strongly associated with harmful health effects such as hypertension and heart disease.
summary: Exercise and stress reduction

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2143
content: For people who exercise as a way of dealing with stress, it is a good idea to exercise during the time of day that is most beneficial. An early-morning workout might prepare someone for the day’s stressors. A midday workout might provide a break in the day’s stress and allow people to feel less angry and irritable throughout the rest of the day. A workout later in the day or early evening can provide a client with a method for decreasing the tensions and worries of the day and an opportunity to refresh before going home and going to sleep.
summary: Optimal timing for exercise to manage stress

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2144
content: Many people report having better sleep when they exercise regularly. Typical improvements include falling asleep more quickly, longer periods of deep sleep, and feeling more refreshed in the morning (3, 32). Because exercise has a positive impact on stress, anxiety, and depression, all of which interfere with sleep, exercise helps improve sleep. Outdoor exercise is beneficial because it also provides light therapy, adding extra sleep benefits.
summary: Exercise and sleep improvement

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2145
content: Important qualities of a good night's sleep include resistance to stress-related illnesses and immune function. A good night's sleep allows you to start the day feeling invigorated and refreshed. A good night's sleep reduces feelings of stress and improves mood. A good night's sleep allows you to experience all the effects of regular exercise.
summary: Benefits of a good night's sleep

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2146
content: Regular exercise is associated with reductions in anxiety and depression. Anxiety refers to feelings of worry, self-doubt, fear, and uncertainty. Trait anxiety is a stable personality trait. If a person is new to exercise and exhibits high trait anxiety, their anxiety around exercise will start off high.
summary: Exercise and mental health

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2147
content: A good night's sleep allows you to experience all the effects of regular exercise. Regular exercise is associated with reductions in anxiety and depression. About one in four Americans suffers from clinical depression. Clinical depression is known for its symptoms of negative mood and feelings of hopelessness, which for many people become so severe they interfere with daily life. Exercise appears to be associated with improved mood in people who have depression. Regular exercise has the greatest impact on depression and is comparable in effectiveness to psychotherapy and medication for mild to moderate depression.
summary: Regular exercise and sufficient sleep contribute to mental well-being by reducing anxiety and depression symptoms.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2148
content: Anxiety refers to feelings of worry, self-doubt, fear, and uncertainty. Trait anxiety is a stable personality trait. If a person is new to exercise and exhibits high trait anxiety, their anxiety around exercise will start off high. If a person is new to exercise and exhibits low trait anxiety, their anxiety around exercise will start off low. State anxiety is situational, temporary feelings of anxiety. Rapid heart rate, sweaty palms, and butterflies in the stomach before a marathon are examples of state anxiety. Some people experience anxiety in certain situations but not all. If a person is new to exercise and exhibits high trait anxiety, their anxiety around exercise will start off high and may continue to increase. If a person is new to exercise and exhibits low trait anxiety, their anxiety around exercise will start off low but will more than likely increase because of the environment.
summary: Anxiety, both trait and state, can influence an individual's experience with exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2149
content: The primary goal of practitioners in an exercise program is to get sedentary or irregularly active individuals to adopt a regular exercise program and keep individuals who are in maintenance active. Some of the first steps in the process for practitioners are to have a basic understanding of what is important to clients, learning to communicate with clients, understanding the stages of change, knowing the benefits of exercise adoption and adherence, and being familiar with clients’ barriers to exercise adoption and adherence to lend support and guidance in motivating clients to regular exercise. Consensus on guidelines to help support the adoption and adherence to an exercise program includes interventions that are applied based on the personal and environmental characteristics and barriers of an individual. Health and fitness professionals should make exercise enjoyable, provide social support, and offer participants a wide range of activities to choose from.
summary: Exercise program practitioners aim to promote exercise adoption and adherence by understanding client needs, providing support, and tailoring interventions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2150
content: Running a successful personal training business, whether as an independent business owner, as an independent contractor, or an employee in a health club setting, is no different than running a successful Fortune 500 business; the motto is always ‘the customer always comes first!’ When profit alone is the primary focus of any business, the source of that profit (nearly always the customer or consumer) at some point will get neglected. Once a business loses sight of what is creating revenue, the revenue will begin to drop if not disappear altogether and threaten the future of the business. Conversely, organizations (and individuals) that possess a fanatical customer focus reinvent themselves continually to provide an ever-growing level of value to their customers. Long-term profit generation is largely the result of creating and keeping customers. Most successful businesses have clearly defined mission and vision statements that define exactly what the business is about and what their goals are; many of them mention ‘customer service.’ Good customer service is all the things that businesses do to attract and keep customers happy that go beyond the day-to-day activities of simply running the business. These activities are intended to increase customer satisfaction. For personal trainers, good customer service starts the second you approach a new prospective client (remember they are sizing you up as well). The ultimate goal of good customer service is to always strive to meet, and when possible exceed, the expectations of your customers. Satisfied and delighted customers are customers who are happy and content, share your name with their friends, and are proud and excited to be your client, and more importantly, customer service is instrumental in the long-term success of any business.
summary: Customer-centric business practices are crucial for long-term success, especially in personal training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2151
content: This chapter provides the personal trainer with the initial steps to take to enter into the personal training profession; basic marketing strategies; and a progressive, customer-focused process for creating a distinctive level of value and developing a highly successful client base. By mastering the OPT™ model, completing NASM’s CPT certification, and applying the tools and solutions learned with clients, you will be able to create or greatly increase your personal level of success. Most personal trainers are passionate about fitness and living a healthy lifestyle. They enjoy learning about new exercises, nutrition requirements, and gym equipment. They believe in this lifestyle so much they’ve decided to make it their profession. However, there is much more to personal training than nutrition and exercise. Personal training requires skills in sales, marketing, and finance. Whether a personal trainer plans to work independently or at a local health club, successfully running a personal training business is no different than starting any other business; it takes time, determination, preparation, and a well thought-out and detailed plan.
