As discussed in chapter 6 (Fitness Assessment), personal trainers must perform detailed and comprehensive fitness assessments to design individualized exercise programs based on their client’s goals, needs, and abilities.
summary: This chapter outlines the essential steps for aspiring personal trainers to establish a successful career, emphasizing the importance of business acumen, client-centered practices, and comprehensive fitness assessments.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2152
content: They believe in this lifestyle so much they’ve decided to make it their profession. However, there is much more to personal training than nutrition and exercise. Personal training requires skills in sales, marketing, and finance. Whether a personal trainer plans to work independently or at a local health club, successfully running a personal training business is no different than starting any other business; it takes time, determination, preparation, and a well thought-out and detailed plan.
summary: Personal training encompasses more than just exercise and nutrition, requiring business acumen and entrepreneurial skills for success.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2153
content: As discussed in chapter 6 (Fitness Assessment), personal trainers must perform detailed and comprehensive fitness assessments to design individualized exercise programs based on their client’s goals, needs, and abilities. Similarly, to have a successful, customer-focused, and profitable career, personal trainers should perform detailed financial assessments and develop strategic business and marketing plans to meet their needs.
summary: Successful personal trainers conduct thorough fitness and business assessments to tailor programs and build a profitable career.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2154
content: The first step in this process is education. Earning a certification from a recognized and accredited organization should be an aspiring fitness trainer’s first goal. Education is vital in teaching individuals the entire scope of practice of personal training, including how to properly assess and instruct their clients through safe and effective workouts. The International Health Racquet & Sportsclub Association (IHRSA) recommends earning a certification from an organization that has either received accreditation or is in the process of receiving accreditation of their programs from an accrediting body such as the National Commission for Certifying Agencies (NCCA) (1).
summary: Formal education and certification are crucial for aspiring fitness trainers to acquire the necessary knowledge and skills.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2155
content: In addition to earning a certification, higher education—such as a college degree in an exercise science related field (e.g., exercise science, exercise physiology, kinesiology, biomechanics, or physical education)—will help enhance marketability, experience, and knowledge regarding health, wellness, and exercise prescription.
summary: Higher education in exercise science complements certification and enhances a personal trainer's expertise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2156
content: Currently there are no government regulations that require. The certifi cation process is complete. There are a number of facilities that allow a gradual development of the skills necessary to become a successful fitness professional. It is important to facilitate the skills learned during the certifi cation course in a pleasant atmosphere where one can make modifi cations in presentation skills, rapport building, and acquire observational skills.
summary: While there are no legal requirements, aspiring trainers can develop their skills through various facilities and practice environments.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2157
content: Some example facilities include YMCA, Jewish Community Centers (JCC), Local Town Recreation & Park Services, Women-only facilities, and Commercial fitness clubs.
summary: Various types of facilities offer opportunities for aspiring fitness professionals to gain experience.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2158
content: Most new professionals become successful when the intangible skills (i.e., building rapport, maintaining basic people skills, and coaching) are developed early on in a career. Many facilities provide the necessary hands-on training needed to help solidify these skills and ensure long-term success.
summary: Developing interpersonal skills and practical experience are crucial for long-term success in the fitness industry.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2159
content: Once the basic characteristics in the fitness professional are harnessed, a more lucrative position in the fitness field is often desired. Although most individuals enter the fitness field with a passion to help others reach healthy goals, a lucrative tenure is usually a foundation for long-term achievement.
summary: Fitness professionals often seek more rewarding positions after gaining experience and establishing a solid foundation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2160
content: A profitable career will help make a personal trainer to earn a certification or college degree. Most gyms and health clubs enforce certification as a minimum requirement. Gyms and health clubs more often are taking IHRSA’s advice, which requires that individuals earn a certification that has earned third-party accreditation. Unaccredited certifications are becoming a thing of the past, and the fitness community is demanding more education from today’s fitness professionals.
summary: Certification is essential for employment in most fitness facilities, with a growing emphasis on accredited programs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2161
content: Personal trainers are health and fitness professionals who perform individualized assessments and design safe, effective, and individualized exercise and conditioning programs that are scientifically valid and based on clinical evidence for clients who have no medical or special needs. They provide guidance to help clients achieve their personal health, fitness, and performance goals via the implementation of exercise programs, nutritional recommendations, and suggestions for lifestyle modification. They hold a current emergency cardiac care (cardiopulmonary resuscitation) certification and respond appropriately during emergency situations. Certified Personal Trainers do not diagnose and/or treat areas of pain or disease, and they refer clients to other health care professionals/practitioners when appropriate. They abide by NASM’s Code of Professional Conduct at all times.
summary: Personal trainers provide individualized exercise programs, nutritional guidance, and emergency care while adhering to ethical standards and referring clients to appropriate healthcare professionals when necessary.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2162
content: A personal trainer may find their profession more enjoyable because it provides financial stability and personal growth. Often, a personal trainer will be required to sell their services to receive monetary rewards (i.e., profit, bonus, or higher salary). Commercial fitness facilities usually rely on revenue growth and member retention for survival. Personal training and other fitness services are sometimes known as 'profit centers' because they add to the base revenue generator. The typical base revenue generator for most clubs is memberships.
summary: Personal training can be a rewarding profession offering financial stability and personal growth, often requiring sales skills to generate revenue for fitness facilities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2163
content: In a commercial fitness facility setting, the personal trainer will be expected to regularly sell personal training services to club attendants. Most staff professionals will have weekly, monthly, or quarterly sales quotas. In most commercial fitness facilities, pay rates are structured on a leveled system that meets the needs of the club and helps develop the selling techniques of the fitness professional. Personal trainers with multiple fitness-related certifications, numerous years of service, or high client retention will often earn a higher base salary or bonus incentive.
summary: Commercial fitness facilities often structure pay based on sales performance and experience, encouraging trainers to develop their sales skills and build their client base.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2164
content: Not all facilities are the same. Some facilities may require no selling on the part of the personal trainer and only require exercise instruction.
summary: Different fitness facilities may have varying expectations for personal trainers, with some focusing solely on exercise instruction while others emphasize sales.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2165
content: Most staff professionals will have weekly, monthly, or quarterly sales quotas. Personal trainers with multiple fitness-related certifications, numerous years of service, or high client retention will often earn a higher base salary or bonus incentive. Not all facilities are the same. Some facilities may require no selling on the part of the personal trainer and only require exercise instruction. These facilities usually have membership advisors that sell fitness services at the point of the club membership sale. Personal trainers should not solely rely on the sales staff but rather be proactive in attaining clientele.
summary: Compensation and sales expectations for personal trainers vary depending on the facility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2166
content: Some commercial fitness clubs also provide in-house training in advanced exercise techniques, nutrition, youth training, senior training, and salesmanship. Some clubs also invest in the latest cardiovascular, strength, balance, and flexibility equipment. This is advantageous because it allows personal trainers to work with the latest equipment and learn updated fitness and conditioning concepts. Ultimately, the more information a personal trainer acquires regarding exercise equipment and exercise techniques, the more marketable they become for professional growth. Coincidently, the more rehearsed and educated a personal trainer becomes with advanced information and tools, the more confidence is developed.
summary: Commercial fitness clubs often offer training and resources to enhance personal trainers' skills and marketability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2167
content: An independent contractor can be a person, business, or corporation that provides goods or services to another entity under terms specified in a contract or within a verbal agreement. Unlike an employee, an independent contractor does not work regularly for an employer but works as and when required. These individuals do not receive the benefits of full-time employees, such as health insurance or retirement packages (e.g., a 401K), but rather work on a contract basis. As an independent contractor, a personal trainer typically pays a fee to a gym or health club in return for the ability to train clients at that facility. Personal trainers can be very successful as independent contractors because they learn the necessary business skills to help them advance into private practice and network with local businesses.
summary: Independent contractors provide fitness services on a contractual basis, offering flexibility and potential for business growth.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2168
content: Although independent contractors are not employed by the club, they still represent the business and service the club provides. Being an independent contractor allows a fitness professional to learn how to budget their time, market themselves within a club, and improve organizational skills. This setting can be a stepping stone to a fitness professional owning a facility or studio.
summary: Independent contracting provides valuable experience and opportunities for personal trainers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2169
content: Some personal trainers work with clients in their homes. Clients receive fitness services in the convenience of their own homes. Fitness professionals gain notoriety in the community through in-home training. Advantages of in-home training include no overhead costs for a physical building and autonomy to develop one's own business model. Disadvantages of in-home training include increased gasoline expenditure due to transporting equipment and potential for sessions to spill over into the next hour.
summary: In-home training offers convenience and flexibility for both clients and trainers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2170
content: Most personal trainers build a clientele within a certain mile-radius and transport only certain pieces of equipment. Other options for in-home training include setting up affiliations with manufacturers that allow clients to purchase exercise equipment.
summary: In-home training logistics and business models vary.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2171
content: Many fitness professionals dream of owning their own facility or studio. Owning a facility can be advantageous because the demand for fitness services can be appealing. Marketing and networking for a facility are done at the discretion of the fitness professional. Owning a facility involves overhead costs, hiring and firing staff, community networking, facility ordinances, taxes, insurance, and maintaining a continuous stream of clientele.
summary: Owning a fitness facility presents both opportunities and challenges.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2172
content: Most fitness professionals who own a facility are experienced and understand the nuances of entering the business world.
summary: Successful facility ownership requires experience and business acumen.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2173
content: Aspiring personal trainers need to write a first-rate resume. A resume outlines a fitness professional's education, skills, and experience. A resume is a selling tool that helps employers see how a trainer might contribute to their company. Effective resumes clearly focus on a specific job title and address the employer's needs. Knowing about the duties and skills required for a specific position helps aspiring applicants organize and tailor their resume around these points to get noticed and be successful.
summary: A well-crafted resume is essential for job applications in the fitness industry.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2174
content: An ‘objective’ is a statement that expresses an employment goal in one or two short phrases. Aspiring personal trainers should emphasize what they can offer rather than what they are seeking.
summary: A strong resume objective highlights the applicant's value proposition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2175
content: A resume is a selling tool that helps employers see how a trainer might contribute to their company. A resume is a selling tool that outlines a fitness professional’s education, skills, and experience so employers can see at a glance how they might contribute to their company. The most effective resumes are clearly focused on a specific job title and address the employer’s stated requirements for the position.
summary: Resumes are essential for showcasing a fitness professional's qualifications and how they can benefit a potential employer.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2176
content: Effective resumes clearly focus on a specific job title and address the employer’s needs. Knowing about the duties and skills required for a specific position helps aspiring applicants organize and tailor their resume around these points to get noticed and be successful.
summary: Tailoring a resume to a specific job and highlighting relevant skills is crucial for success.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2177
content: Aspiring personal trainers should emphasize what they can offer rather than what they are seeking. An ‘objective’ is a statement that expresses an employment goal in one or two short phrases.
summary: Resumes should focus on the applicant's strengths and contributions, rather than their desired outcomes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2178
content: New college graduates should list their education before their work experience, especially if they have limited work history. Fitness professionals with no or little college experience should list their work experience first. Listing the most attractive features near the beginning of a resume will help give a good first impression to potential employers.
summary: Resume structure should prioritize relevant experience based on the applicant's background.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2179
content: Commercial fitness clubs offer advantages such as structured training programs, equipment access, and established client bases. Commercial fitness clubs have disadvantages such as limited earning potential, quotas, and competition from other trainers.
summary: Commercial fitness clubs provide structure and resources but may limit earning potential and create competition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2180
content: Independent contractors have the advantage of setting their own rates and schedules. Independent contractors have the disadvantage of needing to find their own clients and manage their own business expenses.
summary: Independent contractors enjoy flexibility but must handle client acquisition and business management.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2181
content: Owning a facility offers the advantage of complete control over operations and potential for high earnings. Owning a facility has the disadvantages of high startup costs, responsibility for all aspects of the business, and potential for financial risk.
summary: Owning a facility offers autonomy and high earning potential but comes with significant risks and responsibilities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2182
content: Personal training requires skills in sales, marketing, and finance.
summary: Successful personal trainers need a diverse skillset beyond fitness expertise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2183
content: Successfully running a personal training business takes time, determination, preparation, and a well-thought-out plan.
summary: Building a successful personal training business demands dedication, planning, and perseverance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2184
content: The first step to becoming a certified personal trainer is education. Education is vital in teaching the fitness professional how to properly assess and instruct their clients through safe and effective workouts.
summary: Education is fundamental to becoming a qualified personal trainer.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2185
content: The IHRSA recommends earning a certification from an organization that has either received accreditation or is in the process of receiving accreditation of their programs.
summary: Choosing an accredited certification program is recommended for aspiring personal trainers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2186
content: Aspiring personal trainers should list all work experience that has taught important, relevant, and applicable skills such as sales, customer service, leadership, and multitasking, even if the work experience was not in the fitness industry.
summary: Highlighting transferable skills from any work experience is valuable for personal training resumes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2187
content: List references on a separate sheet of paper. Avoid listing family members as references. Former managers, supervisors, mentors, and college professors make excellent references.
summary: Providing strong references is essential for job applications.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2188
content: Avoid spelling and grammar errors. Ask a mentor, teacher, or friend to review your resume.
summary: Proofreading and seeking feedback are crucial for a polished resume.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2189
content: Use clean, high-quality, white, 8½- × 11-inch paper and a plain, easy to read font.
summary: Presentation matters: use professional formatting for your resume.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2190
content: After dropping off resumes with potential employers, aspiring personal trainers should follow up with a “thank you” phone call or email.
summary: Following up after submitting a resume demonstrates professionalism and interest.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2191
content: Fitness professionals who are seeking employment should practice their interviewing skills before their first interview with a hiring manager. Answers during an interview should be concise, eloquent, and personable.
summary: Preparation and strong communication skills are essential for successful interviews.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2192
content: Before the interview begins, it is important to shake the interviewer’s hand, smile, and make eye contact when introduced. While conversing it is important for an individual to talk about their good qualities.
summary: First impressions and positive self-presentation are crucial during interviews.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2193
content: Personal training can relate to many fields and most health clubs are willing to hire new trainers as long as they show potential and an eagerness to learn.
summary: Personal training offers diverse opportunities and employers value potential and a willingness to learn.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2194
content: Personal trainers who want to get noticed in a sea of applicants for that ‘most desired’ training position need a well-structured resume. Many personal trainers mistakenly identify marketing as simply advertising a product or service. Although advertising is an element of marketing, it is not the only or the most important part. Merriam Webster’s dictionary defines marketing as ‘the process or techniques of promoting, selling, and distributing a product or service’ (2).
summary: A well-structured resume and a comprehensive understanding of marketing are essential for personal training success.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2195
content: It is a good idea for fitness professionals who are seeking employment to practice their interviewing skills before an actual interview. Answers during an interview should be concise, eloquent, and personable.
summary: Preparation for job interviews is crucial for fitness professionals, emphasizing concise, eloquent, and personable communication.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2196
content: Many personal trainers mistakenly identify marketing as simply advertising a product or service. Although advertising is an element of marketing, it is not the only or the most important part. Merriam Webster’s dictionary defines marketing as ‘the process or techniques of promoting, selling, and distributing a product or service’ (2). Although this definition is rather simplistic, it begins to demonstrate how marketing encompasses many elements above and beyond creating signage and other forms of advertisements.
summary: Marketing encompasses more than just advertising; it involves promoting, selling, and distributing products or services effectively.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2197
content: Marketing is the process of promoting a product or service (such as personal training) by communicating the features and benefits to potential customers. Effective marketing requires identifying the customer’s needs, developing the appropriate products or services to satisfy those needs, and promoting services and solutions in a cost-effective manner. The ultimate goal of marketing is to understand the wants and needs of consumers so well that selling becomes nonessential because the product or service sells itself.
summary: Marketing involves understanding customer needs, developing solutions, and promoting them effectively to achieve a point where sales are driven by demand.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2198
content: Whether working in a commercial facility, as an independent contractor, or as a business owner, personal trainers must develop a comprehensive marketing plan to achieve a full book of business. This requires a detailed understanding of potential clients’ wants, needs, demographics, and socioeconomic statuses. For example, if a personal trainer is working in a health club that mostly caters to working parents and young adults, he or she should provide services to accommodate these individuals’ goals and work or school schedules, such as early morning and evening classes. Conversely, if a personal trainer works mostly with stay-at-home parents and retirees, this personal trainer can create incentive packages for training during afternoon hours when clubs are typically less crowded.
summary: Personal trainers need a comprehensive marketing plan tailored to their target audience's demographics and needs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2199
content: To have a successful marketing plan, fitness professionals must have an understanding of the marketing mix, also known as the four Ps of marketing. The marketing mix refers to activities that a personal trainer can control to produce the response he or she wants from a target market. The four Ps of marketing include: Product, Price, Place (distribution), and Promotion.
summary: The marketing mix, consisting of Product, Price, Place, and Promotion, is essential for a successful marketing plan.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2200
content: Product: The specific product or service offered to customers. Price: The amount charged for a product or service, including volume discounts, seasonal pricing, and bundle packages. Place (distribution): Channels a product or service will go through to reach the customer. Promotion: The communication of information about a product or service with the goal of generating a positive customer response. Some marketing communication strategies include advertising, sales, social media, and public relations.
summary: The four Ps of marketing: Product, Price, Place, and Promotion, are detailed and explained.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2201
content: Most services provided by a personal trainer are sold by the hour or by the session. It might be assumed that the product is the education that the client receives from working with the trainer. This type of education may come in the form of how to use equipment properly, how to eat correctly, or how to lose weight. Others may venture to say that the motivation to exercise is the product being sold. The product delivered to the customer is the result that is trying to be achieved. If a client wants to lose weight, he or she may opt to get involved in personal training to “have the motivation to go to the gym” or “learn how to exercise properly”; however, these are paths that lead to the end result of weight loss. It is important for a personal trainer always to keep in mind that the obligation lies in the result rather than the path. Remembering this will allow you to never lose sight of your purpose and need for this position. It is when the fitness professional assumes the service they are providing is a path rather than a result that professional productivity declines and the impact of the business is lost.
summary: The core product a personal trainer offers is the desired result for the client, not just the training sessions themselves.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2202
content: Having a specialty or a niche is a great way to help personal trainers stand out and provide a unique product in a competitive market. Earning additional certifications, advanced specializations, or a higher educational degree may enable fitness professionals to offer their services to a select group of clients. Fitness professionals may choose to become specialists in weight management, corrective exercise, sport-specific conditioning, or even working with a certain sex or age group. Targeting a specific category with many potential clients and very few other trainers can position the fitness professional to dominate their specific niche and greatly improve their earning potential.
summary: Specializing in a niche market can help personal trainers stand out and increase their earning potential.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2203
content: Personal training pay rates vary across the country and depend on many factors including the socioeconomic status of clientele; the type of training offered (boot camps, small-group training, personal training); volume discounts; and the education and experience level of the fitness professional. Personal training in populated and affluent areas is generally more expensive than in rural areas. Before determining a set price, fitness professionals should research the demographics and socioeconomic status of potential clients in their area. Equally important is research into competitor’s prices. Based on this information, fitness professionals can begin to find an appropriate price for their services. Keep in mind that there is no such thing as the “perfect price”; inevitably, a small percentage of clients will be unable to afford your services.
summary: Personal training rates are influenced by various factors, including location, clientele, training type, and market competition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2204
content: In the case of personal training, most think of a gym or health club; however, new trends in the fitness industry are expanding the possibilities for where personal training can be offered.
summary: Personal training opportunities are expanding beyond traditional gyms and health clubs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2205
content: Before determining a set price, fitness professionals should research the demographics and socioeconomic status of potential clients in their area. Equally important is research into competitor’s prices. Based on this information, fitness professionals can begin to find an appropriate price for their services. Keep in mind that there is no such thing as the “perfect price”; inevitably, a small percentage of clients will be unable to afford your services.
summary: Pricing strategies for fitness professionals should consider client demographics, competitor pricing, and the understanding that not all potential clients will be able to afford services.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2206
content: In the case of personal training, most think of a gym or health club; however, new trends in the fitness industry are expanding the possibilities for where personal training can be offered. Emerging industries are allowing personal trainers to offer their services through a variety of distribution channels including online and phone coaching, youth sports groups and after-school programs, boot-camps held at local parks, schools, or beaches, corporate wellness programs, sport training centers, and senior centers.
summary: Personal training opportunities extend beyond traditional gyms, encompassing diverse settings like online platforms, community programs, and specialized fitness centers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2207
content: Many personal trainers mistakenly identify marketing as simply advertising a product or service. Advertising is an element of marketing, but it is not the only nor the most important part. Effective marketing requires identifying the customer’s needs, developing the appropriate products or services to satisfy those needs, and promoting services and solutions in a cost-effective manner.
summary: Marketing for fitness professionals involves more than just advertising; it requires understanding client needs, developing suitable services, and promoting them effectively.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2208
content: To have a successful marketing plan, fitness professionals must have an understanding of the marketing mix, also known as the ‘four Ps of marketing.’ The marketing mix refers to activities that a personal trainer can control to produce the response he or she wants from a target market. The four Ps of marketing include Product, Price, Place (distribution), and Promotion.
summary: A successful marketing plan for fitness professionals relies on understanding the 'four Ps of marketing': Product, Price, Place, and Promotion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2209
content: Product refers to the specific product or service offered to customers. Price refers to the amount charged for a product or service including volume discounts, seasonal pricing, and bundle packages. Place (distribution) refers to the channels a product or service will go through to reach the customer. Promotion refers to the communication of information about a product or service with the goal of generating a positive customer response.
summary: The four Ps of marketing encompass Product, Price, Place, and Promotion, each playing a crucial role in a fitness professional's marketing strategy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2210
content: Some marketing communication strategies include advertising, sales, social media, and public relations. Web-based communications such as a professional website; online advertising (banner ads); and social media (e.g., Facebook, Twitter, and LinkedIn) are extremely useful and engaging mediums by which personal trainers can advertise their services. Online marketing can be a successful form of advertising that will help personal trainers maximize their return on investment. The unique features of social media enable personal trainers to advertise and interact with potential clients in real time.
summary: Effective marketing for fitness professionals utilizes various strategies, including traditional advertising, online platforms, and social media engagement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2211
content: Promoting personal training services involves communicating important information to potential clients with the goal of generating a positive customer response. Some marketing communication strategies include traditional advertising (e.g., print advertisements, posters, and brochures); promotional sales; sponsorships; and community service.
summary: Promoting personal training services involves communicating value to potential clients through various strategies, including traditional advertising, promotions, and community engagement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2212
content: Promotions can be categorized into two separate entities: push or pull. Advertising pulls a consumer towards a fitness professional by making the consumer aware of his or her services. An incentive, such as seasonal or bulk discounts, pushes a personal trainer’s services by encouraging potential clients to purchase in volume.
summary: Promotional strategies can be categorized as 'pull' (advertising) or 'push' (incentives), each aiming to attract clients differently.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2213
content: Success in the fitness industry depends, to a great degree, on reputation. Those known for excellence and distinction in their profession gain a competitive advantage over everyone else working in the same position. It is essential to have a strong commitment to excellence, knowledge, and professionalism to be thought of with high regard. The best personal trainers operate with the utmost level of integrity and refuse to settle for anything less than their personal best.
summary: Reputation is crucial for success in the fitness industry; personal trainers who demonstrate excellence, integrity, and commitment to their craft build a strong reputation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2214
content: To develop a reputation as an excellent personal trainer, one must first develop a reputation for uncompromising customer service. Uncompromising customer service means being unwavering in providing an experience and level of assistance that is rarely, if ever, experienced anywhere else. Develop an obsession for becoming artistic in your approach to helping people.
summary: Building a strong reputation as a personal trainer starts with providing exceptional customer service and approaching client interactions with creativity and dedication.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2215
content: The most successful personal trainers never settle for average, never stop trying to improve themselves or their business, never settle for second best, and are always looking ahead. Most if not all buying decisions are based on emotion. Clients have many choices regarding where to exercise, and who to exercise with. How a client goes about choosing a personal trainer is based on how they feel (often first impressions) about a given facility and the personal trainers that work there.
summary: Successful personal trainers are driven by continuous improvement, understand the emotional basis of client decisions, and prioritize creating positive first impressions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2216
content: This feeling is often based on the level of customer service received and that client’s perception of the value offered. Those who adapt to uncompromising customer service as the minimal acceptable standard of professionalism will give clients positive feelings. Relationships are the most powerful competitive advantage in a service profession. Successful personal trainers never think of any of their clients as an interruption of work; instead they are the entire reason their business exists in the first place!
summary: Positive client experiences, driven by excellent customer service and perceived value, are key to building lasting relationships and success in the fitness industry.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2217
content: How a client goes about choosing a personal trainer is based on how they feel (often first impressions) about a given facility and the personal trainers that work there. This feeling is often based on the level of customer service received and that client’s perception of the value offered. Those who adapt to uncompromising customer service as the minimal acceptable standard of professionalism will give clients positive feelings. Relationships are the most powerful competitive advantage in a service profession.
summary: Client selection is influenced by first impressions, customer service, and perceived value. Uncompromising customer service and strong relationships are key to success.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2218
content: Successful personal trainers never think of any of their clients as an interruption of work; instead they are the entire reason their business exists in the first place! Keys to developing an uncompromising customer service philosophy include taking every opportunity to meet and get to know all potential clientele. Each and every time you walk into the club or onto the workout floor is an opportunity to create a professional relationship with a potential new client and, eventually, make a sale. Remember to represent a positive image and high level of professionalism every minute of the day.
summary: Clients are the reason for a personal trainer's business.  Developing a customer-centric philosophy involves actively engaging with potential clients and maintaining a professional image at all times.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2219
content: Never give the impression that any question is inconvenient, unnecessary, or unintelligent. Express ideas well through verbal communication, vocal tonality, and body language, which all work together to convey a message. Obsess on opportunities to create moments that strengthen professional relationships. Do not merely receive complaints, but take ownership of them.
summary: Effective communication and relationship building are crucial.  Respond to all inquiries thoughtfully, and take ownership of client concerns.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2220
content: Successful personal trainers routinely ask themselves the following question: If my entire future were based on the way this one client evaluated his experience with me right now, would I change anything? They may also ask, Did I exceed this client’s expectations? If the answer to either of these questions is “no, ” committed fitness professionals would continue to look for ways to improve themselves. NASM’s education, application, integrity, solutions, and tools are the foundation for creating “yes” answers to these questions and providing optimal performance and results for clients.
summary: Committed trainers constantly strive for improvement by reflecting on client experiences and seeking to exceed expectations. NASM's resources support this pursuit of excellence.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2221
content: The primary purpose of a business is to create and keep customers. A customer-focused process, using the keys to uncompromising customer service, will create a distinctive level of value and develop success in finding, building, and retaining clients. Establish a reputation for excellence and distinction as a fitness professional, then develop a reputation for uncompromising customer service. Leave each club member with a positive impression.
summary: The core of business success is customer acquisition and retention.  Uncompromising customer service and a strong reputation are essential for achieving this.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2222
content: A client’s buying decisions are based on how he or she feels about a certain facility and its fitness professionals. These feelings are influenced by the level of customer service received and perception of value offered. As such, clients are never an interruption of work, but rather the entire purpose behind it. Each client’s experience should be treated as if it affects the entire career of the fitness professional, because it does.
summary: Client decisions are driven by feelings and perceptions of value.  Every client interaction is crucial and should be treated with utmost importance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2223
content: Fitness clientele may seek out the services of a personal trainer for a variety of reasons, including the desire to improve their appearance, sports performance, or general health and to increase their physical capacity in activities of daily living. Whatever the reasons clients seek out personal trainers, the ultimate motivation is the desire to improve their quality of life.
summary: Clients seek personal training for various reasons, ultimately aiming to enhance their overall well-being and quality of life.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2224
content: Advanced technology is not synonymous with better health although that is a widely held assumption. If anything, because advances in technology have replaced so many aspects of everyday movement, in reality it is associated more so with poor health than anything else. Decreases in physical activity are directly associated with greater risk and rates of obesity, diabetes, heart disease, and musculoskeletal dysfunction, as well as numerous other chronic diseases (3–8). It is obvious that the need for professional, personalized physical training is increasing.
summary: Technological advancements, while often perceived as beneficial, can contribute to poor health due to reduced physical activity. This highlights the growing need for personalized fitness training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2225
content: Personal fitness training may once have been viewed as a luxury, but it is moving toward becoming a necessity. In the very near future, health care may play a pivotal role in the success and growth of the fitness industry. NASM’s certified personal trainers will be qualified to deliver the OPT system and tools, producing remarkable results for clients.
summary: Personal training is evolving from a luxury to a necessity. The healthcare industry is poised to play a significant role in this growth, with NASM-certified trainers leading the way in delivering effective training programs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2226
content: In today’s sedentary culture, who is a potential client? Everybody! It is difficult to imagine anyone who does not have a desire to look and feel better, perform at a higher level, be healthier and more confident, enjoy a better quality of life, and reduce chronic pain.
summary: In today's sedentary society, everyone has the potential to benefit from personal training, seeking improvements in health, well-being, and quality of life.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2227
content: Far too often, young, inexperienced, or sometimes even experienced, personal trainers never reach their full career potential because they do not know how to sell themselves. They confuse selling their services as selling in the traditional sense (such as selling products). Personal trainers should view sales as self-promotion. The first step in learning to self-promote is to not be afraid to approach potential new clients. Personal trainers can change the life of a client.
summary: Many personal trainers fail to reach their full potential due to a lack of self-promotion skills.  They should view sales as self-promotion and confidently approach potential clients, recognizing their ability to positively impact lives.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2228
content: Far too often, young, inexperienced, or sometimes even experienced, personal trainers never reach their full career potential because they do not know how to sell themselves. They confuse selling their services as selling in the traditional sense (such as selling products). Personal trainers should view sales as self-promotion.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of self-promotion for personal trainers, distinguishing it from traditional sales.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2229
content: The first step in learning to self-promote is to not be afraid to approach potential new clients. Openly provide uncompromising customer service by saying 'hello' to every member while working out or working a shift. Make eye contact, smile, and be personable to every member. Offer members a towel or some water. Roam the floor, clean up equipment, and make sure that the workout floor is impeccable and represents the highest standard of appearance. Do not hide behind the fitness desk, a computer, or a magazine. At the very least, simply introduce yourself by name and ask members for their names as well. Let members know that you are there to enrich the club experience by attending to any need.
summary: This chunk outlines practical tips for personal trainers to initiate positive interactions with potential clients and create a welcoming gym environment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2230
content: First interactions with new members should be professional, pleasant, and most important, nonthreatening. Once you have made an initial contact with a new member, you are in a better position to offer valuable assistance. If a member is performing an exercise incorrectly, tell him or her about a positive benefit of that exercise. Offer to help that person 'maximize the exercise'. State that you have just attended a seminar or read a book that offers an alternative to what he or she is already doing. If the member says 'No, thanks', leave him or her alone. However, if he or she says 'Yes', provide specific help with the exercise and articulate the benefit that the member will receive related directly to his or her goal. Continue to offer him or her assistance throughout the workout.
summary: This chunk provides guidance on how to approach new members, offer assistance, and tailor advice to their specific needs and goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2231
content: Potential clients are very unreceptive to any approach that may challenge their belief systems or judgments regarding an exercise or competencies. Avoid any of the following opening lines: 'May I make a suggestion?', 'Can I recommend a better way of doing that?', 'Can I show you a different technique?', 'Let me show you the right way.', 'Can I help you with that?', and 'What’s your goal for that exercise?'
summary: This chunk advises personal trainers to avoid potentially confrontational language when interacting with potential clients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2232
content: The increased need for personal training does not guarantee the success of all fitness professionals. Just because people have a need or a desire does not mean that they will take action. In the clients’ minds, the value of the service must outweigh the cost. Cost refers to the price of the services as well as the time, effort, and commitment involved in training. Individuals may have reservations created by the potential for failure.
summary: This chunk highlights the importance of demonstrating the value of personal training services to overcome potential client hesitations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2233
content: It is the fitness professional’s personal responsibility to create and display the value of their services. To be successful at attracting and keeping clients, personal trainers must be familiar with the NASM–BOC Code of Professional Conduct and they must use the systematic approach presented throughout this text. In addition to adhering to the NASM–BOC Code of Professional Conduct, NASM personal trainers shall: Excel at using NASM’s integrated fitness assessments (including subjective and objective information) and various movement observations to accurately assess the needs and goals of the individual. Have the ability to design an OPT training program and correctly fill out an OPT template. Explain to clients how each component of the exercise program focuses on their personal goals and needs. Be able to demonstrate exercises and implement individually tailored exercise programs for a wide range of clients. Demonstrate to clients how the OPT system offers them the greatest benefits possible.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of professional conduct, knowledge of the OPT system, and client-centered training practices for successful personal trainers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2234
content: Clients need to believe in the personal trainer they choose to work with and trust that he or she will help them achieve their personal health and wellness goals and objectives. It is important to remember that the OPT system is a map to success and a systematic approach that ensures results. NASM personal trainers shall: Maintain their competencies through continuing education, Adhere to safe and ethical training practices (e.g., OSHA), Adhere to strict facility maintenance (e.g., equipment, safety, layout, disinfection), Understand scope of practice with respect to special considerations for training diverse clientele (e.g., age, sex, cultural background, and ability), Adhere to professionalism and ethical business practices (e.g., liability insurance, client referrals, medical clearance, physical appearance and attire, timeliness, sexual harassment awareness, client confidentiality (e.g., HIPAA))
summary: This chunk stresses the importance of client trust, the effectiveness of the OPT system, and adherence to ethical and professional standards for NASM trainers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2235
content: For a complete transcript of the NASM Code of Professional Conduct, log onto www.nasm.org. Clients use the services of personal trainers for a variety of reasons, but all with the motivation to improve quality of life. In today’s sedentary culture, everyone can be considered a potential client.
summary: This chunk provides a call to action for accessing the NASM Code of Professional Conduct and highlights the broad potential client base for personal trainers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2236
content: It is important to remember that the OPT system is a map to success and a systematic approach that ensures results. To secure a client, the fitness professional must use the OPT system as a map to success and use its systematic approach, which ensures results by: Using NASM’s integrated fitness assessment and movement observations to accurately assess and design exercise programs based on the needs and goals of the individual. Designing OPT training programs and correctly filling out an OPT template. Explaining to clients how the OPT system offers the highest benefit and how each program component focuses on personal goals and needs. Explaining first, then demonstrating exercises to new clients.
summary: The OPT system is a systematic approach to fitness that ensures results. It involves assessment, program design, and client education.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2237
content: NASM personal trainers shall: Maintain their competencies through continuing education, Adhere to safe and ethical training practices (e.g., OSHA), Adhere to strict facility maintenance (e.g., equipment, safety, layout, disinfection), Understand scope of practice with respect to special considerations for training diverse clientele (e.g., age, sex, cultural background, and ability), Adhere to professionalism and ethical business practices (e.g., liability insurance, client referrals, medical clearance, physical appearance and attire, timeliness, sexual harassment awareness, client confidentiality (e.g., HIPAA)). For a complete transcript of the NASM Code of Professional Conduct, log onto www.nasm.org.
summary: NASM personal trainers must adhere to ethical and professional standards, including continuing education, safe practices, and client confidentiality.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2238
content: Clients use the services of personal trainers for a variety of reasons, but all with the motivation to improve quality of life. In today’s sedentary culture, everyone can be considered a potential client. There are countless people who desire to look and feel better, be healthier and more confident, perform better, enjoy a higher quality of life, and reduce chronic pain. Personal training is moving toward becoming a necessity, and health care may soon play a pivotal role in the success and growth of the industry.
summary: Personal training is increasingly sought after as people strive to improve their health, well-being, and quality of life.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2239
content: Simply because someone has a desire to do so does not mean that person will take action. The value of personal training services must outweigh their price, time, effort, and commitment. It is the fitness professional’s personal responsibility to create and display that value.
summary: Potential clients need to perceive the value of personal training to overcome barriers and commit to it.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2240
content: Personal trainers need to be proactive when approaching prospective new clients. Avoid making members feel uncomfortable by saying things such as, ‘Can I recommend a better way of doing that?’ Instead, do things such as: Roam the floor and say ‘Hello’ to every member, while offering towels and water and making sure that everything is clean and safe. Introduce yourself and ask members for their names, letting them know that you can help them with any of their needs. Avoid overeducating new clients. If a member is performing an exercise incorrectly, find out what it is about the exercise that is important to him or her. Then, offer to help that person ‘maximize the exercise.’ Use movement and postural observations for screening and the OPT model to customize the exercise and other recommendations. Continue to offer the potential client assistance throughout the workout.
summary: Effective client acquisition involves proactive engagement, building rapport, and offering personalized assistance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2241
content: There are many negative connotations regarding sales. Many personal trainers are reluctant to ask for a sale because they associate selling with either rejection or doing something that is ethically wrong. It is not through manipulation but concern and professionalism that sales are created.
summary: Personal trainers should approach sales with professionalism and genuine concern for client well-being, avoiding manipulative tactics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2242
content: There are numerous benefits related to performance, health, self-esteem, and quality of life that can be gained through the fitness professional’s services, using the OPT solutions and tools. The fact is that until a potential client makes a purchase, he or she cannot benefit from these services. The sale is the first essential step to helping a client to achieve results.
summary: The sale is crucial for clients to access the benefits of personal training and achieve their goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2243
content: Developing a list of good clientele depends on how well personal trainers learn to self-promote themselves while out on the floor. Personal trainers should not make the mistake of relying on sales consultants to schedule all potential client orientations, because it is a philosophy that is dependent on the productivity of someone else and causes personal trainers to give up control of how many presentations they are able to make each week. Set up a plan based on a desired annual income goal. Use the following 10 steps and corresponding questions to direct the design of that plan. Step 1: What is the Desired Annual Income? An annual income goal is the achievable desired sum total of monthly earnings over 12 months. After clearly identifying a desired annual income, move on to step 2. Example: Based on her expenses and lifestyle, Christy would like to earn $40,000 annually as a fitness professional. Step 2: How Much Must Be Earned per Week to Achieve the Annual Goal?
summary: Personal trainers should actively promote themselves and develop a plan to achieve their desired income goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2244
content: An annual income goal is the achievable desired sum total of monthly earnings over 12 months. Example: Based on her expenses and lifestyle, Christy would like to earn $40,000 annually as a fitness professional.
summary: Defines an annual income goal and provides an example.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2245
content: Step 2: How Much Must Be Earned per Week to Achieve the Annual Goal? Divide the desired annual income by 50 to figure out what will need to be earned on a weekly basis. Example: Christy divides $40,000 by 50, which equals $800. She knows that she will need to earn $800 each week to hit her goal.
summary: Explains how to calculate weekly earnings needed to reach an annual income goal.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2246
content: Step 3: To Earn the Weekly Goal, How Many Sessions Need to Be Performed? To earn the weekly amount, how many clients or sessions are needed each week? To establish this number, take the weekly goal and divide it by the amount earned per session. Example: Christy divides $800 by $25 per hour. Now, Christy knows that she will need to perform 32 sessions per week to hit her goal. Also, take the current average number of paid sessions performed weekly and divide it by the number of clients currently signed up. Example: Christy currently performs 20 sessions per week and has 11 regular clients. She divides and gets a number of 1.82. So, Christy divides her goal of 32 sessions by 1.82 and can now estimate that she needs at least 18 clients to hit her income goals.
summary: Determines the number of sessions needed per week to achieve the weekly income goal.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2247
content: Personal trainers need to determine their closing percentage. The closing percentage is determined by dividing the total number of people helped on the floor by the number of people who purchased training packages. Christy spoke to 60 members in the last 30 days. Christy signed up 5 members as clients. Christy's closing rate is 8%.
summary: Explains how to calculate a personal trainer's closing percentage.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2248
content: Unrealistic timeframes will likely lead to frustration and disappointment. If a timeframe is set too far in the future, it will not create the sense of urgency necessary to maximize performance. Christy has 11 clients and needs at least 18 to hit her goals. Christy wants to gain at least 7 more clients. Christy decided to set a timeframe of 3 weeks to gain 7 new clients. Take the desired number of new clients and divide that number by the closing percentage. Christy wants 7 new clients and her closing rate is 8%. Christy must have excellent contact with at least 88 members during the next 3 weeks to come close to her goal of 7 new clients. Christy needs to contact at least 88 members during the next 3 weeks.
summary: Emphasizes the importance of realistic timeframes and provides an example of setting a goal and calculating the necessary contacts.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2249
content: Christy divides 88 by 3 and realizes that she has to contact about 30 members each week. Christy is aiming to contact 30 members each week. Christy works 5 days per week. Christy divides 30 by 5 and discovers that she only needs to talk to 6 members each day. Christy generally conducts four 1-hour sessions each day during her 8-hour shift. Christy also takes a half-hour lunch and usually conducts one half-hour orientation. Christy has 3 hours on the floor to contact her goal of 6 members. Christy divides 6 by 3 and must talk to a member every 30 minutes to achieve her goals.
summary: Details a plan for contacting potential clients, breaking down the task into daily and hourly goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2250
content: For each contact in that hour, provide measurable, personalized assistance that is related to the goal of the person being approached. These contacts, even if they do not develop into sales, add to a valuable future prospect base. The NASM-certified personal trainer possesses core competencies of individualized assessment, OPT program design, and exercise selection. These personal trainers automatically have the capacity to provide a personalized, results-oriented experience for any member they make a connection with. If a good level of rapport has been built with a member, do not be afraid to ask him or her for their contact information.
summary: Provides advice on how to make effective contact with potential clients and build rapport.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2251
content: For each contact in that hour, provide measurable, personalized assistance that is related to the goal of the person being approached. These contacts, even if they do not develop into sales, add to a valuable future prospect base.  If a good level of rapport has been built with a member, do not be afraid to ask him or her for their contact information. Offer to develop a few exercises to help him or her achieve the goals discussed. Contact the member and arrange a time to assist them in implementing the new exercises during his or her next visit to the club.
summary: Building rapport and offering personalized assistance during initial contacts can lead to future sales opportunities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2252
content: Within 24 hours, mail the member a handwritten thank-you card for the time they spent with you in the club. Give the card 2 to 3 days to arrive, and then call the member 1 week from initial contact. Make sure the member got the card. Thank him or her personally for his or her time. Let the member know that you have thought about his or her goals and would like to go over some exercises that will be helpful. Be clear that it will only take about 10 minutes the next time he or she is in the club. Determine the next time he or she is coming to the club. Schedule an informal appointment during his or her next visit.
summary: Following up with a thank-you card and call, and scheduling a follow-up appointment to discuss exercise options.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2253
content: Every exercise is an assessment. Regardless of the exercise that a member was doing on the floor, write down what you saw according to the five kinetic chain checkpoints in general movement and postural observations. Write and keep detailed notes on each member.
summary: Observing and documenting a member's exercise technique provides valuable assessment information.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2254
content: Implement a couple of the exercises on the floor and explain how they relate to the member’s goal. Offer the member a more thorough assessment, an individualized program design, and a single training session to maximize the results he or she is currently seeing. Directly ask the member to sign up for a package of sessions.
summary: Demonstrating exercises, offering assessments and program design, and directly asking for a sale.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2255
content: Most sales are lost because they are not asked for. Failure to close a sale comes down to one or more of the following four reasons: there was not enough value built into the sale, an insufficient level of rapport makes the potential client hesitant to go ahead, the health and fitness professional did not affirmatively ask for the sale, or the potential client legitimately does not have the ability to pay. The first three reasons for failure to close a sale can be directly controlled.
summary: The importance of asking for the sale and addressing common reasons for sales failure.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2256
content: Many personal trainers are able to offer high value and have outstanding personalities, but do not ask for the sale because they fear rejection. After following the above 10 steps and then directly asking for the sale, 9 of 10 members will say no. Even with a 10% closing ratio, thousands of personal trainers in the industry have been able to develop a steady clientele.
summary: Overcoming the fear of rejection and understanding that a high rejection rate is normal for sales.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2257
content: If the personal trainer has established rapport, had empathy for why the client’s goals are important to him or her, conducted a thorough assessment, and made the right program recommendations, then rejection is considerably less likely. When a potential client says yes to a product, program, or personal training package, the following steps should be taken: finish the sales transaction, schedule the client’s first appointment within 48 hours, send the client a thank-you card within 24 hours, call to confirm before the first appointment, go over the client’s goals again and briefly reiterate how he or she will achieve those goals, and congratulate the client and acknowledge him or her for taking the first step in achieving his or her goals.
summary: Increasing the likelihood of a sale through rapport, empathy, and thorough assessment, and following up with the client after a successful sale.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2258
content: If a potential client declines to take the appointment or purchase services, the following steps should be taken: remain professional and helpful, thank the potential client for participating in the session, make sure to have the potential client’s contact information, ask to call him or her in a couple of weeks to check on program status, send a thank-you card immediately, schedule a follow-up call in 14 days in a daily planner, every 30 days, send information that pertains to the potential client’s goals (such as pertinent points from article clippings, trade journals, fitness websites, and so forth), make sure to follow through on all of the above tasks, and keep a record of all points of contact.
summary: Handling a declined sale professionally, maintaining contact, and providing valuable information to the potential client.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2259
content: Successful personal trainers keep in consistent contact. Successful personal trainers understand that a ‘no’ today is not a ‘no’ indefinitely. Successful personal trainers realize that contacts equal opportunity and that the more contacts they make, the more opportunity they will have to change lives. Successful personal trainers know that the greater the number of people who consider them ‘expert’ resources, the larger their referral base will be.
summary: The importance of consistent contact, understanding that rejection is not permanent, and building a referral base.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2260
content: The NASM-certified personal trainer possesses core competencies of individualized assessment, OPT program design, and exercise selection. These personal trainers automatically have the capacity to provide a personalized, results-oriented experience for any member they make a connection with.
summary: The qualifications and capabilities of a NASM-certified personal trainer.

summary: This training plan outlines the details for John Smith, a client of Brian Sutton, on 8/01/13. The goal is to achieve power through kettlebell program phase 5.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2441
content: SMR: Calves, IT-Band, Lats should be performed for 1 set with a duration of 30 seconds. Hold each tender area for 30 seconds.
summary: Self-myofascial release (SMR) should be performed on the calves, IT-band, and lats for 30 seconds each, holding tender areas for an additional 30 seconds.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2442
content: Dynamic Stretch: Tube Walking, Multiplanar Lunges, Med Ball Lift and Chop should be performed for 1 set with 10 reps.
summary: Dynamic stretching includes Tube Walking, Multiplanar Lunges, and Med Ball Lift and Chop, each performed for 1 set of 10 reps.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2443
content: Ball Med Ball Pullover Throw should be performed for 2 sets with 12 reps with an explosive tempo and 0 rest. Multiplanar Single-Leg Hop w/ Stabilization should be performed for 2 sets with 10 reps with a medium tempo and 60 seconds rest.
summary: Ball Med Ball Pullover Throw is performed for 2 sets of 12 reps explosively with no rest. Multiplanar Single-Leg Hop w/ Stabilization is performed for 2 sets of 10 reps with a medium tempo and 60 seconds rest between sets.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2444
content: Chest Barbell Bench Press should be performed for 3 sets with 10 reps with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest. MB Chest Pass should be performed for 3 sets with 10 reps with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest. KB Row: 1-arm should be performed for 3 sets with 10 reps with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest. Soccer Throw should be performed for 3 sets with 10 reps with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest.
summary: Chest Barbell Bench Press, MB Chest Pass, KB Row: 1-arm, and Soccer Throw are performed for 3 sets of 10 reps each with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest between sets.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2445
content: Standing KB Overhead Press should be performed for 3 sets with 10 reps with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest. MB Front Oblique Throw should be performed for 3 sets with 10 reps with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest. KB Lunge: Sagittal Plane should be performed for 3 sets with 10 reps with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest. Squat Jump should be performed for 3 sets with 10 reps with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest.
summary: Standing KB Overhead Press, MB Front Oblique Throw, KB Lunge: Sagittal Plane, and Squat Jump are performed for 3 sets of 10 reps each with an explosive tempo and 2 minutes rest between sets.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2446
content: Treadmill should be performed for 1 set with a duration of 5–10 minutes. Brisk walk; gradually reduce speed.
summary: Cardiovascular exercise involves a treadmill session for 5-10 minutes, starting with a brisk walk and gradually reducing speed.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2447
content: Static Stretch: Calves, Hip Flexors, Lats should be performed for 1 set with 30 seconds hold for each stretch.
summary: Static stretching includes holding stretches for the calves, hip flexors, and lats for 30 seconds each.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2448
content: This program focuses on power training. It includes a mix of kettlebell exercises and traditional exercises. Perform resistance exercises for each body part as a superset.
summary: This program emphasizes power training, combining kettlebell exercises and traditional exercises. Resistance exercises should be performed as supersets.
