id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2519
content: 621Appendix C
summary: This chunk contains a string.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2520
content: The traditional perception of muscles is that they work concentrically and predominantly in one plane of motion. To more effectively understand motion, it is imperative to view muscles functioning in all planes of motion and through the entire muscle action spectrum. The following section describes the isolated (concentric) and integrated (isometric, eccentric) actions of the major muscles of the human movement system.
summary: This chunk discusses the limitations of viewing muscles solely in one plane of motion and emphasizes the importance of understanding their function across all planes and muscle action types.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2521
content: The following section describes each muscle's origin, insertion, and function. The origin refers to the proximal attachment site that remains relatively fixed during contraction. The insertion refers to the muscle's distal attachment site to a moveable bone.
summary: This chunk defines key anatomical terms: origin and insertion, which describe a muscle's attachment points.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2522
content: The origin of the Anterior Tibialis muscle is the proximal attachments of the tibia and fibula of the lower leg. The insertion of the Anterior Tibialis muscle is the medial and lateral malleoli of the ankle and the first metatarsal. The isolated function of the Anterior Tibialis muscle is dorsiflexion and inversion of the foot. The integrated function of the Anterior Tibialis muscle is dorsiflexion and inversion of the foot. The integrated function of the Anterior Tibialis muscle also assists in stabilizing the ankle during walking and running.
summary: This chunk describes the origin, insertion, and function of the Anterior Tibialis muscle, highlighting both its isolated and integrated actions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2523
content: The origin of the Posterior Tibialis muscle is the proximal attachments of the tibia and fibula of the lower leg and the interosseous membrane. The insertion of the Posterior Tibialis muscle is the medial aspect of the calcaneus and the plantar fascia. The isolated function of the Posterior Tibialis muscle is plantarflexion and inversion of the foot. The integrated function of the Posterior Tibialis muscle is plantarflexion and inversion of the foot. The integrated function of the Posterior Tibialis muscle also assists in stabilizing the ankle during walking and running.
summary: This chunk describes the origin, insertion, and function of the Posterior Tibialis muscle, highlighting both its isolated and integrated actions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2524
content: The origin of the Soleus muscle is the proximal attachments of the tibia and fibula of the lower leg and the interosseous membrane. The insertion of the Soleus muscle is the Achilles tendon. The isolated function of the Soleus muscle is plantarflexion of the foot. The integrated function of the Soleus muscle is plantarflexion of the foot and assists in stabilizing the ankle during walking and running.
summary: This chunk describes the origin, insertion, and function of the Soleus muscle, highlighting both its isolated and integrated actions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2525
content: The origin of the Gastrocnemius muscle is the proximal attachments of the tibia and fibula of the lower leg and the interosseous membrane. The insertion of the Gastrocnemius muscle is the Achilles tendon. The isolated function of the Gastrocnemius muscle is plantarflexion of the foot. The integrated function of the Gastrocnemius muscle is plantarflexion of the foot and assists in stabilizing the ankle during walking and running.
summary: This chunk describes the origin, insertion, and function of the Gastrocnemius muscle, highlighting both its isolated and integrated actions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2526
content: The origin of the Peroneus Longus muscle is the proximal attachments of the fibula and the interosseous membrane. The insertion of the Peroneus Longus muscle is the lateral malleolus of the ankle and the plantar fascia. The isolated function of the Peroneus Longus muscle is eversion and plantarflexion of the foot. The integrated function of the Peroneus Longus muscle is eversion and plantarflexion of the foot and assists in stabilizing the ankle during walking and running.
summary: This chunk describes the origin, insertion, and function of the Peroneus Longus muscle, highlighting both its isolated and integrated actions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2527
content: The origin of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle is the ischial tuberosity. The insertion of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle is the head of the fibula. The isolated function of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and external rotation of the leg. The integrated function of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and external rotation of the leg. The integrated function of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle also assists in stabilizing the knee during walking and running.
summary: This chunk describes the origin, insertion, and function of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle, highlighting both its isolated and integrated actions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2528
content: The origin of the Biceps Femoris—Short Head muscle is the linea aspera of the femur.
summary: This chunk describes the origin of the Biceps Femoris—Short Head muscle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2529
content: The origin of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle is the ischial tuberosity of the pelvis. The insertion of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle is the head of the fibula. The isolated function of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and external rotation of the leg. The integrated function of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and external rotation of the leg. The integrated function of the Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle also assists in stabilizing the knee during walking and running.
summary: The Biceps Femoris—Long Head muscle originates at the ischial tuberosity and inserts on the fibula, primarily acting to extend the hip, flex the knee, and externally rotate the leg, also contributing to knee stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2530
content: The origin of the Biceps Femoris—Short Head muscle is the linea aspera of the femur. The insertion of the Biceps Femoris—Short Head muscle is the head of the fibula. The isolated function of the Biceps Femoris—Short Head muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and external rotation of the leg. The integrated function of the Biceps Femoris—Short Head muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and external rotation of the leg. The integrated function of the Biceps Femoris—Short Head muscle also assists in stabilizing the knee during walking and running.
summary: The Biceps Femoris—Short Head muscle originates on the femur and inserts on the fibula, performing similar functions to the Long Head, contributing to hip extension, knee flexion, external rotation, and knee stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2531
content: The origin of the semimembranosus muscle is the ischial tuberosity of the pelvis. The insertion of the semimembranosus muscle is the medial aspect of the tibial plateau of the tibia. The isolated function of the semimembranosus muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and internal rotation of the knee. The integrated function of the semimembranosus muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and internal rotation of the knee. The integrated function of the semimembranosus muscle also assists in the stabilization of the hip joint.
summary: The semimembranosus muscle originates on the pelvis and inserts on the tibia, primarily extending the hip, flexing the knee, and internally rotating the knee, also contributing to hip stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2532
content: The origin of the semitendinosus muscle is the ischial tuberosity of the pelvis and the upper part of the adductor magnus muscle. The insertion of the semitendinosus muscle is the medial aspect of the tibial plateau of the tibia. The isolated function of the semitendinosus muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and internal rotation of the knee. The integrated function of the semitendinosus muscle is hip extension, knee flexion, and internal rotation of the knee. The integrated function of the semitendinosus muscle also assists in the stabilization of the hip joint.
summary: The semitendinosus muscle originates on the pelvis and adductor magnus, inserting on the tibia, performing similar functions to the semimembranosus, extending the hip, flexing the knee, internally rotating the knee, and contributing to hip stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2533
content: The origin of the vastus lateralis muscle is the lateral and inferior part of the femoral shaft and the intertrochanteric line of the femur. The insertion of the vastus lateralis muscle is the patellar tendon and the lateral aspect of the tibial tuberosity. The isolated function of the vastus lateralis muscle is knee extension. The integrated function of the vastus lateralis muscle is knee extension, hip flexion, and internal rotation of the knee. The integrated function of the vastus lateralis muscle also assists in the stabilization of the knee joint.
summary: The vastus lateralis muscle originates on the femur and inserts on the patella and tibia, primarily extending the knee, also contributing to hip flexion, internal rotation, and knee stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2534
content: The origin of the vastus medialis muscle is the medial part of the femoral shaft and the intertrochanteric line of the femur. The insertion of the vastus medialis muscle is the patellar tendon and the medial aspect of the tibial tuberosity. The isolated function of the vastus medialis muscle is knee extension. The integrated function of the vastus medialis muscle is knee extension, hip flexion, and internal rotation of the knee. The integrated function of the vastus medialis muscle also assists in the stabilization of the knee joint.
summary: The vastus medialis muscle originates on the femur and inserts on the patella and tibia, primarily extending the knee, also contributing to hip flexion, internal rotation, and knee stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2535
content: The origin of the vastus intermedius muscle is the anterior and lateral part of the femoral shaft. The insertion of the vastus intermedius muscle is the patellar tendon and the middle aspect of the tibial tuberosity. The isolated function of the vastus intermedius muscle is knee extension. The integrated function of the vastus intermedius muscle is knee extension, hip flexion, and internal rotation of the knee. The integrated function of the vastus intermedius muscle also assists in the stabilization of the knee joint.
summary: The vastus intermedius muscle originates on the femur and inserts on the patella and tibia, primarily extending the knee, also contributing to hip flexion, internal rotation, and knee stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2536
content: The origin of the rectus femoris muscle is the anterior inferior iliac spine and the superior part of the rectus femoris muscle. The insertion of the rectus femoris muscle is the patellar tendon and the middle aspect of the tibial tuberosity. The isolated function of the rectus femoris muscle is knee extension and hip flexion. The integrated function of the rectus femoris muscle is knee extension, hip flexion, and internal rotation of the knee. The integrated function of the rectus femoris muscle also assists in the stabilization of the hip joint.

The isolated function of the scalenes muscles is elevation of the first and second ribs. The integrated function of the scalenes muscles is elevation of the first and second ribs, along with other muscles.
summary: The scalenes muscles, originating from the cervical vertebrae and inserting into the second rib, primarily elevate the first and second ribs, acting both independently and as part of a synergistic group.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2592
content: The longus colli muscle originates from the anterior surface of the transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae. The longus colli muscle inserts into the anterior arch of the atlas. The isolated function of the longus colli muscle is flexion of the neck, lateral flexion of the neck, and rotation of the neck. The integrated function of the longus colli muscle is flexion of the neck, lateral flexion of the neck, and rotation of the neck, along with other muscles.
summary: The longus colli muscle, originating from the cervical vertebrae and inserting into the atlas, contributes to neck flexion, lateral flexion, and rotation, acting both independently and as part of a larger muscle group.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2593
content: The origin of the Longus Capitis muscle is the transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae. The insertion of the Longus Capitis muscle is the inferior surface of the superior articular process of the atlas.
summary: The Longus Capitis muscle originates from the cervical vertebrae and inserts into the atlas, contributing to neck movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2594
content: The Subscapularis muscle has an isolated function of internal rotation of the shoulder. The integrated function of the Subscapularis muscle is internal rotation of the shoulder.
summary: The Subscapularis muscle is responsible for internal rotation of the shoulder, both independently and as part of a larger muscle group.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2595
content: The longus colli muscle inserts into the anterior arch of the atlas. The isolated function of the longus colli muscle is flexion of the neck, lateral flexion of the neck, and rotation of the neck. The integrated function of the longus colli muscle is flexion of the neck, lateral flexion of the neck, and rotation of the neck, along with other muscles.
summary: Describes the insertion, isolated function, and integrated function of the longus colli muscle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2596
content: The origin of the Longus Capitis muscle is the transverse processes of the cervical vertebrae. The insertion of the Longus Capitis muscle is the inferior surface of the superior articular process of the atlas. The isolated function of the Longus Capitis muscle is flexion of the cervical spine. The integrated function of the Longus Capitis muscle is flexion of the cervical spine and lateral flexion of the neck.
summary: Details the origin, insertion, and functions of the Longus Capitis muscle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2597
content: The traditional formula for determining Heart Rate Max (HRmax) is 220-age. Updated research has shown that the formula 220-age has little scientific merit for use in exercise physiology and related fields. The regression formula 208−(0.7 × age) has been adopted for determining HRmax.
summary: Explains the evolution of formulas for determining Heart Rate Max (HRmax).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2598
content: In the 2nd and 3rd trimesters of pregnancy, power and speed assessments should be avoided. In the 2nd and 3rd trimesters of pregnancy, push-up assessments should be modified to be performed on the knees. In the 2nd and 3rd trimesters of pregnancy, the single-leg squat assessment should be modified to a single-leg balance. In the 2nd and 3rd trimesters of pregnancy, the range of motion (ROM) for the Overhead Squat (OHS) should be decreased.
summary: Provides modifications for fitness assessments during pregnancy (2nd and 3rd trimesters).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2599
content: The Rockport walk test is a preferred cardiorespiratory assessment for obese populations. For obese populations, a single-leg balance should be considered in replacement of the single-leg squat. For obese populations, modified push-ups on a bench or from the knees should be considered for push-up tests.
summary: Recommends assessments and modifications for obese populations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2600
content: Clients with low back pain should be watched for anterior or posterior rotation of the pelvis during the overhead squat, pushing, and pulling assessments. The push-up position may be too difficult for clients with low back pain, in which case they may perform the assessment on their knees or with their hands on a bench.
summary: Offers guidance for assessing clients with low back pain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2601
content: Clients with a history of shoulder injuries should be watched for the arms falling forward during the overhead squat and/or the shoulders elevating and the head migrating forward during pushing and pulling assessments. Positions that place a large amount of stress on the shoulder complex should be eliminated or adjusted to decrease stress for clients with a history of shoulder injuries.
summary: Provides recommendations for assessing clients with shoulder injuries.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2602
content: An athlete whose knee excessively adducts during the single-leg squat assessment should probably not do a shark skill test. The fitness professional may modify the shark skill test to be performed using two legs versus one if the assessment is key to the client’s programming and demands of his or her sport.
summary: Advises on modifications for the shark skill test based on knee movement and client needs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2603
content: Gravity is a constant downward directed force that can have a great impact on the demands of the core, particularly in the prone position. The force of gravity can be manipulated to lessen the demands on the core by decreasing the body’s lever length in relation to gravity. This can be done by putting an individual in a more inclined position (e.g., hands on a bench with feet on the floor) or bringing the pivot point closer to the center of gravity (e.g., performing prone assessments with knees on the floor).
summary: Explains how gravity affects core demands and offers modifications to lessen the impact.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2604
content: The purpose of the vertical jump test is to assess lower extremity power. The client stands with their side next to a wall and reaches up with the hand closest to the wall, keeping the feet flat on the ground, and the point of the fingertips is marked or recorded. This is called the standing reach height. The client then stands away from the wall, leaps vertically (without stepping) as high as possible, using both arms and legs to assist in projecting the body upwards, and touches the wall. The difference in distance between the standing reach height and the jump height is the score. The best of three attempts is recorded for the vertical jump test. When reassessing, the individual’s jump height should be higher.
summary: Describes the vertical jump test procedure and scoring.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2605
content: The purpose of the 40-yard dash is to assess acceleration and speed. The 40-yard dash is best performed on a track or field of at least 60 yards. The client starts from a comfortable, stationary, three-point-stance position with the front foot behind the starting line. The client should hold the starting position for 3 seconds prior to starting the 40-yard dash. When ready, the client sprints to the end cone. Timing begins at the client’s first movement and stops the moment the client’s chest crosses the end cone. Typically, two trials are allowed for the 40-yard dash, with the best time recorded. When reassessing, the individual’s time should be less for the 40-yard dash.
summary: Details the 40-yard dash test procedure and scoring.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2606
content: The client should hold the starting position for 3 seconds prior to starting the 40-yard dash. When ready, the client sprints to the end cone. Timing begins at the client’s first movement and stops the moment the client’s chest crosses the end cone. Typically, two trials are allowed for the 40-yard dash, with the best time recorded. When reassessing, the individual’s time should be less for the 40-yard dash.
summary: Instructions and protocol for the 40-yard dash assessment, emphasizing improvement upon reassessment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2607
content: The purpose of the pro shuttle test is to assess speed, explosion, body control, and the ability to change direction (agility). Three marker cones are placed along a line 5 yards apart for the pro shuttle test. The client stands at the middle cone for the pro shuttle test. On the signal “Go,” the client turns and runs 5 yards to the right side and touches the line with the right hand. The client then runs 10 yards to the left and touches the other line with the left hand. Finally, the client turns and finishes by running back through the start/finish line (the middle cone). The best time seen in three trials is recorded for the pro shuttle test. When reassessed, the time should be lower for the pro shuttle test.
summary: Description of the pro shuttle test, its purpose, and the execution protocol, with emphasis on improvement upon reassessment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2608
content: The purpose of the LEFT test is to assess agility, acceleration, deceleration, and neuromuscular control. Two marker cones are placed 10 yards apart for the LEFT test. On the signal “Go,” the client sprints from cone 1 to cone 2, backpedals back to cone 1, side shuffles to cone 2 then to cone 1, Carioca’s to cone 2 then to cone 1, and finishes with a sprint to cone 2. The time is recorded for the LEFT test. When reassessed, the time should be lower for the LEFT test.
summary: Description of the LEFT test, its purpose, and the execution protocol, with emphasis on improvement upon reassessment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2609
content: The purpose of the gait assessment is to assess dynamic posture during ambulation. Clients with turned out feet can use the treadmill after doing flexibility, and if the speed is at a level where they can focus on keeping the five kinetic chain checkpoints aligned. Gait treadmill walking assessment compensations include feet flattening/knees moving inward. Gait treadmill walking assessment compensations include low back arches. Gait treadmill walking assessment compensations include shoulders rounding. Gait treadmill walking assessment compensations include feet flattening and/or turning out. Gait treadmill walking assessment compensations include pelvic rotation. Gait treadmill walking assessment compensations include hip hikes. Gait treadmill walking assessment compensations include head forward. Clients with turned out feet can use the elliptical trainer with the foot positioned straight and the pad will help maintain it. Clients with turned out feet can use the rowing machine because the foot position does not require as much control. Clients with turned out feet can use the versa climber because it uses a foot pad that can assist in maintaining a neutral foot position. Cycling with upright posture is better for clients with upper body compensations. Standing upright is better for the upper body and puts the weight back on the legs where it should be. Clients should avoid holding the treadmill rails, which pulls the upper body forward into the compensation. If the TV is positioned too high, the individual may have to put the head into excessive extension, leading to the head protruding forward.
summary: Description of gait assessment, treadmill compensations, and exercise recommendations for clients with turned out feet, emphasizing proper posture and alignment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2610
content: Cardiac rehabilitation is a four-phase process that is important for those who have experienced a heart attack, heart valve repair, artery bypass surgery, angioplasty, or those who suffer from chronic chest pain. Phase One of Cardiac Rehab is conducted at a medical facility and generally involves 36 outpatient visits over 12 weeks, which include directly supervised exercise and lifestyle modification education. Phase Two of Cardiac Rehab is prescribed by a physician if continued improvement under direct supervision is needed. It may last up to another 12 weeks and is also conducted in a cardiac rehabilitation setting. Phases Three–Four of Cardiac Rehab are self-paced programs that involve exercise direction and motivation to sustain lifestyle changes and healthy behaviors. These phases may be conducted outside of a medical facility but require interaction between the physician and the fitness professional to ensure safety.
summary: Description of the four phases of cardiac rehabilitation, outlining the progression from supervised to self-paced programs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2611
content: The principle of progressive resistance exercise shows that resistance training programs should start with light loads and increase as strength adaptations are achieved. Two ways that the OPT model can be used in an undulating, nonlinear fashion are using weekly cycles composed of various phases of the OPT model and using weekly cycles composed of one phase of the OPT model, but with intensities and volume varied throughout the week.
summary: Explanation of progressive resistance exercise and two methods for implementing the OPT model in a nonlinear fashion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2612
content: Phase One of Cardiac Rehab is conducted at a medical facility and generally involves 36 outpatient visits over 12 weeks, which include directly supervised exercise and lifestyle modification education. Phase Two of Cardiac Rehab is prescribed by a physician if continued improvement under direct supervision is needed. It may last up to another 12 weeks and is also conducted in a cardiac rehabilitation setting. Phases Three–Four of Cardiac Rehab are self-paced programs that involve exercise direction and motivation to sustain lifestyle changes and healthy behaviors. These phases may be conducted outside of a medical facility but require interaction between the physician and the fitness professional to ensure safety.
summary: Cardiac Rehab progresses through four phases, starting with supervised exercise and lifestyle modification, transitioning to self-paced programs with ongoing professional guidance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2613
content: The principle of progressive resistance exercise shows that resistance training programs should start with light loads and increase as strength adaptations are achieved.
summary: Progressive resistance exercise involves gradually increasing weight or resistance as strength improves.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2614
content: Two ways that the OPT model can be used in an undulating, nonlinear fashion are using weekly cycles composed of various phases of the OPT model and using weekly cycles composed of one phase of the OPT model, but with intensities and volume varied throughout the week.
summary: The OPT model can be implemented with undulating cycles, either varying phases weekly or varying intensity and volume within a single phase.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2615
content: To activate the correct muscles during the floor bridge, the client should move their feet to hip width and keep them straight to avoid activating the adductors. The supine bridge engages the gluteal muscles, which should be very powerful muscles. The prone iso-abs teaches the muscles that are designed to stabilize the spine to engage. A common hand and arm position during prone iso-abs is to bring the hands together, internally rotating at the shoulders. The arms should run parallel to each other when performing the prone iso-abs exercise.
summary: Proper form for floor bridges, prone iso-abs, and supine bridges emphasizes glute activation and spinal stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2616
content: During single-leg balance exercise, monitor feet turn out as the foot will turn out with the increased challenge of balance training in an attempt to increase the base of support. During single-leg balance exercise, monitor anterior pelvic tilt as it is an exercise that can be incredibly demanding on the muscles around the hips and spine. During single-leg stance, the gluteal muscles need to work with the abdominal muscles to keep the hips and the stance leg in proper position. During the staggered stance, it is very common for the rear leg to turn out, exacerbating the compensation of the feet turning out. The rear leg should be straight, and if necessary, the client can get the rear leg into a triple-extended position.
summary: Single-leg balance exercises require attention to foot position, pelvic tilt, and glute activation to maintain stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2617
content: If the hip flexors are restricting motion, then the front of the hips will likely be pulled down, exacerbating the compensation. Performing many repetitions of lat pull-downs, pull-ups, or heavy chest exercises may make the compensation worse. If the client moves into an anterior pelvic tilt, overhead movements should be avoided until proper extensibility has been restored to the lats and pectorals.
summary: Muscle imbalances, particularly tight hip flexors and overactive chest muscles, can lead to anterior pelvic tilt and compensation during exercises.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2618
content: Try to avoid the negative connotation that often comes with the word ‘homework.’ It is important to get clients to realize that repetition enables them to progress more quickly. Many clients pursuing a weight loss goal suffer from a lack of motivation or even anxiety toward exercise. Some will be motivated to go to the gym and perform an hour-long workout session without the trainer, whereas others may prefer to perform flexibility and light cardio, such as a walk in the neighborhood.
summary: Client motivation and exercise adherence can vary, emphasizing the importance of personalized programming and positive reinforcement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2619
content: To encourage completion, training should begin with simple tasks, and fitness professionals should periodically assess the client’s readiness to take on more complex exercises. A training template should track the phase of training, exercise selection, intensity, volume, and outcome.
summary: Effective training programs involve progressive overload, regular assessment, and meticulous tracking of progress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2620
content: Muscle imbalances can in large part be attributed to the client’s lifestyle and daily activity pattern. There is no amount of foam-rolling, stretching, or strengthening that can correct poor daily habits. Fitness professionals should teach their clients how to move when performing basic tasks throughout their day. The client learning how to move properly when not in the gym may be the most important factor when considering muscle imbalances. This will require the fitness professional to provide the client with movement-specific homework.
summary: Addressing muscle imbalances requires not only exercise but also correcting poor movement patterns in daily life.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2621
content: The squat uses two of the largest muscle groups in the body, the back and the gluteals, as well as all of the muscles around the core. The squat requires work from many very large muscles, and adequate range of motion in some very important joints. The lunge is similar to a squat with a different footprint. The deadlift is important for clients who lack the range of motion to squat but need to work their gluteal muscles.
summary: The squat, lunge, and deadlift are compound exercises targeting major muscle groups and requiring proper form and range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2622
content: The feet typically turn out during calf raises because the calves are shortened and overactive. The knees cave in during adductor machine exercises because the adductors are already overactive. The abductor machine may appear to be indicated with knees caving in. Leg extension machines focus on the quadriceps muscle group, so isolating the quads with that movement compensation could potentially make it worse. The leg press is a quad-dominant machine and does not allow the gluteal muscles to work to their full potential. Some of the adductors also serve as hip flexors (pectineus and adductor brevis). Isolating the adductors will potentially make the compensation worse. Leg raises strengthen the hip flexors, which are overactive in an anterior pelvic tilt. Leg extension is designed to isolate the quadriceps, so isolating the quadriceps is not recommended in the presence of an anterior pelvic tilt.
summary: Common exercise compensations and modifications for addressing muscle imbalances.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2623
content: Rowing motions will teach scapular retraction and depression as well as strengthen muscles that are often weak. The scapular exercise uses the entire shoulder girdle in the most functional plane of motion. This is the recommended beginning exercise for most clients once the client is able to coordinate the muscular activity. Can be used with any movement compensation as long as the client can safely perform it.
summary: Scapular exercises, particularly rowing motions, are crucial for shoulder health and can be adapted to various movement compensations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2624
content: Leg extension machines focus on the quadriceps muscle group, so isolating the quads with that movement compensation could potentially make it worse. Leg extension is designed to isolate the quadriceps, so isolating the quadriceps is not recommended in the presence of an anterior pelvic tilt.
summary: Isolating the quadriceps, particularly with leg extensions, can worsen anterior pelvic tilt.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2625
content: The leg press is a quad-dominant machine and does not allow the gluteal muscles to work to their full potential. Leg curl may be performed incorrectly due to inflexibility of the quadriceps and hip flexors, weakness of the hamstrings, and overactivity of the low back muscles, all indicated by anterior pelvic tilt.
summary: Leg press and leg curl can be problematic for individuals with anterior pelvic tilt.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2626
content: Some of the adductors also serve as hip flexors (pectineus and adductor brevis). Isolating the adductors will potentially make the compensation worse. Leg raises strengthen the hip flexors, which are overactive in an anterior pelvic tilt.
summary: Exercises targeting adductors and hip flexors can exacerbate anterior pelvic tilt.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2627
content: Muscles that control the scapula in upward rotation should be worked on before performing lat strengthening work.
summary: Prioritize scapular stability exercises before lat strengthening.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2628
content: The chest press machine is often used with a significant amount of weight, which increases the strength of the prime movers without teaching proper stabilization. The shoulder press is not harmful, but if the lats are inflexible then clients will likely arch their low back in order to successfully execute the shoulder press.
summary: Heavy chest press and shoulder press can neglect stabilization and potentially lead to poor form.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2629
content: Women should consume 2.7 L (91 oz.) of water per day. Men should consume 3.7 L (125 oz.) of water per day.
summary: Daily water intake recommendations differ based on gender.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2630
content: 2 hours pre-exercise, consume 14–20 oz. of water. 15 minutes pre-exercise, consume 16 oz. of water, if tolerated. During exercise, consume 4–8 oz. of water every 15–20 minutes or 16–32 ounces every hour. Postexercise, consume 50 ounces for every kilogram (2.2 pounds) of body weight lost.
summary: Hydration guidelines for exercise include pre-exercise, during exercise, and post-exercise intake.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2631
content: Food provides 600–800 mL of water intake. Beverages provide 1,000 mL of water intake. Metabolic water (from digestion) provides 200–300 mL of water intake.
summary: Water intake comes from various sources, including food, beverages, and metabolism.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2632
content: Urine provides 900–1,200 mL of water output. Mild sweating provides 400 mL of water output. Lungs provide 300 mL of water output. Feces provide 200 mL of water output.
summary: Water output occurs through urine, sweat, respiration, and feces.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2633
content: Dry mouth, headache, rapid heartbeat, sleepiness or tiredness, constipation, rapid breathing, thirst, dizziness, fever, decreased urine output, sunken eyes, delirium, dry skin, low blood pressure, and unconsciousness are signs of dehydration.
summary: Dehydration manifests through various physical and cognitive symptoms.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2634
content: Mobile applications can provide guidance to clients while they are traveling or even through remote personal training. Mobile applications can organize and track exercise order (with visual demonstrations of exercise technique), sets, reps, and even rest periods can be prescribed through an app, allowing the client to complete workouts on their own while on vacation, traveling for business, or away from the gym.
summary: Mobile apps offer remote training and workout tracking capabilities.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2635
content: Professionals can use mobile applications to monitor workload, whether the workout is performed in person or remote. Mobile applications can facilitate encouragement and motivation from the professional and surrounding community by using social networks.
summary: Mobile apps enable remote monitoring and community support for clients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2636
content: Nutritional apps can provide valuable information and assistance on client’s habits throughout the day/week.
summary: Nutritional apps support healthy eating habits and provide guidance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2637
content: Ensure high fluid intake for several days before competition (urine should be pale in color). Consume 14–20 ounces (1.75–2.5 cups) of fluid 2 hours before exercise. Consume 16 ounces about 15 minutes before exercise, if tolerated.
summary: Pre-competition hydration guidelines emphasize fluid intake days before and before exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2638
content: Consume water or sports drinks rather than soda or juice. Fluid absorption is accelerated with a 6% carbohydrate drink (any popular sports drink). Cold water or fluid is more rapidly absorbed.
summary: Hydration recommendations prioritize water and sports drinks over sugary beverages, emphasizing cold temperature and carbohydrate content for faster absorption.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2639
content: Drink 4–8 ounces (0.5–1.0 cup) every 15–20 minutes or 16–32 ounces of fluid every hour during exercise. If the weather is very hot, more fluid may be required during exercise.
summary: Fluid intake during exercise should be consistent and adjusted based on environmental conditions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2640
content: Consuming fluids with 500–700 grams of sodium per 33 ounces of water enhances fluid replacement during exercise. Drink sports drinks containing 6–8% glucose for exercise lasting longer than 60 minutes. Sodas, teas, and juices are not ideal, and may result in the reverse of the desired effect during exercise. Drinking plain water without electrolytes can also be a problem during exercise.
summary: Fluid replacement strategies for exercise include sodium and glucose supplementation, while avoiding sugary drinks and emphasizing electrolyte balance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2641
content: If the weather is very hot, more fluid may be required during exercise. Drinking plain water without electrolytes can also be a problem during exercise. Consume 50 ounces of fluid for every kilogram (2.2 pounds) of body weight lost after exercise. For exercise longer than 1 hour in duration, consuming a drink containing sodium and glucose will promote rapid rehydration after exercise.
summary: Fluid intake during and after exercise, especially in hot weather, should consider electrolytes and fluid replacement needs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2642
content: Consuming fluids with 500–700 grams of sodium per 33 ounces of water enhances fluid replacement during exercise. Drink sports drinks containing 6–8% glucose for exercise lasting longer than 60 minutes. Take electrolyte pills with water and/or eat food if sports drinks are not favorable during exercise.
summary: Fluid replacement strategies for exercise, including sports drinks and electrolyte supplementation, are discussed.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2643
content: Activity trackers can track movement and provide information regarding physical activity patterns. By monitoring physical activity throughout the day among their clients, fitness professionals can help guide people toward greater activity and facilitate faster results. Some activity trackers allow for the monitoring of heart rate, which can be valuable data for a fitness professional. Lower heart rate during work and rest signifies a more efficient and fit cardiorespiratory system.
summary: Activity trackers and heart rate monitoring can be used by fitness professionals to assess and guide client activity levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2644
content: Some activity trackers can track sleep patterns, enabling for the evaluation of quality of sleep along with alterations in training plans as needed. Fitness professionals should explain to clients how various mobile apps will be utilized and why their use is beneficial.
summary: Activity trackers can also monitor sleep, and fitness professionals should educate clients on app usage.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2645
content: Each social media outlet is a powerful method of inexpensive promotion of personal training services and offers a unique opportunity for the fitness professional to position himself/herself. Social media is instrumental in helping develop relationships between professionals and their clients. The primary goals of utilizing social media to further personal training business are: Connect with current and potential clients outside of the gym, Establish a position of expertise and leadership, Share information about services, events, and training opportunities.
summary: Social media is a valuable tool for fitness professionals to promote their services, connect with clients, and establish expertise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2646
content: Additional goals of utilizing social media to further personal training business are: Encourage and motivate clients, Provide educational messages, Show relevancy in the industry, Establish credibility. Examples of educational posts that can help the professional gain credibility include: Short videos discussing exercise techniques for various modalities, Blog posts discussing a recent research article, Suggestions for creative ways of overcoming barriers to fitness, Recommendations of training strategies for specific populations such as weight loss or older adults, Examples of training plans for a variety of clients, Philosophical statements that describe the fitness professional’s approach to program design along with examples of how programs are altered based on individual needs, Fitness tip of the day.
summary: Social media can be used to educate clients, build credibility, and provide ongoing support.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2647
content: Determining what fitness trends are simply fads and what technologies will develop and thrive is helpful to planning ahead in the fitness industry. It may be difficult to really know what consumers will gravitate towards in coming years. Wearable technology is an area that will continue to see the greatest interest and development in fitness technology. Devices that can be synced to smart phones to track and display exercise habits in real time may be an area of rapid growth as well as the development of new apps and greater physiological variables being monitored by each device.
summary: The fitness industry should anticipate future trends and technological advancements, particularly in wearable technology.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2648
content: The development of fitness apps is projected to continue, especially now that app developers are becoming more accessible to fitness professionals.
summary: Fitness apps are expected to continue evolving and becoming more accessible to fitness professionals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2649
content: Self-confidence can lead to greater exercise adherence, which, in turn, results in more confidence, which leads to increased exercise. Fitness professionals should try to build their clients’ confidence so they can initiate and maintain a commitment to exercise.
summary: Building client confidence is crucial for exercise adherence and long-term success.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2650
content: Performance accomplishments are the strongest source of self-confidence. Performance accomplishments focus on personal task improvement and success, rather than on comparisons with others. Watching other, similar individuals successfully perform the desired task can increase a person’s confidence in his or her ability to complete the task. Being persuaded by someone else (e.g., fitness professional, coach, friend) that he or she can perform the task successfully can increase confidence. When people imagine themselves performing a task, it increases their confidence that they will actually be able to perform it.
summary: Performance accomplishments, social influence, and visualization can all contribute to increased self-confidence.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2651
content: Motivational interviewing has been defined as a collaborative person-centered form of guiding to elicit and strengthen motivation for change. The foundation of motivational interviewing is an empathetic, person-centered style, emphasizing evoking and strengthening the client’s own motivation for healthy change. Motivational interviewing involves knowledge and skills in four areas: expressing empathy, helping the client realize the gap between values and problematic behavior (developing discrepancy), respecting the client’s resistance as being normal, and supporting the client’s self-efficacy. It is the client’s task, not the fitness professional’s, to articulate the client’s ambivalence (exercise vs. not exercising). Motivation to change is elicited from the client. Readiness to change is not a client trait, but rather a fluctuating product of interpersonal interaction (the fitness professional might be assuming a greater readiness for change than is the case).
summary: Motivational interviewing is a collaborative approach that emphasizes client-driven change and utilizes empathy, discrepancy, and support to enhance motivation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2652
content: The autonomy-supportive style of coaching focuses on creating an environment that emphasizes self-improvement, rather than beating others (i.e., direct competition). Fitness professionals can support their clients’ autonomy by providing choices within limits, offering rationales for activity structures, recognizing clients’ feelings and perspectives, creating opportunities for clients to demonstrate initiative, avoiding overt control and criticism, providing informational feedback, and limiting clients’ ego involvement throughout their program (i.e., focus on self-improvement instead of comparing to others).
summary: Autonomy-supportive coaching emphasizes client self-improvement and provides choices, support, and feedback to foster intrinsic motivation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2653
content: It is the client’s task, not the fitness professional’s, to articulate the client’s ambivalence (exercise vs. not exercising). Motivation to change is elicited from the client. Readiness to change is not a client trait, but rather a fluctuating product of interpersonal interaction (the fitness professional might be assuming a greater readiness for change than is the case).
summary: Client motivation and readiness for change are emphasized, highlighting the importance of client self-reflection and avoiding assumptions by the fitness professional.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2654
content: The autonomy-supportive style of coaching focuses on creating an environment that emphasizes self-improvement, rather than beating others (i.e., direct competition). Fitness professionals can support their clients’ autonomy by providing choices within limits, offering rationales for activity structures, recognizing clients’ feelings and perspectives, creating opportunities for clients to demonstrate initiative, avoiding overt control and criticism, providing informational feedback, and limiting clients’ ego involvement throughout their program (i.e., focus on self-improvement instead of comparing to others).
summary: Autonomy-supportive coaching prioritizes client self-improvement and agency through supportive practices and avoiding competitive pressures.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2655
content: Prompting is a cue that initiates a behavior. Prompts can be verbal, physical, or symbolic. A verbal prompt can be a slogan or phrase that encourages the client. A physical prompt might be helping someone get over a ‘sticking point’ in an activity. A symbolic prompt generally reminds a person to begin or continue a behavior, such as leaving out one’s workout gear the night before to promote physical activity.
summary: Various types of prompts (verbal, physical, symbolic) can be used to initiate and encourage desired behaviors in clients.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2656
content: The goal is to increase cues for the desired behavior and decrease cues for competing behaviors. Written statements that outline specific behaviors and establish consequences for fulfillment (or lack thereof) are known as contracts. Contracts typically specify expectations, responsibilities, and contingencies for behavioral change. The purpose of contracts is to help the client take action, establishing criteria for meeting goals, and providing a means for clarifying consequences. Contracts increase the individual’s public commitment and foster a sense of self-control.
summary: Contracts and behavioral prompting strategies are used to increase desired behaviors and decrease competing ones, promoting client commitment and self-control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2657
content: A performance or attendance graph usually represents data in a form that is easily understood by everyone involved. The visual representation of progress by a chart is extremely helpful because a client can note even small changes in behavior and performance. This public information allows fitness professionals and the clients to offer praise and encouragement for the client on the chart. Capitalizing on individuals’ inherent interest to reach certain outcomes provides periodic, positive feedback on the progress that has been made. Feedback is provided with specific information on performing the behavior in question.
summary: Performance tracking and feedback are crucial for motivating clients and highlighting progress, fostering a sense of accomplishment and encouraging continued effort.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2658
content: This approach focuses on ways to help someone solve current issues. The focus will rely upon addressing thoughts and behaviors that are detrimental to the growth of the individual. Association occurs when the focus is on internal body feedback (e.g., how their muscles feel or their breathing feels). Dissociation occurs when the focus is on the external environment (e.g., noticing how pretty the scenery is or listening to music while exercising).
summary: Problem-solving and mindfulness techniques, focusing on internal body feedback (association) or external environment (dissociation), can be used to address individual needs and promote positive change.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2659
content: Social support refers to an individual’s favorable attitude toward another person’s involvement in an exercise program. This approach emphasizes the internal enjoyment and fun of exercise, and making it something to look forward to—not just as a means to an external goal such as weight loss (Kimiecik, 2002). Professionals should help clients find activities they enjoy.
summary: Encouraging social support and focusing on intrinsic enjoyment of exercise are key to promoting long-term adherence and positive experiences.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2660
content: Keeping accurate records is an important part of a fitness professional’s job both for business purposes and for designing and implementing workouts. Details matter in running a successful long-lasting business. Fitness professionals often see several clients in a row, which makes it all the more important to write down an outline for the workout in each person’s file. Having a chart/folder to refer to, and making notes during or soon after the session, makes for better and more efficient fitness professionals.
summary: Maintaining detailed records is essential for both client progress tracking and business success, ensuring efficient and personalized service.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2661
content: It is important that fitness professionals record not only clients’ objective workout results, but also what was discussed during the workout. Fitness professionals should keep two types of records. The first type is concerned with clients’ progress. Clients come in with different abilities, needs, and goals; these should be individualized and tracked on a daily, weekly, or monthly basis, depending on what is most germane to the client. The other type of records that professionals should keep is concerned with the business aspect of the job.
summary: Fitness professionals should maintain two types of records: client progress and business-related information, ensuring personalized care and efficient business operations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2662
content: The specific items recorded will vary based on the job responsibilities, but the following are some typical records that are kept on a daily, weekly, monthly, or yearly basis: Number of days worked Number of days off Average income (per hour)
summary: Business-related records, such as days worked, days off, and average income, are essential for tracking performance and managing the fitness professional's business.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2663
content: The other type of records that professionals should keep is concerned with the business aspect of the job. The specific items recorded will vary based on the job responsibilities, but the following are some typical records that are kept on a daily, weekly, monthly, or yearly basis: Number of days worked, Average income (per hour), Number of days off, Client hours cancelled/rescheduled, Percentage of cancellations that were paid, Mileage driven (to in-home clients), Average number of sessions, Average time spent in the gym not getting paid.
summary: This chunk outlines the types of business-related records professionals should keep, including daily, weekly, monthly, and yearly data points.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2664
content: A multitude of tactics can be used to study the content you have learned in your CPT course to prepare for the credentialing exam. Use the study approach that maximizes your ability to retain and recall the information you learned. However, regardless of the study approach, it is important to prepare for the CPT exam in a kinesthetic way. This will allow you to have the same memory recall while taking the CPT exam. Performing an isolated contraction would give you firsthand experience on how a problematic muscle feels. So, if you are stuck on a question that tests the concept of a problematic muscle, you may practice the movement of the muscle to refresh your memory.
summary: This chunk provides advice on effective study strategies for the CPT exam, emphasizing kinesthetic learning and practical application.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2665
content: Don’t try to learn everything the night before the exam. Structure your schedule to allow for ample time to study and review the CPT material. Get 8 hours of sleep before the exam. It is essential to get enough rest to be able to focus during the exam. Eat before the exam. Ensuring you have sufficient energy to sit through the exam is vital for your ability to focus.
summary: This chunk offers practical tips for exam day preparation, including scheduling, sleep, and nutrition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2666
content: Focus on the test in front of you. If you don’t know an answer, mark it and move on. Spending time on a question you don’t know will do little good. Don’t worry if others finish before you. Finishing first or last has nothing to do with your score. Make good use of the time allotted to you.
summary: This chunk provides test-taking strategies, emphasizing focus, pacing, and managing anxiety.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2667
content: All questions are multiple choice, providing you four answer choices with 4 choices. The CPT exam questions are focused on the objective criteria rather than the subjective criteria.
summary: This chunk clarifies the format and content focus of the CPT exam.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2668
content: The A-Band is the region of the sarcomere where myosin filaments are predominantly seen with minor overlap of the actin filaments.
summary: This chunk defines the A-Band within the sarcomere.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2669
content: Abduction is a movement in the frontal plane away from the midline of the body.
summary: This chunk defines abduction as a movement in the frontal plane.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2670
content: Acceleration is when a muscle exerts more force than is being placed on it, the muscle will shorten; also known as a concentric contraction or force production.
summary: This chunk defines acceleration and its relationship to concentric contractions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2671
content: Acidosis is the accumulation of excessive hydrogen that causes increased acidity of the blood and muscle.
summary: This chunk defines acidosis and its physiological effects.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2672
content: Actin is one of the two major myofilaments, actin is the ‘thin’ filament that acts along with myosin to produce muscular contraction.
summary: This chunk defines actin and its role in muscle contraction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2673
content: An action potential is a nerve impulse that allows neurons to transmit information.
summary: This chunk defines an action potential and its function in neuronal communication.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2674
content: Active flexibility is the ability of agonists and synergists to move a limb through the full range of motion while their functional antagonist is being stretched.
summary: This chunk defines active flexibility and its components.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2675
content: Active-isolated stretch is the process of using agonists and synergists to dynamically move the joint into a range of motion.
summary: This chunk defines active-isolated stretch and its technique.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2676
content: Acute variables are important components that specify how each exercise is to be performed.
summary: This chunk emphasizes the importance of acute variables in exercise prescription.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2677
content: Adaptive means capable of changing for a specific use.
summary: This chunk defines the term 'adaptive'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2678
content: Adduction is movement in the frontal plane back toward the midline of the body.
summary: This chunk defines adduction as a movement in the frontal plane.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2679
content: Adenosine diphosphate (ADP) is a high-energy compound occurring in all cells from which adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is formed.
summary: This chunk defines ADP and its role in ATP production.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2680
content: Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is an energy storage and transfer unit within the cells of the body.
summary: This chunk defines ATP and its function in cellular energy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2681
content: Adequate intake (AI) is a recommended average daily nutrient intake level, based on observed (or experimentally determined) approximations or estimates of nutrient intake that are assumed to be adequate for a group (or groups) of healthy people; this measure is used when an RDA cannot be determined.
summary: This chunk defines Adequate Intake (AI) as a nutrient intake recommendation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2682
content: Advanced stage is the second stage of the dynamic pattern perspective theory when learners gain the ability to alter and manipulate the movements more efficiently to adapt to environmental changes.
summary: This chunk defines the 'Advanced stage' within the dynamic pattern perspective theory.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2683
content: Aerobic means activities requiring oxygen.
summary: This chunk defines 'aerobic' as relating to activities requiring oxygen.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2684
content: Adenosine diphosphate (ADP) is a high-energy compound occurring in all cells from which adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is formed. Adenosine triphosphate (ATP) is an energy storage and transfer unit within the cells of the body.
summary: ATP and ADP are key energy molecules within cells.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2685
content: Adequate intake (AI) is a recommended average daily nutrient intake level, based on observed (or experimentally determined) approximations or estimates of nutrient intake that are assumed to be adequate for a group (or groups) of healthy people; this measure is used when an RDA cannot be determined.
summary: AI is a recommended nutrient intake level used when a Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) cannot be established.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2686
content: Aerobic means activities requiring oxygen. Anaerobic activities do not require oxygen.
summary: Aerobic and anaerobic activities differ in their oxygen requirements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2687
content: Afferent neurons (also known as sensory neurons) gather incoming sensory information from the environment and deliver it to the central nervous system.
summary: Afferent neurons transmit sensory information to the central nervous system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2688
content: Agility is the ability to accelerate, decelerate, stabilize, and change direction quickly while maintaining proper posture.
summary: Agility involves rapid movement changes while maintaining balance and posture.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2689
content: An agonist is a muscle that is the primary mover in a joint motion; also known as a prime mover. Antagonists are muscles that act in direct opposition to agonists (prime movers).
summary: Agonists and antagonists are muscles that work together to produce movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2690
content: The alarm reaction is the first stage of the General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS), the initial reaction to a stressor.  Autogenic inhibition is the process by which neural impulses that sense tension are greater than the impulses that cause muscles to contract, providing an inhibitory effect to the muscle spindles.
summary: The alarm reaction is the initial response to stress, while autogenic inhibition is a muscle inhibition mechanism.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2691
content: Altered reciprocal inhibition is the concept of muscle inhibition, caused by a tight agonist, which inhibits its functional antagonist.
summary: Altered reciprocal inhibition describes how muscle tightness can inhibit opposing muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2692
content: The amortization phase is the electromechanical delay a muscle experiences in the transition from eccentric to concentric muscle action.
summary: The amortization phase is the delay between eccentric and concentric muscle contractions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2693
content: The anaerobic threshold is the point during high-intensity activity when the body can no longer meet its demand for oxygen and anaerobic metabolism predominates; it is also called the lactate threshold.
summary: The anaerobic threshold marks the point where anaerobic metabolism becomes dominant during intense exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2694
content: Anatomic locations refers to terms that describe locations on the body. The anatomic position is the position with the body erect with the arms at the sides and the palms forward. The anatomic position is the position of reference for anatomic nomenclature. Anatomic terms such as anterior and posterior, medial and lateral, and abduction and adduction apply to the body when it is in the anatomic position.
summary: Anatomic terms describe body locations relative to a standard anatomical position.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2695
content: An annual plan is a generalized training plan that spans 1 year to show when the client will progress between phases.
summary: An annual plan outlines a year-long training progression.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2696
content: Anterior (or ventral) means on the front of the body.
summary: Anterior refers to the front of the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2697
content: The aortic semilunar valve controls blood flow from the left ventricle to the aorta going to the entire body.
summary: The aortic semilunar valve regulates blood flow from the left ventricle to the aorta.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2698
content: The appendicular skeleton is the portion of the skeletal system that includes the upper and lower extremities.
summary: The appendicular skeleton comprises the limbs and their attachments.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2699
content: Arteries are vessels that transport blood away from the heart. Arterioles are small terminal branches of an artery, which end in capillaries.
summary: Arteries carry blood away from the heart, branching into arterioles that lead to capillaries.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2700
content: Arteriosclerosis is a general term that refers to hardening (and loss of elasticity) of arteries. Atherosclerosis is 1. clogging, narrowing, and hardening of the body’s large arteries and medium-sized blood vessels. Atherosclerosis can lead to stroke, heart attack, eye problems, and kidney problems. 2. buildup of fatty plaques in arteries that leads to narrowing and reduced blood flow.
summary: Arteriosclerosis and atherosclerosis describe hardening and narrowing of arteries, often due to plaque buildup.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2701
content: Arthritis is chronic inflammation of the joints.
summary: Arthritis is a persistent inflammation of the joints.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2702
content: Arthrokinematics is 1. joint motion. 2. the motions of joints in the body. Arthrokinetic dysfunction is 1. a biomechanical and neuromuscular dysfunction in which forces at the joint are altered, resulting in abnormal joint movement and proprioception. 2. altered forces at the joint that result in abnormal muscular activity and impaired neuromuscular communication at the joint. Arthrokinetic inhibition is the neuromuscular phenomenon that occurs when a joint dysfunction inhibits the muscles that surround the joint.
summary: Arthrokinematics refers to joint motion, while arthrokinetic dysfunction and inhibition describe problems with joint movement and muscle function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2703
content: Articular (hyaline) cartilage is cartilage that covers the articular surfaces of bones. Articulation is junctions of bones, muscles, and connective tissue at which movement occurs; it is also known as a joint.
summary: Articular cartilage covers joint surfaces, and articulation refers to the points where bones, muscles, and connective tissue connect for movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2704
content: Assessment is a process of determining the importance, size, or value of something.
summary: Assessment involves evaluating the significance or worth of something.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2705
content: The association stage is Fitts’ second stage in which learners become more consistent with their movement with practice.
summary: The association stage in learning involves increased consistency and refinement of movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2706
content: Atmospheric pressure is everyday pressure in the air.
summary: Atmospheric pressure is the air pressure we experience daily.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2707
content: The atrioventricular (AV) node is a small mass of specialized cardiac muscle fibers, located in the wall of the right atrium of the heart, that receives heartbeat impulses from the sinoatrial node and directs them to the walls of the ventricles. Atrioventricular Valves allow for proper blood flow from the atria to the ventricles.
summary: The AV node and atrioventricular valves regulate heart rhythm and blood flow between the heart chambers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2708
content: The atrium is the superior chamber of the heart that receives blood from the veins and forces it into the ventricles.
summary: The atrium is the upper chamber of the heart that receives blood and pumps it to the ventricles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2709
content: Augmented feedback is information provided by some external source such as a fitness professional, videotape, or a heart rate monitor.
summary: Augmented feedback is external information provided to learners during training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2710
content: The atrioventricular (AV) node is a small mass of specialized cardiac muscle fibers, located in the wall of the right atrium of the heart, that receives heartbeat impulses from the sinoatrial node and directs them to the walls of the ventricles. Atrioventricular Valves allow for proper blood flow from the atria to the ventricles. The atrium is the superior chamber of the heart that receives blood from the veins and forces it into the ventricles. The bicuspid (mitral) valve has two cusps that control the blood flow from the left atrium to the left ventricle.
summary: This chunk describes the structure and function of the heart's chambers, valves, and the AV node.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2711
content: Blood is fluid that circulates in the heart, arteries, capillaries, and veins, carries nutrients and oxygen to all parts of the body, and also rids the body of waste products. Blood vessels are a network of hollow tubes that circulate blood throughout the body. Blood lipids, also known as cholesterol and triglycerides, are carried in the bloodstream by protein molecules known as high-density lipoproteins (HDL) and low-density lipoproteins (LDL). Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels, and the site of exchange of chemicals and water between the blood and the tissues.
summary: This chunk explains the composition, function, and circulation of blood within the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2712
content: Bones provide a resting ground for muscles and protection of vital organs.
summary: This chunk describes the primary functions of the skeletal system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2713
content: The axial skeleton is the portion of the skeletal system that consists of the skull, rib cage, and vertebral column.
summary: This chunk defines the axial skeleton and its components.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2714
content: Balance is the ability to sustain or return the body’s center of mass or line of gravity over its base of support. Balance is also when the body is in equilibrium and stationary, meaning no linear or angular movement.
summary: This chunk defines balance and its two aspects: dynamic and static.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2715
content: Ball-and-socket joints are the most-mobile joints that allow motion in all three planes. Examples include the shoulder and hip.
summary: This chunk describes the characteristics and examples of ball-and-socket joints.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2716
content: Backside mechanics is proper alignment of the rear leg and pelvis during sprinting, which includes ankle plantar flexion, knee extension, hip extension, and neutral pelvis.
summary: This chunk defines backside mechanics and its components during sprinting.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2717
content: The basal ganglia is a portion of the lower brain that is instrumental in the initiation and control of repetitive voluntary movements such as walking and running.
summary: This chunk describes the function of the basal ganglia in movement control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2718
content: Beta-oxidation (α-oxidation) is the breakdown of triglycerides into smaller subunits called free fatty acids (FFAs) to convert FFAs into acyl-CoA molecules, which then are available to enter the Krebs cycle and ultimately lead to the production of additional ATP.
summary: This chunk explains the process of beta-oxidation in energy production.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2719
content: Bioenergetics is the study of energy in the human body. The bioenergetic continuum is the three main pathways used by the kinetic chain to produce ATP.
summary: This chunk defines bioenergetics and its central concept, the bioenergetic continuum.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2720
content: Biomechanics is a study that uses principles of physics to quantitatively study how forces interact within a living body. Biomechanics is also the science concerned with the internal and external forces acting on the human body and the effects produced by these forces.
summary: This chunk defines biomechanics and its scope.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2721
content: Bipenniform muscle fibers are muscle fibers that are arranged with short, oblique fibers that extend from both sides of a long tendon. An example is the rectus femoris.
summary: This chunk describes the structure and example of bipenniform muscle fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2722
content: The brainstem is the link between the sensory and motor nerves coming from the brain to the body and vice versa.
summary: This chunk defines the function of the brainstem in neural communication.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2723
content: A calorie is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of 1 g of water 1°C. A Calorie is a unit of expression of energy equal to 1000 cal. It is the amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a kilogram or liter of water 1°C.
summary: This chunk defines the calorie and Calorie units of energy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2724
content: Cancer is any of various types of malignant neoplasms, most of which invade surrounding tissues, may metastasize to several sites, and are likely to recur after attempted removal and to cause death of the patient unless adequately treated.
summary: This chunk defines cancer and its characteristics.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2725
content: Carbohydrates are organic compounds of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen, which include starches, cellulose, and sugars, and are an important source of energy. All carbohydrates are eventually broken down in the body to glucose, a simple sugar. Carbohydrates are neutral compounds of carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen (such as sugars, starches, and celluloses), which make up a large portion of animal foods.
summary: This chunk describes the composition and function of carbohydrates.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2726
content: Cardiac muscle is heart muscle.
summary: This chunk defines cardiac muscle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2727
content: Cardiac output (Q) is heart rate multiplied by stroke volume, the overall performance of the heart.
summary: This chunk defines cardiac output and its components.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2728
content: Cardiorespiratory fitness is the ability of the circulatory and respiratory systems to supply oxygen-rich blood to skeletal muscles during sustained physical activity.
summary: This chunk defines cardiorespiratory fitness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2729
content: The cardiorespiratory system is a system of the body composed of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems.
