summary: This chunk defines the cardiorespiratory system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2730
content: Cardiorespiratory training is any physical activity that involves and places stress on the cardiorespiratory system.
summary: This chunk defines cardiorespiratory training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2731
content: The cardiovascular control center (CVC) directs impulses that will either increase or decrease cardiac output and peripheral resistance based on feedback from all structures involved.
summary: This chunk describes the function of the cardiovascular control center.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2732
content: The cardiovascular system is a system of the body composed of the heart, blood, and blood vessels.
summary: This chunk defines the cardiovascular system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2733
content: The cell body is the portion of the neuron that contains the nucleus, lysosomes, mitochondria, and a Golgi complex.
summary: This chunk defines the cell body of a neuron.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2734
content: An axon is a cylindric projection from the cell body that transmits nervous impulses to other neurons or effector sites.
summary: This chunk defines the structure and function of an axon.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2735
content: Autogenic inhibition is the process by which neural impulses that sense tension are greater than the impulses that cause muscles to contract, providing an inhibitory effect to the muscle spindles.
summary: This chunk describes the mechanism of autogenic inhibition.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2736
content: Augmented feedback is information provided by some external source such as a fitness professional, videotape, or a heart rate monitor.
summary: This chunk defines augmented feedback.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2737
content: The autonomous stage is Fitt’s third stage of motor learning in which the learner has refined the skill to a level of automation.
summary: This chunk describes the autonomous stage of motor learning.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2738
content: Bracing occurs when you have contracted both the abdominal, lower back, and buttock muscles at the same time.
summary: This chunk defines bracing.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2739
content: Progressive overload is the gradual increase of stress placed on the body during training.
summary: This chunk defines progressive overload.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2740
content: The cardiorespiratory system is a system of the body composed of the cardiovascular and respiratory systems. Cardiorespiratory training is any physical activity that involves and places stress on the cardiorespiratory system. The cardiovascular system is a system of the body composed of the heart, blood, and blood vessels. The cardiovascular control center (CVC) directs impulses that will either increase or decrease cardiac output and peripheral resistance based on feedback from all structures involved. The central controller controls heart rate, left ventricular contractility, and arterial blood pressure by manipulating the sympathetic and parasympathetic nervous systems.
summary: This chunk defines the cardiorespiratory system, its components, and how it's influenced during training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2741
content: The central nervous system is the portion of the nervous system that consists of the brain and spinal cord. The cerebellum is a portion of the lower brain that compares sensory information from the body and the external environment with motor information from the cerebral cortex to ensure smooth coordinated movement. The cerebral cortex is a portion of the central nervous system that consists of the frontal lobe, parietal lobe, occipital lobe, and temporal lobe.
summary: This chunk describes the structure and function of the central nervous system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2742
content: The cell body is the portion of the neuron that contains the nucleus, lysosomes, mitochondria, and a Golgi complex. Dendrites are a portion of the neuron responsible for gathering information from other structures.
summary: This chunk defines the basic components of a neuron.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2743
content: Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease is the condition of altered airflow through the lungs, generally caused by airway obstruction as a result of mucus production.
summary: This chunk defines Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2744
content: Circuit Training System consists of a series of exercises that an individual performs one after another with minimal rest.
summary: This chunk defines Circuit Training System.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2745
content: Co-contraction is when muscles contract together in a force-couple.
summary: This chunk defines Co-contraction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2746
content: The Cognitive Stage is Fitts's first stage of motor learning that describes how the learner spends much of the time thinking about what they are about to perform.
summary: This chunk defines the Cognitive Stage of motor learning.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2747
content: Compound-Sets involve the performance of two exercises for antagonistic muscles. For example, a set of bench presses followed by cable rows (chest/back).
summary: This chunk defines Compound-Sets.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2748
content: Concentric Muscle Action is when a muscle is exerting force greater than the resistive force, resulting in shortening of the muscle.
summary: This chunk defines Concentric Muscle Action.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2749
content: Conduction Passageway consists of all the structures that air travels through before entering the respiratory passageway.
summary: This chunk defines the Conduction Passageway.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2750
content: Condyles are projections protruding from the bone to which muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach; also known as a process, epicondyle, tubercle, and trochanter.
summary: This chunk defines Condyles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2751
content: A Condyloid Joint is a joint where the condyle of one bone fits into the elliptical cavity of another bone to form the joint. An example would include the knee joint.
summary: This chunk defines a Condyloid Joint.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2752
content: Contralateral means positioned on the opposite side of the body.
summary: This chunk defines Contralateral.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2753
content: Controlled Instability is a training environment that is as unstable as can safely be controlled by an individual.
summary: This chunk defines Controlled Instability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2754
content: Core refers to 1. the center of the body and the beginning point for movement. 2. the structures that make up the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex (LPHC), including the lumbar spine, the pelvic girdle, abdomen, and the hip joint.
summary: This chunk defines the Core.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2755
content: Core Strength is the ability of the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex musculature to control an individual’s constantly changing center of gravity.
summary: This chunk defines Core Strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2756
content: The Coronal Plane is an imaginary plane that bisects the body to create front and back halves; also known as the frontal plane.
summary: This chunk defines the Coronal Plane.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2757
content: Corrective Flexibility is designed to improve muscle imbalances and altered arthrokinematics.
summary: This chunk defines Corrective Flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2758
content: Creatine Phosphate is a high-energy phosphate molecule that is stored in cells and can be used to resynthesize ATP immediately.
summary: This chunk defines Creatine Phosphate.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2759
content: The Cumulative Injury Cycle is a cycle whereby an injury will induce inflammation, muscle spasm, adhesions, altered neuromuscular control, and muscle imbalances.
summary: This chunk defines the Cumulative Injury Cycle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2760
content: Davis’s Law states that soft tissue models along the line of stress.
summary: This chunk defines Davis’s Law.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2761
content: Decelerate is when the muscle is exerting less force than is being placed on it, the muscle lengthens; also known as an eccentric muscle action or force reduction.
summary: This chunk defines Decelerate.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2762
content: Deconditioned is a state of lost physical fitness, which may include muscle imbalances, decreased flexibility, and a lack of core and joint stability.
summary: This chunk defines Deconditioned.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2763
content: Chemoreceptors are sensory receptors that respond to chemical interaction (smell and taste).
summary: This chunk defines Chemoreceptors.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2764
content: Delayed-Onset Muscle Soreness is pain or discomfort often felt 24 to 72 hours after intense exercise or unaccustomed physical activity.
summary: This chunk defines Delayed-Onset Muscle Soreness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2765
content: Diabetes Mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder caused by insulin deficiency, which impairs carbohydrate usage and enhances usage of fats and proteins.
summary: This chunk defines Diabetes Mellitus.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2766
content: The diaphysis is the shaft portion of a long bone.
summary: This chunk defines the Diaphysis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2767
content: A dietary supplement is a substance that completes or makes an addition to daily dietary intake.
summary: This chunk defines a Dietary Supplement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2768
content: Diffusion is the process of getting oxygen from the environment to the tissues of the body.
summary: This chunk defines Diffusion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2769
content: Collagen is a protein that is found in connective tissue that provides tensile strength. Collagen, unlike elastin, is not very elastic.
summary: This chunk defines Collagen.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2770
content: Depressions are flattened or indented portions of bone, which can be muscle attachment sites.
summary: This chunk defines Depressions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2771
content: Delayed-Onset Muscle Soreness is pain or discomfort often felt 24 to 72 hours after intense exercise or unaccustomed physical activity.  Excess Postexercise Oxygen Consumption (EPOC) is the state in which the body’s metabolism is elevated after exercise.
summary: This chunk describes Delayed-Onset Muscle Soreness and Excess Postexercise Oxygen Consumption (EPOC).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2772
content: Diabetes Mellitus is a chronic metabolic disorder caused by insulin deficiency, which impairs carbohydrate usage and enhances usage of fats and proteins.
summary: This chunk defines Diabetes Mellitus and its metabolic effects.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2773
content: The diaphysis is the shaft portion of a long bone. The epiphyseal plates are the region of long bone connecting the diaphysis to the epiphysis. It is a layer of subdividing cartilaginous cells in which growth in length of the diaphysis occurs. The epiphysis is the end of long bones, which is mainly composed of cancellous bone, and house much of the red marrow involved in red blood cell production. They are also one of the primary sites for bone growth.
summary: This chunk describes the anatomy of long bones, including the diaphysis, epiphyseal plates, and epiphysis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2774
content: A dietary supplement is a substance that completes or makes an addition to daily dietary intake.
summary: This chunk defines a dietary supplement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2775
content: Diffusion is the process of getting oxygen from the environment to the tissues of the body.
summary: This chunk describes the process of diffusion in relation to oxygen transport.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2776
content: Distal refers to a position farthest from the center of the body, or point of reference. Dorsal refers to a position on the back or toward the back of the body.
summary: This chunk defines the anatomical terms 'distal' and 'dorsal'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2777
content: Dorsiflexion, when applied to the ankle, is the ability to bend at the ankle, moving the front of the foot upward.
summary: This chunk defines dorsiflexion as it relates to the ankle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2778
content: Dynamic balance is the ability to move and change directions under various conditions without falling.
summary: This chunk defines dynamic balance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2779
content: The drawing-in maneuver is the activation of the transverse abdominis, multifidus, pelvic floor muscles, and diaphragm to provide core stabilization.
summary: This chunk describes the drawing-in maneuver and its role in core stabilization.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2780
content: Dynamic functional flexibility is multiplanar soft tissue extensibility with optimal neuromuscular efficiency throughout the full range of motion.
summary: This chunk defines dynamic functional flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2781
content: Dynamic joint stabilization is the ability of the stabilizing muscles of a joint to produce optimum stabilization during functional, multiplanar movements.
summary: This chunk defines dynamic joint stabilization.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2782
content: The Dynamic Pattern Perspective (DPP) is the theory that suggests that movement patterns are produced as a result of the combined interactions among many systems (nervous, muscular, skeletal, mechanical, environmental, past experiences, and so forth).
summary: This chunk describes the Dynamic Pattern Perspective (DPP).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2783
content: Dynamic range of motion is the combination of flexibility and the nervous system’s ability to control this range of motion efficiently.
summary: This chunk defines dynamic range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2784
content: Dynamic stabilization occurs when a muscle is exerting force equal to the force being placed on it. It is also known as an isometric contraction.
summary: This chunk defines dynamic stabilization and its relationship to isometric contractions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2785
content: Dynamic stretching uses the force production of a muscle and the body’s momentum to take a joint through the full available range of motion.
summary: This chunk describes dynamic stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2786
content: An eccentric muscle action occurs when a muscle develops tension while lengthening.
summary: This chunk defines an eccentric muscle action.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2787
content: Effectors are any structure innervated by the nervous system including organs, glands, muscle tissue, connective tissue, blood vessels, bone marrow, and so forth. Efferent neurons are neurons that transmit nerve impulses from the brain or spinal cord to the effector sites such as muscles or glands; also known as motor neurons.
summary: This chunk defines effectors and efferent neurons.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2788
content: Elastin is a protein found in connective tissue that has elastic properties.
summary: This chunk describes elastin and its properties.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2789
content: Empathy is the action of awareness, understanding, and sensitivity of the thoughts, emotions, and experience of another without personally having gone through the same.
summary: This chunk defines empathy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2790
content: The endocrine system is the system of glands in the human body that is responsible for producing hormones.
summary: This chunk describes the endocrine system.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2791
content: The endomysium is the deepest layer of connective tissue that surrounds individual muscle fibers.
summary: This chunk defines the endomysium.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2792
content: Endurance strength is the ability to produce and maintain force for prolonged periods.
summary: This chunk defines endurance strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2793
content: Energy is the capacity to do work. Energy-utilizing refers to when energy is gathered from an energy-yielding source by some storage unit (ATP) and then transferred to a site that can use this energy.
summary: This chunk defines energy and energy-utilizing processes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2794
content: Enjoyment is the amount of pleasure derived from performing a physical activity.
summary: This chunk defines enjoyment in the context of physical activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2795
content: An epicondyle is a projection protruding from the bone to which muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach; also known as a condyle, process, tubercle, and trochanter.
summary: This chunk defines an epicondyle and its function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2796
content: The epimysium is a layer of connective tissue that is underneath the fascia and surrounds the muscle.
summary: This chunk defines the epimysium.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2797
content: Equilibrium is a condition of balance between opposed forces, influences, or actions.
summary: This chunk defines equilibrium.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2798
content: Erythrocytes are red blood cells.
summary: This chunk defines erythrocytes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2799
content: The Estimated Average Requirement (EAR) is the average daily nutrient intake level that is estimated to meet the requirement of half the healthy individuals who are in a particular life stage and gender group.
summary: This chunk defines the Estimated Average Requirement (EAR).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2800
content: Eversion is a movement in which the inferior calcaneus moves laterally.
summary: This chunk defines eversion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2801
content: Exercise Imagery is the process created to produce internalized experiences to support or enhance exercise participation.
summary: This chunk describes exercise imagery.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2802
content: Exercise Metabolism is the examination of bioenergetics as it relates to the unique physiologic changes and demands placed on the body during exercise.
summary: This chunk defines exercise metabolism.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2803
content: Exercise Order refers to the order in which the exercises are performed during a workout.
summary: This chunk defines exercise order.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2804
content: Excess Postexercise Oxygen Consumption (EPOC) is the state in which the body’s metabolism is elevated after exercise.  Exercise Metabolism is the examination of bioenergetics as it relates to the unique physiologic changes and demands placed on the body during exercise.
summary: EPOC describes the elevated metabolism after exercise, while Exercise Metabolism examines the bioenergetics involved during exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2805
content: Exercise Imagery is the process created to produce internalized experiences to support or enhance exercise participation.
summary: Exercise Imagery uses internalized experiences to support or enhance exercise participation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2806
content: The General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) is a syndrome in which the kinetic chain responds and adapts to imposed demands. General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) is a term used to describe how the body responds and adapts to stress. Exhaustion is the third stage of the General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS), when prolonged stress or stress that is intolerable produces exhaustion or distress to the system.
summary: The General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) describes the body's response and adaptation to stress, with exhaustion being a potential outcome of prolonged stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2807
content: The Expert Stage is the third stage of the dynamic pattern perspective model in which the learner now focuses on recognizing and coordinating their joint motions in the most efficient manner.
summary: The Expert Stage in motor learning focuses on efficient joint coordination.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2808
content: Expiration is the process of actively or passively relaxing the inspiratory muscles to move air out of the body.
summary: Expiration involves relaxing inspiratory muscles to expel air from the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2809
content: Explosive Strength is the ability to develop a sharp rise in force production once a movement pattern has been initiated.
summary: Explosive Strength refers to the ability to rapidly generate force.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2810
content: Extensibility is the capability to be elongated or stretched. Extension is a straightening movement in which the relative angle between two adjacent segments increases.
summary: Extensibility is the ability to stretch, while extension is a straightening movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2811
content: External Feedback is information provided by some external source, such as a health and fitness professional, videotape, mirror, or heart rate monitor, to supplement the internal environment.
summary: External Feedback comes from external sources to supplement internal sensory information.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2812
content: External Rotation is the rotation of a joint away from the middle of the body.
summary: External Rotation involves rotating a joint away from the body's midline.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2813
content: A Fan-Shaped Muscle is a muscular fiber arrangement that has muscle fibers span out from a narrow attachment at one end to a broad attachment at the other end. An example would be the pectoralis major. A fusiform muscular fiber arrangement has a full muscle belly that tapers off at both ends. An example would include the biceps brachii.
summary: Fan-shaped and fusiform are two muscle fiber arrangements with distinct shapes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2814
content: Fascia is the outermost layer of connective tissue that surrounds the muscle. A Fascicle is a grouping of muscle fibers that house myofibrils.
summary: Fascia is the outer connective tissue surrounding muscles, while a fascicle is a bundle of muscle fibers within a muscle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2815
content: Fast Twitch Fibers are muscle fibers that can also be characterized by the term type IIA and IIB. These fibers contain fewer capillaries, mitochondria, and myoglobin. These fibers fatigue faster than type I fibers.
summary: Fast Twitch Fibers (type IIA and IIB) are characterized by their rapid fatigue compared to slow-twitch fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2816
content: Fat is one of the three main classes of foods and a source of energy in the body. Fats help the body use some vitamins and keep the skin healthy. They also serve as energy stores for the body. In food, there are two types of fats, saturated and unsaturated.
summary: Fat is a macronutrient that provides energy, supports vitamin absorption, and serves as an energy store.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2817
content: Feedback is 1. The utilization of sensory information and sensorimotor integration to aid the kinetic chain in the development of permanent neural representations of motor patterns. 2. The use of sensory information and sensorimotor integration to help the human movement system in motor learning.
summary: Feedback utilizes sensory information and sensorimotor integration to aid motor learning and the development of motor patterns.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2818
content: Flat Bones are a classification of bone that is involved in protection and provides attachment sites for muscles. Examples include the sternum and scapulae.
summary: Flat bones provide protection and muscle attachment sites.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2819
content: Flexibility is the normal extensibility of all soft tissues that allows the full range of motion of a joint. Flexibility Training is physical training of the body that integrates various stretches in all three planes of motion to produce the maximum extensibility of tissues.
summary: Flexibility is the range of motion allowed by soft tissues, while Flexibility Training aims to improve this range.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2820
content: Flexion is a bending movement in which the relative angle between two adjacent segments decreases.
summary: Flexion is a bending movement that decreases the angle between body segments.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2821
content: Force is an influence applied by one object to another, which results in an acceleration or deceleration of the second object. A Force-Couple is muscle groups moving together to produce movement around a joint.
summary: Force is an influence that causes acceleration or deceleration, while a Force-Couple involves multiple muscles working together to produce movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2822
content: The Force-Velocity Curve is the ability of muscles to produce force with increasing velocity.
summary: The Force-Velocity Curve describes the relationship between muscle force and velocity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2823
content: Formed Elements refers to the cellular component of blood that includes erythrocytes, leukocytes, and thrombocytes.
summary: Formed Elements are the cellular components of blood, including red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2824
content: A Fossa is a depression or indented portion of bone, which could be a muscle attachment site; also known as a depression.
summary: A Fossa is a depression or indentation in bone, often serving as a muscle attachment site.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2825
content: Frequency is the number of training sessions in a given timeframe.
summary: Frequency refers to the number of training sessions within a specific period.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2826
content: The frontal lobe is a portion of the cerebral cortex that contains structures necessary for the planning and control of voluntary movement.
summary: The frontal lobe is involved in planning and controlling voluntary movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2827
content: The frontal plane is an imaginary bisector that divides the body into front and back halves.
summary: The frontal plane is an imaginary line dividing the body into front and back sections.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2828
content: Frontside mechanics is proper alignment of the lead leg and pelvis during sprinting, which includes ankle dorsiflexion, knee flexion, hip flexion, and neutral pelvis.
summary: Frontside mechanics refers to proper alignment during sprinting, involving ankle, knee, and hip movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2829
content: Fructose is known as fruit sugar; it is a member of the simple sugars carbohydrate group found in fruits, honey, and syrups, and certain vegetables.
summary: Fructose is a simple sugar found in fruits, honey, syrups, and some vegetables.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2830
content: Functional efficiency is the ability of the nervous and muscular systems to move in the most efficient manner while placing the least amount of stress on the kinetic chain.
summary: Functional efficiency describes movement that is efficient and minimizes stress on the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2831
content: Functional flexibility is integrated, multiplanar, soft tissue extensibility with optimum neuromuscular control through the full range of motion.
summary: Functional flexibility involves integrated, multidirectional soft tissue extensibility with optimal neuromuscular control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2832
content: Functional strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to perform dynamic eccentric, isometric, and concentric contractions efficiently in a multiplanar environment.
summary: Functional strength refers to the ability to perform dynamic contractions in various directions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2833
content: Exercise Order refers to the order in which the exercises are performed during a workout. Exercise Selection is the process of choosing appropriate exercises for a client’s program.
summary: Exercise Order and Exercise Selection are key considerations in program design.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2834
content: Excitation-Contraction Coupling is the process of neural stimulation creating a muscle contraction.
summary: Excitation-Contraction Coupling describes the process of neural stimulation leading to muscle contraction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2835
content: Functional flexibility is integrated, multiplanar, soft tissue extensibility with optimum neuromuscular control through the full range of motion. Functional strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to perform dynamic eccentric, isometric, and concentric contractions efficiently in a multiplanar environment.
summary: Defines functional flexibility and functional strength, emphasizing multiplanar movement and neuromuscular control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2836
content: General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) is a syndrome in which the kinetic chain responds and adapts to imposed demands. General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) is a term used to describe how the body responds and adapts to stress.
summary: Explains General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS) as the body's response and adaptation to imposed demands and stress.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2837
content: A fusiform muscular fiber arrangement has a full muscle belly that tapers off at both ends. An example would include the biceps brachii.
summary: Describes the fusiform muscle fiber arrangement and provides an example (biceps brachii).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2838
content: A general warm-up consists of movements that do not necessarily have any movement specificity to the actual activity to be performed. A general warm-up is low-intensity exercise consisting of movements that do not necessarily relate to the more intense exercise that is to follow.
summary: Defines a general warm-up as low-intensity, non-specific movements preceding more intense exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2839
content: A gliding joint is a nonaxial joint that moves back and forth or side to side. Examples would include the carpals of the hand and the facet joints.
summary: Defines a gliding joint and provides examples (carpals, facet joints).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2840
content: Gluconeogenesis is the formation of glucose from noncarbohydrate sources, such as amino acids. Glucose is a simple sugar manufactured by the body from carbohydrates, fat, and to a lesser extent protein, which serves as the body’s main source of fuel.
summary: Explains gluconeogenesis (glucose formation from non-carbohydrate sources) and the role of glucose as the body's primary fuel source.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2841
content: The glycemic index is a ranking of carbohydrate-containing foods based on the food’s effect on blood sugar compared with a standard reference food’s effect. Glycogen is the complex carbohydrate molecule used to store carbohydrates in the liver and muscle cells. When carbohydrate energy is needed, glycogen is converted into glucose for use by the muscle cells.
summary: Defines the glycemic index and explains glycogen's role in carbohydrate storage and glucose production.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2842
content: Golgi tendon organs are receptors sensitive to change in tension of the muscle and the rate of that change.
summary: Describes the function of Golgi tendon organs in sensing muscle tension and its rate of change.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2843
content: Goniometric assessment is a technique measuring angular measurement and joint range of motion.
summary: Defines goniometric assessment as a technique for measuring joint range of motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2844
content: Gravity is the attraction between earth and the objects on earth. Ground reaction force (GRF) is the equal and opposite force that is exerted back onto the body with every step that is taken.
summary: Defines gravity and ground reaction force (GRF) in relation to movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2845
content: The heart is a hollow muscular organ that pumps a circulation of blood through the body by means of rhythmic contraction. Heart rate (HR) is the rate at which the heart pumps.
summary: Describes the heart's function and defines heart rate (HR).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2846
content: Hemoglobin is oxygen-carrying component of red blood cells and also gives blood its red color.
summary: Explains the role of hemoglobin in oxygen transport and blood color.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2847
content: Hierarchical theories are theories that propose all planning and implementation of movement result from one or more higher brain centers.
summary: Describes hierarchical theories of movement planning and execution.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2848
content: A hinge joint is a uniaxial joint that allows movement in one plane of motion. Examples would include the elbow and ankle.
summary: Defines a hinge joint and provides examples (elbow, ankle).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2849
content: Hobbies are activities that a client may partake in regularly, but which may not necessarily be athletic in nature.
summary: Defines hobbies as regular activities that may or may not be athletic.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2850
content: Homeostasis is the ability or tendency of an organism or a cell to maintain internal equilibrium by adjusting its physiologic processes.
summary: Defines homeostasis as the body's ability to maintain internal balance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2851
content: Horizontal abduction is movement of the arm or thigh in the transverse plane from an anterior position to a lateral position. Horizontal adduction is movement of the arm or thigh in the transverse plane from a lateral position to an anterior position.
summary: Defines horizontal abduction and adduction movements in the transverse plane.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2852
content: Horizontal loading is performing all sets of an exercise or body part before moving on to the next exercise or body part.
summary: Defines horizontal loading as completing all sets for one exercise or body part before moving to the next.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2853
content: Human movement science is the study of functional anatomy, functional biomechanics, motion learning, and motor control.
summary: Defines human movement science as the study of functional anatomy, biomechanics, motion learning, and motor control.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2854
content: The human movement system is the combination and interrelation of the nervous, muscular, and skeletal systems.
summary: Defines the human movement system as the interconnectedness of the nervous, muscular, and skeletal systems.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2855
content: Hypercholesterolemia is chronic high levels of cholesterol in the bloodstream. Hyperglycemia is abnormally high blood sugar. Hyperlipidemia is elevated levels of blood fats (e.g., triglycerides, cholesterol).
summary: Defines hypercholesterolemia, hyperglycemia, and hyperlipidemia as conditions involving elevated blood cholesterol, sugar, and fats, respectively.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2856
content: Hyperextension is extension of a joint beyond the normal limit or range of motion. Hypertension is consistently elevated arterial blood pressure, which, if sustained at a high enough level, is likely to induce cardiovascular or end-organ damage.
summary: Defines hyperextension and hypertension as conditions involving joint extension beyond the normal range and consistently elevated blood pressure, respectively.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2857
content: Hypertrophy is enlargement of skeletal muscle fibers in response to overcoming force from high volumes of tension.
summary: Defines hypertrophy as muscle fiber enlargement due to resistance training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2858
content: The H-zone is the area of the sarcomere where only myosin filaments are present. The I-band is the area of the sarcomere where only actin filaments are present.
summary: Describes the H-zone and I-band within the sarcomere, specifying the filament types present in each.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2859
content: Inferior is positioned below a point of reference. Insertion is the part of a muscle by which it is attached to the part to be moved—compare with origin.
summary: Defines inferior positioning and the concept of muscle insertion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2860
content: Inspiration is the process of actively contracting the inspiratory muscles to move air into the body.
summary: Defines inspiration as the active process of inhaling.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2861
content: The H-zone is the area of the sarcomere where only myosin filaments are present. The I-band is the area of the sarcomere where only actin filaments are present.
summary: Describes the H-zone and I-band within the sarcomere.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2862
content: Inferior is positioned below a point of reference. Insertion is the part of a muscle by which it is attached to the part to be moved—compare with origin.
summary: Defines anatomical terms: inferior and insertion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2863
content: Inspiration is the process of actively contracting the inspiratory muscles to move air into the body. Insulin is a protein hormone released by the pancreas that helps glucose move out of the blood and into the cells in the body, where the glucose can be used as energy and nourishment.
summary: Explains the processes of inspiration and insulin function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2864
content: Integrated Cardiorespiratory Training programs systematically progress clients through various stages to achieve optimal levels of physiologic, physical, and performance adaptations by placing stress on the cardio-respiratory system. A systematic problem-solving method that provides the fitness professional with a basis for making educated decisions about exercise and acute variable selection is called the Integrated Fitness Profile.
summary: Describes Integrated Cardiorespiratory Training and the Integrated Fitness Profile.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2865
content: Integrated Flexibility Training is a multifaceted approach integrating various flexibility techniques to achieve optimum soft tissue extensibility in all planes of motion. To move with efficiency, forces must be dampened (eccentrically), stabilized (isometrically), and then accelerated (concentrically) according to the Integrated Performance Paradigm.
summary: Explains Integrated Flexibility Training and the Integrated Performance Paradigm.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2866
content: Integrated Training applies all forms of training such as integrated flexibility training, integrated cardiorespiratory training, neuromuscular stabilization (balance), core stabilization, and reactive neuromuscular training (power), and integrated strength training.
summary: Defines Integrated Training and its various components.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2867
content: The ability of the nervous system to analyze and interpret sensory information to allow for proper decision making, which produces the appropriate response, is called Integrative (Function of Nervous System).
summary: Defines the Integrative Function of the Nervous System.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2868
content: The level of demand that a given activity places on the body is called Intensity. Intermittent Claudication is the manifestation of the symptoms caused by peripheral arterial disease.
summary: Defines Intensity and describes Intermittent Claudication.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2869
content: The process whereby sensory information is used by the body to reactively monitor movement and the environment is called Internal Feedback. Internal Rotation is rotation of a joint toward the middle of the body.
summary: Explains Internal Feedback and defines Internal Rotation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2870
content: Interneurons transmit nerve impulses from one neuron to another.
summary: Describes the function of interneurons.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2871
content: The ability of the neuromuscular system to allow all muscles to work together with proper activation and timing between them is called Intermuscular Coordination. The ability of the neuromuscular system to allow optimal levels of motor unit recruitment and synchronization within a muscle is called Intramuscular Coordination.
summary: Defines Intermuscular and Intramuscular Coordination.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2872
content: Pressure within the thoracic cavity is called Intrapulmonary Pressure.
summary: Defines Intrapulmonary Pressure.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2873
content: Inversion is a movement in which the inferior calcaneus moves medially. Ipsilateral means positioned on the same side of the body.
summary: Defines Inversion and Ipsilateral.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2874
content: A classification of bone that has its own unique shape and function, which does not fit the characteristics of the other categories is called Irregular Bones. Examples include the vertebrae and pelvic bones.
summary: Defines Irregular Bones and provides examples.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2875
content: Isokinetic Muscle Action occurs when a muscle shortens at a constant speed over the full range of motion. Isometric Muscle Action occurs when a muscle is exerting force equal to the force being placed on it leading to no visible change in the muscle length.
summary: Describes Isokinetic and Isometric Muscle Actions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2876
content: Junctions of bones, muscles, and connective tissue at which movement occurs; also known as an articulation, are called Joints.
summary: Defines Joints.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2877
content: Movement in a plane occurs about an axis running perpendicular to the plane.
summary: Describes movement in relation to planes and axes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2878
content: Receptors surrounding a joint that respond to pressure, acceleration, and deceleration of the joint are called Joint Receptors. Resistance to unwanted movement is called Joint Stiffness.
summary: Defines Joint Receptors and Joint Stiffness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2879
content: A unit of expression of energy equal to 1000 calories is called a Kilocalorie. The amount of heat energy required to raise the temperature of a kilogram or liter of water 1°Celsius.
summary: Defines a Kilocalorie.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2880
content: Force is called Kinetic.
summary: Defines Kinetic Force.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2881
content: The combination and interrelation of the nervous, muscular, and skeletal systems is called the Kinetic Chain.
summary: Defines the Kinetic Chain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2882
content: A method of feedback that provides information about the quality of the movement pattern performed is called Knowledge of Performance (KP). A method of feedback after the completion of a movement to inform the client about the outcome of their performance is called Knowledge of Results (KR).
summary: Defines Knowledge of Performance (KP) and Knowledge of Results (KR).

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2883
content: Exaggerated outward curvature of the thoracic region of the spinal column resulting in a rounded upper back is called Kyphosis.
summary: Defines Kyphosis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2884
content: An acid produced by glucose-burning cells when these cells have an insufficient supply of oxygen is called Lactic Acid.
summary: Defines Lactic Acid.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2885
content: Positioned toward the outside of the body is called Lateral. The bending of the spine (cervical, thoracic, or lumbar) from side to side is called Lateral Flexion.
summary: Defines Lateral and Lateral Flexion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2886
content: Acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the size of the force causing it, in the same direction as the force, and inversely proportional to the size of the object, according to the Law of Acceleration. Every force produced by one object onto another produces an opposite force of equal magnitude, according to the Law of Action-Reaction.
summary: States the Law of Acceleration and the Law of Action-Reaction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2887
content: An acid produced by glucose-burning cells when these cells have an insufficient supply of oxygen is called Lactic Acid.  Weight reduction can only take place when there is more energy burned than consumed, according to the Law of Thermodynamics.
summary: This chunk explains the concept of lactic acid production during exercise and the fundamental principle of weight loss through energy balance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2888
content: Positioned toward the outside of the body is called Lateral. The bending of the spine (cervical, thoracic, or lumbar) from side to side is called Lateral Flexion.
summary: This chunk defines the anatomical terms 'lateral' and 'lateral flexion'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2889
content: Acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the size of the force causing it, in the same direction as the force, and inversely proportional to the size of the object, according to the Law of Acceleration. Every force produced by one object onto another produces an opposite force of equal magnitude, according to the Law of Action-Reaction.
summary: This chunk describes Newton's Laws of Motion: Law of Acceleration and Law of Action-Reaction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2890
content: Two bodies have an attraction to each other that is directly proportional to their masses and inversely proportional to the square of their distance from each other, according to the Law of Gravitation.
summary: This chunk explains Newton's Law of Gravitation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2891
content: The resting length of a muscle and the tension the muscle can produce at this resting length is called the Length-Tension Relationship.
summary: This chunk defines the Length-Tension Relationship in muscle physiology.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2892
content: White blood cells are called Leukocytes. Primary connective tissue that connects bones together and provides stability, input to the nervous system, guidance, and the limitation of improper joint movement is called Ligament.
summary: This chunk defines the terms 'leukocytes' and 'ligament'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2893
content: The maximum force a muscle can produce in a single contraction is called Limit Strength.
summary: This chunk defines 'limit strength'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2894
content: A group of compounds that includes triglycerides (fats and oils), phospholipids, and sterols is called Lipids.
summary: This chunk defines 'lipids'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2895
content: A characteristic of bone that has a long cylindric body with irregular or widened bony ends is called Long Bones. Examples include the clavicle and humerus.
summary: This chunk defines 'long bones'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2896
content: A muscle fiber arrangement in which its fibers run parallel to the line of pull is called Longitudinal Muscle Fiber. An example would include the sartorius.
summary: This chunk defines 'longitudinal muscle fiber'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2897
content: The lower brain includes the brainstem, basal ganglia, and cerebellum.
summary: This chunk lists the components of the lower brain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2898
content: An individual with lower-extremity postural distortion has increased lumbar lordosis and an anterior pelvic tilt.
summary: This chunk describes a postural distortion pattern.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2899
content: The lumbar spine, commonly referred to as the small of the back, is located between the thorax (chest) and the pelvis.
summary: This chunk defines the location of the lumbar spine.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2900
content: The lumbo-pelvic-hip complex involves the anatomic structures of the lumbar and thoracic spines, the pelvic girdle, and the hip joint.
summary: This chunk defines the lumbo-pelvic-hip complex.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2901
content: Lumbo-pelvic-hip postural distortion is characterized by altered joint mechanics that lead to increased lumbar extension and decreased hip extension.
summary: This chunk describes the characteristics of lumbo-pelvic-hip postural distortion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2902
content: Maximal oxygen consumption (VO2max) is the highest rate of oxygen transport and utilization achieved at maximal physical exertion.
summary: This chunk defines VO2max.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2903
content: Maximal strength is the maximum force an individual’s muscle can produce in a single voluntary effort, regardless of the rate of force production.
summary: This chunk defines maximal strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2904
content: Mechanical specificity refers to the specific muscular exercises using different weights and movements that are performed to increase strength or endurance in certain body parts. Mechanical specificity also refers to the weight and movements placed on the body.
summary: This chunk defines mechanical specificity in training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2905
content: Mechanoreceptors are sensory receptors responsible for sensing distortion in body tissues.
summary: This chunk defines mechanoreceptors.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2906
content: Medial means positioned near the middle of the body. The mediastinum is the space in the chest between the lungs that contains all the internal organs of the chest except the lungs.
summary: This chunk defines 'medial' and describes the mediastinum.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2907
content: The medullary cavity is the central cavity of bone shafts where marrow is stored.
summary: This chunk defines the medullary cavity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2908
content: Metabolic specificity refers to the specific muscular exercises using different levels of energy that are performed to increase endurance, strength, or power. Metabolic specificity also refers to the energy demand placed on the body.
summary: This chunk defines metabolic specificity in training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2909
content: Metabolism encompasses all the chemical reactions that occur in the body to maintain itself. Metabolism is the process in which nutrients are acquired, transported, used, and disposed of by the body.
summary: This chunk defines metabolism.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2910
content: Mitochondria are the principal energy source of the cell. Mitochondria convert nutrients into energy as well as performing many other specialized tasks.
summary: This chunk describes the function of mitochondria.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2911
content: The M-line is the portion of the sarcomere where the myosin filaments connect with very thin filaments called titin and create an anchor for the structures of the sarcomere.
summary: This chunk defines the M-line in muscle structure.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2912
content: Mode refers to the type of exercise performed.
summary: This chunk defines 'mode' in exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2913
content: Momentum is the product of the size of the object (mass) and its velocity (speed with which it is moving).
summary: This chunk defines 'momentum'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2914
content: A monthly plan is a generalized training plan that spans one month and shows which phases will be required each day of each week.
summary: This chunk defines a 'monthly plan'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2915
content: Motor behavior is the manner in which the nervous, skeletal, and muscular systems interact to produce an observable mechanical response to the incoming sensory information from the internal and external environments. Motor behavior is also a motor response to internal and external environmental stimuli.
summary: This chunk defines 'motor behavior'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2916
content: Motor control involves the structures and mechanisms that the nervous system uses to gather sensory information and integrate it with previous experiences to produce a motor response.
summary: This chunk defines 'motor control'.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2917
content: Motor behavior is the manner in which the nervous, skeletal, and muscular systems interact to produce an observable mechanical response to the incoming sensory information from the internal and external environments. Motor behavior is also a motor response to internal and external environmental stimuli.
summary: Motor behavior describes the interaction of the nervous, skeletal, and muscular systems to produce movement in response to internal and external stimuli.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2918
content: Motor control involves the structures and mechanisms that the nervous system uses to gather sensory information and integrate it with previous experiences to produce a motor response. Motor control is also how the central nervous system integrates internal and external sensory information with previous experiences to produce a motor response.
summary: Motor control encompasses the nervous system's processes for integrating sensory information and past experiences to generate movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2919
content: Motor learning is the integration of motor control processes with practice and experience that lead to relatively permanent changes in the capacity to produce skilled movements.
summary: Motor learning involves the acquisition and refinement of motor skills through practice and experience.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2920
content: Motor (function of nervous system) is the neuromuscular response to the sensory information. Motor (efferent) neurons transmit nerve impulses from the brain and spinal cord to effector sites. A motor unit consists of a motor neuron and all of the muscle fibers it innervates.
summary: Motor function involves the transmission of nerve impulses from the nervous system to muscles, activating muscle fibers through motor units.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2921
content: Muscle fiber arrangement refers to the manner in which the fibers are situated in relation to the tendon. Multipenniform muscles have multiple tendons with obliquely running muscle fibers.
summary: Muscle fiber arrangement describes the organization of muscle fibers relative to the tendon, with multipenniform muscles having multiple tendons and obliquely oriented fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2922
content: Muscle fiber recruitment refers to the recruitment pattern of muscle fibers or motor units in response to creating force for a specific movement.
summary: Muscle fiber recruitment describes the process of activating specific muscle fibers or motor units to generate force for a particular movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2923
content: The muscle action spectrum includes concentric, eccentric, and isometric actions.
summary: Muscle actions encompass concentric (shortening), eccentric (lengthening), and isometric (constant length) contractions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2924
content: Muscle hypertrophy is characterized by the increase in the cross-sectional area of individual muscle fibers and is believed to result from an increase in the myofibril proteins. Muscle hypertrophy is also the enlargement of skeletal muscle fibers in response to overcoming force from high volumes of tension.
summary: Muscle hypertrophy involves the enlargement of muscle fibers due to increased myofibril proteins, resulting from resistance training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2925
content: Muscle imbalance is an alteration of muscle length surrounding a joint.
summary: Muscle imbalance refers to discrepancies in muscle length around a joint, potentially leading to functional limitations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2926
content: Muscle spindles are receptors sensitive to change in the length of the muscle and the rate of that change.
summary: Muscle spindles are sensory receptors that detect changes in muscle length and the speed of those changes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2927
content: Muscle synergies are groups of muscles that are recruited by the central nervous system to provide movement.
summary: Muscle synergies are coordinated groups of muscles activated by the nervous system to produce movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2928
content: Muscular endurance is a muscle’s ability to contract for an extended period. Muscular endurance is also the ability to produce and maintain force production over prolonged periods of time.
summary: Muscular endurance refers to a muscle's capacity to sustain contractions for extended durations or maintain force production over time.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2929
content: The muscular system is a series of muscles that moves the skeleton.
summary: The muscular system is responsible for skeletal movement through the contraction and relaxation of muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2930
content: Myofibrils are a portion of muscle that contains myofilaments. Myofilaments are the contractile components of muscle, actin, and myosin. Myosin is one of the two major myofilaments known as the thick filament that works with actin to produce muscular contraction.
summary: Myofibrils are muscle components containing myofilaments (actin and myosin), with myosin playing a key role in muscle contraction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2931
content: The nervous system is a conglomeration of billions of cells specifically designed to provide a communication network within the human body.
summary: The nervous system is a vast network of cells responsible for communication and control throughout the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2932
content: Neural Activation is the contraction of a muscle generated by neural stimulation. Neural Adaptation is an adaptation to strength training in which muscles are under the direct command of the nervous system.
summary: Neural activation refers to muscle contraction initiated by nerve impulses, while neural adaptation involves the nervous system's increased control over muscles during strength training.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2933
content: Neuromuscular Efficiency is the ability of the neuromuscular system to enable all muscles to efficiently work together in all planes of motion. Neuromuscular Efficiency is the ability of the neuromuscular system to allow agonists, antagonists, and stabilizers to work synergistically to produce, reduce, and dynamically stabilize the entire kinetic chain in all three planes of motion.
summary: Neuromuscular efficiency describes the coordinated and effective functioning of muscles and the nervous system across all movement planes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2934
content: The Neuromuscular Junction is the point at which the neuron meets the muscle to allow the action potential to continue its impulse.
summary: The neuromuscular junction is the site where nerve impulses are transmitted from a neuron to a muscle fiber.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2935
content: Neuromuscular Specificity refers to the speed of contraction and exercise selection. Neuromuscular Specificity is the specific muscular exercises using different speeds and styles that are performed to increase neuromuscular efficiency.
summary: Neuromuscular specificity emphasizes the importance of tailoring exercises to target specific movement patterns and speeds to enhance neuromuscular efficiency.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2936
content: A Neuron is the functional unit of the nervous system.
summary: Neurons are the fundamental building blocks of the nervous system, responsible for transmitting information.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2937
content: Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers that cross the neuromuscular junction (synapse) to transmit electrical impulses from the nerve to the muscle.
summary: Neurotransmitters are chemical substances that facilitate communication between nerves and muscles across the neuromuscular junction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2938
content: A Neutralizer is a muscle that counteracts the unwanted action of other muscles.
summary: Neutralizer muscles oppose the actions of other muscles to control movement and prevent unwanted actions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2939
content: Nociceptors are sensory receptors that respond to pain.
summary: Nociceptors are sensory receptors that detect and transmit pain signals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2940
content: Momentum is the product of the size of the object (mass) and its velocity (speed with which it is moving).
summary: Momentum is a measure of an object's mass in motion, calculated as the product of its mass and velocity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2941
content: A monthly plan is a generalized training plan that spans one month and shows which phases will be required each day of each week.
summary: A monthly training plan outlines the overall training structure for a month, specifying the phases and daily requirements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2942
content: Motor development is the change in motor skill behavior over time throughout the lifespan.
summary: Motor development encompasses the changes in motor skills that occur throughout a person's life.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2943
content: The multiple-set system consists of performing multiple sets of the same exercise.
summary: The multiple-set system involves performing repetitions of the same exercise in multiple sets.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2944
content: A multisensory condition is a training environment that provides heightened stimulation to proprioceptors and mechanoreceptors.
summary: A multisensory training environment enhances sensory input, particularly proprioception and mechanoreception.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2945
content: A Neuron is the functional unit of the nervous system. Neurotransmitters are chemical messengers that cross the neuromuscular junction (synapse) to transmit electrical impulses from the nerve to the muscle.
summary: Neurons and neurotransmitters are fundamental to the nervous system's communication with muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2946
content: Neuromuscular Specificity refers to the speed of contraction and exercise selection.
summary: Neuromuscular specificity emphasizes the importance of exercise selection and contraction speed for training adaptations.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2947
content: Nociceptors are sensory receptors that respond to pain.
summary: Nociceptors are responsible for the sensation of pain.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2948
content: Nonsynovial Joints are joints that do not have a joint cavity, connective tissue, or cartilage.
summary: Nonsynovial joints lack the typical features of synovial joints.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2949
content: The Novice Stage is the first stage of the dynamic pattern perspective model in which the learner simplifies movements by minimizing the specific timing of joint motions, which tends to result in movement that is rigid and jerky.
summary: The novice stage of learning involves simplifying movements and lacks precise timing.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2950
content: Nutrition is the process by which a living organism assimilates food and uses it for growth and repair of tissues.
summary: Nutrition is essential for growth, repair, and overall bodily function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2951
content: Obesity is the condition of being considerably overweight, and refers to a person with a body mass index of 30 or greater, or who is at least 30 pounds over the recommended weight for their height. Obesity is the condition of subcutaneous fat exceeding the amount of lean body mass.
summary: Obesity is defined by a high body mass index and excess subcutaneous fat.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2952
content: Overweight refers to a person with a body mass index of 25 to 29, or who is between 25 to 30 pounds over the recommended weight for their height.
summary: Overweight is a condition characterized by a body mass index between 25 and 29.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2953
content: The Occipital Lobe is a portion of the cerebral cortex that deals with vision.
summary: The occipital lobe is responsible for processing visual information.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2954
content: Optimal Strength is the ideal level of strength that an individual needs to perform functional activities.
summary: Optimal strength refers to the amount of strength necessary for everyday tasks.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2955
content: The Origin is the more fixed, central, or larger attachment of a muscle—compare with insertion.
summary: The origin of a muscle is its fixed point of attachment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2956
content: Osteoarthritis is arthritis in which cartilage becomes soft, frayed, or thins out, as a result of trauma or other conditions.
summary: Osteoarthritis is a type of arthritis characterized by cartilage damage.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2957
content: Osteoblasts are a type of cell that is responsible for bone formation. Osteoclasts are a type of bone cell that removes bone tissue.
summary: Osteoblasts build bone, while osteoclasts break down bone tissue.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2958
content: Osteopenia is a decrease in the calcification or density of bone as well as reduced bone mass. Osteoporosis is a condition in which there is a decrease in bone mass and density as well as an increase in the space between bones, resulting in porosity and fragility.
summary: Osteopenia and osteoporosis are conditions involving decreased bone density and strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2959
content: Overtraining is excessive frequency, volume, or intensity of training, resulting in fatigue, which is also caused by a lack of proper rest and recovery.
summary: Overtraining occurs when training exceeds the body's ability to recover, leading to fatigue.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2960
content: Oxygen Uptake is the usage of oxygen by the body. Oxygen Uptake Reserve (V. o 2 R) is the difference between resting and maximal or peak oxygen consumption.
summary: Oxygen uptake refers to the body's oxygen consumption, while oxygen uptake reserve measures the difference between resting and maximal oxygen consumption.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2961
content: The Parietal Lobe is a portion of the cerebral cortex that is involved with sensory information.
summary: The parietal lobe processes sensory information.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2962
content: Pattern Overload is repetitive physical activity that moves through the same patterns of motion, placing the same stresses on the body over time. Pattern Overload is consistently repeating the same pattern of motion, which may place abnormal stresses on the body.
summary: Pattern overload involves repetitive movements that can lead to stress on the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2963
content: Perception is the integrating of sensory information with past experiences or memories.
summary: Perception involves combining sensory input with past experiences.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2964
content: Perimysium is the connective tissue that surrounds fascicles.
summary: Perimysium is connective tissue surrounding muscle fascicles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2965
content: Periodization is the division of a training program into smaller, progressive stages.
summary: Periodization structures training programs into distinct phases.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2966
content: Periosteum is a dense membrane composed of fibrous connective tissue that closely wraps (invests) all bone, except that of the articulating surfaces in joints, which are covered by a synovial membrane.
summary: Periosteum is a dense membrane covering bones, except for joint surfaces.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2967
content: Peripheral Arterial Disease is a condition characterized by narrowing of the major arteries that are responsible for supplying blood to the lower extremities.
summary: Peripheral arterial disease involves narrowing of arteries supplying blood to the legs and feet.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2968
content: Peripheral Heart Action System (PHA) is a variation of circuit training in which the client performs four to six exercises in a row, rests for 30 to 45 seconds, then moves to the next sequence of different exercise and continues the pattern.
summary: PHA is a circuit training variation involving sets of exercises with short rest periods.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2969
content: Peripheral Nervous System is cranial and spinal nerves that spread throughout the body.
summary: The peripheral nervous system consists of nerves extending from the brain and spinal cord.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2970
content: Peripheral Vascular Disease is a group of diseases in which blood vessels become restricted or blocked, typically as a result of atherosclerosis.
summary: Peripheral vascular disease involves restricted or blocked blood vessels, often due to atherosclerosis.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2971
content: Phases of Training are smaller divisions of training progressions that fall within the three building blocks of training.
summary: Phases of training are subdivisions within larger training progressions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2972
content: Photoreceptors are sensory receptors that respond to light (vision).
summary: Photoreceptors are responsible for vision by detecting light.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2973
content: Physical Activity Readiness Questionnaire (PAR-Q) is a questionnaire that has been designed to help qualify a person for low-to-moderate-to-high activity levels.
summary: The PAR-Q assesses an individual's readiness for different activity levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2974
content: Pivot Joint allows movement in predominately the transverse plane; examples would include the atlantoaxial joint at the base of the skull and between the radioulnar joint.
summary: Pivot joints allow primarily rotational movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2975
content: Plane of Motion refers to the plane (sagittal, frontal, or transverse) in which the exercise is performed.
summary: Plane of motion describes the axis along which an exercise occurs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2976
content: Plantarflexion is ankle motion such that the toes are pointed toward the ground.
summary: Plantarflexion is the downward movement of the foot.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2977
content: Photoreceptors are sensory receptors that respond to light (vision).
summary: Photoreceptors are responsible for vision by responding to light.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2978
content: Physical Activity Readiness Questionnaire (PAR-Q) is a questionnaire that has been designed to help qualify a person for low-to-moderate-to-high activity levels.
summary: The PAR-Q is a questionnaire used to assess an individual's readiness for different activity levels.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2979
content: Pivot Joint allows movement in predominately the transverse plane; examples would include the atlantoaxial joint at the base of the skull and between the radioulnar joint.
summary: Pivot joints allow primarily rotational movement, exemplified by the atlantoaxial and radioulnar joints.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2980
content: Plane of Motion refers to the plane (sagittal, frontal, or transverse) in which the exercise is performed.
summary: The plane of motion describes the axis along which an exercise is performed.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2981
content: Plantarflexion is ankle motion such that the toes are pointed toward the ground.
summary: Plantarflexion is the downward movement of the foot, pointing the toes towards the ground.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2982
content: Plasma is an aqueous liquidlike component of blood.
summary: Plasma is the liquid portion of blood.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2983
content: Plyometric (Reactive) Training are exercises that generate quick, powerful movements involving an explosive concentric muscle contraction preceded by an eccentric muscle action.
summary: Plyometric training involves explosive movements using eccentric and concentric muscle contractions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2984
content: Posterior (Dorsal) is on the back of the body.
summary: Posterior refers to the back side of the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2985
content: Posterior Pelvic Tilt is a movement in which the pelvis rotates backward.
summary: Posterior pelvic tilt involves rotating the pelvis backward.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2986
content: Postural Distortion Patterns are predictable patterns of muscle imbalances.
summary: Postural distortion patterns are common imbalances in muscle groups.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2987
content: Postural Equilibrium is the ability to efficiently maintain balance throughout the body segments.
summary: Postural equilibrium refers to the body's ability to maintain balance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2988
content: Posture is the position and bearing of the body for alignment and function of the kinetic chain.
summary: Posture describes the body's alignment and position for proper movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2989
content: Power is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce the greatest force in the shortest time.
summary: Power is the ability to generate maximum force quickly.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2990
content: Pregnancy is the condition of a female who contains an unborn child within the body.
summary: Pregnancy is the state of carrying a developing fetus.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2991
content: Preprogrammed activation of muscles in healthy people occurs automatically and independently of other muscles before movement.
summary: Muscle activation before movement is automatic and independent of other muscles.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2992
content: Prime Mover is the muscle that acts as the initial and main source of motive power.
summary: The prime mover is the primary muscle responsible for a movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2993
content: The Principle of Individualism refers to the uniqueness of a program to the client for whom it is designed.
summary: Exercise programs should be tailored to individual needs and goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2994
