content: The Principle of Overload implies that there must be a training stimulus provided that exceeds the current capabilities of the kinetic chain to elicit the optimal physical, physiologic, and performance adaptations.
summary: Overload is necessary for training adaptations to occur.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2995
content: The Principle of Progression refers to the intentional manner in which a program is designed to progress according to the physiologic capabilities of the kinetic chain and the goals of the client.
summary: Exercise programs should progressively increase in difficulty.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2996
content: The Principle of Specificity or Specific Adaptation to Imposed Demands (SAID Principle) states that the body will adapt to the specific demands that are placed on it.
summary: The body adapts specifically to the type of training it receives.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2997
content: Processes are projections protruding from the bone where muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach.
summary: Processes are bony projections for muscle and ligament attachment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2998
content: Program Design is a purposeful system or plan put together to help an individual achieve a specific goal.
summary: Program design involves creating a structured plan to achieve fitness goals.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_2999
content: Pronation is a triplanar movement that is associated with force reduction.
summary: Pronation is a multi-directional movement that reduces force.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3000
content: Proprioception is the cumulative sensory input to the central nervous system from all mechanoreceptors that sense body position and limb movements.
summary: Proprioception is the body's awareness of its position and movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3001
content: A Proprioceptively Enriched Environment is an unstable (yet controllable) physical situation in which exercises are performed that causes the body to use its internal balance and stabilization mechanisms.
summary: Proprioceptively enriched environments challenge balance and stability.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3002
content: Protein is amino acids linked by peptide bonds, which consist of carbon, hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, and usually sulfur, and that have several essential biologic compounds.
summary: Protein is composed of amino acids and is essential for various biological functions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3003
content: Proximal is positioned nearest the center of the body or point of reference.
summary: Proximal refers to a body part's location closer to the center of the body.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3004
content: Deoxygenated blood is pumped from the right ventricle to the lungs through Pulmonary Arteries.
summary: Deoxygenated blood is transported from the right ventricle to the lungs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3005
content: Pulmonary Capillaries surround the alveolar sacs. As oxygen fills the sacs it diffuses across the capillary membranes and into the bloodstream.
summary: Oxygen diffuses from the lungs into the bloodstream through pulmonary capillaries.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3006
content: The Pulmonary Semilunar Valve controls blood flow from the right ventricle to the pulmonary arteries going to the lungs.
summary: The pulmonary semilunar valve regulates blood flow from the right ventricle to the lungs.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3007
content: The Pyramid System involves a triangle or step approach that either progresses up in weight with each set or decreases weight with each set.
summary: The pyramid system is a training method that progressively increases or decreases weight during sets.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3008
content: Pyruvate is a byproduct of anaerobic glycolysis.
summary: Pyruvate is produced during anaerobic energy production.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3009
content: Quadrilateral muscle fiber is an arrangement of muscle fibers that is usually flat and four-sided. An example would include the rhomboid.
summary: Quadrilateral muscle fibers are flat and four-sided, like those in the rhomboid muscle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3010
content: Quickness is the ability to react and change body position with maximal rate of force production, in all planes of motion and from all body positions, during functional activities.
summary: Quickness involves reacting and changing position rapidly with maximum force.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3011
content: Range of motion refers to the range that the body or bodily segments move during an exercise.
summary: Range of motion describes the extent of movement possible during an exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3012
content: Rapport is an aspect of a relationship characterized by similarity, agreement, or congruity.
summary: Rapport describes a sense of connection and understanding in a relationship.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3013
content: Muscular force can be amplified by increasing the rate of incoming impulses from the motor neuron after all prospective motor units have been activated.
summary: Muscle force can be increased by increasing the rate of nerve impulses.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3014
content: Rate of force production is the ability of muscles to exert maximal force output in a minimal amount of time.
summary: Rate of force production is the speed at which muscles generate maximum force.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3015
content: Reactive strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to switch from an eccentric contraction to a concentric contraction quickly and efficiently.
summary: Reactive strength involves the rapid transition from an eccentric to a concentric muscle contraction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3016
content: Reactive training are exercises that use quick, powerful movements involving an eccentric contraction immediately followed by an explosive concentric contraction.
summary: Reactive training utilizes explosive movements with eccentric and concentric contractions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3017
content: Rapport is an aspect of a relationship characterized by similarity, agreement, or congruity.
summary: Rapport describes the similarity and agreement within a relationship.

content: Muscular force can be amplified by increasing the rate of incoming impulses from the motor neuron after all prospective motor units have been activated.
summary: Increasing the rate of motor neuron impulses can amplify muscular force.

content: Rate of force production is the ability of muscles to exert maximal force output in a minimal amount of time.
summary: Rate of force production measures the speed at which muscles generate maximum force.

summary: Reactive strength describes the neuromuscular system's ability to transition from eccentric to concentric contractions rapidly.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3021
content: Reactive training are exercises that use quick, powerful movements involving an eccentric contraction immediately followed by an explosive concentric contraction.
summary: Reactive training involves exercises with quick, powerful movements alternating between eccentric and concentric contractions.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3022
content: Reciprocal inhibition is the simultaneous contraction of one muscle and the relaxation of its antagonist to allow movement to take place.
summary: Reciprocal inhibition describes the simultaneous contraction of one muscle and relaxation of its opposing muscle to facilitate movement.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3023
content: Recommended Dietary Allowance (RDA) is the average daily nutrient intake level that is sufficient to meet the nutrient requirement of nearly all (97 to 98%) healthy individuals who are in a particular life stage and gender group.
summary: RDA represents the average daily nutrient intake needed to meet the requirements of most healthy individuals in a specific life stage and gender.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3024
content: Recreation is a client’s physical activities outside of their work environment.
summary: Recreation encompasses physical activities individuals engage in outside their work.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3025
content: Relative flexibility is the tendency of the body to seek the path of least resistance during functional movement patterns.
summary: Relative flexibility refers to the body's tendency to move along the path of least resistance during functional movements.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3026
content: Relative strength is the maximum force that an individual can generate per unit of body weight, regardless of the time of force development.
summary: Relative strength measures the maximum force an individual can produce per unit of body weight.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3027
content: Remodeling is the process of resorption and formation of bone.
summary: Remodeling describes the continuous process of bone resorption and formation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3028
content: Repetition is one complete movement of a single exercise.
summary: A repetition consists of one full execution of a single exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3029
content: Repetition tempo is the speed with which each repetition is performed.
summary: Repetition tempo refers to the speed at which each repetition of an exercise is executed.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3030
content: Resistance development is the second stage of the General Adaptation Syndrome (GAS), when the body increases its functional capacity to adapt to the stressor.
summary: Resistance development is the second stage of GAS, where the body adapts to stress by increasing its functional capacity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3031
content: Respiratory passageway collects the channelled air coming from the conducting passageway.
summary: The respiratory passageway collects air channeled from the conducting passageway.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3032
content: Respiratory pump is composed of skeletal structures (bones) and soft tissues (muscles) that work together to allow proper respiratory mechanics to occur and help pump blood back to the heart during inspiration.
summary: The respiratory pump, consisting of bones and muscles, facilitates respiratory mechanics and blood circulation during inhalation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3033
content: Respiratory system is a system of organs (the lungs and respiratory passageways) that collects oxygen from the external environment and transports it to the bloodstream.
summary: The respiratory system, comprising the lungs and passageways, collects oxygen and delivers it to the bloodstream.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3034
content: Rest interval is the time taken to recuperate between sets.
summary: Rest interval refers to the time allotted for recovery between sets of exercise.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3035
content: Restrictive lung disease is the condition of a fibrous lung tissue, which results in a decreased ability to expand the lungs.
summary: Restrictive lung disease involves thickened lung tissue, hindering lung expansion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3036
content: Rheumatoid Arthritis is a type of arthritis that primarily affects connective tissues.
summary: Rheumatoid Arthritis primarily affects connective tissues.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3037
content: Rheumatoid Arthritis involves thickening of articular soft tissue and extension of synovial tissue over eroded articular cartilages.
summary: Rheumatoid Arthritis is characterized by thickened articular soft tissue and synovial tissue extending over damaged cartilage.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3038
content: Roll is a type of joint motion that depicts the rolling of one joint surface on another.
summary: Roll describes the rolling motion of one joint surface over another.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3039
content: An example of roll is the femoral condyles rolling over the tibial condyles during a squat.
summary: The femoral condyles rolling over the tibial condyles during a squat exemplifies the roll joint motion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3040
content: Root Cause Analysis is a method of asking questions step-by-step to discover the initial cause of a fault.
summary: Root Cause Analysis systematically investigates to identify the root cause of a problem.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3041
content: Rotary Motion is the movement of bones around joints.
summary: Rotary Motion refers to the movement of bones around joints.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3042
content: A Saddle Joint is a type of joint where one bone is shaped as a saddle and the other bone is shaped as the rider.
summary: A Saddle Joint features one bone shaped like a saddle and the other like a rider.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3043
content: The only example of a Saddle Joint is in the carpometacarpal joint in the thumb.
summary: The carpometacarpal joint in the thumb is the sole example of a Saddle Joint.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3044
content: The Sagittal Plane is an imaginary bisector that divides the body into left and right halves.
summary: The Sagittal Plane is an imaginary line dividing the body into left and right sections.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3045
content: The Sarcolemma is a plasma membrane that surrounds muscle fibers.
summary: The Sarcolemma is the plasma membrane enclosing muscle fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3046
content: A Sarcomere is the functional unit of muscle that produces muscular contraction and consists of repeating sections of actin and myosin.
summary: A Sarcomere is the muscle's functional unit, responsible for contraction and composed of repeating actin and myosin sections.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3047
content: Sarcoplasm contains glycogen, fats, minerals, and oxygen within the sarcolemma.
summary: Sarcoplasm, within the sarcolemma, stores glycogen, fats, minerals, and oxygen.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3048
content: Scapular Depression is the downward (inferior) motion of the scapula.
summary: Scapular Depression refers to the downward movement of the scapula.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3049
content: Scapular Elevation is the upward (superior) motion of the scapula.
summary: Scapular Elevation describes the upward movement of the scapula.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3050
content: Scapular Protraction is the abduction of the scapula; shoulder blades move away from the midline.
summary: Scapular Protraction involves the shoulder blades moving away from the midline.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3051
content: Scapular Retraction is the adduction of the scapula; shoulder blades move toward the midline.
summary: Scapular Retraction involves the shoulder blades moving towards the midline.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3052
content: Self-Myofascial Release is a form of flexibility that focuses on the fascial system in the body.
summary: Self-Myofascial Release targets the fascial system to improve flexibility.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3053
content: Self-Organization is a theory based on the dynamic pattern perspective that provides the body with the ability to overcome changes.
summary: Self-Organization theory explains the body's ability to adapt and overcome changes through dynamic patterns.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3054
content: Semilunar Valves allow for proper blood flow from the ventricles to the aorta and pulmonary arteries.
summary: Semilunar Valves regulate blood flow from the ventricles to the aorta and pulmonary arteries.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3055
content: Sensation is the process whereby sensory information is received by the receptor and transferred to the spinal cord for reflexive motor behavior or to higher cortical areas for processing.
summary: Sensation involves receiving sensory information, transmitting it to the spinal cord for reflexes or higher brain areas for processing.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3056
content: Sensorimotor Integration is the ability of the nervous system to gather and interpret sensory information to anticipate, select, and execute the proper motor response.
summary: Sensorimotor Integration describes the nervous system's ability to process sensory information to plan and execute appropriate motor responses.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3057
content: Self-Myofascial Release is a form of flexibility that focuses on the fascial system in the body. Self-Organization is a theory based on the dynamic pattern perspective that provides the body with the ability to overcome changes.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3058
content: Semilunar Valves allow for proper blood flow from the ventricles to the aorta and pulmonary arteries. The sinoatrial (SA) node is a specialized area of cardiac tissue located in the right atrium of the heart. The sinoatrial (SA) node initiates the electrical impulses that determine the heart rate and is often termed the pacemaker for the heart.
summary: This chunk explains the function of semilunar valves and the sinoatrial node in the heart.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3059
content: Sensation is the process whereby sensory information is received by the receptor and transferred to the spinal cord for reflexive motor behavior or to higher cortical areas for processing. Sensorimotor Integration is the ability of the nervous system to gather and interpret sensory information to anticipate, select, and execute the proper motor response. Sensorimotor Integration is also the cooperation of the nervous and muscular system in gathering and interpreting information and executing movement.  Sensors provide feedback from the effectors to the central controller and cardiovascular control system. Sensors include baroreceptors, chemoreceptors, and muscle afferents. Sensory Feedback is the process whereby sensory information is used to reactively monitor movement and the environment. Sensory (Function of Nervous System) is the ability of the nervous system to sense changes in either the internal or external environment.
summary: This chunk defines and explains various aspects of sensory processing and integration.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3060
content: Sensory (Afferent) Neurons transmit nerve impulses from effector sites (such as muscles and organs) via receptors to the brain and spinal cord.
summary: This chunk describes the function of sensory neurons.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3061
content: The skeletal system is the body’s framework composed of bones and joints. Short bones appear cubical in shape. Examples of short bones include the carpals and tarsals.
summary: This chunk defines the skeletal system and provides information about short bones.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3062
content: A skin-fold caliper is an instrument with two adjustable legs used to measure the thickness of a skin fold.
summary: This chunk describes the function of a skin-fold caliper.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3063
content: Slide is a joint motion that depicts the sliding of one joint surface across another. An example of slide joint motion is the tibial condyles moving across the femoral condyles during a knee extension.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3064
content: The sliding filament theory is the proposed process by which the contraction of the filaments within the sarcomere takes place.
summary: This chunk describes the sliding filament theory of muscle contraction.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3065
content: Slow twitch fibers are another term for type I muscle fibers. Slow twitch fibers are characterized by a greater amount of capillaries, mitochondria, and myoglobin. Slow twitch fibers usually have a higher endurance capacity than fast twitch fibers.
summary: This chunk describes the characteristics and properties of slow twitch muscle fibers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3066
content: A specific warm-up consists of low-intensity exercise consisting of movements that mimic those that will be included in the more intense exercise that is to follow.
summary: This chunk defines a specific warm-up.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3067
content: Speed is the ability to move the body in one intended direction as fast as possible. Speed strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce the greatest possible force in the shortest possible time.
summary: This chunk defines speed and speed strength.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3068
content: Spin is a joint motion that depicts the rotation of one joint surface on another. An example of spin joint motion is the head of the radius rotating on the end of the humerus during pronation and supination of the forearm.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3069
content: The split-routine system incorporates training an individual’s body parts with a high volume on separate days.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3070
content: Stability is the ability of the body to maintain postural equilibrium and support joints during movement. Stabilization endurance is the ability of the stabilization mechanisms of the kinetic chain to sustain proper levels of stabilization to allow for prolonged neuromuscular efficiency. Stabilization strength is the ability of the stabilizing muscles to provide dynamic joint stabilization and postural equilibrium during functional activities. Stabilizers are muscles that support or stabilize the body while the prime movers and synergists perform the movement patterns.
summary: This chunk defines and explains stability, stabilization endurance, stabilization strength, and the role of stabilizers.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3071

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3072
content: Static stretching is the process of passively taking a muscle to the point of tension and holding the stretch for a minimum of 30 seconds.
summary: This chunk defines static stretching.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3073
content: Strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce internal tension to overcome an external load. Strength endurance is the ability of the body to repeatedly produce high levels of force for prolonged periods.
summary: This chunk defines strength and strength endurance.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3074
content: Stride length is the distance covered with each stride.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3076

content: Starting strength is the ability to produce high levels of force at the beginning of a movement. Strength is the ability of the neuromuscular system to produce internal tension to overcome an external load. Strength endurance is the ability of the body to repeatedly produce high levels of force for prolonged periods.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3078

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3087

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3089

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3097

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3098

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3099

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3102

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3103

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3104

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3105

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3108

summary: Definition of a trochanter as a bony projection for muscle attachment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3116
content: A tubercle is a projection protruding from the bone to which muscles, tendons, and ligaments can attach; also known as a condyle, process, epicondyle, and trochanter.
summary: Definition of a tubercle as a bony projection for muscle attachment.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3117
content: Type refers to the type or mode of physical activity that an individual is engaged in.
summary: Definition of 'type' in the context of physical activity.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3118
content: Unipenniform muscle fibers are muscle fibers that are arranged with short, oblique fibers that extend from one side of a long tendon. An example would include the tibialis posterior.
summary: Description of unipenniform muscle fiber arrangement and an example muscle.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3119
content: Upper-Extremity Postural Distortion is an individual who exhibits a forward head, rounded shoulder posture.
summary: Definition of Upper-Extremity Postural Distortion.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3120
content: The Valsalva Maneuver is a maneuver in which a person tries to exhale forcibly with a closed glottis (windpipe) so that no air exits through the mouth or nose as, for example, in lifting a heavyweight. The Valsalva maneuver impedes the return of venous blood to the heart.
summary: Description of the Valsalva Maneuver and its physiological effect.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3121
content: Veins are vessels that transport blood from the capillaries toward the heart.
summary: Definition of veins and their function in blood circulation.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3122
content: Ventilation is the actual process of moving air in and out of the body.
summary: Definition of ventilation as the process of breathing.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3123
content: Ventilatory Threshold is the point during graded exercise in which ventilation increases disproportionately to oxygen uptake, signifying a switch from predominantly aerobic energy production to anaerobic energy production.
summary: Definition of Ventilatory Threshold and its significance in exercise physiology.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3124
content: Ventral refers to a position on the front or toward the front of the body.
summary: Definition of 'ventral' as a directional term in anatomy.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3125
content: Ventricles are the inferior chamber of the heart that receives blood from its corresponding atrium and, in turn, forces blood into the arteries.
summary: Description of the ventricles as the lower chambers of the heart.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3126
content: Venules are the very small veins that connect capillaries to the larger veins.
summary: Definition of venules as small blood vessels connecting capillaries to veins.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3127
content: The Vertebral Column is a series of irregularly shaped bones called vertebrae that houses the spinal cord.
summary: Description of the vertebral column and its function.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3128
content: Vertical Loading is alternating body parts trained from set to set, starting from the upper extremity and moving to the lower extremity.
summary: Definition of Vertical Loading as a training method.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3129
content: VO2max is the highest rate of oxygen transport and utilization achieved at maximal physical exertion.
summary: Definition of VO2max as a measure of aerobic fitness.

id: NASM Essentials of Personal Fitness Training_3130
content: A Weekly Plan is a training plan of specific workouts that spans 1 week and shows which exercises are required each day of the week.
summary: Definition of a Weekly Plan in the context of training.
